subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Two coherent light waves, each of intensity {tex}{I}_{0}{/tex} superpose each other and produce interference pattern on a screen. Obtain the expression for the resultant intensity at a point where the phase difference between the waves is {tex}\phi{/tex}. Write its maximum and minimum possible values.
  2. In a single slit diffraction experiment, the aperture of the slit is 3 mm and the separation between the slit and the screen is 1.5 m. A monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm is normally incident on the slit. Calculate the distance of
    1. first order minimum, and
    2. second order maximum, from the centre of the screen.
question_text: Two coherent light waves, each of intensity {tex}{I}_{0}{/tex} superpose each other and produce interference pattern on a screen. Obtain the expression for the resultant intensity at a point where the phase difference between the waves is {tex}\phi{/tex} . Write its maximum and minimum possible values. In a single slit diffraction experiment, the aperture of the slit is 3 mm and the separation between the slit and the screen is 1.5 m. A monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm is normally incident on the slit. Calculate the distance of first order minimum, and second order maximum, from the centre of the screen.
explanation: {tex} y_1=a \cos \omega t {/tex} {tex} y_2=a \cos (\omega t+\phi) {/tex} According to Principle of Superposition {tex} {y} =y_1+y_2 {/tex} {tex} =a[\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)] {/tex} {tex} =2 a \cos \phi / 2 \cos (\omega t+\phi / 2) {/tex} {tex} {y}=\operatorname{Acos}(\omega {t}+\phi / 2) {/tex} where, {tex}{A}=2 a \cos \phi / 2{/tex} {tex} I=k A^2 {/tex} {tex} I=k\left(4 a^2 \cos ^2 \phi / 2\right) {/tex} {tex} I=4 I_0 \cos ^2 \phi / 2 {/tex} Maximum value {tex}I=4 I_0{/tex} Minimum value {tex}I=0{/tex} Position of first order minimum {tex} y =\frac{n \lambda D}{a} {/tex} {tex} y_1 =\frac{\lambda D}{a} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{3 \times 10^{-3}}=3 \times 10^{-4} {~m} {/tex} Position of second order maximum {tex} y_n=(2 n+1) \frac{\lambda D}{2 a} {/tex} {tex} n=2, y_2 =\frac{5 \lambda D}{2 a} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{5 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}=7.5 \times 10^{-4} {~m} {/tex}
solution: {tex} y_1=a \cos \omega t {/tex} {tex} y_2=a \cos (\omega t+\phi) {/tex} According to Principle of Superposition {tex} {y} =y_1+y_2 {/tex} {tex} =a[\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)] {/tex} {tex} =2 a \cos \phi / 2 \cos (\omega t+\phi / 2) {/tex} {tex} {y}=\operatorname{Acos}(\omega {t}+\phi / 2) {/tex} where, {tex}{A}=2 a \cos \phi / 2{/tex} {tex} I=k A^2 {/tex} {tex} I=k\left(4 a^2 \cos ^2 \phi / 2\right) {/tex} {tex} I=4 I_0 \cos ^2 \phi / 2 {/tex} Maximum value {tex}I=4 I_0{/tex} Minimum value {tex}I=0{/tex} Position of first order minimum {tex} y =\frac{n \lambda D}{a} {/tex} {tex} y_1 =\frac{\lambda D}{a} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{3 \times 10^{-3}}=3 \times 10^{-4} {~m} {/tex} Position of second order maximum {tex} y_n=(2 n+1) \frac{\lambda D}{2 a} {/tex} {tex} n=2, y_2 =\frac{5 \lambda D}{2 a} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{5 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}=7.5 \times 10^{-4} {~m} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} y_1=a \cos \omega t {/tex}
    {tex} y_2=a \cos (\omega t+\phi) {/tex}
    According to Principle of Superposition
    {tex} {y} =y_1+y_2 {/tex}
    {tex} =a[\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)] {/tex}
    {tex} =2 a \cos \phi / 2 \cos (\omega t+\phi / 2) {/tex}
    {tex} {y}=\operatorname{Acos}(\omega {t}+\phi / 2) {/tex}
    where, {tex}{A}=2 a \cos \phi / 2{/tex}
    {tex} I=k A^2 {/tex}
    {tex} I=k\left(4 a^2 \cos ^2 \phi / 2\right) {/tex}
    {tex} I=4 I_0 \cos ^2 \phi / 2 {/tex}
    Maximum value {tex}I=4 I_0{/tex}
    Minimum value {tex}I=0{/tex}
    1. Position of first order minimum
      {tex} y =\frac{n \lambda D}{a} {/tex}
      {tex} y_1 =\frac{\lambda D}{a} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{3 \times 10^{-3}}=3 \times 10^{-4} {~m} {/tex}
    2. Position of second order maximum
      {tex} y_n=(2 n+1) \frac{\lambda D}{2 a} {/tex}
      {tex} n=2, y_2 =\frac{5 \lambda D}{2 a} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{5 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}=7.5 \times 10^{-4} {~m} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Using Huygens' principle, explain the refraction of a plane wavefront, propagating in air, at a plane interface between air and glass. Hence verify Snell's law.
  2. Use mirror formula to deduce that a convex mirror always produces a virtual image of an object kept in front of it.
question_text: Using Huygens' principle, explain the refraction of a plane wavefront, propagating in air, at a plane interface between air and glass. Hence verify Snell's law. Use mirror formula to deduce that a convex mirror always produces a virtual image of an object kept in front of it.
explanation: {tex}\sin {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} {tex}\sin {r}=\frac{{AE}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{c} / {n}_{1}}{{c} / {n}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{n}_{2}}{{n}_{1}}{/tex} or {tex}{n}_{1} \sin {i}={n}_{2} \sin {r}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{{u}}=\frac{1}{{f}}{/tex} {tex}u \lt 0, f \gt 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{(-u)}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}} \text { is positive }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {v} \text { is positive } \Rightarrow \text { virtual image }{/tex}
solution: {tex}\sin {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} {tex}\sin {r}=\frac{{AE}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{c} / {n}_{1}}{{c} / {n}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{n}_{2}}{{n}_{1}}{/tex} or {tex}{n}_{1} \sin {i}={n}_{2} \sin {r}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{{u}}=\frac{1}{{f}}{/tex} {tex}u \lt 0, f \gt 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{(-u)}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}} \text { is positive }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {v} \text { is positive } \Rightarrow \text { virtual image }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\sin {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex}
    {tex}\sin {r}=\frac{{AE}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{c} / {n}_{1}}{{c} / {n}_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{n}_{2}}{{n}_{1}}{/tex} or {tex}{n}_{1} \sin {i}={n}_{2} \sin {r}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}+\frac{1}{{u}}=\frac{1}{{f}}{/tex}
    {tex}u \lt 0, f \gt 0{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{(-u)}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{{v}} \text { is positive }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {v} \text { is positive } \Rightarrow \text { virtual image }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: In Young's double slit experiment, the screen is moved 30 cm towards the slits. As a consequence, the fringe width of the pattern changes by 0.09 mm. If the slits separation used is 2 mm, calculate the wavelength of light used in the experiment.
question_text: In Young's double slit experiment, the screen is moved 30 cm towards the slits. As a consequence, the fringe width of the pattern changes by 0.09 mm. If the slits separation used is 2 mm, calculate the wavelength of light used in the experiment.
explanation: {tex}\beta_{1}=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{D_{2} \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}=\frac{\lambda}{d}\left(D_{1}-D_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}==\frac{\lambda}{d}\left[D-\left(D-30 \times 10^{-2}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{\left(\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}\right) d}{30 \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=6 \times 10^{-7} {~m}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\beta_{1}=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{D_{2} \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}=\frac{\lambda}{d}\left(D_{1}-D_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}==\frac{\lambda}{d}\left[D-\left(D-30 \times 10^{-2}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{\left(\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}\right) d}{30 \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=6 \times 10^{-7} {~m}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\beta_{1}=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
{tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{D_{2} \lambda}{d}{/tex}
{tex}\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}=\frac{\lambda}{d}\left(D_{1}-D_{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}==\frac{\lambda}{d}\left[D-\left(D-30 \times 10^{-2}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\lambda=\frac{\left(\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}\right) d}{30 \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda=6 \times 10^{-7} {~m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
Write two differences in the patterns of double-slit interference experiment and single-slit diffraction experiment.
Light waves from two pinholes illuminated by two sodium lamps do not produce interference patterns. Explain why.
question_text: Write two differences in the patterns of double-slit interference experiment and single-slit diffraction experiment. Light waves from two pinholes illuminated by two sodium lamps do not produce interference patterns. Explain why.
explanation: Interference pattern Diffraction pattern The interference pattern has a number of equally spaced bright and dark bands. The diffraction pattern has a central bright maximum which is twice as wide as the other maxima. The interference pattern is obtained by superposing two waves originating from the two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is obtained by superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit. The maximas are of same intensity. The intensity falls as we go to successive maxima away from the centre, on either side. Two sodium lamps are incoherent source of light.
solution: Interference pattern Diffraction pattern The interference pattern has a number of equally spaced bright and dark bands. The diffraction pattern has a central bright maximum which is twice as wide as the other maxima. The interference pattern is obtained by superposing two waves originating from the two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is obtained by superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit. The maximas are of same intensity. The intensity falls as we go to successive maxima away from the centre, on either side. Two sodium lamps are incoherent source of light.
solution_html:
Interference pattern Diffraction pattern
The interference pattern has a number of equally spaced bright and dark bands. The diffraction pattern has a central bright maximum which is twice as wide as the other maxima.
The interference pattern is obtained by superposing two waves originating from the two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is obtained by superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit.
The maximas are of same intensity. The intensity falls as we go to successive maxima away from the centre, on either side.

Two sodium lamps are incoherent source of light.

subject: Physics
question_html:
In Young's double slit experiment, the separation between the two slits is 1.0 mm and the screen is 1.0 m away from the slits. A beam of light consisting of two wavelengths 500 nm and 600 nm is used to obtain interference fringes. Calculate:

  1. the distance between the first maxima for the two wavelengths.
  2. the least distance from the central maximum, where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide.
question_text: In Young's double slit experiment, the separation between the two slits is 1.0 mm and the screen is 1.0 m away from the slits. A beam of light consisting of two wavelengths 500 nm and 600 nm is used to obtain interference fringes. Calculate: the distance between the first maxima for the two wavelengths. the least distance from the central maximum, where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide.
explanation: Distance {tex}=\frac{{n} \lambda_1 {D}}{{d}}-\frac{{n} \lambda_2 {D}}{{d}}{/tex} For {tex}{n}=1{/tex} {tex} \text { Distance } =\frac{(600-500) \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} =10^{-4} {~m} {/tex} {tex} {n} \lambda_1 \frac{{D}}{{d}}=({n}+1) \lambda_2 \frac{{D}}{{d}}{/tex} {tex} n \times 600 \times 10^{-9}=(n+1) \times 500 \times 10^{-9} {/tex} {tex} n=5 {/tex} {tex} {x}=5 \times \frac{\lambda_1 {D}}{{~d}} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{5 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}} =3 {~mm}{/tex}
solution: Distance {tex}=\frac{{n} \lambda_1 {D}}{{d}}-\frac{{n} \lambda_2 {D}}{{d}}{/tex} For {tex}{n}=1{/tex} {tex} \text { Distance } =\frac{(600-500) \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} =10^{-4} {~m} {/tex} {tex} {n} \lambda_1 \frac{{D}}{{d}}=({n}+1) \lambda_2 \frac{{D}}{{d}}{/tex} {tex} n \times 600 \times 10^{-9}=(n+1) \times 500 \times 10^{-9} {/tex} {tex} n=5 {/tex} {tex} {x}=5 \times \frac{\lambda_1 {D}}{{~d}} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{5 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}} =3 {~mm}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Distance {tex}=\frac{{n} \lambda_1 {D}}{{d}}-\frac{{n} \lambda_2 {D}}{{d}}{/tex}
    For {tex}{n}=1{/tex}
    {tex} \text { Distance } =\frac{(600-500) \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}} {/tex}
    {tex} =10^{-4} {~m} {/tex}
  2. {tex} {n} \lambda_1 \frac{{D}}{{d}}=({n}+1) \lambda_2 \frac{{D}}{{d}}{/tex}
    {tex} n \times 600 \times 10^{-9}=(n+1) \times 500 \times 10^{-9} {/tex}
    {tex} n=5 {/tex}
    {tex} {x}=5 \times \frac{\lambda_1 {D}}{{~d}} {/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{5 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}} =3 {~mm}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the angle of diffraction (in degrees) for first secondary maximum of the pattern due to diffraction at a single slit. The width of the slit and wavelength of light used are 0.55 mm and 550 nm, respectively.
question_text: Find the angle of diffraction (in degrees) for first secondary maximum of the pattern due to diffraction at a single slit. The width of the slit and wavelength of light used are 0.55 mm and 550 nm, respectively.
explanation: {tex}\theta=(2 {n}+1) \frac{\lambda}{2 {a}}{/tex} For first secondary maxima {tex}{n}=1{/tex} {tex}\theta=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 {a}}{/tex} {tex}\theta=\frac{3 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 0.55 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}\theta=1.5 \times 10^{-3}{/tex} radian {tex}=0.086{/tex} degree
solution: {tex}\theta=(2 {n}+1) \frac{\lambda}{2 {a}}{/tex} For first secondary maxima {tex}{n}=1{/tex} {tex}\theta=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 {a}}{/tex} {tex}\theta=\frac{3 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 0.55 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}\theta=1.5 \times 10^{-3}{/tex} radian {tex}=0.086{/tex} degree
solution_html:
{tex}\theta=(2 {n}+1) \frac{\lambda}{2 {a}}{/tex}
For first secondary maxima {tex}{n}=1{/tex}
{tex}\theta=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 {a}}{/tex}
{tex}\theta=\frac{3 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 0.55 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
{tex}\theta=1.5 \times 10^{-3}{/tex}
radian {tex}=0.086{/tex} degree

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define 'wavefront' of a light wave. A plane wavefront is refracted from a convex lens. Draw the shape of the refracted wavefront.
  2. A plane wave travelling in a medium is incident on a plane surface separating this medium from a rarer medium. Draw a diagram to show refraction of the wave. Hence, verify Snell's law.
question_text: Define 'wavefront' of a light wave. A plane wavefront is refracted from a convex lens. Draw the shape of the refracted wavefront. A plane wave travelling in a medium is incident on a plane surface separating this medium from a rarer medium. Draw a diagram to show refraction of the wave. Hence, verify Snell's law.
explanation: Wave front- It is a continuous locus at every point on it, all the particles of medium are vibrating in same phase. Considering triangle ABC and AEC {tex}\operatorname{Sin} {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\operatorname{Sin} r=\frac{A E}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\frac{\operatorname{Sin} {i}}{\operatorname{Sin} {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}=\frac{{n}_{2}}{{n}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}{n}_{1} \operatorname{Sin} {i}={n}_{2} \operatorname{Sin} {r}{/tex}
solution: Wave front- It is a continuous locus at every point on it, all the particles of medium are vibrating in same phase. Considering triangle ABC and AEC {tex}\operatorname{Sin} {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\operatorname{Sin} r=\frac{A E}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\frac{\operatorname{Sin} {i}}{\operatorname{Sin} {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}=\frac{{n}_{2}}{{n}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}{n}_{1} \operatorname{Sin} {i}={n}_{2} \operatorname{Sin} {r}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Wave front- It is a continuous locus at every point on it, all the particles of medium are vibrating in same phase.


  2. Considering triangle ABC and AEC
    {tex}\operatorname{Sin} {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} ...(i)
    {tex}\operatorname{Sin} r=\frac{A E}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} ...(ii)
    {tex}\frac{\operatorname{Sin} {i}}{\operatorname{Sin} {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}=\frac{{n}_{2}}{{n}_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}{n}_{1} \operatorname{Sin} {i}={n}_{2} \operatorname{Sin} {r}{/tex}

question_html: In Young's double slit experimental set-up, the intensity of the central maximum is {tex}{I}_{{o}}{/tex}. Calculate the intensity at a point where the path difference between two interfering waves is {tex}\lambda / 3{/tex}.
question_text: In Young's double slit experimental set-up, the intensity of the central maximum is {tex}{I}_{{o}}{/tex} . Calculate the intensity at a point where the path difference between two interfering waves is {tex}\lambda / 3{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \Delta x {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{3} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \pi}{3} {/tex} {tex} I^{\prime} =4 I \cos ^2 \phi / 2 {/tex} {tex}\text { Given } 4 I=I_0 {/tex} {tex} =I_0 \cos ^2 2 \pi / 6 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{I_0}{4} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \Delta x {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{3} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \pi}{3} {/tex} {tex} I^{\prime} =4 I \cos ^2 \phi / 2 {/tex} {tex}\text { Given } 4 I=I_0 {/tex} {tex} =I_0 \cos ^2 2 \pi / 6 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{I_0}{4} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \Delta x {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{3} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{2 \pi}{3} {/tex}
{tex} I^{\prime} =4 I \cos ^2 \phi / 2 {/tex} {tex}\text { Given } 4 I=I_0 {/tex}
{tex} =I_0 \cos ^2 2 \pi / 6 {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{I_0}{4} {/tex}

question_html:
1. "You cannot see a person standing on the other side of a boundary wall but can hear him." Explain with reason.
  2. Light of wavelength 750 nm is incident normally on a slit of width 1.5 mm. Diffraction pattern is obtained on a screen 1.0 m away from the slit. Find the distance of the nearest point from the central maxima at which the intensity is zero.
question_text: "You cannot see a person standing on the other side of a boundary wall but can hear him." Explain with reason. Light of wavelength 750 nm is incident normally on a slit of width 1.5 mm. Diffraction pattern is obtained on a screen 1.0 m away from the slit. Find the distance of the nearest point from the central maxima at which the intensity is zero.
explanation: Wavelength of light is very small as compared to size of obstacles so diffraction of light is not seen easily. But sound waves have large wavelength, so they get diffracted easily by obstacles. Position of first minima {tex} x= \frac{\lambda D}{a} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{750 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} =0.5 {~mm} {/tex}
solution: Wavelength of light is very small as compared to size of obstacles so diffraction of light is not seen easily. But sound waves have large wavelength, so they get diffracted easily by obstacles. Position of first minima {tex} x= \frac{\lambda D}{a} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{750 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} =0.5 {~mm} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Wavelength of light is very small as compared to size of obstacles so diffraction of light is not seen easily. But sound waves have large wavelength, so they get diffracted easily by obstacles.
  2. Position of first minima
    {tex} x= \frac{\lambda D}{a} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{750 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex}
    {tex} =0.5 {~mm} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: In a double slit experiment, the two slits are 1.5 mm apart. The slits are illuminated by a mixture of lights of wavelengths of 600 nm and 400 nm and the interference pattern is observed on a screen 1.5 m away from the slits. Find the minimum distance of the point from the central maximum at which bright fringes of the interference patterns of the two wavelengths coincide.
question_text: In a double slit experiment, the two slits are 1.5 mm apart. The slits are illuminated by a mixture of lights of wavelengths of 600 nm and 400 nm and the interference pattern is observed on a screen 1.5 m away from the slits. Find the minimum distance of the point from the central maximum at which bright fringes of the interference patterns of the two wavelengths coincide.
explanation: {tex}n \lambda_{1}=(n+1) \lambda_{2}{/tex} {tex}n \times 600=(n+1) \times 400{/tex} {tex}\therefore n=2{/tex} {tex}\therefore x=\frac{2 \lambda_{1} D}{d}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}x=1.2 \times 10^{-3} {~m}{/tex}
solution: {tex}n \lambda_{1}=(n+1) \lambda_{2}{/tex} {tex}n \times 600=(n+1) \times 400{/tex} {tex}\therefore n=2{/tex} {tex}\therefore x=\frac{2 \lambda_{1} D}{d}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}x=1.2 \times 10^{-3} {~m}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}n \lambda_{1}=(n+1) \lambda_{2}{/tex}
{tex}n \times 600=(n+1) \times 400{/tex}
{tex}\therefore n=2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore x=\frac{2 \lambda_{1} D}{d}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
{tex}x=1.2 \times 10^{-3} {~m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Find the intensity at a point on the screen in Young's double slit experiment, at which the interfering waves of intensity {tex}{I}_{0}{/tex} each, have a path difference of (i) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex}, and (ii) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the intensity at a point on the screen in Young's double slit experiment, at which the interfering waves of intensity {tex}{I}_{0}{/tex} each, have a path difference of (i) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex} , and (ii) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\Delta \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta x{/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{3}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}I =4 I_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} {tex}I =4 I_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}I =I_{0}{/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}=\pi{/tex} {tex}I=4 I_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}I=0{/tex}
solution: {tex}\Delta \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta x{/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{3}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}I =4 I_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} {tex}I =4 I_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}I =I_{0}{/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}=\pi{/tex} {tex}I=4 I_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}I=0{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}\Delta \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta x{/tex}
    {tex}\Delta \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{3}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}
    {tex}I =4 I_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}I =4 I_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
    {tex}I =I_{0}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}=\pi{/tex}
    {tex}I=4 I_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}I=0{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: In a double-slit experiment, {tex}6^{\text {th }}{/tex} dark fringe is observed at a certain point of the screen. A transparent sheet of thickness {tex}t{/tex} and refractive index {tex}n{/tex} is now introduced in the path of one of the two interfering waves to increase its phase by {tex}2 \pi(n-1) t / \lambda{/tex}. The pattern is shifted and {tex}8^{\text {th }}{/tex} bright fringe is observed at the same point. Find the relation for thickness t in terms of n and {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
question_text: In a double-slit experiment, {tex}6^{\text {th }}{/tex} dark fringe is observed at a certain point of the screen. A transparent sheet of thickness {tex}t{/tex} and refractive index {tex}n{/tex} is now introduced in the path of one of the two interfering waves to increase its phase by {tex}2 \pi(n-1) t / \lambda{/tex} . The pattern is shifted and {tex}8^{\text {th }}{/tex} bright fringe is observed at the same point. Find the relation for thickness t in terms of n and {tex}\lambda{/tex} .
explanation: Phase difference for {tex}6^{\text {th }}{/tex} dark fringe {tex}=11 \pi{/tex} Phase difference for {tex}8^{\text {th }}{/tex} bright fringe {tex}=16 \pi{/tex} {tex} \Delta \phi+\phi_6 =\phi_8 {/tex} {tex} 2 \pi({n}-1) \frac{{t}}{\lambda}+11 \pi =16 \pi {/tex} {tex} {t} =\frac{5 \lambda}{2({n}-1)} {/tex}
solution: Phase difference for {tex}6^{\text {th }}{/tex} dark fringe {tex}=11 \pi{/tex} Phase difference for {tex}8^{\text {th }}{/tex} bright fringe {tex}=16 \pi{/tex} {tex} \Delta \phi+\phi_6 =\phi_8 {/tex} {tex} 2 \pi({n}-1) \frac{{t}}{\lambda}+11 \pi =16 \pi {/tex} {tex} {t} =\frac{5 \lambda}{2({n}-1)} {/tex}
solution_html:
Phase difference for {tex}6^{\text {th }}{/tex} dark fringe {tex}=11 \pi{/tex}
Phase difference for {tex}8^{\text {th }}{/tex} bright fringe {tex}=16 \pi{/tex}
{tex} \Delta \phi+\phi_6 =\phi_8 {/tex}
{tex} 2 \pi({n}-1) \frac{{t}}{\lambda}+11 \pi =16 \pi {/tex}
{tex} {t} =\frac{5 \lambda}{2({n}-1)} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. The amplitude of a light wave becomes n times. This results in intensity of the wave becoming m times. What is the relation between n and m?
  2. White light is incident on three identical surfaces - a black surface, a yellow surface and a white surface, one by one. For which surface, the pressure exerted on the surface by the incident light will be
    1. maximum
    2. minimum?

Justify your answer.
question_text: The amplitude of a light wave becomes n times. This results in intensity of the wave becoming m times. What is the relation between n and m? White light is incident on three identical surfaces - a black surface, a yellow surface and a white surface, one by one. For which surface, the pressure exerted on the surface by the incident light will be maximum minimum? Justify your answer.
explanation: Intensity (I) {tex}\alpha{/tex} (amplitude) {tex} \Rightarrow {m}={n}^2 {/tex} White surface As white surface will reflect maximum light falling on it, the change in momentum of light will be maximum and as a consequence the pressure exerted by the light will be maximum. Black surface As black surface absorbs maximum light falling on it, the change in momentum of light will be minimum and as a consequence the pressure exerted by the light will be minimum.
solution: Intensity (I) {tex}\alpha{/tex} (amplitude) {tex} \Rightarrow {m}={n}^2 {/tex} White surface As white surface will reflect maximum light falling on it, the change in momentum of light will be maximum and as a consequence the pressure exerted by the light will be maximum. Black surface As black surface absorbs maximum light falling on it, the change in momentum of light will be minimum and as a consequence the pressure exerted by the light will be minimum.
solution_html:
1. Intensity (I) {tex}\alpha{/tex} (amplitude)
    {tex} \Rightarrow {m}={n}^2 {/tex}
    1. White surface
      As white surface will reflect maximum light falling on it, the change in momentum of light will be maximum and as a consequence the pressure exerted by the light will be maximum.
    2. Black surface
      As black surface absorbs maximum light falling on it, the change in momentum of light will be minimum and as a consequence the pressure exerted by the light will be minimum.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A physics teacher wants to demonstrate interference with the help of double slit experiment using a laser beam of 633 nm wavelength. Since the hall is large enough, interference pattern is formed on the wall 5.0 m from the slits. For clear and comfortable view by all the students they want the fringe width 5 mm.
    1. Find the slit separation for obtaining the desired interference pattern.
    2. How far will the first minimum be from the central maximum?
  2. A parallel beam of light of wavelength 650 nm passes through a slit of width 0.6 mm. The diffraction pattern is obtained on a screen kept 60 cm away from the slit. Find the distance between first order minima on both sides of the central maximum.
question_text: A physics teacher wants to demonstrate interference with the help of double slit experiment using a laser beam of 633 nm wavelength. Since the hall is large enough, interference pattern is formed on the wall 5.0 m from the slits. For clear and comfortable view by all the students they want the fringe width 5 mm. Find the slit separation for obtaining the desired interference pattern. How far will the first minimum be from the central maximum? A parallel beam of light of wavelength 650 nm passes through a slit of width 0.6 mm. The diffraction pattern is obtained on a screen kept 60 cm away from the slit. Find the distance between first order minima on both sides of the central maximum.
explanation: Slit separation {tex} \beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d} {/tex} {tex} {d}=\frac{{D} \lambda}{\beta} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{633 \times 10^{-9} \times 5}{5 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} =633 \times 10^{-6} {~m} {/tex} {tex} =633 \mu {~m} {/tex} Distance of first minimum from central maximum {tex} {x}_{{n}}=\frac{(2 {n}-1) \lambda {D}}{2 {~d}} {/tex} {tex} {n}=1 {/tex} {tex} {x}=\frac{633 \times 10^{-9} \times 5}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} {x}=316.5 \times 10^{-6} {~m} {/tex} {tex} {x}=316.5 \mu {~m} {/tex} Distance between first order minima on both the side {tex} {W}=\frac{2 {D} \lambda}{{~d}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \times 650 \times 10^{-9}}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}} \times 60 \times 10^{-2} {/tex} {tex} =1.3 \times 10^{-3} {~m} {/tex}
solution: Slit separation {tex} \beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d} {/tex} {tex} {d}=\frac{{D} \lambda}{\beta} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{633 \times 10^{-9} \times 5}{5 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} =633 \times 10^{-6} {~m} {/tex} {tex} =633 \mu {~m} {/tex} Distance of first minimum from central maximum {tex} {x}_{{n}}=\frac{(2 {n}-1) \lambda {D}}{2 {~d}} {/tex} {tex} {n}=1 {/tex} {tex} {x}=\frac{633 \times 10^{-9} \times 5}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex} {tex} {x}=316.5 \times 10^{-6} {~m} {/tex} {tex} {x}=316.5 \mu {~m} {/tex} Distance between first order minima on both the side {tex} {W}=\frac{2 {D} \lambda}{{~d}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{2 \times 650 \times 10^{-9}}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}} \times 60 \times 10^{-2} {/tex} {tex} =1.3 \times 10^{-3} {~m} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Slit separation
      {tex} \beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d} {/tex}  
      {tex} {d}=\frac{{D} \lambda}{\beta} {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{633 \times 10^{-9} \times 5}{5 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex}
      {tex} =633 \times 10^{-6} {~m} {/tex}
      {tex} =633 \mu {~m} {/tex}
    2. Distance of first minimum from central maximum
      {tex} {x}_{{n}}=\frac{(2 {n}-1) \lambda {D}}{2 {~d}} {/tex}
      {tex} {n}=1 {/tex}
      {tex} {x}=\frac{633 \times 10^{-9} \times 5}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} {/tex}
      {tex} {x}=316.5 \times 10^{-6} {~m} {/tex}
      {tex} {x}=316.5 \mu {~m} {/tex}
  2. Distance between first order minima on both the side
    {tex} {W}=\frac{2 {D} \lambda}{{~d}} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{2 \times 650 \times 10^{-9}}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}} \times 60 \times 10^{-2} {/tex}
    {tex} =1.3 \times 10^{-3} {~m} {/tex}

question_html: In a double slit experiment, it is observed that the angular width of one fringe formed on the screen is {tex}0.2^{\circ}{/tex}. The wavelength of light used in the experiment is 500 nm. Calculate the separation of the two slits.
question_text: In a double slit experiment, it is observed that the angular width of one fringe formed on the screen is {tex}0.2^{\circ}{/tex} . The wavelength of light used in the experiment is 500 nm. Calculate the separation of the two slits.
explanation: {tex} \text { Angular width }(\theta) =\frac{\lambda}{{d}} {/tex} {tex} {d} =\frac{\lambda}{\theta} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{500 \times 10^{-9}}{0.2 \times \frac{\pi}{180}} {/tex} {tex} {~d} =\frac{45}{\pi} \times 10^{-5} {~m} {/tex} {tex} =0.14 {~mm} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \text { Angular width }(\theta) =\frac{\lambda}{{d}} {/tex} {tex} {d} =\frac{\lambda}{\theta} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{500 \times 10^{-9}}{0.2 \times \frac{\pi}{180}} {/tex} {tex} {~d} =\frac{45}{\pi} \times 10^{-5} {~m} {/tex} {tex} =0.14 {~mm} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \text { Angular width }(\theta) =\frac{\lambda}{{d}} {/tex}
{tex} {d} =\frac{\lambda}{\theta} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{500 \times 10^{-9}}{0.2 \times \frac{\pi}{180}} {/tex}
{tex} {~d} =\frac{45}{\pi} \times 10^{-5} {~m} {/tex}
{tex} =0.14 {~mm} {/tex}

question_html: Using the Huygens' principle, briefly describe reflection of a plane wavefront from a reflecting surface. Hence, prove the laws of reflection.
question_text: Using the Huygens' principle, briefly describe reflection of a plane wavefront from a reflecting surface. Hence, prove the laws of reflection.
explanation: Consider a plane wave {tex}A B{/tex} incident at an angle {tex}i{/tex} on a reflecting surface {tex}M N{/tex} . v represents speed of the wave in the medium and {tex}\tau{/tex} represents the time taken by wavefront to advance from point B to C . {tex} {BC}=\vartheta \tau {/tex} To construct the reflected wavefront, a sphere of radius {tex}{v} \tau{/tex} from point A is drawn. {tex} {AE}={BC}=\vartheta \tau {/tex} Also {tex}\triangle {EAC} \cong \Delta {BAC}{/tex} {tex} \therefore {i}={r} {/tex}
solution: Consider a plane wave {tex}A B{/tex} incident at an angle {tex}i{/tex} on a reflecting surface {tex}M N{/tex} . v represents speed of the wave in the medium and {tex}\tau{/tex} represents the time taken by wavefront to advance from point B to C . {tex} {BC}=\vartheta \tau {/tex} To construct the reflected wavefront, a sphere of radius {tex}{v} \tau{/tex} from point A is drawn. {tex} {AE}={BC}=\vartheta \tau {/tex} Also {tex}\triangle {EAC} \cong \Delta {BAC}{/tex} {tex} \therefore {i}={r} {/tex}
solution_html:
Consider a plane wave {tex}A B{/tex} incident at an angle {tex}i{/tex} on a reflecting surface {tex}M N{/tex}. v represents speed of the wave in the medium and {tex}\tau{/tex} represents the time taken by wavefront to advance from point B to C .
{tex} {BC}=\vartheta \tau {/tex}
To construct the reflected wavefront, a sphere of radius {tex}{v} \tau{/tex} from point A is drawn.
{tex} {AE}={BC}=\vartheta \tau {/tex}
Also {tex}\triangle {EAC} \cong \Delta {BAC}{/tex}
{tex} \therefore {i}={r} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Show the refraction of light wave at a plane interface using Huygens' principle and prove Snell's law.
question_text: Show the refraction of light wave at a plane interface using Huygens' principle and prove Snell's law.
explanation: Consider the triangles ABC and AEC, {tex} \operatorname{Sin} {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_1 \tau}{{AC}} {/tex} ...(i) {tex} \operatorname{Sin} {r}=\frac{{AE}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_2 \tau}{{AC}} {/tex} ...(ii) Divide eq. (i) & (ii):- {tex} \frac{\operatorname{Sin} i}{\operatorname{Sin} r}=\frac{v_1}{v_2} {/tex} ...(iii) Refractive index of medium 1, {tex} {n}_1=\frac{{c}}{{v}_1} {/tex} ...(iv) Refractive index of medium 2, {tex} {n}_2=\frac{{c}}{{v}_2} {/tex} ...(v) From eq. (iii); (iv) & (v):- {tex} \frac{{n}_2}{{n}_1}=\frac{\operatorname{Sin} {i}}{\operatorname{Sin} {r}} {/tex}
solution: Consider the triangles ABC and AEC, {tex} \operatorname{Sin} {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_1 \tau}{{AC}} {/tex} ...(i) {tex} \operatorname{Sin} {r}=\frac{{AE}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_2 \tau}{{AC}} {/tex} ...(ii) Divide eq. (i) & (ii):- {tex} \frac{\operatorname{Sin} i}{\operatorname{Sin} r}=\frac{v_1}{v_2} {/tex} ...(iii) Refractive index of medium 1, {tex} {n}_1=\frac{{c}}{{v}_1} {/tex} ...(iv) Refractive index of medium 2, {tex} {n}_2=\frac{{c}}{{v}_2} {/tex} ...(v) From eq. (iii); (iv) & (v):- {tex} \frac{{n}_2}{{n}_1}=\frac{\operatorname{Sin} {i}}{\operatorname{Sin} {r}} {/tex}
solution_html:
Consider the triangles ABC and AEC,
{tex} \operatorname{Sin} {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_1 \tau}{{AC}} {/tex} ...(i)
{tex} \operatorname{Sin} {r}=\frac{{AE}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_2 \tau}{{AC}} {/tex} ...(ii)
Divide eq. (i) & (ii):-
{tex} \frac{\operatorname{Sin} i}{\operatorname{Sin} r}=\frac{v_1}{v_2} {/tex} ...(iii)
Refractive index of medium 1,
{tex} {n}_1=\frac{{c}}{{v}_1} {/tex} ...(iv)
Refractive index of medium 2,
{tex} {n}_2=\frac{{c}}{{v}_2} {/tex} ...(v)
From eq. (iii); (iv) & (v):-
{tex} \frac{{n}_2}{{n}_1}=\frac{\operatorname{Sin} {i}}{\operatorname{Sin} {r}} {/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Write the conditions under which two light waves originating from two coherent sources can interfere each other (i) constructively, and (ii) destructively, in terms of wavelength. Can these be applied for two lights originating from two sodium lamps? Give reason.
  2. Monochromatic light of green colour is used in Young's double slit experiment and an interference pattern is observed on a screen. If the green light is replaced by red monochromatic light of the same intensity, how will the fringe width of interference pattern be affected? Justify your answer.
question_text: Write the conditions under which two light waves originating from two coherent sources can interfere each other (i) constructively, and (ii) destructively, in terms of wavelength. Can these be applied for two lights originating from two sodium lamps? Give reason. Monochromatic light of green colour is used in Young's double slit experiment and an interference pattern is observed on a screen. If the green light is replaced by red monochromatic light of the same intensity, how will the fringe width of interference pattern be affected? Justify your answer.
explanation: Conditions for constructive interference {tex}\Delta={n} \lambda{/tex} Conditions for destructive interference {tex}\Delta=\left({n}+\frac{1}{2}\right) \lambda{/tex} {tex}{n}=0,1,2 \ldots{/tex} No Two independent monochromatic source of light can not be coherent Fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\text {red }} \gt \lambda_{\text {green }}{/tex} {tex}\beta_{\text {red }} \gt \beta_{\text {green }}{/tex}
solution: Conditions for constructive interference {tex}\Delta={n} \lambda{/tex} Conditions for destructive interference {tex}\Delta=\left({n}+\frac{1}{2}\right) \lambda{/tex} {tex}{n}=0,1,2 \ldots{/tex} No Two independent monochromatic source of light can not be coherent Fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\text {red }} \gt \lambda_{\text {green }}{/tex} {tex}\beta_{\text {red }} \gt \beta_{\text {green }}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Conditions for constructive interference
      {tex}\Delta={n} \lambda{/tex}
    2. Conditions for destructive interference
      {tex}\Delta=\left({n}+\frac{1}{2}\right) \lambda{/tex}
      {tex}{n}=0,1,2 \ldots{/tex}
    No
    Two independent monochromatic source of light can not be coherent
  2. Fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_{\text {red }} \gt \lambda_{\text {green }}{/tex}
    {tex}\beta_{\text {red }} \gt \beta_{\text {green }}{/tex}

question_html:
A double slit set-up was initially placed in a tank filled with water and the interference pattern was obtained using a laser light. When water is replaced by a transparent liquid of refractive index {tex}{n} \gt {n}_{\text {water }}{/tex},what will be the effect on the following?

question_text: A double slit set-up was initially placed in a tank filled with water and the interference pattern was obtained using a laser light. When water is replaced by a transparent liquid of refractive index {tex}{n} \gt {n}_{\text {water }}{/tex} ,what will be the effect on the following? Speed, frequency and wavelength of the light of laser beam. The fringe width, shape of interference fringes and shift in the position of central maximum.
explanation: Speed of light will decrease Frequency remains unaffected Wavelength decreases Fringe width decreases Shapes does not change Position of central maxima does not change.
solution: Speed of light will decrease Frequency remains unaffected Wavelength decreases Fringe width decreases Shapes does not change Position of central maxima does not change.

question_html:
1. Define a wavefront. An incident plane wave falls on a convex lens and gets refracted through it. Draw a diagram to show the incident and refracted wavefront.
  2. A beam of light coming from a distant source is refracted by a spherical glass ball (refractive index 1.5) of radius 15 cm. Draw the ray diagram and obtain the position of the final image formed.
question_text: Define a wavefront. An incident plane wave falls on a convex lens and gets refracted through it. Draw a diagram to show the incident and refracted wavefront. A beam of light coming from a distant source is refracted by a spherical glass ball (refractive index 1.5) of radius 15 cm. Draw the ray diagram and obtain the position of the final image formed.
explanation: A wavefront is a locus of all the points which oscillate in phase. From {tex}{I}^{{st}}{/tex} surface, Refraction is from rarer to denser medium and object is at {tex}\infty{/tex} {tex}{n}_{1}=1, {n}_{2} =1.5, {R}=15 {~cm}, {u}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\frac{{n}_{2}}{{v}}-\frac{{n}_{1}}{{u}} =\frac{{n}_{2}-{n}_{1}}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{{v}}-\frac{1}{\infty} =\frac{1.5-1}{15}{/tex} {tex}{v} =45 {~cm}{/tex} From {tex}2{ }^{\text {nd }}{/tex} surface, Refraction is from denser to rarer medium and object is at 15 cm {tex}{n}_{1}=1, {n}_{2}=1.5, {R}=-15 {~cm}, {u}=15 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{n}_{1}}{{v}}-\frac{{n}_{2}}{{u}} =\frac{{n}_{1}-{n}_{2}}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}-\frac{1.5}{15} =\frac{1-1.5}{-15}{/tex} {tex}{v} =7.5 {~cm}{/tex}
solution: A wavefront is a locus of all the points which oscillate in phase. From {tex}{I}^{{st}}{/tex} surface, Refraction is from rarer to denser medium and object is at {tex}\infty{/tex} {tex}{n}_{1}=1, {n}_{2} =1.5, {R}=15 {~cm}, {u}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\frac{{n}_{2}}{{v}}-\frac{{n}_{1}}{{u}} =\frac{{n}_{2}-{n}_{1}}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1.5}{{v}}-\frac{1}{\infty} =\frac{1.5-1}{15}{/tex} {tex}{v} =45 {~cm}{/tex} From {tex}2{ }^{\text {nd }}{/tex} surface, Refraction is from denser to rarer medium and object is at 15 cm {tex}{n}_{1}=1, {n}_{2}=1.5, {R}=-15 {~cm}, {u}=15 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{n}_{1}}{{v}}-\frac{{n}_{2}}{{u}} =\frac{{n}_{1}-{n}_{2}}{{R}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{v}}-\frac{1.5}{15} =\frac{1-1.5}{-15}{/tex} {tex}{v} =7.5 {~cm}{/tex}

question_html:
1. What are coherent sources? Why are they necessary for observing a sustained interference pattern?
    2. Lights from two independent sources are not coherent. Explain.
  2. Two slits 0.1 mm apart are arranged 1.20 m from a screen. Light of wavelength 600 nm from a distant source is incident on the slits.
    1. How far apart will adjacent bright interference fringes be on the screen?
    2. Find the angular width (in degree) of the first bright fringe.
question_text: What are coherent sources? Why are they necessary for observing a sustained interference pattern? Lights from two independent sources are not coherent. Explain. Two slits 0.1 mm apart are arranged 1.20 m from a screen. Light of wavelength 600 nm from a distant source is incident on the slits. How far apart will adjacent bright interference fringes be on the screen? Find the angular width (in degree) of the first bright fringe.
explanation: If the phase difference between the displacement produced by each of the wave from two sources does not change with time then two sources are said to be coherent. Two independent sources will never be coherent because phase difference between them will not be constant. Distance between adjacent bright fringe {tex}={/tex} fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.2}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}}=7.2 {~mm}{/tex} {tex}\theta =\frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{600 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}}=6 \times 10^{-3} {rad}=0.34^{\circ}{/tex}
solution: If the phase difference between the displacement produced by each of the wave from two sources does not change with time then two sources are said to be coherent. Two independent sources will never be coherent because phase difference between them will not be constant. Distance between adjacent bright fringe {tex}={/tex} fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.2}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}}=7.2 {~mm}{/tex} {tex}\theta =\frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{600 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}}=6 \times 10^{-3} {rad}=0.34^{\circ}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. If the phase difference between the displacement produced by each of the wave from two sources does not change with time then two sources are said to be coherent.
    2. Two independent sources will never be coherent because phase difference between them will not be constant.
    1. Distance between adjacent bright fringe {tex}={/tex} fringe width
      {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex}
      {tex}= \frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.2}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}}=7.2 {~mm}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\theta =\frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{600 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}}=6 \times 10^{-3} {rad}=0.34^{\circ}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Light consisting of two wavelengths 600 nm and 480 nm is used to obtain interference fringes in a double slit experiment. The screen is placed 1.0 m away from slits which are 1.0 nm apart.
    1. Calculate the distance of the third bright fringe on the screen from the central maximum for wavelength 600 nm .
    2. Find the least distance from the central maximum where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide.
    1. Draw the variation of intensity with angle of diffraction in single slit diffraction pattern. Write the expression for value of angle corresponding to zero intensity locations.
    2. In what way diffraction of light waves differs from diffraction of sound waves?
question_text: Light consisting of two wavelengths 600 nm and 480 nm is used to obtain interference fringes in a double slit experiment. The screen is placed 1.0 m away from slits which are 1.0 nm apart. Calculate the distance of the third bright fringe on the screen from the central maximum for wavelength 600 nm . Find the least distance from the central maximum where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide. Draw the variation of intensity with angle of diffraction in single slit diffraction pattern. Write the expression for value of angle corresponding to zero intensity locations. In what way diffraction of light waves differs from diffraction of sound waves?
explanation: Distance of the nth bright fringe from the central maximum {tex}\left({x}_{{n}}\right)=\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex} For {tex}{n}=3{/tex} {tex} {X}_3 =\frac{3 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{1 \times 10^{-9}} {/tex} {tex} =1800 {~m} {/tex} {tex} {n}_1 \lambda_1={n}_2 \lambda_2 {/tex} {tex} {n}_1 \times 600={n}_2 \times 480 {/tex} {tex} \frac{{n}_1}{{n}_2}=\frac{480}{600} {/tex} {tex} \frac{{n}_1}{{n}_2}=\frac{4}{5} {/tex} Position of the {tex}4^{\text {th }}{/tex} bright fringe of {tex}600 {~nm}=4 \times 600=2400 {~m}{/tex} Angle of diffraction for zero intensity, {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a} ; {n}=0,1,2, \ldots \ldots \ldots{/tex} . Diffraction of the light waves is not generally seen as compared to diffraction of sound waves as light waves have low wavelength.
solution: Distance of the nth bright fringe from the central maximum {tex}\left({x}_{{n}}\right)=\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex} For {tex}{n}=3{/tex} {tex} {X}_3 =\frac{3 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{1 \times 10^{-9}} {/tex} {tex} =1800 {~m} {/tex} {tex} {n}_1 \lambda_1={n}_2 \lambda_2 {/tex} {tex} {n}_1 \times 600={n}_2 \times 480 {/tex} {tex} \frac{{n}_1}{{n}_2}=\frac{480}{600} {/tex} {tex} \frac{{n}_1}{{n}_2}=\frac{4}{5} {/tex} Position of the {tex}4^{\text {th }}{/tex} bright fringe of {tex}600 {~nm}=4 \times 600=2400 {~m}{/tex} Angle of diffraction for zero intensity, {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a} ; {n}=0,1,2, \ldots \ldots \ldots{/tex} . Diffraction of the light waves is not generally seen as compared to diffraction of sound waves as light waves have low wavelength.
solution_html:
1. Distance of the nth bright fringe from the central maximum {tex}\left({x}_{{n}}\right)=\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex}
      For {tex}{n}=3{/tex}
      {tex} {X}_3 =\frac{3 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{1 \times 10^{-9}} {/tex}
      {tex} =1800 {~m} {/tex}
    2. {tex} {n}_1 \lambda_1={n}_2 \lambda_2 {/tex}
      {tex} {n}_1 \times 600={n}_2 \times 480 {/tex}
      {tex} \frac{{n}_1}{{n}_2}=\frac{480}{600} {/tex}
      {tex} \frac{{n}_1}{{n}_2}=\frac{4}{5} {/tex}
      Position of the {tex}4^{\text {th }}{/tex} bright fringe of {tex}600 {~nm}=4 \times 600=2400 {~m}{/tex}

    1. Angle of diffraction for zero intensity, {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a} ; {n}=0,1,2, \ldots \ldots \ldots{/tex}.
    2. Diffraction of the light waves is not generally seen as compared to diffraction of sound waves as light waves have low wavelength.

subject: Physics
question_html: In a Young's double-slit experiment, two light waves, each of intensity {tex}I_{0}{/tex}, interfere at a point, having a path difference {tex}\frac{\lambda}{8}{/tex} on the screen. Find the intensity at this point.
question_text: In a Young's double-slit experiment, two light waves, each of intensity {tex}I_{0}{/tex} , interfere at a point, having a path difference {tex}\frac{\lambda}{8}{/tex} on the screen. Find the intensity at this point.
explanation: {tex}\text { Phase difference }=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \text { path difference }{/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta x{/tex} {tex}\because \Delta x=\frac{\lambda}{8}(\text { given }){/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{8}{/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}I= I_{\circ}+I_{\circ}+2 \sqrt{I_{\circ} I_{\circ}} \cos \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}=2 I_{\circ}+2 I_{\circ} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}I= 2 I_{\circ}\left(1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}= I_{\circ}(2+\sqrt{2}){/tex} {tex}I= 3.414 I_{\circ}{/tex} Alternatively Phase difference {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times{/tex} path difference {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta x{/tex} {tex}\because \Delta x=\frac{\lambda}{8}(\text { given }){/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{8}{/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}I=4 I_{\circ} \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}I=4 I_{\circ} \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{8}\right){/tex}
solution: {tex}\text { Phase difference }=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \text { path difference }{/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta x{/tex} {tex}\because \Delta x=\frac{\lambda}{8}(\text { given }){/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{8}{/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}I= I_{\circ}+I_{\circ}+2 \sqrt{I_{\circ} I_{\circ}} \cos \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}=2 I_{\circ}+2 I_{\circ} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}I= 2 I_{\circ}\left(1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}= I_{\circ}(2+\sqrt{2}){/tex} {tex}I= 3.414 I_{\circ}{/tex} Alternatively Phase difference {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times{/tex} path difference {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta x{/tex} {tex}\because \Delta x=\frac{\lambda}{8}(\text { given }){/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{8}{/tex} {tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}I=4 I_{\circ} \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}I=4 I_{\circ} \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{8}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\text { Phase difference }=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \text { path difference }{/tex}
{tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta x{/tex}
{tex}\because \Delta x=\frac{\lambda}{8}(\text { given }){/tex}
{tex}\Delta \phi =\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}I= I_{\circ}+I_{\circ}+2 \sqrt{I_{\circ} I_{\circ}} \cos \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=2 I_{\circ}+2 I_{\circ} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}I= 2 I_{\circ}\left(1+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}= I_{\circ}(2+\sqrt{2}){/tex}
{tex}I= 3.414 I_{\circ}{/tex}

Alternatively
Phase difference {tex}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times{/tex} path difference
{tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta x{/tex}
{tex}\because \Delta x=\frac{\lambda}{8}(\text { given }){/tex}
{tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{8}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta \phi=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}I=4 I_{\circ} \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}I=4 I_{\circ} \cos ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{8}\right){/tex}

question_html: In a diffraction experiment, the slit is illuminated by light of wavelength 600 nm. The first minimum of the pattern falls at {tex}\theta=30^{\circ}{/tex}. Calculate the width of the slit.
question_text: In a diffraction experiment, the slit is illuminated by light of wavelength 600 nm. The first minimum of the pattern falls at {tex}\theta=30^{\circ}{/tex} . Calculate the width of the slit.
explanation: Condition for Minima a {tex}\sin \theta={n} \lambda{/tex} For First Minima {tex}{n}=1{/tex} a {tex}\sin 30^{\circ}=600 \times 10^{-9} {~m}{/tex} {tex}{a} \times \frac{1}{2}=600 \times 10^{-9} {~m}{/tex} {tex}{a} =1200 \times 10^{-9} {~m}{/tex} {tex}=1.2 \times 10^{-6} {~m}{/tex}
solution: Condition for Minima a {tex}\sin \theta={n} \lambda{/tex} For First Minima {tex}{n}=1{/tex} a {tex}\sin 30^{\circ}=600 \times 10^{-9} {~m}{/tex} {tex}{a} \times \frac{1}{2}=600 \times 10^{-9} {~m}{/tex} {tex}{a} =1200 \times 10^{-9} {~m}{/tex} {tex}=1.2 \times 10^{-6} {~m}{/tex}
solution_html:
Condition for Minima
a {tex}\sin \theta={n} \lambda{/tex}
For First Minima {tex}{n}=1{/tex}
a {tex}\sin 30^{\circ}=600 \times 10^{-9} {~m}{/tex}
{tex}{a} \times \frac{1}{2}=600 \times 10^{-9} {~m}{/tex}
{tex}{a} =1200 \times 10^{-9} {~m}{/tex}
{tex}=1.2 \times 10^{-6} {~m}{/tex}

question_html: In a Young's double-slit experiment with light of wavelength 520 nm, the fringe width is 0.52 mm. What will be the fringe width if the entire apparatus is immersed in water (refractive index {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex})?
question_text: In a Young's double-slit experiment with light of wavelength 520 nm, the fringe width is 0.52 mm. What will be the fringe width if the entire apparatus is immersed in water (refractive index {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex} )?
explanation: {tex} \beta =\frac{D \lambda}{d} {/tex} {tex} \frac{\beta^{\prime}}{\beta} =\frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{v / v}{c / v}=\frac{1}{n} {/tex} {tex} \beta^{\prime} =\frac{\beta}{n} {/tex} {tex} \beta^{\prime} =\frac{0.52}{4 / 3} {/tex} {tex} =0.39 {~mm} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \beta =\frac{D \lambda}{d} {/tex} {tex} \frac{\beta^{\prime}}{\beta} =\frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{v / v}{c / v}=\frac{1}{n} {/tex} {tex} \beta^{\prime} =\frac{\beta}{n} {/tex} {tex} \beta^{\prime} =\frac{0.52}{4 / 3} {/tex} {tex} =0.39 {~mm} {/tex}

{tex} \frac{\beta^{\prime}}{\beta} =\frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{v / v}{c / v}=\frac{1}{n} {/tex}

question_html: In a Young's double-slit experiment with monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm, and slit separation of 2 mm, fringes are observed on a screen at some distance. Calculate the change in fringe width if the screen is moved towards the slits by 10 cm.
question_text: In a Young's double-slit experiment with monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm, and slit separation of 2 mm, fringes are observed on a screen at some distance. Calculate the change in fringe width if the screen is moved towards the slits by 10 cm.
explanation: {tex} \beta =\frac{\lambda D}{d} {/tex} {tex} \beta^{\prime} =\frac{\lambda D^{\prime}}{d} {/tex} {tex} \Delta \beta =\beta-\beta^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{d}\left[D-D^{\prime}\right] {/tex} {tex} \Delta \beta =\frac{\lambda}{d}[D-(D-0.1)] {/tex} {tex} \Delta \beta =\frac{600 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times 0.1 {/tex} {tex} =3 \times 10^{-5} {~m} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \beta =\frac{\lambda D}{d} {/tex} {tex} \beta^{\prime} =\frac{\lambda D^{\prime}}{d} {/tex} {tex} \Delta \beta =\beta-\beta^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{d}\left[D-D^{\prime}\right] {/tex} {tex} \Delta \beta =\frac{\lambda}{d}[D-(D-0.1)] {/tex} {tex} \Delta \beta =\frac{600 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times 0.1 {/tex} {tex} =3 \times 10^{-5} {~m} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \beta =\frac{\lambda D}{d} {/tex}
{tex} \beta^{\prime} =\frac{\lambda D^{\prime}}{d} {/tex}
{tex} \Delta \beta =\beta-\beta^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{d}\left[D-D^{\prime}\right] {/tex}
{tex} \Delta \beta =\frac{\lambda}{d}[D-(D-0.1)] {/tex}
{tex} \Delta \beta =\frac{600 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times 0.1 {/tex}
{tex} =3 \times 10^{-5} {~m} {/tex}

question_html: State Huygens principle. Using it, draw a diagram and discuss the case of refraction of plane wave of light from a rarer medium to a denser medium at their plane interface. Hence derive Snell's law.
question_text: State Huygens principle. Using it, draw a diagram and discuss the case of refraction of plane wave of light from a rarer medium to a denser medium at their plane interface. Hence derive Snell's law.
explanation: Huygen’s Principle: Each point of the wavefront is the source of a secondary disturbance and the wavelets emanating from these points spread out in all directions with the speed of the wave. These wavelets emanating from the wavefront are usually referred to as secondary wavelets and if we draw a common tangent to all these spheres, we obtain the new position of the wavefront at a later time. Discussion: In time {tex}\tau{/tex} distance BC travelled by the incident plane wavefront with the velocity {tex}v_1{/tex} is {tex}v_1 \tau{/tex} . To determine the shape of the refracted wave wavefront, draw a sphere of radius {tex}v_2 \tau(={AE}){/tex} from point A and draw a tangent from {tex}C{/tex} to {tex}E{/tex} as shown in the diagram. From the above diagram {tex}\triangle A B C{/tex} and {tex}\triangle A E C{/tex} {tex} \sin i=\frac{B C}{A C}=\frac{v_1 \tau}{A C} {/tex} and {tex}\sin r=\frac{A E}{A C}=\frac{\nu_2 \tau}{A C}{/tex} {tex} \frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{v_1}{v_2} {/tex} ...(1) As {tex}{n}_1=\frac{c}{v_1}{/tex} and {tex}{n}_2=\frac{c}{v_2}{/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\frac{n_1}{n_2} {/tex} ...(2) From equations (1) and (2) {tex} n_1 \sin i=n_2 \sin r {/tex}
solution: Huygen’s Principle: Each point of the wavefront is the source of a secondary disturbance and the wavelets emanating from these points spread out in all directions with the speed of the wave. These wavelets emanating from the wavefront are usually referred to as secondary wavelets and if we draw a common tangent to all these spheres, we obtain the new position of the wavefront at a later time. Discussion: In time {tex}\tau{/tex} distance BC travelled by the incident plane wavefront with the velocity {tex}v_1{/tex} is {tex}v_1 \tau{/tex} . To determine the shape of the refracted wave wavefront, draw a sphere of radius {tex}v_2 \tau(={AE}){/tex} from point A and draw a tangent from {tex}C{/tex} to {tex}E{/tex} as shown in the diagram. From the above diagram {tex}\triangle A B C{/tex} and {tex}\triangle A E C{/tex} {tex} \sin i=\frac{B C}{A C}=\frac{v_1 \tau}{A C} {/tex} and {tex}\sin r=\frac{A E}{A C}=\frac{\nu_2 \tau}{A C}{/tex} {tex} \frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{v_1}{v_2} {/tex} ...(1) As {tex}{n}_1=\frac{c}{v_1}{/tex} and {tex}{n}_2=\frac{c}{v_2}{/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\frac{n_1}{n_2} {/tex} ...(2) From equations (1) and (2) {tex} n_1 \sin i=n_2 \sin r {/tex}
solution_html:
Huygen’s Principle:
Each point of the wavefront is the source of a secondary disturbance and the wavelets emanating from these points spread out in all directions with the speed of the wave. These wavelets emanating from the wavefront are usually referred to as secondary wavelets and if we draw a common tangent to all these spheres, we obtain the new position of the wavefront at a later time.

Discussion: In time {tex}\tau{/tex} distance BC travelled by the incident plane wavefront with the velocity {tex}v_1{/tex} is {tex}v_1 \tau{/tex}. To determine the shape of the refracted wave wavefront, draw a sphere of radius {tex}v_2 \tau(={AE}){/tex} from point A and draw a tangent from {tex}C{/tex} to {tex}E{/tex} as shown in the diagram.
From the above diagram
{tex}\triangle A B C{/tex} and {tex}\triangle A E C{/tex}
{tex} \sin i=\frac{B C}{A C}=\frac{v_1 \tau}{A C} {/tex}
and {tex}\sin r=\frac{A E}{A C}=\frac{\nu_2 \tau}{A C}{/tex}
{tex} \frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{v_1}{v_2} {/tex} ...(1)
As {tex}{n}_1=\frac{c}{v_1}{/tex} and {tex}{n}_2=\frac{c}{v_2}{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\frac{n_1}{n_2} {/tex} ...(2)
From equations (1) and (2)
{tex} n_1 \sin i=n_2 \sin r {/tex}

question_html: Light of wavelength 640 nm incident on the slits in a Young's double-slit experiment produces an interference pattern of fringe width {tex}7 .2 {~mm}{/tex}. Calculate the wavelength of light which will produce a pattern of fringe width {tex}8 .1 {~mm}{/tex}.
question_text: Light of wavelength 640 nm incident on the slits in a Young's double-slit experiment produces an interference pattern of fringe width {tex}7 .2 {~mm}{/tex} . Calculate the wavelength of light which will produce a pattern of fringe width {tex}8 .1 {~mm}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d} {/tex} {tex} \frac{\beta_1}{\beta_2}=\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} {/tex} {tex} \lambda_2=\frac{\beta_1}{\beta_2} \times \lambda_1 {/tex} {tex} \lambda_2=\frac{8.1}{7.2} \times 640 {/tex} {tex} \lambda_2=720 {~nm} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d} {/tex} {tex} \frac{\beta_1}{\beta_2}=\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} {/tex} {tex} \lambda_2=\frac{\beta_1}{\beta_2} \times \lambda_1 {/tex} {tex} \lambda_2=\frac{8.1}{7.2} \times 640 {/tex} {tex} \lambda_2=720 {~nm} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{\beta_1}{\beta_2}=\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} {/tex}
{tex} \lambda_2=\frac{\beta_1}{\beta_2} \times \lambda_1 {/tex}
{tex} \lambda_2=\frac{8.1}{7.2} \times 640 {/tex}
{tex} \lambda_2=720 {~nm} {/tex}

question_html: In a Young's double-slit experiment, the intensity at the central maximum in the interference pattern on the screen is {tex}{I}_{0}{/tex}. Find the intensity at a point on the screen where the path difference between the interfering waves is {tex}\frac{\lambda}{6}{/tex}.
question_text: In a Young's double-slit experiment, the intensity at the central maximum in the interference pattern on the screen is {tex}{I}_{0}{/tex} . Find the intensity at a point on the screen where the path difference between the interfering waves is {tex}\frac{\lambda}{6}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}I_{0} =I_{1}+I_{2}+2 \sqrt{I_{1}} \sqrt{I_{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} {tex}=I+I+2 I \cos 0^{0}{/tex} {tex}=2 I+2 I=4 I{/tex} {tex}I=I_{0} / 4{/tex} Intensity at point P {tex}I^{\prime} =I+I+2 I \cos \pi / 3{/tex} {tex}=3 I{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 I_{0}}{4}{/tex}
solution: {tex}I_{0} =I_{1}+I_{2}+2 \sqrt{I_{1}} \sqrt{I_{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} {tex}=I+I+2 I \cos 0^{0}{/tex} {tex}=2 I+2 I=4 I{/tex} {tex}I=I_{0} / 4{/tex} Intensity at point P {tex}I^{\prime} =I+I+2 I \cos \pi / 3{/tex} {tex}=3 I{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 I_{0}}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I_{0} =I_{1}+I_{2}+2 \sqrt{I_{1}} \sqrt{I_{2}} \cos \phi{/tex}
{tex}=I+I+2 I \cos 0^{0}{/tex}
{tex}=2 I+2 I=4 I{/tex}
{tex}I=I_{0} / 4{/tex}
Intensity at point P
{tex}I^{\prime} =I+I+2 I \cos \pi / 3{/tex}
{tex}=3 I{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3 I_{0}}{4}{/tex}

question_html: The ratio of the intensities at maxima to minima in Young's double-slit experiment is {tex}25: 9{/tex}. Calculate the ratio of intensities of the interfering waves.
question_text: The ratio of the intensities at maxima to minima in Young's double-slit experiment is {tex}25: 9{/tex} . Calculate the ratio of intensities of the interfering waves.
explanation: Given, {tex}\frac{I_{\text {max }}}{I_{\text {min }}}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{I}_{\max }}{{I}_{\min }}=\frac{\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex} {tex}3\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)=5\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{I}_{1}}{{I}_{1}}=\frac{16}{1}{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}\frac{I_{\text {max }}}{I_{\text {min }}}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{I}_{\max }}{{I}_{\min }}=\frac{\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex} {tex}3\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)=5\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{I}_{1}}{{I}_{1}}=\frac{16}{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\frac{I_{\text {max }}}{I_{\text {min }}}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{I}_{\max }}{{I}_{\min }}=\frac{\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{25}{9}{/tex}
{tex}3\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)=5\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{I}_{1}}{{I}_{1}}=\frac{16}{1}{/tex}

question_html: Two waves, each of amplitude '{tex}a{/tex}' and frequency '{tex}\omega{/tex}' emanating from two coherent sources of light superpose at a point. If the phase difference between the two waves is {tex}\phi{/tex}, obtain an expression for the resultant intensity at that point.
question_text: Two waves, each of amplitude ' {tex}a{/tex} ' and frequency ' {tex}\omega{/tex} ' emanating from two coherent sources of light superpose at a point. If the phase difference between the two waves is {tex}\phi{/tex} , obtain an expression for the resultant intensity at that point.
explanation: {tex} x_1=a \cos \omega t {/tex} {tex} x_2=a \cos (\omega t+\phi) {/tex} {tex} x=x_1+x_2 {/tex} {tex} =a(\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)) {/tex} {tex} =a\left(2 \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right) \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} =2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2} \cos \left(\omega {t}+\frac{\phi}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \text { Intensity } {/tex} {tex} {I}={K}{/tex} (amplitude) 2 where K is a constant. {tex} =K\left(2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =4 I_0 \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2} {/tex} {tex} {I}_0={Ka}^2 {/tex} = intensity of each incident wave.
solution: {tex} x_1=a \cos \omega t {/tex} {tex} x_2=a \cos (\omega t+\phi) {/tex} {tex} x=x_1+x_2 {/tex} {tex} =a(\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)) {/tex} {tex} =a\left(2 \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right) \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} =2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2} \cos \left(\omega {t}+\frac{\phi}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \text { Intensity } {/tex} {tex} {I}={K}{/tex} (amplitude) 2 where K is a constant. {tex} =K\left(2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right)^2 {/tex} {tex} =4 I_0 \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2} {/tex} {tex} {I}_0={Ka}^2 {/tex} = intensity of each incident wave.
solution_html:
{tex} x_1=a \cos \omega t {/tex}
{tex} x_2=a \cos (\omega t+\phi) {/tex}
{tex} x=x_1+x_2 {/tex}
{tex} =a(\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)) {/tex}
{tex} =a\left(2 \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right) \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2} \cos \left(\omega {t}+\frac{\phi}{2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \text { Intensity } {/tex}
{tex} {I}={K}{/tex} (amplitude)2 where K is a constant.
{tex} =K\left(2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right)^2 {/tex}
{tex} =4 I_0 \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2} {/tex}
{tex} {I}_0={Ka}^2 {/tex} = intensity of each incident wave.

subject: Physics
question_html: What is a sustained or stable interference pattern? What are the conditions for obtaining such an interference pattern?
question_text: What is a sustained or stable interference pattern? What are the conditions for obtaining such an interference pattern?
explanation: A sustained or stable interference pattern is a consistent arrangement of alternating light and dark regions formed by the constructive and destructive interference of coherent waves. To achieve such a pattern, the following conditions must be met: Coherent Sources : The waves must have a constant phase relationship. Monochromatic Light : A single wavelength of light is required. Overlap of Waves : The two waves must overlap in space. Path Difference : A measurable path difference must exist between the waves to ensure proper interference.
solution: A sustained or stable interference pattern is a consistent arrangement of alternating light and dark regions formed by the constructive and destructive interference of coherent waves. To achieve such a pattern, the following conditions must be met: Coherent Sources : The waves must have a constant phase relationship. Monochromatic Light : A single wavelength of light is required. Overlap of Waves : The two waves must overlap in space. Path Difference : A measurable path difference must exist between the waves to ensure proper interference.
solution_html:
A sustained or stable interference pattern is a consistent arrangement of alternating light and dark regions formed by the constructive and destructive interference of coherent waves. To achieve such a pattern, the following conditions must be met:

  1. Coherent Sources: The waves must have a constant phase relationship.
  2. Monochromatic Light: A single wavelength of light is required.
  3. Overlap of Waves: The two waves must overlap in space.
  4. Path Difference: A measurable path difference must exist between the waves to ensure proper interference.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Diffraction and interference are closely related phenomena that occur together. Diffraction is the phenomenon of bending of light around the edges of the obstacle, while interference is the combination of waves that results in a new wave pattern. In order to get interference, there must be at least two waves that are diffracting. So while diffraction can occur without interference, interference cannot occur without diffraction.
Two slits of width {tex}2 \mu \mathrm{~m}{/tex} each in an opaque material are separated by a distance of 6 m. Monochromatic light of wavelength 450 nm is incident normally on the slits. One finds a combined interference and diffraction pattern on the screen.

  1. The number of peaks of the interference fringes formed within the central peak of the envelope of the diffraction pattern will be:
    1. 2
    2. 3
    3. 4
    4. 6
  2. The number of peaks of the interference formed if the slit width is doubled while keeping the distance between the slits same will be:
    1. 1
    2. 2
    3. 3
    4. 4
    1. If instead of 450 nm light, another light of wavelength 680 nm is used, number of peaks of the interference formed in the central peak of the envelope of the diffraction pattern will be:
      1. 2
      2. 4
      3. 6
      4. 9

OR

    1. Consider the diffraction of light by a single slit described in this case study. The first minimum falls at an angle equal to:
      1. sin-1 (0.12)
      2. sin-1 (0.225)
      3. sin-1 (0.32)
      4. sin-1 (0.45)
  2. The number of bright fringes formed due to interference on 1 m of screen placed at {tex}\frac{4}{3} {~m}{/tex} away from the slits is:
    1. 2
    2. 3
    3. 6
    4. 10
question_text: Diffraction and interference are closely related phenomena that occur together. Diffraction is the phenomenon of bending of light around the edges of the obstacle, while interference is the combination of waves that results in a new wave pattern. In order to get interference, there must be at least two waves that are diffracting. So while diffraction can occur without interference, interference cannot occur without diffraction. Two slits of width {tex}2 \mu \mathrm{~m}{/tex} each in an opaque material are separated by a distance of 6 m. Monochromatic light of wavelength 450 nm is incident normally on the slits. One finds a combined interference and diffraction pattern on the screen. The number of peaks of the interference fringes formed within the central peak of the envelope of the diffraction pattern will be: 2 3 4 6 The number of peaks of the interference formed if the slit width is doubled while keeping the distance between the slits same will be: 1 2 3 4 If instead of 450 nm light, another light of wavelength 680 nm is used, number of peaks of the interference formed in the central peak of the envelope of the diffraction pattern will be: 2 4 6 9 OR Consider the diffraction of light by a single slit described in this case study. The first minimum falls at an angle equal to: sin -1 (0.12) sin -1 (0.225) sin -1 (0.32) sin -1 (0.45) The number of bright fringes formed due to interference on 1 m of screen placed at {tex}\frac{4}{3} {~m}{/tex} away from the slits is: 2 3 6 10
solution_html:
1. (d) 6
  2. (c) 3
    1. (c) 6

OR

    1. (b) sin-1 (0.225)
  2. (d) 10

question_html:
1. Draw the shape of intensity distribution curve of the fringes due to diffraction at a single slit.
  2. Derive the relation for the power of combination of two lenses placed in contact co-axially.
question_text: Draw the shape of intensity distribution curve of the fringes due to diffraction at a single slit. Derive the relation for the power of combination of two lenses placed in contact co-axially.
explanation: For the image formed by the second lens B, we get {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{u}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}{/tex} ...(1) {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_{1}}=\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} ...(2) Adding eqs (1) and (2) we get {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} ...(3) If the two lens system is regarded as equivalent to a single lens of focal length {tex}f{/tex} , we have {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} ...(4) From eqs (3) & (4) {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}+\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}{/tex} As, {tex}{P}=\frac{1}{{f}}{/tex} {tex}\text { Hence, } {P}={P}_{1}+{P}_{2}{/tex}
solution: For the image formed by the second lens B, we get {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{u}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}{/tex} ...(1) {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_{1}}=\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} ...(2) Adding eqs (1) and (2) we get {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} ...(3) If the two lens system is regarded as equivalent to a single lens of focal length {tex}f{/tex} , we have {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} ...(4) From eqs (3) & (4) {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}+\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}{/tex} As, {tex}{P}=\frac{1}{{f}}{/tex} {tex}\text { Hence, } {P}={P}_{1}+{P}_{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
2. For the image formed by the second lens B, we get
    {tex}\frac{1}{{v}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{u}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}{/tex} ...(1)
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{v_{1}}=\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} ...(2)
    Adding eqs (1) and (2) we get
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}{/tex} ...(3)
    If the two lens system is regarded as equivalent to a single lens of focal length {tex}f{/tex}, we have
    {tex}\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}{/tex} ...(4)
    From eqs (3) & (4)
    {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}+\frac{1}{{f}_{2}}{/tex}
    As, {tex}{P}=\frac{1}{{f}}{/tex}
    {tex}\text { Hence, } {P}={P}_{1}+{P}_{2}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Differentiate between a wavefront and a ray.
  2. State Huygen's principle and verify laws of reflection using suitable diagram.
  3. In Young's double slit experiment, the slits {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} are 3 mm apart and the screen is placed 1.0 m away from the slits. It is observed that the fourth bright fringe is at a distance of 5 mm from the second dark fringe. Find the wavelength of light used.
question_text: Differentiate between a wavefront and a ray. State Huygen's principle and verify laws of reflection using suitable diagram. In Young's double slit experiment, the slits {tex}{S}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{S}_{2}{/tex} are 3 mm apart and the screen is placed 1.0 m away from the slits. It is observed that the fourth bright fringe is at a distance of 5 mm from the second dark fringe. Find the wavelength of light used.
explanation: Wavefront is a surface of constant phase. Ray - The straight line path along which light travels (or energy propagates). Huygens' Principle - Each point of the wave front is the source of secondary disturbance and the wavelets emanating from the points spread out in all directions with speed of wave. The wavelets emanating from wave front are usually referred to as secondary wavelets. A common tangent to all these spheres gives the new position of the wave front at a later time. Triangles EAC and BAC are congruent therefore {tex}\angle {i}=\angle {r}{/tex} Position of {tex}4{ }^{\text {th }}{/tex} bright fringe {tex}{x}_{4(\text { bright })}=4 \frac{{D} \lambda}{{~d}}{/tex} Position of {tex}2^{\text {nd }}{/tex} dark fringe {tex}x_{2(\text { dark })}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}x_{4(\text { bright })}-x_{2(\text { dark })}=5 {~mm}{/tex} {tex}4 \frac{D \lambda}{d}-\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{d}=5 \times 10^{-3}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=6 \times 10^{-6} {~m}{/tex}
solution: Wavefront is a surface of constant phase. Ray - The straight line path along which light travels (or energy propagates). Huygens' Principle - Each point of the wave front is the source of secondary disturbance and the wavelets emanating from the points spread out in all directions with speed of wave. The wavelets emanating from wave front are usually referred to as secondary wavelets. A common tangent to all these spheres gives the new position of the wave front at a later time. Triangles EAC and BAC are congruent therefore {tex}\angle {i}=\angle {r}{/tex} Position of {tex}4{ }^{\text {th }}{/tex} bright fringe {tex}{x}_{4(\text { bright })}=4 \frac{{D} \lambda}{{~d}}{/tex} Position of {tex}2^{\text {nd }}{/tex} dark fringe {tex}x_{2(\text { dark })}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}x_{4(\text { bright })}-x_{2(\text { dark })}=5 {~mm}{/tex} {tex}4 \frac{D \lambda}{d}-\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{d}=5 \times 10^{-3}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=6 \times 10^{-6} {~m}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Wavefront is a surface of constant phase.
    Ray - The straight line path along which light travels (or energy propagates).
  2. Huygens' Principle - Each point of the wave front is the source of secondary disturbance and the wavelets emanating from the points spread out in all directions with speed of wave. The wavelets emanating from wave front are usually referred to as secondary wavelets. A common tangent to all these spheres gives the new position of the wave front at a later time.

    Triangles EAC and BAC are congruent therefore {tex}\angle {i}=\angle {r}{/tex}
  3. Position of {tex}4{ }^{\text {th }}{/tex} bright fringe
    {tex}{x}_{4(\text { bright })}=4 \frac{{D} \lambda}{{~d}}{/tex}
    Position of {tex}2^{\text {nd }}{/tex} dark fringe
    {tex}x_{2(\text { dark })}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
    {tex}x_{4(\text { bright })}-x_{2(\text { dark })}=5 {~mm}{/tex}
    {tex}4 \frac{D \lambda}{d}-\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{d}=5 \times 10^{-3}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda=6 \times 10^{-6} {~m}{/tex}
