question_html:
1. Discuss briefly diffraction of light from a single slit and draw the shape of the diffraction pattern.
  2. An object is placed between the pole and the focus of a concave mirror. Using mirror formula, prove mathematically that it produces a virtual and an enlarged image.
question_text: Discuss briefly diffraction of light from a single slit and draw the shape of the diffraction pattern. An object is placed between the pole and the focus of a concave mirror. Using mirror formula, prove mathematically that it produces a virtual and an enlarged image.
explanation: A beam of light falls normally on a single slit and bends around its corners. This phenomenon is called diffraction. When a beam of light falls normally on a narrow single slit, then diffracted light goes on to meet on a screen. It is observed that at the center of the screen intensity is maximum and goes on decreasing as one move away from the center on either side of screen. {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{u f}{u-f}{/tex} Following new cartesian sign conversion {tex}v=\frac{(-u)(-f)}{-u-(-f)}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{u f}{f-u} \text { as } f \gt u{/tex} v is +ve, So image is virtual. {tex}{m}=-\frac{{v}}{{u}}=\frac{{f}}{{f}-{u}} \gt 1{/tex} i.e. Enlarged image
solution: A beam of light falls normally on a single slit and bends around its corners. This phenomenon is called diffraction. When a beam of light falls normally on a narrow single slit, then diffracted light goes on to meet on a screen. It is observed that at the center of the screen intensity is maximum and goes on decreasing as one move away from the center on either side of screen. {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{u f}{u-f}{/tex} Following new cartesian sign conversion {tex}v=\frac{(-u)(-f)}{-u-(-f)}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{u f}{f-u} \text { as } f \gt u{/tex} v is +ve, So image is virtual. {tex}{m}=-\frac{{v}}{{u}}=\frac{{f}}{{f}-{u}} \gt 1{/tex} i.e. Enlarged image
solution_html:
1. A beam of light falls normally on a single slit and bends around its corners. This phenomenon is called diffraction.
    When a beam of light falls normally on a narrow single slit, then diffracted light goes on to meet on a screen. It is observed that at the center of the screen intensity is maximum and goes on decreasing as one move away from the center on either side of screen.
  2. {tex}\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}{/tex}
    {tex}v=\frac{u f}{u-f}{/tex}
    Following new cartesian sign conversion
    {tex}v=\frac{(-u)(-f)}{-u-(-f)}{/tex}
    {tex}v=\frac{u f}{f-u} \text { as } f \gt u{/tex}
    v is +ve, So image is virtual.
    {tex}{m}=-\frac{{v}}{{u}}=\frac{{f}}{{f}-{u}} \gt 1{/tex} i.e. Enlarged image

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A plane light wave propagating from a rarer into a denser medium, is incident at an angle {tex}i{/tex} on the surface separating two media. Using Huygen's principle, draw the refracted wave and hence verify Snell's law of refraction.
  2. In a Young's double slit experiment, the slits are separated by 0.30 mm and the screen is kept 1.5 m away. The wavelength of light used is 600 nm. Calculate the distance between the central bright fringe and the {tex}4^{\text {th }}{/tex} dark fringe.
question_text: A plane light wave propagating from a rarer into a denser medium, is incident at an angle {tex}i{/tex} on the surface separating two media. Using Huygen's principle, draw the refracted wave and hence verify Snell's law of refraction. In a Young's double slit experiment, the slits are separated by 0.30 mm and the screen is kept 1.5 m away. The wavelength of light used is 600 nm. Calculate the distance between the central bright fringe and the {tex}4^{\text {th }}{/tex} dark fringe.
explanation: Considering triangles ABC and AEC {tex}\sin {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} and ...(1) {tex}\sin {r}=\frac{{AE}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} ...(2) From equation (1) and equation (2) {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}{/tex} ...(3) If c represents the speed of light in vacuum, then {tex}{n}_{1}=\frac{{c}}{{v}_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}{n}_{2}=\frac{{c}}{{v}_{2}}{/tex} In terms of refractive indices {tex}{n}_{1} \sin {i}={n}_{2} \sin {r}{/tex} which is Snell's law of refraction. {tex}{X}_{4} =\frac{(2 {n}-1) \lambda {D}}{2 {~d}}{/tex} {tex}{X}_{4} =\frac{(2 \times 4-1) \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{2 \times 0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}=1.05 \times 10^{-2} {~m}{/tex}
solution: Considering triangles ABC and AEC {tex}\sin {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} and ...(1) {tex}\sin {r}=\frac{{AE}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} ...(2) From equation (1) and equation (2) {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}{/tex} ...(3) If c represents the speed of light in vacuum, then {tex}{n}_{1}=\frac{{c}}{{v}_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}{n}_{2}=\frac{{c}}{{v}_{2}}{/tex} In terms of refractive indices {tex}{n}_{1} \sin {i}={n}_{2} \sin {r}{/tex} which is Snell's law of refraction. {tex}{X}_{4} =\frac{(2 {n}-1) \lambda {D}}{2 {~d}}{/tex} {tex}{X}_{4} =\frac{(2 \times 4-1) \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{2 \times 0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}=1.05 \times 10^{-2} {~m}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Considering triangles ABC and AEC
    {tex}\sin {i}=\frac{{BC}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{1} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} and ...(1)
    {tex}\sin {r}=\frac{{AE}}{{AC}}=\frac{{v}_{2} \tau}{{AC}}{/tex} ...(2)
    From equation (1) and equation (2)
    {tex}\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}}=\frac{{v}_{1}}{{v}_{2}}{/tex} ...(3)
    If c represents the speed of light in vacuum, then
    {tex}{n}_{1}=\frac{{c}}{{v}_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}{n}_{2}=\frac{{c}}{{v}_{2}}{/tex}
    In terms of refractive indices
    {tex}{n}_{1} \sin {i}={n}_{2} \sin {r}{/tex}
    which is Snell's law of refraction.
  2. {tex}{X}_{4} =\frac{(2 {n}-1) \lambda {D}}{2 {~d}}{/tex}
    {tex}{X}_{4} =\frac{(2 \times 4-1) \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{2 \times 0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    {tex}=1.05 \times 10^{-2} {~m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Describe a simple activity to observe diffraction pattern due to a single slit.
  2. The figure below shows an equiconvex lens (of refractive index {tex}1.50){/tex} in contact with a liquid layer on top of a plane mirror. A small needle with its tip on the principal axis is moved along the axis until its inverted image is found at the position of the needle. The distance of the needle from the lens is measured to be 45.0 cm. When the liquid is removed and the experiment is repeated, the new distance is 30.0 cm. Find the refractive index of the liquid.
question_text: Describe a simple activity to observe diffraction pattern due to a single slit. The figure below shows an equiconvex lens (of refractive index {tex}1.50){/tex} in contact with a liquid layer on top of a plane mirror. A small needle with its tip on the principal axis is moved along the axis until its inverted image is found at the position of the needle. The distance of the needle from the lens is measured to be 45.0 cm. When the liquid is removed and the experiment is repeated, the new distance is 30.0 cm. Find the refractive index of the liquid.
explanation: We hold two razor blades in such a way that their edges are parallel and with a narrow slit in between. Keep the slit parallel to the filament of electric bulb, right in front of the eye. A diffraction is seen with its bright and dark bands. {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=(\mu-1)\left[\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right]{/tex} Focal length of convex lens, {tex}{f}_{1}=30 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{30}=(1.5-1)\left[\frac{1}{{R}}-\frac{1}{(-{R})}\right]{/tex} {tex}{R}=30 {~cm}{/tex} focal length of combination, {tex}{f}=45 {~cm}{/tex} focal length of plane concave lens of liquid. {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{2}} =\frac{1}{{f}}-\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{2}} =\frac{1}{45}-\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}{f}_{2} =-90 {~cm}{/tex} Using lens maker's formula {tex}\frac{1}{-90} =\left(\mu_{1}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{-30}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex} {tex}\mu_{1} =\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
solution: We hold two razor blades in such a way that their edges are parallel and with a narrow slit in between. Keep the slit parallel to the filament of electric bulb, right in front of the eye. A diffraction is seen with its bright and dark bands. {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=(\mu-1)\left[\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right]{/tex} Focal length of convex lens, {tex}{f}_{1}=30 {~cm}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{30}=(1.5-1)\left[\frac{1}{{R}}-\frac{1}{(-{R})}\right]{/tex} {tex}{R}=30 {~cm}{/tex} focal length of combination, {tex}{f}=45 {~cm}{/tex} focal length of plane concave lens of liquid. {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{2}} =\frac{1}{{f}}-\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{2}} =\frac{1}{45}-\frac{1}{30}{/tex} {tex}{f}_{2} =-90 {~cm}{/tex} Using lens maker's formula {tex}\frac{1}{-90} =\left(\mu_{1}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{-30}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex} {tex}\mu_{1} =\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. We hold two razor blades in such a way that their edges are parallel and with a narrow slit in between. Keep the slit parallel to the filament of electric bulb, right in front of the eye.
    A diffraction is seen with its bright and dark bands.
  2. {tex}\frac{1}{{f}}=(\mu-1)\left[\frac{1}{{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{{R}_{2}}\right]{/tex}
    Focal length of convex lens, {tex}{f}_{1}=30 {~cm}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{30}=(1.5-1)\left[\frac{1}{{R}}-\frac{1}{(-{R})}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}{R}=30 {~cm}{/tex}
    focal length of combination, {tex}{f}=45 {~cm}{/tex}
    focal length of plane concave lens of liquid.
    {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{2}} =\frac{1}{{f}}-\frac{1}{{f}_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{{f}_{2}} =\frac{1}{45}-\frac{1}{30}{/tex}
    {tex}{f}_{2} =-90 {~cm}{/tex}
    Using lens maker's formula
    {tex}\frac{1}{-90} =\left(\mu_{1}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{-30}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\mu_{1} =\frac{4}{3}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A ray of light is incident on a surface separating air from a denser medium A of refractive index {tex}\mu_{1}{/tex}. It is then made incident on the parallel surface of another medium B of refractive index {tex}\mu_{2}{/tex} at the same angle of incidence. If the angle of refraction in the two media are {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}35^{\circ}{/tex} respectively, then in which one of the two media (A or B) will light travel faster and why?
  2. The intensity of the two interfering waves in Young's double slit experiment is {tex}{I}_{0}{/tex} each. Find the intensity at a point on the screen where path difference between the interfering waves is (i) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}, and (ii) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex}.
question_text: A ray of light is incident on a surface separating air from a denser medium A of refractive index {tex}\mu_{1}{/tex} . It is then made incident on the parallel surface of another medium B of refractive index {tex}\mu_{2}{/tex} at the same angle of incidence. If the angle of refraction in the two media are {tex}30^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}35^{\circ}{/tex} respectively, then in which one of the two media (A or B) will light travel faster and why? The intensity of the two interfering waves in Young's double slit experiment is {tex}{I}_{0}{/tex} each. Find the intensity at a point on the screen where path difference between the interfering waves is (i) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} , and (ii) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex} .
explanation: Light travels faster in medium 'B' {tex}\mu_{1}=\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\mu_{2}=\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}=\frac{\sin {r}_{2}}{\sin {r}_{1}}=\frac{\sin 35^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}=\frac{{v}_{2}}{{v}_{1}} \gt 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore {v}_{2} \gt {v}_{1}{/tex} {tex}{I}=4 {I}_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{2}=\pi{/tex} {tex}{I}=4 {I}_{0}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{3}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}{I}=4 {I}_{0}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}{I}={I}_{0}{/tex}
solution: Light travels faster in medium 'B' {tex}\mu_{1}=\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\mu_{2}=\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}=\frac{\sin {r}_{2}}{\sin {r}_{1}}=\frac{\sin 35^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}=\frac{{v}_{2}}{{v}_{1}} \gt 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore {v}_{2} \gt {v}_{1}{/tex} {tex}{I}=4 {I}_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{2}=\pi{/tex} {tex}{I}=4 {I}_{0}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{3}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}{I}=4 {I}_{0}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}{I}={I}_{0}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Light travels faster in medium 'B'
    {tex}\mu_{1}=\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}_{1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\mu_{2}=\frac{\sin {i}}{\sin {r}_{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}=\frac{\sin {r}_{2}}{\sin {r}_{1}}=\frac{\sin 35^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}=\frac{{v}_{2}}{{v}_{1}} \gt 1{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {v}_{2} \gt {v}_{1}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{I}=4 {I}_{0} \cos ^{2} \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
    1. {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{2}=\pi{/tex}
      {tex}{I}=4 {I}_{0}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{2}=0{/tex}
    2. {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{3}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}
      {tex}{I}=4 {I}_{0}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}\right)^{2}{/tex}
      {tex}{I}={I}_{0}{/tex}

Write two points of difference between interference and diffraction patterns.
question_text: Diffraction of light is bending of light around the corners of an object whose size is comparable with the wavelength of light. Diffraction actually defines the limits of ray optics. This limit for optical instruments is set by the wavelength of light. An experimental arrangement is set up to observe the diffraction pattern due to a single slit. Write two points of difference between interference and diffraction patterns.
explanation: Differences Interference Pattern Diffraction Pattern All the maxima are equally spaced. Width of Central bright maxima is twice the width of the other maxima. The dark fringe is having zero intensity. The dark fringe is not completely dark. All the maxima are of the same intensity. There is a sharp decrease in the intensity of maxima after the central bright maxima.
solution: Differences Interference Pattern Diffraction Pattern All the maxima are equally spaced. Width of Central bright maxima is twice the width of the other maxima. The dark fringe is having zero intensity. The dark fringe is not completely dark. All the maxima are of the same intensity. There is a sharp decrease in the intensity of maxima after the central bright maxima.
solution_html:
Differences

Interference Pattern Diffraction Pattern
All the maxima are equally spaced. Width of Central bright maxima is twice the width of the other maxima.
The dark fringe is having zero intensity. The dark fringe is not completely dark.
All the maxima are of the same intensity. There is a sharp decrease in the intensity of maxima after the central bright maxima.

Two students are separated by a 7 m partition wall in a room 10 m high. If both light and sound waves can bend around obstacles, how is it that the students are unable to see each other even though they can converse easily?
question_text: Diffraction of light is bending of light around the corners of an object whose size is comparable with the wavelength of light. Diffraction actually defines the limits of ray optics. This limit for optical instruments is set by the wavelength of light. An experimental arrangement is set up to observe the diffraction pattern due to a single slit. Two students are separated by a 7 m partition wall in a room 10 m high. If both light and sound waves can bend around obstacles, how is it that the students are unable to see each other even though they can converse easily?
explanation: The opening (slit) is 3m; which is of the order of the wavelength of sound waves whereas it is very large compare to the wavelength of light. Hence, sound can bend around the obstacle while light cannot.
solution: The opening (slit) is 3m; which is of the order of the wavelength of sound waves whereas it is very large compare to the wavelength of light. Hence, sound can bend around the obstacle while light cannot.

Hence, sound can bend around the obstacle while light cannot.

question_html: State Huygen's principle. How did Huygens explain the absence of the backwave?
question_text: State Huygen's principle. How did Huygens explain the absence of the backwave?
explanation: Statement: Each point of the wavefront is the source of secondary disturbance in all directions. Common tangent to all the secondary wavelets gives new position of the wavefront. Explanation: Light energy cannot travel in backward direction.
solution: Statement: Each point of the wavefront is the source of secondary disturbance in all directions. Common tangent to all the secondary wavelets gives new position of the wavefront. Explanation: Light energy cannot travel in backward direction.
solution_html:
Statement: Each point of the wavefront is the source of secondary disturbance in all directions.
Common tangent to all the secondary wavelets gives new position of the wavefront.
Explanation: Light energy cannot travel in backward direction.

subject: Physics
question_html: Using Huygens' principle, draw a ray diagram showing the propagation of a plane wave refracting at a plane surface separating two media. Also verify Snell's law of refraction.
question_text: Using Huygens' principle, draw a ray diagram showing the propagation of a plane wave refracting at a plane surface separating two media. Also verify Snell's law of refraction.
explanation: AB is incident wave front, incident at an angle i. Let {tex}\tau{/tex} be time taken by the wave front to travel distance BC. BC = v 1 {tex}\tau{/tex} where v 1 is speed of wave in medium 1. To determine shape of refracted wave front, we draw a sphere of radius v 2 {tex}\tau{/tex} , where v 2 is speed of wave in medium 2. CE represents a tangent drawn from point C on sphere, CE is the refracted wave front. sin i = {tex}\frac{B C}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1 \tau}{A C}{/tex} sin r = {tex}\frac{A E}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_2 \tau}{A C}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\mathrm{n}_{21}{/tex}
solution: AB is incident wave front, incident at an angle i. Let {tex}\tau{/tex} be time taken by the wave front to travel distance BC. BC = v 1 {tex}\tau{/tex} where v 1 is speed of wave in medium 1. To determine shape of refracted wave front, we draw a sphere of radius v 2 {tex}\tau{/tex} , where v 2 is speed of wave in medium 2. CE represents a tangent drawn from point C on sphere, CE is the refracted wave front. sin i = {tex}\frac{B C}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1 \tau}{A C}{/tex} sin r = {tex}\frac{A E}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_2 \tau}{A C}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\mathrm{n}_{21}{/tex}
solution_html:
AB is incident wave front, incident at an angle i. Let {tex}\tau{/tex} be time taken by the wave front to travel distance BC.
BC = v1 {tex}\tau{/tex} where v1 is speed of wave in medium 1.
To determine shape of refracted wave front, we draw a sphere of radius v2 {tex}\tau{/tex}, where v2 is speed of wave in medium 2.
CE represents a tangent drawn from point C on sphere, CE is the refracted wave front.
sin i = {tex}\frac{B C}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1 \tau}{A C}{/tex}
sin r = {tex}\frac{A E}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_2 \tau}{A C}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\mathrm{n}_{21}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two coherent monochromatic light beams of intensities I and 4I superpose each other. Find the ratio of maximum and minimum intensities in the resulting beam.
question_text: Two coherent monochromatic light beams of intensities I and 4I superpose each other. Find the ratio of maximum and minimum intensities in the resulting beam.
explanation: {tex}\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{\left(\sqrt{I_1}+\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2}{\left(\sqrt{I_1}-\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{I_1+I_2+2 \sqrt{I_1} I_2}{I_1+I_2-2 \sqrt{I_1} I_2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5 I+4 I}{5 I-4 I}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9}{1}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{\left(\sqrt{I_1}+\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2}{\left(\sqrt{I_1}-\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{I_1+I_2+2 \sqrt{I_1} I_2}{I_1+I_2-2 \sqrt{I_1} I_2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5 I+4 I}{5 I-4 I}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9}{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{\left(\sqrt{I_1}+\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2}{\left(\sqrt{I_1}-\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{I_1+I_2+2 \sqrt{I_1} I_2}{I_1+I_2-2 \sqrt{I_1} I_2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5 I+4 I}{5 I-4 I}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{9}{1}{/tex}

question_html:
A plane wave-front propagating in a medium of refractive index {tex}\mu_1{/tex} is incident on a plane surface making an angle of incidence (i). It enters into a medium of refractive index {tex}\mu_2\left(\mu_2>\mu_1\right){/tex}.
Use Huygen's construction of secondary wavelets to trace the retracted wave-front. Hence verify Snell's law of refraction.
question_text: A plane wave-front propagating in a medium of refractive index {tex}\mu_1{/tex} is incident on a plane surface making an angle of incidence (i). It enters into a medium of refractive index {tex}\mu_2\left(\mu_2>\mu_1\right){/tex} . Use Huygen's construction of secondary wavelets to trace the retracted wave-front. Hence verify Snell's law of refraction.
explanation: AB is incident wavefront, incident at an angle i . let {tex}\tau{/tex} be the time taken by wavefront to travel distance BC. BC = v 1 {tex}\tau{/tex} where v 1 is speed of wave in medium 1 To determine shape of refracted wavefront, we draw a sphere of radius {tex}\mathrm{V}_2 \tau{/tex} , where v 2 is speed of wave in medium 2. CE represents a tangent drawn from point C on sphere, CE is the refracted wavefront. sin i = {tex}\frac{B C}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1 \tau}{A C}{/tex} and sin r = {tex}\frac{A E}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_2 \tau}{A C}{/tex} Where i and r are the angles of incidence and angle of refraction, respectively. {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}{/tex} {tex}\mu_1{/tex} sin i = {tex}\mu_2{/tex} sin r Hence, Snell’s law of refraction is verified.
solution: AB is incident wavefront, incident at an angle i . let {tex}\tau{/tex} be the time taken by wavefront to travel distance BC. BC = v 1 {tex}\tau{/tex} where v 1 is speed of wave in medium 1 To determine shape of refracted wavefront, we draw a sphere of radius {tex}\mathrm{V}_2 \tau{/tex} , where v 2 is speed of wave in medium 2. CE represents a tangent drawn from point C on sphere, CE is the refracted wavefront. sin i = {tex}\frac{B C}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1 \tau}{A C}{/tex} and sin r = {tex}\frac{A E}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_2 \tau}{A C}{/tex} Where i and r are the angles of incidence and angle of refraction, respectively. {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}{/tex} {tex}\mu_1{/tex} sin i = {tex}\mu_2{/tex} sin r Hence, Snell’s law of refraction is verified.
solution_html:
AB is incident wavefront, incident at an angle i . let {tex}\tau{/tex} be the time taken by wavefront to travel distance BC.
BC = v1{tex}\tau{/tex} where v1 is speed of wave in medium 1
To determine shape of refracted wavefront, we draw a sphere of radius {tex}\mathrm{V}_2 \tau{/tex}, where v2 is speed of wave in medium 2.
CE represents a tangent drawn from point C on sphere, CE is the refracted wavefront.
sin i = {tex}\frac{B C}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1 \tau}{A C}{/tex} and
sin r = {tex}\frac{A E}{A C}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_2 \tau}{A C}{/tex}
Where i and r are the angles of incidence and angle of refraction, respectively.
{tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_1}{\mathrm{v}_2}=\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}{/tex}
{tex}\mu_1{/tex} sin i = {tex}\mu_2{/tex} sin r
Hence, Snell’s law of refraction is verified.

question_html:
1. In which case is diffraction effect more dominant - slit formed by 2 blades or slit formed by two fingers?
  2. Yellow light ({tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o{/tex}) illuminates a single slit of width 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m. Calculate (i) the distance between two dark lines on either side of central maximum, in the diffraction pattern observed on a screen kept 1.5 m away from the slit, and (ii) the angular spread of the first minimum.
question_text: In which case is diffraction effect more dominant - slit formed by 2 blades or slit formed by two fingers? Yellow light ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o{/tex} ) illuminates a single slit of width 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m. Calculate (i) the distance between two dark lines on either side of central maximum, in the diffraction pattern observed on a screen kept 1.5 m away from the slit, and (ii) the angular spread of the first minimum.
explanation: Slit formed by two blades separation between two dark lines {tex}=\frac{2 \lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 6000 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.5}{1 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m {tex}\phi=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6000 \times 10^{-10}}{1 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 rad Hence, the angular spread of the first minimum is 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 rad.
solution: Slit formed by two blades separation between two dark lines {tex}=\frac{2 \lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 6000 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.5}{1 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m {tex}\phi=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6000 \times 10^{-10}}{1 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 rad Hence, the angular spread of the first minimum is 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 rad.
solution_html:
Slit formed by two blades

  1. separation between two dark lines
    {tex}=\frac{2 \lambda D}{d}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2 \times 6000 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.5}{1 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m
  2. {tex}\phi=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6000 \times 10^{-10}}{1 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex}
    = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 rad
    Hence, the angular spread of the first minimum is  6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 rad.

question_html: Using Huygen's construction, show how a plane wave is reflected from a surface. Hence verify the law of reflection.
question_text: Using Huygen's construction, show how a plane wave is reflected from a surface. Hence verify the law of reflection.
explanation: Consider a plane wave AB incident at an angle i on a reflecting surface MN. If v is the speed of the wave in the medium and if {tex}\tau{/tex} represents the time taken by the wavefront to advance from the point B to C then the distance covered by light from B to C is BC = v {tex}\tau{/tex} In order to construct the reflected wavefront, draw a sphere of radius v {tex}\tau{/tex} from the point A. Let CE represent the tangent plane, drawn from the point C to this sphere. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AEC and {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC AE = BC = v {tex}\tau{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex} CEA = {tex}\angle{/tex} ABC (90 o each) AC is common side {tex}\triangle A E C \cong \triangle A E C{/tex} {tex}\because \angle i=\angle r{/tex} Hence the law of reflection is verified.
solution: Consider a plane wave AB incident at an angle i on a reflecting surface MN. If v is the speed of the wave in the medium and if {tex}\tau{/tex} represents the time taken by the wavefront to advance from the point B to C then the distance covered by light from B to C is BC = v {tex}\tau{/tex} In order to construct the reflected wavefront, draw a sphere of radius v {tex}\tau{/tex} from the point A. Let CE represent the tangent plane, drawn from the point C to this sphere. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AEC and {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC AE = BC = v {tex}\tau{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex} CEA = {tex}\angle{/tex} ABC (90 o each) AC is common side {tex}\triangle A E C \cong \triangle A E C{/tex} {tex}\because \angle i=\angle r{/tex} Hence the law of reflection is verified.
solution_html:
Consider a plane wave AB incident at an angle i on a reflecting surface MN. If v is the speed of the wave in the medium and if {tex}\tau{/tex} represents the time taken by the wavefront to advance from the point B to C then the distance covered by light from B to C is 
BC = v{tex}\tau{/tex}
In order to construct the reflected wavefront, draw a sphere of radius v{tex}\tau{/tex} from the point A. Let CE represent the tangent plane, drawn from the point C to this sphere.
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AEC and {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC
AE = BC = v {tex}\tau{/tex}
{tex}\angle{/tex}CEA = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC (90o each)
AC is common side
{tex}\triangle A E C \cong \triangle A E C{/tex}
{tex}\because \angle i=\angle r{/tex}
Hence the law of reflection is verified.

question_text: A plane wave is incident on a concave mirror of radius of curvature R. The reflected wave is a spherical wave of radius:

question_html: In a Young's double slit experimental set-up, the intensity of the light waves from two coherent sources are in the ratio of 9 : 1. Find the ratio of intensity of bright and dark fringes in the interference pattern.
question_text: In a Young's double slit experimental set-up, the intensity of the light waves from two coherent sources are in the ratio of 9 : 1. Find the ratio of intensity of bright and dark fringes in the interference pattern.
explanation: {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{a^2}{b^2}=\frac{9}{1}{/tex} a and b are amplitude of two weaves. {tex}\frac{a}{b}=\frac{3}{1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{\text {bright }}}{I_{\text {dork }}}=\frac{(a+b)^2}{(a-b)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(3 b+b)^2}{(3 b-b)^2}=\frac{16 b^2}{4 b^2}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{a^2}{b^2}=\frac{9}{1}{/tex} a and b are amplitude of two weaves. {tex}\frac{a}{b}=\frac{3}{1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{\text {bright }}}{I_{\text {dork }}}=\frac{(a+b)^2}{(a-b)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(3 b+b)^2}{(3 b-b)^2}=\frac{16 b^2}{4 b^2}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{a^2}{b^2}=\frac{9}{1}{/tex} a and b are amplitude of two weaves.
{tex}\frac{a}{b}=\frac{3}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{I_{\text {bright }}}{I_{\text {dork }}}=\frac{(a+b)^2}{(a-b)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{(3 b+b)^2}{(3 b-b)^2}=\frac{16 b^2}{4 b^2}=\frac{4}{1}{/tex}

question_html: In a Young's double slit experimental set-up, the intensity of the light waves from two coherent sources are in the ratio of 4 : 9. Find the ratio of intensity of bright and dark fringes in the interference pattern.
question_text: In a Young's double slit experimental set-up, the intensity of the light waves from two coherent sources are in the ratio of 4 : 9. Find the ratio of intensity of bright and dark fringes in the interference pattern.
explanation: {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{a^2}{b^2}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex} a and b are amplitude of two waves. {tex}\frac{a}{b}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{\text {bright }}}{I_{\text {dark }}}=\frac{(a+b)^2}{(a-b)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{2}{3} b+b\right)^2}{\left(\frac{2}{3} b-b\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^2 b^2}{\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)^2 b^2}={/tex} {tex}\frac{5^2}{3^2} \times \frac{3^2}{12}{/tex} {tex}= \frac {25} {1} {/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{a^2}{b^2}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex} a and b are amplitude of two waves. {tex}\frac{a}{b}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{\text {bright }}}{I_{\text {dark }}}=\frac{(a+b)^2}{(a-b)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{2}{3} b+b\right)^2}{\left(\frac{2}{3} b-b\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^2 b^2}{\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)^2 b^2}={/tex} {tex}\frac{5^2}{3^2} \times \frac{3^2}{12}{/tex} {tex}= \frac {25} {1} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{a^2}{b^2}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
a and b are amplitude of two waves.
{tex}\frac{a}{b}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{I_{\text {bright }}}{I_{\text {dark }}}=\frac{(a+b)^2}{(a-b)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{2}{3} b+b\right)^2}{\left(\frac{2}{3} b-b\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^2 b^2}{\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)^2 b^2}={/tex} {tex}\frac{5^2}{3^2} \times \frac{3^2}{12}{/tex} {tex}= \frac {25} {1} {/tex}

question_html:
1. The interference pattern is not observed in Young's double slit experiment when the two sources S1 and  S2 are far apart. Explain.
  2. Mention the conditions for the two sources to be coherent.
  3. What is the effect on the interference pattern in a Young's double slit experiment, if the source of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is replaced by another source of wavelength 1.5 {tex}\lambda{/tex}, with the interference pattern still observable?
question_text: The interference pattern is not observed in Young's double slit experiment when the two sources S 1 and S 2 are far apart. Explain. Mention the conditions for the two sources to be coherent. What is the effect on the interference pattern in a Young's double slit experiment, if the source of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is replaced by another source of wavelength 1.5 {tex}\lambda{/tex} , with the interference pattern still observable?
explanation: fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} so as d increases {tex}\beta{/tex} decrease so fringes will not be resolved and observable. (they do not appear separate) Conditions:- Two sources produce waves of same frequency. Two sources produce waves of constant phase difference. {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} so if {tex}\lambda{/tex} changes by 1.5 {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} , fringe width increases, so more brighter will be the fringes.
solution: fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} so as d increases {tex}\beta{/tex} decrease so fringes will not be resolved and observable. (they do not appear separate) Conditions:- Two sources produce waves of same frequency. Two sources produce waves of constant phase difference. {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} so if {tex}\lambda{/tex} changes by 1.5 {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} , fringe width increases, so more brighter will be the fringes.
solution_html:
1. fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
    so as d increases {tex}\beta{/tex} decrease so fringes will not be resolved and observable. (they do not appear separate)
  2. Conditions:-
    • Two sources produce waves of same frequency.
    • Two sources produce waves of constant phase difference.
  3. {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
    so if {tex}\lambda{/tex} changes by 1.5{tex}\lambda{/tex}
    {tex}\beta{/tex}, fringe width increases, so more brighter will be the fringes.

subject: Physics
question_html:
How will the interference pattern in Young's double-slit experiment be affected if:

  1. The screen is moved away from the plane of the slits.
  2. The source slit is moved away from the plane of the slits.
  3. The phase difference between the light waves emanating from the two slits S1 and S2 changes from 0 to {tex}\pi{/tex} and remains constant.
question_text: How will the interference pattern in Young's double-slit experiment be affected if: The screen is moved away from the plane of the slits. The source slit is moved away from the plane of the slits. The phase difference between the light waves emanating from the two slits S 1 and S 2 changes from 0 to {tex}\pi{/tex} and remains constant.
explanation: {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} is fringe width D is distance of screen from slit d is distance between two slits ​​​​​​ {tex}\lambda{/tex} is wavelength of light When screen moves away D increases therefore fringe width increases. When the source slit is moved away from the plane of the slits, d increases and fringe pattern decreases and interference pattern disappears. When phase difference changes, the central fringe will become dark.
solution: {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} is fringe width D is distance of screen from slit d is distance between two slits ​​​​​​ {tex}\lambda{/tex} is wavelength of light When screen moves away D increases therefore fringe width increases. When the source slit is moved away from the plane of the slits, d increases and fringe pattern decreases and interference pattern disappears. When phase difference changes, the central fringe will become dark.
solution_html:
{tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} is fringe width
D is distance of screen from slit
d is distance between two slits
​​​​​​{tex}\lambda{/tex} is wavelength of light

  1. When screen moves away D increases therefore fringe width increases.
  2. When the source slit is moved away from the plane of the slits, d increases and fringe pattern decreases and interference pattern disappears.
  3. When phase difference changes, the central fringe will become dark.

subject: Physics
question_html: In single slit diffraction, explain why the maxima at {tex}\theta=\left(\mathrm{n}+\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{\lambda}{\mathrm{a}}\right){/tex} becomes weaker and weaker as n increases. State two important differences between interference and diffraction pattern.
question_text: In single slit diffraction, explain why the maxima at {tex}\theta=\left(\mathrm{n}+\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(\frac{\lambda}{\mathrm{a}}\right){/tex} becomes weaker and weaker as n increases. State two important differences between interference and diffraction pattern.
explanation: As n increases. deviation of light from straight direction increases thus the light spread out more and which result in decreasing intensity of bright fringes. In interference pattern the fringes are of equal width (dark and bright) and are of equal intensity and in diffraction the central maxima is brightest and its width is wider twice as compare to the other maxima and intensity of bands goes on decreasing as we move away from the Centre.
solution: As n increases. deviation of light from straight direction increases thus the light spread out more and which result in decreasing intensity of bright fringes. In interference pattern the fringes are of equal width (dark and bright) and are of equal intensity and in diffraction the central maxima is brightest and its width is wider twice as compare to the other maxima and intensity of bands goes on decreasing as we move away from the Centre.
solution_html:
As n increases. deviation of light from straight direction increases thus the light spread out more and which result in decreasing intensity of bright fringes.
In interference pattern the fringes are of equal width (dark and bright) and are of equal intensity and in diffraction the central maxima is brightest and its width is wider twice as compare to the other maxima and intensity of bands goes on decreasing as we move away from the Centre.

subject: Physics
question_html: A plane wavefront is incident on a surface separating two media of refractive indices n1 and n2 (> n1). With the help of a suitable diagram, explain its propagation from the rarer to denser medium. Hence, verify Snell's law.
question_text: A plane wavefront is incident on a surface separating two media of refractive indices n 1 and n 2 (> n 1 ). With the help of a suitable diagram, explain its propagation from the rarer to denser medium. Hence, verify Snell's law.
explanation: Let XY be interface of two mediums and c 1 and c 2 are velocity of light is rarer and denser medium respectively. Then, refractive index of denser medium relative to rarer medium {tex}\mu=\frac{c_1}{c_2}{/tex} Also, according to Huygens principle, every point of plane wavefront AB acts as a source of secondary wavelet. Let the secondary wavelets takes time t from B to strike at XY surface at A' BA' = c 1 {tex}\times{/tex} t in same time t wavelet from A travels with speed c 2 and strikes B' AB' = c 2 {tex}\times{/tex} t In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AA'B sin i = {tex}\frac{B A^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_1 t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AA'B' sin r = {tex}\frac{A B^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_2 \times t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{c_1}{c_2}{/tex} {tex}=\mu=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} This verifies the snell's law of refraction.
solution: Let XY be interface of two mediums and c 1 and c 2 are velocity of light is rarer and denser medium respectively. Then, refractive index of denser medium relative to rarer medium {tex}\mu=\frac{c_1}{c_2}{/tex} Also, according to Huygens principle, every point of plane wavefront AB acts as a source of secondary wavelet. Let the secondary wavelets takes time t from B to strike at XY surface at A' BA' = c 1 {tex}\times{/tex} t in same time t wavelet from A travels with speed c 2 and strikes B' AB' = c 2 {tex}\times{/tex} t In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AA'B sin i = {tex}\frac{B A^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_1 t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AA'B' sin r = {tex}\frac{A B^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_2 \times t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{c_1}{c_2}{/tex} {tex}=\mu=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} This verifies the snell's law of refraction.
solution_html:
Let XY be interface of two mediums and c1 and c2 are velocity of light is rarer and denser medium respectively.
Then, refractive index of denser medium relative to rarer medium {tex}\mu=\frac{c_1}{c_2}{/tex}
Also, according to Huygens principle, every point of plane wavefront AB acts as a source of secondary wavelet. Let the secondary wavelets takes time t from B to strike at XY surface at A' 
BA' = c1 {tex}\times{/tex} t
in same time t wavelet from A travels with speed c2 and strikes B'  

AB' = c2 {tex}\times{/tex} t
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AA'B
sin i = {tex}\frac{B A^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_1 t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex}
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AA'B'
sin r = {tex}\frac{A B^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_2 \times t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{c_1}{c_2}{/tex} {tex}=\mu=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} This verifies the snell's law of refraction.

subject: Physics
question_html:
In Young's double slit experiment, the slit separation is 1 mm and the screen is 1 m away from the slits. For a monochromatic light of wavelength 500 nm, find

  1. the distance of third minima, and
  2. the distance of second maxima,

from the central maxima.
question_text: In Young's double slit experiment, the slit separation is 1 mm and the screen is 1 m away from the slits. For a monochromatic light of wavelength 500 nm, find the distance of third minima, and the distance of second maxima, from the central maxima.
explanation: Given distance between two slits d = 1 mm = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m distance of screen D = 1 m {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 500 nm = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m Using condition for minima x = {tex}\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} n = 3 x = {tex}\left(3-\frac{1}{2}\right) \times \frac{1 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.25 mm Using condition for maxima x = {tex}\frac{n D \lambda}{d}{/tex} n = 2 x = {tex}2 \times \frac{500 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1 mm
solution: Given distance between two slits d = 1 mm = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m distance of screen D = 1 m {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 500 nm = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m Using condition for minima x = {tex}\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} n = 3 x = {tex}\left(3-\frac{1}{2}\right) \times \frac{1 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.25 mm Using condition for maxima x = {tex}\frac{n D \lambda}{d}{/tex} n = 2 x = {tex}2 \times \frac{500 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1 mm
solution_html:
Given distance between two slits d = 1 mm = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m
distance of screen D = 1 m
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 500 nm = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m

  1. Using condition for minima 
    x = {tex}\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
    n = 3
    x = {tex}\left(3-\frac{1}{2}\right) \times \frac{1 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.25 mm
  2. Using condition for maxima
    x = {tex}\frac{n D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
    n = 2
    x = {tex}2 \times \frac{500 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1 mm

subject: Physics
question_html:
A parallel beam of light of wavelength 600 nm is incident normally on a slit of width 0.2 mm. If the resulting diffraction pattern is observed on a screen 1 m away, find the distance of

  1. first minimum, and
  2. second maximum, from the central maximum.
question_text: A parallel beam of light of wavelength 600 nm is incident normally on a slit of width 0.2 mm. If the resulting diffraction pattern is observed on a screen 1 m away, find the distance of first minimum, and second maximum, from the central maximum.
explanation: Using the theory of diffraction fringes {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{D}=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} n = 1 a = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m D = 1 m {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m x = {tex}\frac{1 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 3 mm ; so the distance of first minima is 3 mm. {tex}\sin \theta=\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{D}=\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} n = 2 x = {tex}\frac{3 \lambda D}{2 a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{2 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 4.5 mm
solution: Using the theory of diffraction fringes {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{D}=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} n = 1 a = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m D = 1 m {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m x = {tex}\frac{1 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 3 mm ; so the distance of first minima is 3 mm. {tex}\sin \theta=\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{D}=\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} n = 2 x = {tex}\frac{3 \lambda D}{2 a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{2 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 4.5 mm
solution_html:
1. Using the theory of diffraction fringes    {tex}\sin \theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{x}{D}=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex}
    n = 1
    a = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m
    D = 1 m
    {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m
    x = {tex}\frac{1 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    = 3 mm ; so the distance of first minima is 3 mm.
  2. {tex}\sin \theta=\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{x}{D}=\left(n-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    n = 2
    x = {tex}\frac{3 \lambda D}{2 a}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{3 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{2 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    = 4.5 mm

subject: Physics
question_html: Using Huygen's principle, for a plane wavefront incident on a plane reflecting surface, draw the reflected wavefront. Hence prove laws of reflection.
question_text: Using Huygen's principle, for a plane wavefront incident on a plane reflecting surface, draw the reflected wavefront. Hence prove laws of reflection.
explanation: Consider a plane Mirror m 1 m 2 Let AB is the plane wavefront incident on the mirror such that {tex}\angle{/tex} BAA' = {tex}\angle{/tex} i 1, 2, 3 are corresponding incident rays perpendicular to AB. Let the secondary wavelet from B strike mirror at A' in t seconds. As according to Huygen's principle A ' B ' acts as reflected wavefront. BA' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t c = velocity of light similarly secondary wavelet travel in t time from A. AB' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t Thus A'B' is the secondary wavefront after t second. Hence A'B' is the true reflected wavefront. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} s AA'B and AA'B' BA' = AB' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t {tex}\angle{/tex} B = {tex}\angle{/tex} B' = 90 o AA' is common and thus {tex}\triangle{/tex} s are congruent {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex} i = {tex}\angle{/tex} r first law of reflection Also, incident wavefront AB, the reflected wavefront A'B' and reflecting surface m 1 m 2 all are perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Therefore, incident ray, normal to the mirror m 1 m 2 and reflected ray all lie in the plane of the paper. This is second law of reflection.
solution: Consider a plane Mirror m 1 m 2 Let AB is the plane wavefront incident on the mirror such that {tex}\angle{/tex} BAA' = {tex}\angle{/tex} i 1, 2, 3 are corresponding incident rays perpendicular to AB. Let the secondary wavelet from B strike mirror at A' in t seconds. As according to Huygen's principle A ' B ' acts as reflected wavefront. BA' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t c = velocity of light similarly secondary wavelet travel in t time from A. AB' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t Thus A'B' is the secondary wavefront after t second. Hence A'B' is the true reflected wavefront. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} s AA'B and AA'B' BA' = AB' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t {tex}\angle{/tex} B = {tex}\angle{/tex} B' = 90 o AA' is common and thus {tex}\triangle{/tex} s are congruent {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex} i = {tex}\angle{/tex} r first law of reflection Also, incident wavefront AB, the reflected wavefront A'B' and reflecting surface m 1 m 2 all are perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Therefore, incident ray, normal to the mirror m 1 m 2 and reflected ray all lie in the plane of the paper. This is second law of reflection.
solution_html:
Consider a plane Mirror m1 m2
Let AB is the plane wavefront incident on the mirror such that  {tex}\angle{/tex}BAA' = {tex}\angle{/tex}i
1, 2, 3 are corresponding incident rays perpendicular to AB.
 Let the secondary wavelet from B strike mirror at A' in t seconds. As according to Huygen's principle

A'B'  acts as reflected wavefront.

BA' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t
c = velocity of light
similarly secondary wavelet travel in t time from A.
AB' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t
Thus A'B' is the secondary wavefront after t second. Hence A'B' is the true reflected wavefront.
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}s AA'B and AA'B'
BA' = AB' = c {tex}\times{/tex} t
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}B' = 90o
AA' is common and thus {tex}\triangle{/tex}s are congruent
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}i = {tex}\angle{/tex}r first law of reflection
Also, incident wavefront AB, the reflected wavefront A'B' and reflecting surface m1m2 all are perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Therefore, incident ray, normal to the mirror m1m2 and reflected ray all lie in the plane of the paper.
This is second law of reflection.

subject: Physics
question_html:
A plane wavefront of light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident normally on a narrow slit of width a and a diffraction pattern is observed on a screen at a distance D from the slit.

  1. Depict the intensity distribution in the pattern observed.
  2. Obtain the expression for the first maximum from the central maximum.
question_text: A plane wavefront of light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident normally on a narrow slit of width a and a diffraction pattern is observed on a screen at a distance D from the slit. Depict the intensity distribution in the pattern observed. Obtain the expression for the first maximum from the central maximum.
explanation: Let us consider consider a slit AB is made up of two slits each of width {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} . As P 0 is point equidistant from all the huygen's sources therefore all will be in phase to give central maxima for first minima path differences between waves from A and O = {tex}\frac {\lambda} {2}{/tex} Let OP subtends angle {tex}\theta{/tex} at the slit then in {tex}\triangle{/tex} s ABC and OP 0 P With P as the centre and PA as radius, strike an are intersecting PB at C AC can be considered as straight line at right angle to PB thus {tex}\triangle{/tex} s ABC {tex}\sim{/tex} OP 0 P {tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} (PB - PO) + (PO - PA) = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}+\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} and {tex}\theta{/tex} is small so, {tex}\theta_n=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} n = 1, 2, 3 dark fringes Similarly for secondary maxima a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2a}{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} n = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} n = 1, 2, 3...... bright fringes {tex}\theta{/tex} n = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{D}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} n = 1 for first maxima x = {tex}\frac{3 \lambda D}{2a}{/tex}
solution: Let us consider consider a slit AB is made up of two slits each of width {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex} . As P 0 is point equidistant from all the huygen's sources therefore all will be in phase to give central maxima for first minima path differences between waves from A and O = {tex}\frac {\lambda} {2}{/tex} Let OP subtends angle {tex}\theta{/tex} at the slit then in {tex}\triangle{/tex} s ABC and OP 0 P With P as the centre and PA as radius, strike an are intersecting PB at C AC can be considered as straight line at right angle to PB thus {tex}\triangle{/tex} s ABC {tex}\sim{/tex} OP 0 P {tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} (PB - PO) + (PO - PA) = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}+\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} and {tex}\theta{/tex} is small so, {tex}\theta_n=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} n = 1, 2, 3 dark fringes Similarly for secondary maxima a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2a}{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} n = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} n = 1, 2, 3...... bright fringes {tex}\theta{/tex} n = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{D}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} n = 1 for first maxima x = {tex}\frac{3 \lambda D}{2a}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let us consider consider a slit AB is made up of two slits each of width {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex}.
As P0 is point equidistant from all the huygen's sources therefore all will be in phase to give central maxima for first minima path differences between waves from A and O = {tex}\frac {\lambda} {2}{/tex}

Let OP subtends angle  {tex}\theta{/tex} at the slit then in {tex}\triangle{/tex}s ABC and OP0P
With P as the centre and PA as radius, strike an are intersecting PB at C
AC can be considered as straight line at right angle to PB thus {tex}\triangle{/tex}s ABC {tex}\sim{/tex} OP0P
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
(PB - PO) + (PO - PA) = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}+\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
and {tex}\theta{/tex} is small so, {tex}\theta_n=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex}
n = 1, 2, 3 dark fringes
Similarly for secondary maxima
a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}
sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2a}{/tex}
{tex}\theta{/tex}n = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
n = 1, 2, 3...... bright fringes

  2. {tex}\theta{/tex}n = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{x}{D}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    n = 1 for first maxima
    x = {tex}\frac{3 \lambda D}{2a}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Is the speed of light in glass independent of the colour of light? Give reason.
  2. A small bulb is placed at the bottom of a tank containing water to a depth of 70 cm. Find the area of the surface of water through which light from the bulb can emerge out. Given refractive index of water is {tex}\frac 43{/tex}.
question_text: Is the speed of light in glass independent of the colour of light? Give reason. A small bulb is placed at the bottom of a tank containing water to a depth of 70 cm. Find the area of the surface of water through which light from the bulb can emerge out. Given refractive index of water is {tex}\frac 43{/tex} .
explanation: No, it depends on the colour of light. {tex}\mu{/tex} of violet colour is more than red colour and thus speed of violet light is less than red light. Actual depth of bulb d 1 = 0.7 m {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac {4} {3}{/tex} = 1.33 By snell law {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin 90^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{4}=\frac{\sin i}{1}{/tex} sin i = {tex}\frac {3} {4}{/tex} i = 48.75 o In {tex}\triangle{/tex} OBC tan i = {tex}\frac{O C}{O B}=\frac{O C}{0.7}{/tex} OC = tan 48.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.7 = 0.8 m , this can be considered as radius of circle, Area = {tex}\pi R^2{/tex} = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.8) 2 = 2.01 m 2
solution: No, it depends on the colour of light. {tex}\mu{/tex} of violet colour is more than red colour and thus speed of violet light is less than red light. Actual depth of bulb d 1 = 0.7 m {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac {4} {3}{/tex} = 1.33 By snell law {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin 90^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{4}=\frac{\sin i}{1}{/tex} sin i = {tex}\frac {3} {4}{/tex} i = 48.75 o In {tex}\triangle{/tex} OBC tan i = {tex}\frac{O C}{O B}=\frac{O C}{0.7}{/tex} OC = tan 48.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.7 = 0.8 m , this can be considered as radius of circle, Area = {tex}\pi R^2{/tex} = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.8) 2 = 2.01 m 2
solution_html:
1. No, it depends on the colour of light. {tex}\mu{/tex} of violet colour is more than red colour and thus speed of violet light is less than red light.


  2. Actual depth of bulb d1 = 0.7 m
    {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac {4} {3}{/tex} = 1.33
    By snell law {tex}\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin 90^{\circ}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{3}{4}=\frac{\sin i}{1}{/tex}
    sin i = {tex}\frac {3} {4}{/tex}
    i = 48.75o
    In {tex}\triangle{/tex}OBC tan i = {tex}\frac{O C}{O B}=\frac{O C}{0.7}{/tex}
    OC = tan 48.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.7
    = 0.8 m , this can be considered as radius of circle,
    Area = {tex}\pi R^2{/tex}
    = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.8)2
    = 2.01 m2

subject: Physics
question_html:
In a diffraction pattern due to a single slit, how will the angular width of central maximum change, if

  1. Orange light is used in place of green light,
  2. the screen is moved closer to the slit,
  3. the slit width is decreased?

Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: In a diffraction pattern due to a single slit, how will the angular width of central maximum change, if Orange light is used in place of green light, the screen is moved closer to the slit, the slit width is decreased? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: angular width of central maxima of a single slit diffraction is given as {tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} As {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases (orange light has greater wave length) diffraction angle 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} will also increase. Increasing or decreasing closeness of screen and slit does not affect angular width. If a (slit width) decreases, 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} will increase as {tex}2 \theta \propto \frac {1}{a}{/tex}
solution: angular width of central maxima of a single slit diffraction is given as {tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} As {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases (orange light has greater wave length) diffraction angle 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} will also increase. Increasing or decreasing closeness of screen and slit does not affect angular width. If a (slit width) decreases, 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} will increase as {tex}2 \theta \propto \frac {1}{a}{/tex}
solution_html:
angular width of central maxima of a single slit diffraction is given as  {tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}

  1. As {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases (orange light has greater wave length) diffraction angle 2{tex}\theta{/tex} will also increase.
  2. Increasing or decreasing closeness of screen and slit does not affect angular width.
  3. If a (slit width) decreases, 2{tex}\theta{/tex} will increase as {tex}2 \theta \propto \frac {1}{a}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
In a Young's double slit experiment using light of wavelength 600 nm, the slit separation is 0.8 mm and the screen is kept 1.6 m from the plane of the slits. Calculate:

  1. the fringe width
  2. the distance of (a) third minimum and (b) fifth maximum, from the central maximum.
question_text: In a Young's double slit experiment using light of wavelength 600 nm, the slit separation is 0.8 mm and the screen is kept 1.6 m from the plane of the slits. Calculate: the fringe width the distance of (a) third minimum and (b) fifth maximum, from the central maximum.
explanation: Fringe width is given as {tex}\beta=\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = 1.2 mm n = 3 distance of 3rd minima is given as x = {tex}\frac{(2 n-1)}{2} \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{6-1}{2}\right){\times} \frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 3 mm n = 5 distance of 5th minima x = {tex}\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 6 mm
solution: Fringe width is given as {tex}\beta=\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} {tex}\beta{/tex} = 1.2 mm n = 3 distance of 3rd minima is given as x = {tex}\frac{(2 n-1)}{2} \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{6-1}{2}\right){\times} \frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 3 mm n = 5 distance of 5th minima x = {tex}\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 6 mm
solution_html:
1.  Fringe width is given as {tex}\beta=\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex} 
    {tex}\beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    {tex}\beta{/tex} = 1.2 mm
    1. n = 3
      distance of 3rd minima is given as x = {tex}\frac{(2 n-1)}{2} \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex}
      = {tex}\left(\frac{6-1}{2}\right){\times} \frac{600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
      = 3 mm
    2. n = 5
      distance of 5th minima  x = {tex}\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex}
      = {tex}\frac{5 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
      = 6 mm

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. State two conditions for two light sources to be coherent.
  2. Give two points of difference between an interference pattern due to a double-slit and a diffraction pattern due to a single slit.
question_text: State two conditions for two light sources to be coherent. Give two points of difference between an interference pattern due to a double-slit and a diffraction pattern due to a single slit.
explanation: Conditions for light sources to be coherent:- Two sources produce waves of same frequency. Two sources produce waves of constant phase difference. In interference pattern the fringes are equally spaced (dark and bright) and of equal intensity and in diffraction the central maxima is brightest and is wider two times as the other maxima with intensity decreasing away from the Centre.
solution: Conditions for light sources to be coherent:- Two sources produce waves of same frequency. Two sources produce waves of constant phase difference. In interference pattern the fringes are equally spaced (dark and bright) and of equal intensity and in diffraction the central maxima is brightest and is wider two times as the other maxima with intensity decreasing away from the Centre.
solution_html:
1. Conditions for light sources to be coherent:-
    Two sources produce waves of same frequency.
    Two sources produce waves of constant phase difference.
  2. In interference pattern the fringes are equally spaced (dark and bright) and of equal intensity and in diffraction the central maxima is brightest and is wider two times as the other maxima with intensity decreasing away from the Centre.

subject: Physics
question_html: Monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm is incident from air on a water surface. The refractive index of water is 1.33. Find the (i) wavelength, (ii) frequency and (iii) speed, of reflected and refracted light.
question_text: Monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm is incident from air on a water surface. The refractive index of water is 1.33. Find the (i) wavelength, (ii) frequency and (iii) speed, of reflected and refracted light.
explanation: {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.33 {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}{/tex} 1.33 = {tex}\frac{600}{\lambda_2}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} = 451.1 nm ; therefore the wavelength of refracted light is 451.1 nm and wavelength of reflected light remains same i.e 600 nm. Frequency = c {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 = 180 Hz frequency remains same in other medum too. {tex}\mu=\frac{v_1}{v_2}{/tex} 1.33 = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^8}{v_2}{/tex} v 2 = 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s of reflected light
solution: {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.33 {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}{/tex} 1.33 = {tex}\frac{600}{\lambda_2}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} = 451.1 nm ; therefore the wavelength of refracted light is 451.1 nm and wavelength of reflected light remains same i.e 600 nm. Frequency = c {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 = 180 Hz frequency remains same in other medum too. {tex}\mu=\frac{v_1}{v_2}{/tex} 1.33 = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^8}{v_2}{/tex} v 2 = 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s of reflected light
solution_html:
1. {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.33
    {tex}\mu{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}{/tex}
    1.33 = {tex}\frac{600}{\lambda_2}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} = 451.1 nm ; therefore the wavelength of refracted light is 451.1 nm and wavelength  of reflected light remains same i.e 600 nm. 
  2. Frequency = c {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\lambda_1{/tex}
    = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 {tex}\times{/tex} 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9
    = 180 Hz
    frequency remains same in other medum too.
  3. {tex}\mu=\frac{v_1}{v_2}{/tex}
    1.33 = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^8}{v_2}{/tex}
    v2 = 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s of reflected light

subject: Physics
question_html:
How is the spacing between fringes in a double slit experiment affected if:

  1. the slits separation is increased,
  2. the colour of light used is changed from red to blue,
  3. the whole apparatus is submerged in a oil of refractive index 1.2?

Justify your anwer in each case.
question_text: How is the spacing between fringes in a double slit experiment affected if: the slits separation is increased, the colour of light used is changed from red to blue, the whole apparatus is submerged in a oil of refractive index 1.2? Justify your anwer in each case.
explanation: {tex}\beta=n \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} As d increases, {tex}\beta{/tex} decreases so spacing between fringes also decreases. As {tex}\lambda{/tex} decreases, {tex}\beta{/tex} also decreases so spacing between fringes also decreases. As whole apparatus is submerged in oil, {tex}\lambda{/tex} decreases So {tex}\beta{/tex} decreases and spacing between fringes will be narrow.
solution: {tex}\beta=n \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} As d increases, {tex}\beta{/tex} decreases so spacing between fringes also decreases. As {tex}\lambda{/tex} decreases, {tex}\beta{/tex} also decreases so spacing between fringes also decreases. As whole apparatus is submerged in oil, {tex}\lambda{/tex} decreases So {tex}\beta{/tex} decreases and spacing between fringes will be narrow.
solution_html:
{tex}\beta=n \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex}

  1. As d increases, {tex}\beta{/tex} decreases so spacing between fringes also decreases.
  2. As {tex}\lambda{/tex} decreases, {tex}\beta{/tex} also decreases so spacing between fringes also decreases.
  3. As whole apparatus is submerged in oil, {tex}\lambda{/tex} decreases So {tex}\beta{/tex} decreases and spacing between fringes will be narrow.

question_html:
A beam of light consisting of two wavelengths 600 nm and 500 nm is used in a Young's double slit experiment. The slit separation is 1.0 mm and the screen is kept 0.60 m away from the plane of the slits. Calculate:

  1. the distance of the second bright fringe from the central maximum for wavelength 500 nm, and
  2. the least distance from the central maximum where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide.
question_text: A beam of light consisting of two wavelengths 600 nm and 500 nm is used in a Young's double slit experiment. The slit separation is 1.0 mm and the screen is kept 0.60 m away from the plane of the slits. Calculate: the distance of the second bright fringe from the central maximum for wavelength 500 nm, and the least distance from the central maximum where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide.
explanation: n = 2 position of fringes is given as x = {tex}\frac{{ }_{n D \lambda}}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.60 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.6 mm Let n bright fringes of wavelength 600 nm coincide with (n + 1) bright fringes of wavelength 500 nm {tex}n \frac{D \lambda_1}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(n+1) D \lambda_2}{d}{/tex} {tex}\frac{600}{500}{/tex} = {tex}1+\frac{1}{n}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{5}=\frac{1}{n}{/tex} n = 5 x = {tex}\frac{5 \times D \lambda}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \times 500 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.6}{1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.5 nm
solution: n = 2 position of fringes is given as x = {tex}\frac{{ }_{n D \lambda}}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.60 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.6 mm Let n bright fringes of wavelength 600 nm coincide with (n + 1) bright fringes of wavelength 500 nm {tex}n \frac{D \lambda_1}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(n+1) D \lambda_2}{d}{/tex} {tex}\frac{600}{500}{/tex} = {tex}1+\frac{1}{n}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{5}=\frac{1}{n}{/tex} n = 5 x = {tex}\frac{5 \times D \lambda}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \times 500 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.6}{1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.5 nm
solution_html:
1. n = 2
    position of fringes is given as x = {tex}\frac{{ }_{n D \lambda}}{d}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.60 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    = 0.6 mm
  2. Let n bright fringes of wavelength 600 nm coincide with (n + 1) bright fringes of wavelength 500 nm
    {tex}n \frac{D \lambda_1}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(n+1) D \lambda_2}{d}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{600}{500}{/tex} = {tex}1+\frac{1}{n}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{5}=\frac{1}{n}{/tex}
    n = 5
    x = {tex}\frac{5 \times D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{5 \times 500 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.6}{1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    = 1.5 nm

question_html: Briefly explain how bright and dark fringes are formed on the screen in Young's double slit experiment. Hence, derive the expression for the fringe width.
question_text: Briefly explain how bright and dark fringes are formed on the screen in Young's double slit experiment. Hence, derive the expression for the fringe width.
explanation: Let A and B are two fine slits at a distance of d. Let monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is illuminated through these slits. If O is the centre of slit having width d. Consider a point P at a distance x from C. Path difference of two waves = BP - AP In {tex}\triangle{/tex} PBF BP 2 = BF 2 + PF 2 BP = {tex}\left(B F^2+P F^2\right)^\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\left(D^2+(x+\frac{d}{2}^2\right)^\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}D\left[1+\frac{(x+\frac{d}{2})^2}{D^2}\right]^\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}D\left[1+\frac{1}{2} \frac{(x+\frac{d}{2})^2}{D^2}\right]{/tex} Similarly in {tex}\triangle{/tex} PAE AP = {tex}\left(A E^2+P E^2\right)^\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\left.\left[D^2+(x-\frac{d}{2}\right)^2\right]^\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}D\left[1+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\frac{d}{2}}{D^2}\right)^2\right]{/tex} So, BP-AP = {tex}D+\frac{1}{2} \frac{(x+\frac{d}{2})^2}{D}-D-\frac{1}{2 D}(x-\frac{d}{2})^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 D}[\frac {4 x d}{2}]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x d}{D}{/tex} For bright fringes, {tex}\frac{x d}{D}{/tex} = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex} n = 0, 1, 2, 3... n = 0 for central maxima for dark fringes {tex}\frac{x d}{D}{/tex} = {tex}(2 n-1) \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} x = {tex}(2 n-1) \frac{\lambda D}{2 d}{/tex} n = 1, 2 ... by using bright fringes fringe width = {tex}x_n-x_{n-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n \lambda D}{d}-(n-1) \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex}
solution: Let A and B are two fine slits at a distance of d. Let monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is illuminated through these slits. If O is the centre of slit having width d. Consider a point P at a distance x from C. Path difference of two waves = BP - AP In {tex}\triangle{/tex} PBF BP 2 = BF 2 + PF 2 BP = {tex}\left(B F^2+P F^2\right)^\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\left(D^2+(x+\frac{d}{2}^2\right)^\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}D\left[1+\frac{(x+\frac{d}{2})^2}{D^2}\right]^\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}D\left[1+\frac{1}{2} \frac{(x+\frac{d}{2})^2}{D^2}\right]{/tex} Similarly in {tex}\triangle{/tex} PAE AP = {tex}\left(A E^2+P E^2\right)^\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\left.\left[D^2+(x-\frac{d}{2}\right)^2\right]^\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}D\left[1+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\frac{d}{2}}{D^2}\right)^2\right]{/tex} So, BP-AP = {tex}D+\frac{1}{2} \frac{(x+\frac{d}{2})^2}{D}-D-\frac{1}{2 D}(x-\frac{d}{2})^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 D}[\frac {4 x d}{2}]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x d}{D}{/tex} For bright fringes, {tex}\frac{x d}{D}{/tex} = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex} n = 0, 1, 2, 3... n = 0 for central maxima for dark fringes {tex}\frac{x d}{D}{/tex} = {tex}(2 n-1) \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} x = {tex}(2 n-1) \frac{\lambda D}{2 d}{/tex} n = 1, 2 ... by using bright fringes fringe width = {tex}x_n-x_{n-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n \lambda D}{d}-(n-1) \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A and B are two fine slits at a distance of d. Let monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is illuminated through these slits. If O is the centre of slit having width d. Consider a point P at a distance x from C.
Path difference of two waves = BP - AP

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}PBF
BP2 = BF2 + PF2
BP = {tex}\left(B F^2+P F^2\right)^\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\left(D^2+(x+\frac{d}{2}^2\right)^\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}D\left[1+\frac{(x+\frac{d}{2})^2}{D^2}\right]^\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}D\left[1+\frac{1}{2} \frac{(x+\frac{d}{2})^2}{D^2}\right]{/tex}
Similarly in {tex}\triangle{/tex}PAE
AP = {tex}\left(A E^2+P E^2\right)^\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\left.\left[D^2+(x-\frac{d}{2}\right)^2\right]^\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}D\left[1+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\frac{d}{2}}{D^2}\right)^2\right]{/tex}
So, BP-AP
= {tex}D+\frac{1}{2} \frac{(x+\frac{d}{2})^2}{D}-D-\frac{1}{2 D}(x-\frac{d}{2})^2{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2 D}[\frac {4 x d}{2}]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{x d}{D}{/tex}
For bright fringes,
{tex}\frac{x d}{D}{/tex} = n{tex}\lambda{/tex}
x = {tex}\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex}
n = 0, 1, 2, 3...
n = 0 for central maxima
for dark fringes
{tex}\frac{x d}{D}{/tex} = {tex}(2 n-1) \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}
x = {tex}(2 n-1) \frac{\lambda D}{2 d}{/tex} n = 1, 2 ...
by using bright fringes
fringe width = {tex}x_n-x_{n-1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{n \lambda D}{d}-(n-1) \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex}
{tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex}

question_html: A plane wavefront is propagating from a rarer into a denser medium. Use Huygens principle to show the refracted wavefront and verify Snell's law.
question_text: A plane wavefront is propagating from a rarer into a denser medium. Use Huygens principle to show the refracted wavefront and verify Snell's law.
explanation: Let XY be interface and c 1 and c 2 are velocity of light in rarer and denser medium respectively. then, {tex}\mu=\frac{c_1}{c_2}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} is refractive index of medium 2 with respect to medium 1. According to Huygens principle, every point of plane wavefront AB acts as a source of secondary wavelet. Let the secondary wavelets from B strikes XY at A' in t seconds BA' = c 1 {tex}\times{/tex} t Similarly from A secondary wavelet travels in denser medium with velocity c 2 AB' = c 2 {tex}\times{/tex} t In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AA'B sin i = {tex}\frac{B A^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_1 t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AA'B' sin r = {tex}\frac{A B^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_2 \times t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{c_1}{c_2}=u=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} This proves snell's law of refraction.
solution: Let XY be interface and c 1 and c 2 are velocity of light in rarer and denser medium respectively. then, {tex}\mu=\frac{c_1}{c_2}{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} is refractive index of medium 2 with respect to medium 1. According to Huygens principle, every point of plane wavefront AB acts as a source of secondary wavelet. Let the secondary wavelets from B strikes XY at A' in t seconds BA' = c 1 {tex}\times{/tex} t Similarly from A secondary wavelet travels in denser medium with velocity c 2 AB' = c 2 {tex}\times{/tex} t In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AA'B sin i = {tex}\frac{B A^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_1 t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex} In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AA'B' sin r = {tex}\frac{A B^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_2 \times t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{c_1}{c_2}=u=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} This proves snell's law of refraction.
solution_html:
Let XY be interface and c1 and c2 are velocity of light in rarer and denser medium respectively.

then, {tex}\mu=\frac{c_1}{c_2}{/tex}
{tex}\mu{/tex} is refractive index of medium 2 with respect to medium 1.
 According to Huygens principle, every point of plane wavefront AB acts as a source of secondary wavelet. Let the secondary wavelets from B strikes XY at A' in t seconds
BA' = c1 {tex}\times{/tex} t
Similarly from A secondary wavelet travels in denser medium with velocity c2
AB' = c2 {tex}\times{/tex} t
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AA'B
sin i = {tex}\frac{B A^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_1 t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex}
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}AA'B' sin r = {tex}\frac{A B^{\prime}}{A A^{\prime}}=\frac{C_2 \times t}{A A^{\prime}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{c_1}{c_2}=u=\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} This proves  snell's law of refraction.
