question_html: Briefly explain how bright and dark fringes are formed on a screen due to the diffraction at a single slit. Hence, explain why the intensity at the bright fringes decreases sharply as their order (n) increases.
question_text: Briefly explain how bright and dark fringes are formed on a screen due to the diffraction at a single slit. Hence, explain why the intensity at the bright fringes decreases sharply as their order (n) increases.
explanation: Consider a slit AB is made up of two adjacent slits each of width {tex}\frac {a} {2}{/tex} P 0 is point equidistant from all the huygen's sources thus all will be in phase to give central maxima for first minima Path differences between waves from A and O reaching at P = {tex}\frac {\lambda} {2}{/tex} Let OP subtends {tex}\theta{/tex} at the slit then in {tex}\triangle{/tex} s ABC and OP 0 P with P as the centre and PA as radius, strike an arc intersecting PB at C AC can be considered as straight line at right angle to PB thus {tex}\triangle{/tex} s ABC {tex}\sim{/tex} OP 0 P {tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} (PB - PO) + (PO - PA) = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}+\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} and {tex}\theta{/tex} is small so, {tex}\theta_n=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3 dark fringes) Similarly for secondary maxima a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 a}{/tex} {tex}\theta_n=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3... bright fringes) So as n increases {tex}\theta{/tex} n increases thus light spread out over an area thus reducing intensity of bright fringes.
solution: Consider a slit AB is made up of two adjacent slits each of width {tex}\frac {a} {2}{/tex} P 0 is point equidistant from all the huygen's sources thus all will be in phase to give central maxima for first minima Path differences between waves from A and O reaching at P = {tex}\frac {\lambda} {2}{/tex} Let OP subtends {tex}\theta{/tex} at the slit then in {tex}\triangle{/tex} s ABC and OP 0 P with P as the centre and PA as radius, strike an arc intersecting PB at C AC can be considered as straight line at right angle to PB thus {tex}\triangle{/tex} s ABC {tex}\sim{/tex} OP 0 P {tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} (PB - PO) + (PO - PA) = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}+\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} and {tex}\theta{/tex} is small so, {tex}\theta_n=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3 dark fringes) Similarly for secondary maxima a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 a}{/tex} {tex}\theta_n=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3... bright fringes) So as n increases {tex}\theta{/tex} n increases thus light spread out over an area thus reducing intensity of bright fringes.
solution_html:
Consider a slit AB is made up of two adjacent slits each of width {tex}\frac {a} {2}{/tex}

P0 is point equidistant from all the huygen's sources thus all will be in phase to give central maxima
for first minima
Path differences between waves from A and O reaching at P = {tex}\frac {\lambda} {2}{/tex}
Let OP subtends {tex}\theta{/tex} at the slit then in {tex}\triangle{/tex}s ABC and OP0P with P as the centre and PA as radius, strike an arc intersecting PB at C
AC can be considered as straight line at right angle to PB
thus {tex}\triangle{/tex}sABC {tex}\sim{/tex} OP0P
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = a sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
(PB - PO) + (PO - PA) = a sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}+\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} = a sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
and {tex}\theta{/tex} is small so, {tex}\theta_n=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3 dark fringes)
Similarly for secondary maxima
a sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}
sin{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 a}{/tex}
{tex}\theta_n=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3... bright fringes)
So as n increases {tex}\theta{/tex}n increases thus light spread out over an area thus reducing intensity of bright fringes.

question_html: How can you differentiate whether a pattern is produced by a single slit or double slits? Derive the expression for the angular position of (i) bright and (ii) dark fringes produced in a single slit diffraction.
question_text: How can you differentiate whether a pattern is produced by a single slit or double slits? Derive the expression for the angular position of (i) bright and (ii) dark fringes produced in a single slit diffraction.
explanation: Fringes of same width and intensity are produced by double slits and fringes with varying intensity and varying width are produced by single slit. For bright fringes in a single slit the angular position of fringes in single slit is given as {tex}\sin \theta_1=\frac{n \lambda}{2 a}{/tex} n = 0, 1, 2, 3 n = 0 for central maximum For dark fringes in a single slit the angular position of nth dark fringe in single slit is given as {tex}\sin \theta_1=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex}
solution: Fringes of same width and intensity are produced by double slits and fringes with varying intensity and varying width are produced by single slit. For bright fringes in a single slit the angular position of fringes in single slit is given as {tex}\sin \theta_1=\frac{n \lambda}{2 a}{/tex} n = 0, 1, 2, 3 n = 0 for central maximum For dark fringes in a single slit the angular position of nth dark fringe in single slit is given as {tex}\sin \theta_1=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex}
solution_html:
Fringes of same width and intensity are produced by double slits and fringes with varying intensity and varying width are produced by single slit.

  1. For bright fringes in a single slit
                                                                                                         the angular position of fringes in single slit is given as 
    {tex}\sin \theta_1=\frac{n \lambda}{2 a}{/tex}
    n = 0, 1, 2, 3
    n = 0 for central maximum
  2. For dark fringes in a single slit
                                                                                                              the angular position of nth dark fringe in single slit  is given as 
    {tex}\sin \theta_1=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex}

question_html:
A slit of width 0.6 mm is illuminated by a beam of light consisting of two wavelengths 600 nm and 480 nm. The diffraction pattern is observed on a screen 1.0 m from the slit. Find:

  1. The distance of the second bright fringe from the central maximum pertaining to light of 600 nm.
  2. The least distance from the central maximum at which bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide.
question_text: A slit of width 0.6 mm is illuminated by a beam of light consisting of two wavelengths 600 nm and 480 nm. The diffraction pattern is observed on a screen 1.0 m from the slit. Find: The distance of the second bright fringe from the central maximum pertaining to light of 600 nm. The least distance from the central maximum at which bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide.
explanation: n = 2 distance of second bright fringe = {tex}n \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.002 m Let n th bright of 600nm coincide with (n + 1) th bright of 480nm, by the formula of distance of bright bands we get {tex}\frac{n \lambda_1 D}{d}=\frac{(n+1) \lambda_2 D}{d}{/tex} n {tex}\times{/tex} 600 = (n + 1) 480 {tex}\frac{60}{48}=1+\frac{1}{n}{/tex} 1.25 = {tex}1+\frac{1}{n}{/tex} 0.25 = {tex}\frac{1}{n}{/tex} n = 4 So distance = {tex}\frac{4 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.004 m
solution: n = 2 distance of second bright fringe = {tex}n \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.002 m Let n th bright of 600nm coincide with (n + 1) th bright of 480nm, by the formula of distance of bright bands we get {tex}\frac{n \lambda_1 D}{d}=\frac{(n+1) \lambda_2 D}{d}{/tex} n {tex}\times{/tex} 600 = (n + 1) 480 {tex}\frac{60}{48}=1+\frac{1}{n}{/tex} 1.25 = {tex}1+\frac{1}{n}{/tex} 0.25 = {tex}\frac{1}{n}{/tex} n = 4 So distance = {tex}\frac{4 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.004 m
solution_html:
1. n = 2
    distance of second bright fringe = {tex}n \frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{2 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    = 0.002 m
  2. Let nth bright of 600nm coincide with (n + 1)th bright of 480nm, by the formula of distance of bright bands we get 
    {tex}\frac{n \lambda_1 D}{d}=\frac{(n+1) \lambda_2 D}{d}{/tex}
    n {tex}\times{/tex} 600 = (n + 1) 480
    {tex}\frac{60}{48}=1+\frac{1}{n}{/tex}
    1.25 = {tex}1+\frac{1}{n}{/tex}
    0.25 = {tex}\frac{1}{n}{/tex}
    n = 4
    So distance = {tex}\frac{4 \times 600 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    = 0.004 m

question_html: Monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm is incident from air on a water surface. If the refractive index of water is {tex}\frac 43{/tex}, then find the wavelength of the refracted light.
question_text: Monochromatic light of wavelength 600 nm is incident from air on a water surface. If the refractive index of water is {tex}\frac 43{/tex} , then find the wavelength of the refracted light.
explanation: Wavelength of refracted light {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\text {air }}}{n}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 600}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1800}{4}{/tex} = 450 nm
solution: Wavelength of refracted light {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\text {air }}}{n}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 600}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1800}{4}{/tex} = 450 nm
solution_html:
Wavelength of refracted light
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\text {air }}}{n}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 600}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1800}{4}{/tex}
= 450 nm

question_html:
The British physicist Thomas Young explained the interference of light using the principle of superposition of waves. He observed the interference pattern on the screen, in his experimental set-up, known now as Young's double slit experiment. The two slits S1 and S2 were illuminated by light from a slit S. The interference pattern consists of dark and bright bands of light. Such bands are called fringes. The distance between two consecutive bright and dark fringes is called fringe width.


If the screen is moved closer to the plane of slits S1 and S2, then the fringe width:
question_text: The British physicist Thomas Young explained the interference of light using the principle of superposition of waves. He observed the interference pattern on the screen, in his experimental set-up, known now as Young's double slit experiment. The two slits S 1 and S 2 were illuminated by light from a slit S. The interference pattern consists of dark and bright bands of light. Such bands are called fringes. The distance between two consecutive bright and dark fringes is called fringe width. If the screen is moved closer to the plane of slits S 1 and S 2 , then the fringe width:

options.text: will decrease, but the intensity of bright fringe remains the same.

subject: Physics
question_html:
The British physicist Thomas Young explained the interference of light using the principle of superposition of waves. He observed the interference pattern on the screen, in his experimental set-up, known now as Young's double slit experiment. The two slits S1 and S2 were illuminated by light from a slit S. The interference pattern consists of dark and bright bands of light. Such bands are called fringes. The distance between two consecutive bright and dark fringes is called fringe width.


What will happen to the pattern on the screen, when the two slits S1 and S2 are replaced by two independent but identical sources?

subject: Physics
question_html:
The principle of superposition is used to understand the phenomenon of interference of light waves. The principle states that at a particular point, the resultant displacement produced by a number of waves is the vector sum of the displacements produced by each wave. Light waves from two coherent sources produce interference pattern. Thomas Young devised a way to obtain two coherent sources using two identical pinholes (S1 and S2) illuminated by a single monochromatic pinhole source S. Using these sources in his experiment known as Young's double slit experiment, Young studied the interference pattern. The pattern consists of alternate bright and dark fringes. The distance between two successive bright or dark finges depends on the distance between S1 and S2, the distance of the screen from the plane of S1 S2 and the wavelength of light used.


Consider the following waves:

  1. y1 = a sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t
  2. y2 = a sin 2{tex}\omega{/tex}t
  3. y3 = a sin (2{tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex})
  4. y4 = a sin (4{tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex})

Which pair of the waves coming from two sources S1 and S2 will produce interference?
question_text: The principle of superposition is used to understand the phenomenon of interference of light waves. Thomas Young devised a way to obtain two coherent sources using two identical pinholes (S 1 and S 2 ) illuminated by a single monochromatic pinhole source S. Using these sources in his experiment known as Young's double slit experiment, Young studied the interference pattern. The pattern consists of alternate bright and dark fringes. The distance between two successive bright or dark finges depends on the distance between S 1 and S 2 , the distance of the screen from the plane of S 1 S 2 and the wavelength of light used. Consider the following waves: y 1 = a sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t y 2 = a sin 2 {tex}\omega{/tex} t y 3 = a sin (2 {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) y 4 = a sin (4 {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} ) Which pair of the waves coming from two sources S 1 and S 2 will produce interference?

options.explanation: (ii) and (iii)

explanation: (ii) and (iii)

question_html: In interference of light, write the expression for the intensity of resultant wave if I0 is the intensity of light wave from each slit. Hence, obtain an expression for the intensity of resultant wave if the two sources are (i) incoherent, and (ii) coherent.
question_text: In interference of light, write the expression for the intensity of resultant wave if I 0 is the intensity of light wave from each slit. Hence, obtain an expression for the intensity of resultant wave if the two sources are (i) incoherent, and (ii) coherent.
explanation: I = 4I o Cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} <I> = 4 I o <cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} > = 4I o {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 2I o I = 4I o cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} For constructive interference cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 1 I = 4 I o For constructive interference cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 0 I = 0
solution: I = 4I o Cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} <I> = 4 I o <cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} > = 4I o {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 2I o I = 4I o cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} For constructive interference cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 1 I = 4 I o For constructive interference cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 0 I = 0
solution_html:
I = 4Io Cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}

  1. <I> = 4 Io <cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}>
    = 4Io {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    = 2Io

  2. I = 4Io cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
    For constructive interference cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 1
    I = 4 Io
    For constructive interference cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 0
    I = 0

question_html:
1. Explain the formation of the fringes due to diffraction at a single slit, when path difference of light waves from the ends of the slit on reaching a point on the screen is
    1. {tex}\lambda{/tex}, and
    2. {tex}\frac{3 \lambda}{2}{/tex}.
  2. Show the intensity distribution in the fringes due to diffraction at a single slit.
question_text: Explain the formation of the fringes due to diffraction at a single slit, when path difference of light waves from the ends of the slit on reaching a point on the screen is {tex}\lambda{/tex} , and {tex}\frac{3 \lambda}{2}{/tex} . Show the intensity distribution in the fringes due to diffraction at a single slit.
explanation: In diffraction, for path diff. {tex} \lambda{/tex} ; the light waves from two consecutive parts of the slit reach at a point with path diff. of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{ 2}{/tex} having a separation equal to half of the width of the slit. Therefore contribution from two halves of the slit gets cancelled, resulting Zero intensity. For the path diff {tex}\frac{3 \lambda}{2}{/tex} , the light waves from two consecutive parts of the slit having separation equal to one third of the width of the slit reach a point on the screen with path diff. of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} . Therefore, contribution from two consecutive portion of the slit gets cancelled and illumination is due to only one third portion of the slit.
solution: In diffraction, for path diff. {tex} \lambda{/tex} ; the light waves from two consecutive parts of the slit reach at a point with path diff. of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{ 2}{/tex} having a separation equal to half of the width of the slit. Therefore contribution from two halves of the slit gets cancelled, resulting Zero intensity. For the path diff {tex}\frac{3 \lambda}{2}{/tex} , the light waves from two consecutive parts of the slit having separation equal to one third of the width of the slit reach a point on the screen with path diff. of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} . Therefore, contribution from two consecutive portion of the slit gets cancelled and illumination is due to only one third portion of the slit.
solution_html:
1. In diffraction, for path diff. {tex} \lambda{/tex}; the light waves from two consecutive parts of the slit reach at a point with path diff. of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{ 2}{/tex} having a separation equal to half of the width of the slit. Therefore contribution from two halves of the slit gets cancelled, resulting Zero intensity.
    2. For the path diff {tex}\frac{3 \lambda}{2}{/tex}, the light waves from two consecutive parts of the slit having separation equal to one third of the width of the slit reach a point on the screen with path diff. of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}. Therefore, contribution from two consecutive portion of the slit gets cancelled and illumination is due to only one third portion of the slit.

subject: Physics
question_html: In a single slit diffraction experiment, the width of the slit is made double the original width. How does this affect the size and intensity of the central diffraction band.
question_text: In a single slit diffraction experiment, the width of the slit is made double the original width. How does this affect the size and intensity of the central diffraction band.
explanation: Width of central diffraction band {tex} = 2D\frac{\lambda }{d}{/tex} . So, if we double the slit size, the width of the central maxima decreases by half. When the width of the central maxima is reduced to half, the area of the central diffraction band will become one fourth. Therefore, the intensity becomes four times (because I {tex}\propto {/tex} a 2 ).
solution: Width of central diffraction band {tex} = 2D\frac{\lambda }{d}{/tex} . So, if we double the slit size, the width of the central maxima decreases by half. When the width of the central maxima is reduced to half, the area of the central diffraction band will become one fourth. Therefore, the intensity becomes four times (because I {tex}\propto {/tex} a 2 ).
solution_html:
Width of central diffraction band {tex} = 2D\frac{\lambda }{d}{/tex}. So, if we double the slit size, the width of the central maxima decreases by half.
When the width of the central maxima is reduced to half, the area of the central diffraction band will become one fourth. Therefore, the intensity becomes four times (because I {tex}\propto {/tex} a2).

subject: Physics
question_html:
What is the effect on the interference fringes in Young’s double-slit experiment due to each of the following operations? Justify your answers.

  1. The screen is moved away from the plane of the slits.
  2. The separation between slits is increased.
  3. The source slit is moved closer to the plane of double slit.
question_text: What is the effect on the interference fringes in Young’s double-slit experiment due to each of the following operations? Justify your answers. The screen is moved away from the plane of the slits. The separation between slits is increased. The source slit is moved closer to the plane of double slit.
explanation: D is Distance between screen to slits, d is distance between the slits, λ is wavelength Fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} Since {tex}\beta \propto D{/tex} , the fringe width will increase, as screen is moved away. {tex}\beta \propto \frac{1}{d}{/tex} , therefore fringe width will decrease as the separation between slits is increased. Let s be the width of the source slit and S its distance from plane of two slit. For interference fringes to be distinctly seen, the condition {tex}\frac{s}{S}<\frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} should be satisfied, otherwise, the interference patterns produced will overlap.
solution: D is Distance between screen to slits, d is distance between the slits, λ is wavelength Fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} Since {tex}\beta \propto D{/tex} , the fringe width will increase, as screen is moved away. {tex}\beta \propto \frac{1}{d}{/tex} , therefore fringe width will decrease as the separation between slits is increased. Let s be the width of the source slit and S its distance from plane of two slit. For interference fringes to be distinctly seen, the condition {tex}\frac{s}{S}<\frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} should be satisfied, otherwise, the interference patterns produced will overlap.
solution_html:
1.  D is Distance between screen to slits,  d is distance between the slits, λ is wavelength

    Fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} 
    Since {tex}\beta \propto D{/tex}, the fringe width will increase, as screen is moved away.
  2. {tex}\beta \propto \frac{1}{d}{/tex}, therefore fringe width will decrease as the separation between slits is increased.
  3. Let s be the width of the source slit and S its distance from plane of two slit. For interference fringes to be distinctly seen, the condition
    {tex}\frac{s}{S}<\frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} 
    should be satisfied, otherwise, the interference patterns produced will overlap.

question_html: Define wavefront of a travelling wave. Using Huygens principle, obtain the law of refraction at a plane interface when light passes from a denser to rarer medium.
question_text: Define wavefront of a travelling wave. Using Huygens principle, obtain the law of refraction at a plane interface when light passes from a denser to rarer medium.
explanation: Wavefront: A locus of the points which oscillates in the same phase is called a wavefront. Thus, a wavefront is defined as a surface of constant phase. Huygens's Principle states that every point on a wavefront is a source of wavelets, which spread forward at the same speed. The new position of the wavefront at any instant is the envelope of the secondary wavelets at that instant. Law of refraction at rarer medium Consider a plane wavefront AB incident on a plane surface, separating two media 1 and 2. Let v 1 and v 2 be the velocities of light in the two media with v 2 > v 1 . The wavefront first strikes at point A and then at the successive points towards C. According to Hugyen's principle, from each point on AC, the secondary wavelets start growing in the second medium with speed v 2 . Let BC = v 1 t and AD = v 2 t where t = time taken to travel from B to C and from A to D. The tangent plane CD drawn from point C over the hemisphere of radius v 2 t will be our new refracted wavefront. From {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, we have sin i = {tex}\frac{B C}{A C}{/tex} and from {tex}\Delta{/tex} ADC we have sin r = {tex}\frac{AD}{AC}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{B C}{A D}=\frac{v_{1} t}{v_{2} t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\mu{/tex} = constant
solution: Wavefront: A locus of the points which oscillates in the same phase is called a wavefront. Thus, a wavefront is defined as a surface of constant phase. Huygens's Principle states that every point on a wavefront is a source of wavelets, which spread forward at the same speed. The new position of the wavefront at any instant is the envelope of the secondary wavelets at that instant. Law of refraction at rarer medium Consider a plane wavefront AB incident on a plane surface, separating two media 1 and 2. Let v 1 and v 2 be the velocities of light in the two media with v 2 > v 1 . The wavefront first strikes at point A and then at the successive points towards C. According to Hugyen's principle, from each point on AC, the secondary wavelets start growing in the second medium with speed v 2 . Let BC = v 1 t and AD = v 2 t where t = time taken to travel from B to C and from A to D. The tangent plane CD drawn from point C over the hemisphere of radius v 2 t will be our new refracted wavefront. From {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABC, we have sin i = {tex}\frac{B C}{A C}{/tex} and from {tex}\Delta{/tex} ADC we have sin r = {tex}\frac{AD}{AC}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{B C}{A D}=\frac{v_{1} t}{v_{2} t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\mu{/tex} = constant
solution_html:
Wavefront: A locus of the points which oscillates in the same phase is called a wavefront. Thus, a wavefront is defined as a surface of constant phase. Huygens's Principle states that every point on a wavefront is a source of wavelets, which spread forward at the same speed. The new position of the wavefront at any instant is the envelope of the secondary wavelets at that instant.
Law of refraction at rarer medium
Consider a plane wavefront AB incident on a plane surface, separating two media 1 and 2. Let v1 and v2 be the velocities of light in the two media with v2 > v1. The wavefront first strikes at point A and then at the successive points towards C. According to Hugyen's principle, from each point on AC, the secondary wavelets start growing in the second medium with speed v2.
 
Let BC = v1t and AD = v2t
where t = time taken to travel from B to C and from A to D.
The tangent plane CD drawn from point C over the hemisphere of radius v2t will be our new refracted wavefront.
From {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC, we have
sin i = {tex}\frac{B C}{A C}{/tex} 
and from {tex}\Delta{/tex}ADC we have
sin r = {tex}\frac{AD}{AC}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{B C}{A D}=\frac{v_{1} t}{v_{2} t}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\mu{/tex} = constant

question_html:
Two coherent light waves of intensity 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 Wm-2 each super-impose and produce the interference pattern on a screen. At a point where the path difference between the waves is {tex}\lambda{/tex}/6, {tex}\lambda{/tex} being wavelength of the wave, find the

  1. phase difference between the waves.
  2. resultant intensity at the point.
  3. resultant intensity in terms of the intensity at the maximum.
question_text: Two coherent light waves of intensity 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 Wm -2 each super-impose and produce the interference pattern on a screen. At a point where the path difference between the waves is {tex}\lambda{/tex} /6, {tex}\lambda{/tex} being wavelength of the wave, find the phase difference between the waves. resultant intensity at the point. resultant intensity in terms of the intensity at the maximum.
explanation: Phase difference {tex}\emptyset=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{6}=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} I 1 = I 2 + I 3 + {tex}2 \sqrt{I_3 I_2} \cos \emptyset{/tex} = I + I + 2I {tex}\times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 3I = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 W m -2 hence, resultant intensity at the point will be 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 W m -2 . I max = 4I I 1 = {tex}\frac{3 I}{4 I} \times 4 I=\frac{3}{4} I_{\max }{/tex}
solution: Phase difference {tex}\emptyset=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{6}=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} I 1 = I 2 + I 3 + {tex}2 \sqrt{I_3 I_2} \cos \emptyset{/tex} = I + I + 2I {tex}\times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 3I = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 W m -2 hence, resultant intensity at the point will be 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 W m -2 . I max = 4I I 1 = {tex}\frac{3 I}{4 I} \times 4 I=\frac{3}{4} I_{\max }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Phase difference {tex}\emptyset=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \frac{\lambda}{6}=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
  2. I1 = I2 + I3 + {tex}2 \sqrt{I_3 I_2} \cos \emptyset{/tex}
    = I + I + 2I {tex}\times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 3I
    = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 W m-2
    hence, resultant intensity at the point will be 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 W m-2.
  3. Imax = 4I
    I1 = {tex}\frac{3 I}{4 I} \times 4 I=\frac{3}{4} I_{\max }{/tex}

question_html: In a single slit diffraction experiment, the width of the slit is increased. How will the (i) size and (ii) intensity of central bright band be affected? Justify your answer.
question_text: In a single slit diffraction experiment, the width of the slit is increased. How will the (i) size and (ii) intensity of central bright band be affected? Justify your answer.
explanation: The size of the central bright band will decrease. Justification: Angular width = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} , i.e. angular width is inversely proportional to the width of the slit The intensity of central bright band will increase Justification: The amplitude/intensity of light passing through slit has increased.
solution: The size of the central bright band will decrease. Justification: Angular width = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} , i.e. angular width is inversely proportional to the width of the slit The intensity of central bright band will increase Justification: The amplitude/intensity of light passing through slit has increased.
solution_html:
1. The size of the central bright band will decrease.
  2. Justification: Angular width = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}, i.e. angular width is inversely proportional to the width of the slit
  3. The intensity of central bright band will increase
    Justification: The amplitude/intensity of light passing through slit has increased.

explanation: Intensity distribution graph Angular width {tex}\theta_0=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} Angular width decreases Intensity {tex}\propto{/tex} area {tex}\therefore{/tex} intensity increases intensity increases (ii) When separation between slit and screen is decreased no effect on angular width while intensity increases.
solution: Intensity distribution graph Angular width {tex}\theta_0=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} Angular width decreases Intensity {tex}\propto{/tex} area {tex}\therefore{/tex} intensity increases intensity increases (ii) When separation between slit and screen is decreased no effect on angular width while intensity increases.
solution_html:
1. Intensity distribution graph
  2. Angular width
    {tex}\theta_0=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}
    Angular width decreases
    Intensity {tex}\propto{/tex}area {tex}\therefore{/tex} intensity increases
    intensity increases
    (ii) When separation between slit and screen is decreased no effect on angular width while intensity increases.

question_html:
1. If one of two identical slits producing interference in Young’s experiment is covered with glass, so that the light intensity passing through it is reduced to 50%, find the ratio of the maximum and minimum intensity of the fringe in the interference pattern.
  2. What kind of fringes do you expect to observe if white light is used instead of monochromatic light?
question_text: If one of two identical slits producing interference in Young’s experiment is covered with glass, so that the light intensity passing through it is reduced to 50%, find the ratio of the maximum and minimum intensity of the fringe in the interference pattern. What kind of fringes do you expect to observe if white light is used instead of monochromatic light?
explanation: As we know that intensity is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude( I {tex}\propto{/tex} a 2 ) and {tex}\frac{I_\max}{I_\min}=\frac{(\sqrt{I_1}+\sqrt{I_2})^2}{(\sqrt{I_1}-\sqrt{I_2})^2}{/tex} Now according to question, the intensity is reduced to 50%, then {tex}\frac{I_\max}{I_\min}=\frac{(\sqrt I+\sqrt{\frac I2})^2}{(\sqrt I-\sqrt{\frac I2})^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(\sqrt{2}+1)^{2}}{(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{2.414}{0.414}\right)^{2}{/tex} = (5.83) 2 = 33.98 = 34 If the white light is used instead of monochromatic light then we see a sequence in which central fringe will be white and remaining will be coloured in VIBGYOR sequence.
solution: As we know that intensity is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude( I {tex}\propto{/tex} a 2 ) and {tex}\frac{I_\max}{I_\min}=\frac{(\sqrt{I_1}+\sqrt{I_2})^2}{(\sqrt{I_1}-\sqrt{I_2})^2}{/tex} Now according to question, the intensity is reduced to 50%, then {tex}\frac{I_\max}{I_\min}=\frac{(\sqrt I+\sqrt{\frac I2})^2}{(\sqrt I-\sqrt{\frac I2})^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(\sqrt{2}+1)^{2}}{(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{2.414}{0.414}\right)^{2}{/tex} = (5.83) 2 = 33.98 = 34 If the white light is used instead of monochromatic light then we see a sequence in which central fringe will be white and remaining will be coloured in VIBGYOR sequence.
solution_html:
1. As we know that intensity is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude( I {tex}\propto{/tex} a2 ) and
    {tex}\frac{I_\max}{I_\min}=\frac{(\sqrt{I_1}+\sqrt{I_2})^2}{(\sqrt{I_1}-\sqrt{I_2})^2}{/tex}
    Now according to question, the intensity is reduced to 50%, then
    {tex}\frac{I_\max}{I_\min}=\frac{(\sqrt I+\sqrt{\frac I2})^2}{(\sqrt I-\sqrt{\frac I2})^2}{/tex}  = {tex}\frac{(\sqrt{2}+1)^{2}}{(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{2.414}{0.414}\right)^{2}{/tex} = (5.83)2  = 33.98 = 34
  2. If the white light is used instead of monochromatic light then we see a sequence in which central fringe will be white and remaining will be coloured in VIBGYOR sequence.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define the term wavefront. Using Huygen's wave theory, verify the law of reflection.
question_text: Define the term wavefront. Using Huygen's wave theory, verify the law of reflection.
explanation: The wavefront is defined as a surface of constant phase. Consider a plane wave AB incident at an angle ‘i’ on a reflecting surface MN. Let t = time taken by the wavefront to advance from B to C. {tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = vt Let CE represent the tangent plane drawn from point C to the sphere of radius 'vt' having A as its center. Then AE = BC = vt Now consider triangles EAC and BAC, we have {tex}\begin{array}{l}\angle AEC=\angle CBA\\BC=AE=vt\\AC=AC\\\text{ By RHS, }\triangle EAC\cong\triangle BCA\end{array}{/tex} Hence {tex}\angle{/tex} i = {tex}\angle{/tex} r {tex}\therefore{/tex} Angle of incidence = angle of reflection.
solution: The wavefront is defined as a surface of constant phase. Consider a plane wave AB incident at an angle ‘i’ on a reflecting surface MN. Let t = time taken by the wavefront to advance from B to C. {tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = vt Let CE represent the tangent plane drawn from point C to the sphere of radius 'vt' having A as its center. Then AE = BC = vt Now consider triangles EAC and BAC, we have {tex}\begin{array}{l}\angle AEC=\angle CBA\\BC=AE=vt\\AC=AC\\\text{ By RHS, }\triangle EAC\cong\triangle BCA\end{array}{/tex} Hence {tex}\angle{/tex} i = {tex}\angle{/tex} r {tex}\therefore{/tex} Angle of incidence = angle of reflection.
solution_html:
The wavefront is defined as a surface of constant phase.
Consider a plane wave AB incident at an angle ‘i’ on a reflecting surface MN.


Let t = time taken by the wavefront to advance from B to C.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BC = vt
Let CE represent the tangent plane drawn from point C to the sphere of radius 'vt' having A as its center.
Then AE = BC = vt
Now consider triangles EAC and BAC, we have
{tex}\begin{array}{l}\angle AEC=\angle CBA\\BC=AE=vt\\AC=AC\\\text{ By RHS, }\triangle EAC\cong\triangle BCA\end{array}{/tex}
Hence {tex}\angle{/tex}i = {tex}\angle{/tex}r
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Angle of incidence = angle of reflection.

question_html:
1. There are two sets of apparatus of Young's double-slit experiment. Inset A, the phase difference between the two waves emanating from the slits does not change with time, whereas in set B, the phase difference between the two waves from the slits changes rapidly with time. What difference will be observed in the pattern obtained on the screen in the two setups?
  2. Deduce the expression for the resultant intensity in both the above-mentioned setups (A and B), assuming that the waves emanating from the two slits have the same amplitude a and same wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
question_text: There are two sets of apparatus of Young's double-slit experiment. Inset A, the phase difference between the two waves emanating from the slits does not change with time, whereas in set B, the phase difference between the two waves from the slits changes rapidly with time. What difference will be observed in the pattern obtained on the screen in the two setups? Deduce the expression for the resultant intensity in both the above-mentioned setups (A and B), assuming that the waves emanating from the two slits have the same amplitude a and same wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} .
explanation: There are two sets of apparatus of Young's double-slit experiment. In Set A: Stable interference pattern, the positions of maxima and minima do not change with time. In Set B: Positions of maxima and minima will change rapidly with time and an average uniform intensity distribution will be observed on the screen. Expression for the intensity of stable interference pattern in set-A If the displacement produced by slit S 1 is given by y 1 = acos {tex}\omega{/tex} t then, the displacement produced by S 2 would be y 2 = acos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) and the resultant displacement will be given by y = y 1 + y 2 = a[cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} )] = 2acos ( {tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex} )cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex} ) The amplitude of the resultant displacement is 2acos ( {tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex} ) and therefore the intensity at that point will be I = 4I 0 cos 2 ( {tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex} ) {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4l 0 In set B, the intensity will be given by the average intensity is given by :- I = 4I 0 cos 2 ( {tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex} ) I = 2I 0
solution: There are two sets of apparatus of Young's double-slit experiment. In Set A: Stable interference pattern, the positions of maxima and minima do not change with time. In Set B: Positions of maxima and minima will change rapidly with time and an average uniform intensity distribution will be observed on the screen. Expression for the intensity of stable interference pattern in set-A If the displacement produced by slit S 1 is given by y 1 = acos {tex}\omega{/tex} t then, the displacement produced by S 2 would be y 2 = acos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) and the resultant displacement will be given by y = y 1 + y 2 = a[cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} )] = 2acos ( {tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex} )cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex} ) The amplitude of the resultant displacement is 2acos ( {tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex} ) and therefore the intensity at that point will be I = 4I 0 cos 2 ( {tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex} ) {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4l 0 In set B, the intensity will be given by the average intensity is given by :- I = 4I 0 cos 2 ( {tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex} ) I = 2I 0
solution_html:
1. There are two sets of apparatus of Young's double-slit experiment. In Set A: Stable interference pattern, the positions of maxima and minima do not change with time.
    In Set B: Positions of maxima and minima will change rapidly with time and an average uniform intensity distribution will be observed on the screen.
  2. Expression for the intensity of stable interference pattern in set-A
    If the displacement produced by slit S1 is given by 
    y1 = acos {tex}\omega{/tex}t
    then, the displacement produced by S2 would be
    y2 = acos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex})
    and the resultant displacement will be given by
    y = y1 + y2
    = a[cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t + cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex})]
    = 2acos ({tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex})cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex})
    The amplitude of the resultant displacement is 2acos ({tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex}) and therefore the intensity at that point will be
    I = 4I0cos2({tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex})
    {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4l0
    In set B, the intensity will be given by the average intensity is given by :-
    I = 4I0cos2({tex}\frac \phi 2{/tex})
    I = 2I0

question_html:
1. Describe any two characteristic features which distinguish between interference and diffraction phenomena. Derive the expression for the intensity at a point of the interference pattern in Young’s double slit experiment.
  2. In the diffraction due to a single slit experiment, the aperture of the slit is 3 mm. If monochromatic light of wavelength 620 nm is incident normally on the slit, calculate the separation between the first order minima and the 3rd order maxima on one side of the screen. The distance between the slit and the screen is 1.5 m.
question_text: Describe any two characteristic features which distinguish between interference and diffraction phenomena. Derive the expression for the intensity at a point of the interference pattern in Young’s double slit experiment. In the diffraction due to a single slit experiment, the aperture of the slit is 3 mm. If monochromatic light of wavelength 620 nm is incident normally on the slit, calculate the separation between the first order minima and the 3 rd order maxima on one side of the screen. The distance between the slit and the screen is 1.5 m.
explanation: Two characteristic features which distinguish between interference and diffraction phenomena are Interference pattern has number of equally spaced bright and dark bands while diffraction pattern has central bright maximum which is twice as wide as the other maxima. Interference is obtained by the superposing two waves originating from two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is the superposition of the continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit Now displacement produced by source 's 1 ' Y 1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t and displacement produced by the other source ‘s 2 ’ Y 2 = a cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\theta{/tex} ) Resultant displacement Y = Y 1 + Y 2 = a [cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = 2a cos ( {tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} ) cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} ) Amplitude of resultant wave A = 2acos ( {tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} ) Intensity, I {tex}\propto{/tex} A 2 l = KA 2 = K 4a 2 cos 2 ( {tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} ) Distance of First order minima from centre of the central maxima = X D1 = {tex}\frac{\lambda D}{a}{/tex} Distance of third order maxima from centre of the central maxima = X B3 = {tex}\frac{7 D \lambda}{2 a}{/tex} Distance between first order minima and third order maxima = X B3 - X D1 = {tex}\frac{7 D \lambda}{2 a}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{\lambda D}{a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 D \lambda}{2 a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \times 620 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 7.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m
solution: Two characteristic features which distinguish between interference and diffraction phenomena are Interference pattern has number of equally spaced bright and dark bands while diffraction pattern has central bright maximum which is twice as wide as the other maxima. Interference is obtained by the superposing two waves originating from two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is the superposition of the continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit Now displacement produced by source 's 1 ' Y 1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t and displacement produced by the other source ‘s 2 ’ Y 2 = a cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\theta{/tex} ) Resultant displacement Y = Y 1 + Y 2 = a [cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = 2a cos ( {tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} ) cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} ) Amplitude of resultant wave A = 2acos ( {tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} ) Intensity, I {tex}\propto{/tex} A 2 l = KA 2 = K 4a 2 cos 2 ( {tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} ) Distance of First order minima from centre of the central maxima = X D1 = {tex}\frac{\lambda D}{a}{/tex} Distance of third order maxima from centre of the central maxima = X B3 = {tex}\frac{7 D \lambda}{2 a}{/tex} Distance between first order minima and third order maxima = X B3 - X D1 = {tex}\frac{7 D \lambda}{2 a}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{\lambda D}{a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 D \lambda}{2 a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5 \times 620 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 7.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m
solution_html:
1. Two characteristic features which distinguish between interference and diffraction phenomena are
    1. Interference pattern has number of equally spaced bright and dark bands while diffraction pattern has central bright maximum which is twice as wide as the other maxima.
    2. Interference is obtained by the superposing two waves originating from two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is the superposition of the continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit
      Now displacement produced by source 's1'
      Y1 = a cos{tex}\omega{/tex}t
      and displacement produced by the other source ‘s2’
      Y2 = a cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\theta{/tex})
      Resultant displacement Y = Y1 + Y2
      = a [cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t + cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t +{tex}\theta{/tex} )
      = 2a cos ({tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex}) cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t +{tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} )
      Amplitude of resultant wave A = 2acos ({tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex})
      Intensity, I{tex}\propto{/tex} A2
      l = KA2 = K 4a2cos2 ({tex}\frac{\theta}{2}{/tex})
  2. Distance of First order minima from centre of the central maxima =  XD1 = {tex}\frac{\lambda D}{a}{/tex}
    Distance of third order maxima from centre of the central maxima = XB3 = {tex}\frac{7 D \lambda}{2 a}{/tex}
    Distance between first order minima and third order maxima = XB3 - XD1 
    = {tex}\frac{7 D \lambda}{2 a}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{\lambda D}{a}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{5 D \lambda}{2 a}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{5 \times 620 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.5}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    = 7.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Define a wavefront. Using Huygens’ geometrical construction, explain with the help of a diagram how the plane wavefront travels from the instant t1 to t2 in air.
  2. A plane wavefront is incident on a convex lens. Explain, with the help of the diagram, the shape of the refracted wavefront formed.
question_text: Define a wavefront. Using Huygens’ geometrical construction, explain with the help of a diagram how the plane wavefront travels from the instant t 1 to t 2 in air. A plane wavefront is incident on a convex lens. Explain, with the help of the diagram, the shape of the refracted wavefront formed.
explanation: Wave front: It is the locus of the medium or points of a medium which are in the same phase of disturbance Geometrical Construction: The wave is propagating to the right. F 1 F 2 is the plane wavefront at t = 0 & G 1 G 2 is the wave front at a later time t, the lines A 1 , A 2 , B 1 B 2 …etc. are normal to both F 1 F 2 and G 1 G 2 represent rays. A 1 A 2 = B 1 B 2 = c {tex}\tau{/tex} Spherical Wave front The central part of the incident plane wave traverses the thickest portion of the lens and is delayed the most. The emerging wavefront has a depression at the centre and hence the wavefront is spherical and converges to the point F known as the focus.
solution: Wave front: It is the locus of the medium or points of a medium which are in the same phase of disturbance Geometrical Construction: The wave is propagating to the right. F 1 F 2 is the plane wavefront at t = 0 & G 1 G 2 is the wave front at a later time t, the lines A 1 , A 2 , B 1 B 2 …etc. are normal to both F 1 F 2 and G 1 G 2 represent rays. A 1 A 2 = B 1 B 2 = c {tex}\tau{/tex} Spherical Wave front The central part of the incident plane wave traverses the thickest portion of the lens and is delayed the most. The emerging wavefront has a depression at the centre and hence the wavefront is spherical and converges to the point F known as the focus.
solution_html:
1. Wave front: It is the locus of the medium or points of a medium which are in the same phase of disturbance

    Geometrical Construction:
    The wave is propagating to the right. F1F2 is the plane wavefront at t = 0 & G1G2 is the wave front at a later time t, the lines A1, A2, B1B2…etc. are normal to both F1F2 and G1G2 represent rays. A1A2 = B1B2 = c{tex}\tau{/tex}
  2. Spherical Wave front

    The central part of the incident plane wave traverses the thickest portion of the lens and is delayed the most. The emerging wavefront has a depression at the centre and hence the wavefront is spherical and converges to the point F known as the focus.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. What do you understand by coherent sources?
  2. Derive the expression for the intensity distribution in the interference pattern on the screen when two harmonic waves having same amplitude (a) and phase difference ({tex}\phi{/tex}) are superposed on each other. Hence obtain the conditions for constructive and destructive interference.
question_text: What do you understand by coherent sources? Derive the expression for the intensity distribution in the interference pattern on the screen when two harmonic waves having same amplitude (a) and phase difference ( {tex}\phi{/tex} ) are superposed on each other. Hence obtain the conditions for constructive and destructive interference.
explanation: Source which emit waves continuously of the same type same frequency and have a constant or no phase difference between the waves emitted by them are called coherent sources. Displacement product by two sources at a point on the screen is given by y 1 = a cos wt y 2 = a cos (wt + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) By super position (one word) y = y 1 + y 2 {tex}=a[\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)]{/tex} {tex}=\left\{2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right\} \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Intensity at the point {tex}I \alpha\left[2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right]^2{/tex} {tex}I \alpha a^2{/tex} {tex}\frac{I}{I_P}=4 \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=4 I_0 \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} For Constructive Interference: I = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \phi=0, \pm 2 \pi, \pm 4 \pi, \ldots{/tex} . For destructive Interference: I = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \phi= \pm \pi, \pm 3 \pi, \pm 5 \pi \ldots .{/tex} .
solution: Source which emit waves continuously of the same type same frequency and have a constant or no phase difference between the waves emitted by them are called coherent sources. Displacement product by two sources at a point on the screen is given by y 1 = a cos wt y 2 = a cos (wt + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) By super position (one word) y = y 1 + y 2 {tex}=a[\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)]{/tex} {tex}=\left\{2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right\} \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Intensity at the point {tex}I \alpha\left[2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right]^2{/tex} {tex}I \alpha a^2{/tex} {tex}\frac{I}{I_P}=4 \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I=4 I_0 \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} For Constructive Interference: I = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \phi=0, \pm 2 \pi, \pm 4 \pi, \ldots{/tex} . For destructive Interference: I = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \phi= \pm \pi, \pm 3 \pi, \pm 5 \pi \ldots .{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. Source which emit waves continuously of the same type same frequency and have a constant or no phase difference between the waves emitted by them are called coherent sources.
  2. Displacement product by two sources at a point on the screen is given by y1 = a cos wt
    y2 = a cos (wt + {tex}\phi{/tex})
    By super position (one word)
    y = y1 + y2
    {tex}=a[\cos \omega t+\cos (\omega t+\phi)]{/tex}
    {tex}=\left\{2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right\} \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Intensity at the point
    {tex}I \alpha\left[2 a \cos \frac{\phi}{2}\right]^2{/tex}
    {tex}I \alpha a^2{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{I}{I_P}=4 \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow I=4 I_0 \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
    For Constructive Interference:
    I = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}=1{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \phi=0, \pm 2 \pi, \pm 4 \pi, \ldots{/tex}.
    For destructive Interference:
    I = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \phi= \pm \pi, \pm 3 \pi, \pm 5 \pi \ldots .{/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Answer the following questions:

  1. In a single slit diffraction experiment, the width of the slit is made double the original width. How does this affect the size and intensity of the central diffraction band?
  2. How is the width of the central maximum changed when red light is replaced by blue?
  3. In what way is diffraction from each slit related to the interference pattern in a double slit experiment?
question_text: Answer the following questions: In a single slit diffraction experiment, the width of the slit is made double the original width. How does this affect the size and intensity of the central diffraction band? How is the width of the central maximum changed when red light is replaced by blue? In what way is diffraction from each slit related to the interference pattern in a double slit experiment?
explanation: When width is doubled, then {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} reduces to half hence size of the central maximum will get halved and intensity of central maximum will become 4 times. For red light width will be maximum because {tex}\beta=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} Wave length of blue is small as compared to red so width will be less when red is replaced by blue. Interference pattern is observed by superposing two waves originating from the two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is a superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit.
solution: When width is doubled, then {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} reduces to half hence size of the central maximum will get halved and intensity of central maximum will become 4 times. For red light width will be maximum because {tex}\beta=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} Wave length of blue is small as compared to red so width will be less when red is replaced by blue. Interference pattern is observed by superposing two waves originating from the two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is a superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit.
solution_html:
1. When width is doubled, then {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} reduces to half hence size of the central maximum will get halved and intensity of central maximum will become 4 times.
  2. For red light width will be maximum because
    {tex}\beta=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex}
    Wave length of blue is small as compared to red so width will be less when red is replaced by blue.
  3. Interference pattern is observed by superposing two waves originating from the two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is a superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Explain how a diffraction pattern is formed due to interference of secondary wavelets of light waves from a slit.
  2. Sodium light consists of two wavelengths, {tex}5900 \overset{o} A{/tex} and {tex}5960 \overset{o} A{/tex}. If a slit of width {tex}2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} is illuminated by sodium light, find the separation between the first secondary maxima of the diffraction pattern of the two wavelengths on a screen placed 1.5 m away.
question_text: Explain how a diffraction pattern is formed due to interference of secondary wavelets of light waves from a slit. Sodium light consists of two wavelengths, {tex}5900 \overset{o} A{/tex} and {tex}5960 \overset{o} A{/tex} . If a slit of width {tex}2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} is illuminated by sodium light, find the separation between the first secondary maxima of the diffraction pattern of the two wavelengths on a screen placed 1.5 m away.
explanation: Path difference, NP - LP = NQ {tex}=\mathrm{a} \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\approx a \theta{/tex} At C on the screen, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o . All path differences are zero and hence all wavelets meet in phase and produce a maxima at C. At points P on the screen for which path difference is {tex}\lambda, 2 \lambda, 3 \lambda, \ldots \ldots \mathrm{n} \lambda{/tex} ; the wavelets will cancel each other in pairs and produce minima. {tex}\therefore a \theta=n \lambda{/tex} ... condition for minima (n = 1, 2, ...) At points P on the screen for which path difference is {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}, 3 \frac{\lambda}{2} ... (2 n+1) \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} The wavelets produce a maxima due to one uncancelled part of the wavefront. {tex}\therefore a \theta=(2 n+1) \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} ... condition for maxima (n = 1, 2, ...) separation between 1 st secondary maxima of the two wavelengths {tex}=\frac{3 D}{2 d}\left(\lambda_2-\lambda_1\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \times 1.5}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}} \times 60 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} {tex}=67.5 \mu \mathrm{m}{/tex}
solution: Path difference, NP - LP = NQ {tex}=\mathrm{a} \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\approx a \theta{/tex} At C on the screen, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o . All path differences are zero and hence all wavelets meet in phase and produce a maxima at C. At points P on the screen for which path difference is {tex}\lambda, 2 \lambda, 3 \lambda, \ldots \ldots \mathrm{n} \lambda{/tex} ; the wavelets will cancel each other in pairs and produce minima. {tex}\therefore a \theta=n \lambda{/tex} ... condition for minima (n = 1, 2, ...) At points P on the screen for which path difference is {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}, 3 \frac{\lambda}{2} ... (2 n+1) \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} The wavelets produce a maxima due to one uncancelled part of the wavefront. {tex}\therefore a \theta=(2 n+1) \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} ... condition for maxima (n = 1, 2, ...) separation between 1 st secondary maxima of the two wavelengths {tex}=\frac{3 D}{2 d}\left(\lambda_2-\lambda_1\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \times 1.5}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}} \times 60 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}{/tex} {tex}=67.5 \mu \mathrm{m}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Path difference, NP - LP = NQ
    {tex}=\mathrm{a} \sin \theta{/tex}
    {tex}\approx a \theta{/tex}
    At C on the screen, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0o. All path differences are zero and hence all wavelets meet in phase and produce a maxima at C.
    At points P on the screen for which path difference is {tex}\lambda, 2 \lambda, 3 \lambda, \ldots \ldots \mathrm{n} \lambda{/tex}; the wavelets will cancel each other in pairs and produce minima.
    {tex}\therefore a \theta=n \lambda{/tex} ... condition for minima
    (n = 1, 2, ...)
    At points P on the screen for which path difference is {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}, 3 \frac{\lambda}{2} ... (2 n+1) \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}
    The wavelets produce a maxima due to one uncancelled part of the wavefront.
    {tex}\therefore a \theta=(2 n+1) \frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} ... condition for maxima
    (n = 1, 2, ...)
  2. separation between 1st secondary maxima of the two wavelengths
    {tex}=\frac{3 D}{2 d}\left(\lambda_2-\lambda_1\right){/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{3 \times 1.5}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}} \times 60 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
    {tex}=67.5 \mu \mathrm{m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Can the interference pattern be produced by two independent monochromatic sources of light? Explain.
  2. The intensity at the central maximum (O) in a Young’s double slit experimental set-up shown in the figure is IO. If the distance OP equals one-third of the fringe width of the pattern, show that the intensity at point P, would equal {tex}\frac{I_O}{4}{/tex}.
  3. In Young’s double slit experiment, the slits are separated by 0.5 mm and screen is placed 1.0 m away from the slit. It is found that the 5th bright fringe is at a distance of 4.13 mm from the 2nd dark fringe. Find the wavelength of light used.
question_text: Can the interference pattern be produced by two independent monochromatic sources of light? Explain. The intensity at the central maximum (O) in a Young’s double slit experimental set-up shown in the figure is I O . If the distance OP equals one-third of the fringe width of the pattern, show that the intensity at point P, would equal {tex}\frac{I_O}{4}{/tex} . In Young’s double slit experiment, the slits are separated by 0.5 mm and screen is placed 1.0 m away from the slit. It is found that the 5 th bright fringe is at a distance of 4.13 mm from the 2 nd dark fringe. Find the wavelength of light used.
explanation: No. Sustained interference pattern cannot be obtained. Light waves emitted from a source undergoes abrupt phase changes in times of the order of 10 -10 s. So light from two independent sources will not have fixed phase relationship and will be incoherent. x = {tex}\frac{\beta}{3}{/tex} , path difference = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex} phase difference = {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} I = I o cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} I = I o cos 2 ( {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3 \times 2}{/tex} ) = I o cos 2 ( {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} ) I = I o ( {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{I_o}{4}{/tex} Distance of 5 th bright fringe from 2 nd dark fringe x = {tex}\frac{5 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{3 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{2 \mathrm{d}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{2} \frac{\lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x d}{7 \mathrm{D}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 4.13 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{7 \times 1}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0.59 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m = 5900 {tex}\stackrel{o}{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}
solution: No. Sustained interference pattern cannot be obtained. Light waves emitted from a source undergoes abrupt phase changes in times of the order of 10 -10 s. So light from two independent sources will not have fixed phase relationship and will be incoherent. x = {tex}\frac{\beta}{3}{/tex} , path difference = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex} phase difference = {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} I = I o cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} I = I o cos 2 ( {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3 \times 2}{/tex} ) = I o cos 2 ( {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} ) I = I o ( {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{I_o}{4}{/tex} Distance of 5 th bright fringe from 2 nd dark fringe x = {tex}\frac{5 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{3 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{2 \mathrm{d}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{2} \frac{\lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x d}{7 \mathrm{D}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 4.13 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{7 \times 1}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0.59 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m = 5900 {tex}\stackrel{o}{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. No. Sustained interference pattern cannot be obtained. Light waves emitted from a source undergoes abrupt phase changes in times of the order of 10-10s. So light from two independent sources will not have fixed phase relationship and will be incoherent.
  2. x = {tex}\frac{\beta}{3}{/tex}, path difference = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}{/tex}
    phase difference = {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}
    I = Io cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} 
    I = Io cos2 ({tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3 \times 2}{/tex}) = Io cos2 ({tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex})
    I = Io ({tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{I_o}{4}{/tex}
  3. Distance of 5th bright fringe from 2nd dark fringe
    x = {tex}\frac{5 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{3 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{2 \mathrm{d}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{2} \frac{\lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x d}{7 \mathrm{D}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 4.13 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{7 \times 1}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 0.59 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 m = 5900 {tex}\stackrel{o}{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Derive the relation a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} for the first minimum of the diffraction pattern produced due to a single slit of width a using light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
  2. State with reason, how the linear width of central maximum will be affected if (i) monochromatic yellow light is replaced with red light, and (ii) distance between the slit and the screen is increased.
  3. Using the monochromatic light of same wavelength in the experimental set-up of the diffraction pattern as well as in the interference pattern where the slit separation is 1 mm, 10 interference fringes are found to be within the central maximum of the diffraction pattern. Determine the width of the single slit, if the screen is kept at the same distance from the slit in the two cases.
question_text: Derive the relation a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} for the first minimum of the diffraction pattern produced due to a single slit of width a using light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} . State with reason, how the linear width of central maximum will be affected if (i) monochromatic yellow light is replaced with red light, and (ii) distance between the slit and the screen is increased. Using the monochromatic light of same wavelength in the experimental set-up of the diffraction pattern as well as in the interference pattern where the slit separation is 1 mm, 10 interference fringes are found to be within the central maximum of the diffraction pattern. Determine the width of the single slit, if the screen is kept at the same distance from the slit in the two cases.
explanation: From diagram path difference between the waves from L and N = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} When first minimum is obtained at P then path difference {tex}=\lambda{/tex} [imagine the slit be divided into two halves, for each wavelets from first half of the slit has a corresponding wavelet from second half of the slit differing by a path of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} and cancel each other] Condition for first minimum {tex}\therefore \lambda=\mathrm{a} \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\beta_{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{2 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} As we know that wave length {tex}(\lambda){/tex} of red light is more than yellow light. {tex}\therefore \lambda_{\text {red }}>\lambda_{\text {yellow }}{/tex} So, {tex}\therefore \beta_{\text {red }}>\beta_{\text {yellow }}{/tex} Hence, the linear width of the central maximum will increase if monochromatic yellow light is replaced with the red light. If the distance between the slit and screen (d) is increased then also the linear width of the central maximum will increase. {tex}10 \frac{\lambda}{\mathrm{d}}=2 \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{5}{/tex} = 0.2 mm
solution: From diagram path difference between the waves from L and N = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} When first minimum is obtained at P then path difference {tex}=\lambda{/tex} [imagine the slit be divided into two halves, for each wavelets from first half of the slit has a corresponding wavelet from second half of the slit differing by a path of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} and cancel each other] Condition for first minimum {tex}\therefore \lambda=\mathrm{a} \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\beta_{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{2 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex} As we know that wave length {tex}(\lambda){/tex} of red light is more than yellow light. {tex}\therefore \lambda_{\text {red }}>\lambda_{\text {yellow }}{/tex} So, {tex}\therefore \beta_{\text {red }}>\beta_{\text {yellow }}{/tex} Hence, the linear width of the central maximum will increase if monochromatic yellow light is replaced with the red light. If the distance between the slit and screen (d) is increased then also the linear width of the central maximum will increase. {tex}10 \frac{\lambda}{\mathrm{d}}=2 \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{5}{/tex} = 0.2 mm
solution_html:
1. From diagram path difference between the waves from L and N = a sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
    When first minimum is obtained at P then path difference {tex}=\lambda{/tex}
    [imagine the slit be divided into two halves, for each wavelets from first half of the slit has a corresponding wavelet from second half of the slit differing by a path of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} and cancel each other]
    Condition for first minimum
    {tex}\therefore \lambda=\mathrm{a} \sin \theta{/tex}
  2. {tex}\beta_{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{2 \lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}{/tex}
    1. As we know that wave length {tex}(\lambda){/tex} of red light is more than yellow light.
      {tex}\therefore \lambda_{\text {red }}>\lambda_{\text {yellow }}{/tex}
      So, {tex}\therefore \beta_{\text {red }}>\beta_{\text {yellow }}{/tex}
      Hence, the linear width of the central maximum will increase if monochromatic yellow light is replaced with the red light.
    2. If the distance between the slit and screen (d) is increased then also the linear width of the central maximum will increase.
  3. {tex}10 \frac{\lambda}{\mathrm{d}}=2 \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{5}{/tex} = 0.2 mm

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Sketch the refracted wavefront for the incident plane wavefront of light from a distant object passing through a convex lens.
  2. Using Huygens’ principle, verify the laws of refraction when light from a denser medium is incident on a rarer medium.
  3. For yellow light of wavelength 590 nm incident on a glass slab, the refractive index of glass is 1.5. Estimate the speed and wavelength of yellow light inside the glass slab.
question_text: Sketch the refracted wavefront for the incident plane wavefront of light from a distant object passing through a convex lens. Using Huygens’ principle, verify the laws of refraction when light from a denser medium is incident on a rarer medium. For yellow light of wavelength 590 nm incident on a glass slab, the refractive index of glass is 1.5. Estimate the speed and wavelength of yellow light inside the glass slab.
explanation: In right triangle ABC {tex}\sin i=\frac{B C}{A C}{/tex} In {tex}\triangle AEC{/tex} {tex}\sin r=\frac{A E}{A C}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{B C}{A E}=\frac{v_1 \tau}{v_2 \tau}=\frac{v_1}{v_2}=\mu{/tex} Speed of yellow light inside the glass slab {tex} v=\frac{c}{\mu} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} {/tex} {tex} =2 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} {/tex} Wavelength of yellow light inside the glass slab {tex} \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{\mu} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{590}{1.5} \mathrm{~nm} {/tex} = 393.33 nm
solution: In right triangle ABC {tex}\sin i=\frac{B C}{A C}{/tex} In {tex}\triangle AEC{/tex} {tex}\sin r=\frac{A E}{A C}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{B C}{A E}=\frac{v_1 \tau}{v_2 \tau}=\frac{v_1}{v_2}=\mu{/tex} Speed of yellow light inside the glass slab {tex} v=\frac{c}{\mu} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} {/tex} {tex} =2 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} {/tex} Wavelength of yellow light inside the glass slab {tex} \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{\mu} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{590}{1.5} \mathrm{~nm} {/tex} = 393.33 nm
solution_html:
2. In right triangle ABC
    {tex}\sin i=\frac{B C}{A C}{/tex}
    In {tex}\triangle AEC{/tex} {tex}\sin r=\frac{A E}{A C}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{B C}{A E}=\frac{v_1 \tau}{v_2 \tau}=\frac{v_1}{v_2}=\mu{/tex}
  3. Speed of yellow light inside the glass slab
    {tex} v=\frac{c}{\mu} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} {/tex}
    {tex} =2 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} {/tex}
    Wavelength of yellow light inside the glass slab
    {tex} \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{\mu} {/tex}
    {tex} =\frac{590}{1.5} \mathrm{~nm} {/tex}
    = 393.33 nm

subject: Physics
question_html: When a tiny circular obstacle is placed in the path of light from a distant source, a bright spot is seen at the centre of the shadow of the obstacle. Explain why?
question_text: When a tiny circular obstacle is placed in the path of light from a distant source, a bright spot is seen at the centre of the shadow of the obstacle. Explain why?
explanation: Waves diffracted from the edge of the circular obstacle interfere constructively at the center of the shadow of the circular obstacle. As a result, a bright spot is seen at the center of the shadow of the obstacle.
solution: Waves diffracted from the edge of the circular obstacle interfere constructively at the center of the shadow of the circular obstacle. As a result, a bright spot is seen at the center of the shadow of the obstacle.
solution_html: Waves diffracted from the edge of the circular obstacle interfere constructively at the center of the shadow of the circular obstacle. As a result, a bright spot is seen at the center of the shadow of the obstacle.

question_text: A plane wavefront is incident at an angle of incidence i on a reflecting surface. Draw a diagram showing incident wavefront, reflected wavefront and verify the laws of reflection on the basis of Huygen's wave theory.
explanation: In above figure, MN in reflecting surface AB in the incident wave front FC in the reflected wave front. i in the angle of incidence. r in the angle of reflection. Let ‘v’ be the speed of the light wave in the medium and ‘t’ be the time taken by the secondary wavelets to move from the point B to C Then the distance. BC = vt In order to construct the reflected wave front, Let us draw a sphere of radium AE vt, CE represents the tangent drawn from the point e to this sphere, This tangent represents the position of the new reflected wave front. From the figure it is observed that AE = BC vt. the triangles EAC and BAC are congruent {tex}\therefore{/tex} i = r. This is the Law of reflection.

question_html:
1. Write two points to distinguish between interference and diffraction fringes.
  2. In Young's double-slit experiment, fringes are obtained on a screen placed at a certain distance away from the slits. If the screen is moved by 5 cm towards the slits, the fringe width changes by 30 {tex}\mu{/tex}m. Given that the slits are 1 mm apart, calculate the wavelength of the light used.
question_text: Write two points to distinguish between interference and diffraction fringes. In Young's double-slit experiment, fringes are obtained on a screen placed at a certain distance away from the slits. If the screen is moved by 5 cm towards the slits, the fringe width changes by 30 {tex}\mu{/tex} m. Given that the slits are 1 mm apart, calculate the wavelength of the light used.
explanation: Any two points of difference Interference Diffraction Fringes are equally spaced. Fringes are not equally spaced. Intensity is same for all maxima. Intensity falls as we go to successive maxima away from the centre. Superposition of two waves originating from two narrow slits. Superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit. Let D be the distance of the screen from the plane of the slits. We have d= 1mm =10 -3 m. Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} In the first case {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d} \text { or } \beta d=\lambda D{/tex} ...(i) In the second case {tex}\left(\beta-30 \times 10^{-6}\right)=\frac{\lambda(D-0.05)}{d}{/tex} or {tex}\left(\beta-30 \times 10^{-6}\right) d=\lambda(D-0.05){/tex} ...(ii) Subtracting (ii) from (i) we get 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} d = {tex}\lambda \times 0.05{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda =\frac{30 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-2}} \mathrm{m} {/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda = 6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} = 600 nm
solution: Any two points of difference Interference Diffraction Fringes are equally spaced. Fringes are not equally spaced. Intensity is same for all maxima. Intensity falls as we go to successive maxima away from the centre. Superposition of two waves originating from two narrow slits. Superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit. Let D be the distance of the screen from the plane of the slits. We have d= 1mm =10 -3 m. Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex} In the first case {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d} \text { or } \beta d=\lambda D{/tex} ...(i) In the second case {tex}\left(\beta-30 \times 10^{-6}\right)=\frac{\lambda(D-0.05)}{d}{/tex} or {tex}\left(\beta-30 \times 10^{-6}\right) d=\lambda(D-0.05){/tex} ...(ii) Subtracting (ii) from (i) we get 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 {tex}\times{/tex} d = {tex}\lambda \times 0.05{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda =\frac{30 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-2}} \mathrm{m} {/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda = 6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} = 600 nm
solution_html:
1. Any two points of difference
    Interference Diffraction
    Fringes are equally spaced. Fringes are not equally spaced.
    Intensity is same for all maxima. Intensity falls as we go to successive maxima away from the centre.
    Superposition of two waves originating from two narrow slits. Superposition of a continuous family of waves
    originating from each point on a single slit.
       
  2. Let D be the distance of the screen from the plane of the slits.
    We have d= 1mm =10-3 m.
    Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}{/tex}
    In the first case {tex}\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d} \text { or } \beta d=\lambda D{/tex} ...(i)
    In the second case {tex}\left(\beta-30 \times 10^{-6}\right)=\frac{\lambda(D-0.05)}{d}{/tex}
    or {tex}\left(\beta-30 \times 10^{-6}\right) d=\lambda(D-0.05){/tex} ...(ii)
    Subtracting (ii) from (i) we get
    30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} d = {tex}\lambda \times 0.05{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \lambda =\frac{30 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-2}} \mathrm{m} {/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \lambda = 6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{m}{/tex} = 600 nm

question_html: What kind of interference fringes do you expect to observe if white light is used instead of monochromatic light?
question_text: What kind of interference fringes do you expect to observe if white light is used instead of monochromatic light?
explanation: The central fringe remains white. After a few coloured fringes on either side of central fringe, the fringe pattern is lost due to large overlapping.
solution: The central fringe remains white. After a few coloured fringes on either side of central fringe, the fringe pattern is lost due to large overlapping.
