question_html: In a Young's double-slit experiment, fringes are obtained on a screen placed a certain distance away from the slits. If the screen is moved by 5 cm towards the slits, the fringe width changes by 30 {tex}\mu{/tex}m Given that the slits are 1 mm apart, calculate the wavelength of the light used.
question_text: In a Young's double-slit experiment, fringes are obtained on a screen placed a certain distance away from the slits. If the screen is moved by 5 cm towards the slits, the fringe width changes by 30 {tex}\mu{/tex} m Given that the slits are 1 mm apart, calculate the wavelength of the light used.
explanation: Fringe widths in the two cases will be {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} and {tex}\beta^{\prime}=\frac{D^{\prime} \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \beta-\beta^{\prime}=\frac{\left(D-D^{\prime}\right) \lambda}{d}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda=\frac{\left(\beta-\beta^{\prime}\right) d}{D-D^{\prime}}{/tex} Given D - D' = 5 cm = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m and {tex}\beta-\beta^{\prime}{/tex} = 30 {tex}\mu{/tex} m = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m, d = 1 mm = 10 -3 m {tex}\therefore \lambda=\frac{30 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} m = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m = 600 nm
solution: Fringe widths in the two cases will be {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} and {tex}\beta^{\prime}=\frac{D^{\prime} \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \beta-\beta^{\prime}=\frac{\left(D-D^{\prime}\right) \lambda}{d}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda=\frac{\left(\beta-\beta^{\prime}\right) d}{D-D^{\prime}}{/tex} Given D - D' = 5 cm = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m and {tex}\beta-\beta^{\prime}{/tex} = 30 {tex}\mu{/tex} m = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m, d = 1 mm = 10 -3 m {tex}\therefore \lambda=\frac{30 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} m = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m = 600 nm
solution_html:
Fringe widths in the two cases will be
{tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} and {tex}\beta^{\prime}=\frac{D^{\prime} \lambda}{d}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \beta-\beta^{\prime}=\frac{\left(D-D^{\prime}\right) \lambda}{d}{/tex}
or {tex}\lambda=\frac{\left(\beta-\beta^{\prime}\right) d}{D-D^{\prime}}{/tex}
Given D - D' = 5 cm = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 m
and {tex}\beta-\beta^{\prime}{/tex} = 30 {tex}\mu{/tex}m = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 m, d = 1 mm = 10-3 m
{tex}\therefore \lambda=\frac{30 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}{/tex} m = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m = 600 nm

question_html:
1. Define wavefront. Use Huygens' principle to verify the laws of refraction.
  2. In a single slit diffraction experiment, the width of the slit is made double of the original width. How does this affect the size and intensity of the central diffraction band? Explain.
  3. When a tiny circular obstacle is placed in the path of light from a distant source, a bright spot is seen at the center of obstacle. Explain why?
question_text: Define wavefront. Use Huygens' principle to verify the laws of refraction. In a single slit diffraction experiment, the width of the slit is made double of the original width. How does this affect the size and intensity of the central diffraction band? Explain. When a tiny circular obstacle is placed in the path of light from a distant source, a bright spot is seen at the center of obstacle. Explain why?
explanation: Wavefront is the locus of all points in which light waves are in the same phase. The propagation of wave energy is perpendicular to the wavefront. Or A wavefront is defined as the continuous locus of all the particles which are vibrating in the same phase. The angular width of the central fringe = {tex}\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex} where, a is the width of the slit. Hence if the width of the slit is made double, then fringe width becomes half. The intensity of the central fringe becomes four times. This is because the area of central diffraction band would become ( {tex}\frac 14{/tex} )th. This is because of diffraction. The diffraction of waves from the edges of the circular obstacle interferes constructively at the center of the shadow. It results in the formation of a bright spot at the center of the obstacle.
solution: Wavefront is the locus of all points in which light waves are in the same phase. The propagation of wave energy is perpendicular to the wavefront. Or A wavefront is defined as the continuous locus of all the particles which are vibrating in the same phase. The angular width of the central fringe = {tex}\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex} where, a is the width of the slit. Hence if the width of the slit is made double, then fringe width becomes half. The intensity of the central fringe becomes four times. This is because the area of central diffraction band would become ( {tex}\frac 14{/tex} )th. This is because of diffraction. The diffraction of waves from the edges of the circular obstacle interferes constructively at the center of the shadow. It results in the formation of a bright spot at the center of the obstacle.
solution_html:
1. Wavefront is the locus of all points in which light waves are in the same phase. The propagation of wave energy is perpendicular to the wavefront. Or A wavefront is defined as the continuous locus of all the particles which are vibrating in the same phase.
  2. The angular width of the central fringe = {tex}\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex} where, a is the width of the slit. Hence if the width of the slit is made double, then fringe width becomes half. The intensity of the central fringe becomes four times. This is because the area of central diffraction band would become ({tex}\frac 14{/tex})th.
  3. This is because of diffraction. The diffraction of waves from the edges of the circular obstacle interferes constructively at the center of the shadow. It results in the formation of a bright spot at the center of the obstacle.

question_html: In a double-slit experiment, the distance between the slits is 3 mm and the slits are 2 m away from the screen. Two interference patterns can be seen on the screen one due to light with wavelength 480 nm, and the other due to light with wavelength 600 nm. What is the separation on the screen between the fifth-order bright fringes of the two interference patterns?
question_text: In a double-slit experiment, the distance between the slits is 3 mm and the slits are 2 m away from the screen. Two interference patterns can be seen on the screen one due to light with wavelength 480 nm, and the other due to light with wavelength 600 nm. What is the separation on the screen between the fifth-order bright fringes of the two interference patterns?
explanation: Here d = 3 mm = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 2 m Position of 5th bright fringe of wavelength 480 nm, x 5 = {tex}5 \frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{5 \times 2 \times 480 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} m Position of 5th bright fringe of wavelength 600 nm, {tex}x_{5}^{\prime}=\frac{5 \times 2 \times 600 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} m Separation between the 5th order bright fringes of the two patterns, {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = {tex}x_{5}^{\prime}{/tex} - x 5 = {tex}\frac{10^{-5}}{3}{/tex} (600 - 480)m {tex}=\frac{10^{-5} \times 120}{3}{/tex} m = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m
solution: Here d = 3 mm = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 2 m Position of 5th bright fringe of wavelength 480 nm, x 5 = {tex}5 \frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{5 \times 2 \times 480 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} m Position of 5th bright fringe of wavelength 600 nm, {tex}x_{5}^{\prime}=\frac{5 \times 2 \times 600 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} m Separation between the 5th order bright fringes of the two patterns, {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = {tex}x_{5}^{\prime}{/tex} - x 5 = {tex}\frac{10^{-5}}{3}{/tex} (600 - 480)m {tex}=\frac{10^{-5} \times 120}{3}{/tex} m = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m
solution_html:
Here d = 3 mm = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m, D = 2 m
Position of 5th bright fringe of wavelength 480 nm,
x5 = {tex}5 \frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{5 \times 2 \times 480 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}m
Position of 5th bright fringe of wavelength 600 nm,
{tex}x_{5}^{\prime}=\frac{5 \times 2 \times 600 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}m
Separation between the 5th order bright fringes of the two patterns,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}x = {tex}x_{5}^{\prime}{/tex} - x5 = {tex}\frac{10^{-5}}{3}{/tex} (600 - 480)m
{tex}=\frac{10^{-5} \times 120}{3}{/tex}m = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m

question_html: If one of two identical slits producing interference in Young’s experiment is covered with glass, so that the light intensity passing through it is reduced to 50%, find the ratio of the maximum and minimum intensity of the fringe in the interference pattern.
question_text: If one of two identical slits producing interference in Young’s experiment is covered with glass, so that the light intensity passing through it is reduced to 50%, find the ratio of the maximum and minimum intensity of the fringe in the interference pattern.
explanation: Suppose the second slit is covered with a glass sheet. Then, I 2 = 50% of I 1 = {tex}\frac{I_{1}}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}{/tex} = 2 r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{r+1}{r-1}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1}\right)^{2}{/tex} = (3 + {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} ) 2 = 17 + {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} = 17 + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.414 = 33.968 {tex}\simeq{/tex} 34
solution: Suppose the second slit is covered with a glass sheet. Then, I 2 = 50% of I 1 = {tex}\frac{I_{1}}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}{/tex} = 2 r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{r+1}{r-1}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1}\right)^{2}{/tex} = (3 + {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} ) 2 = 17 + {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} = 17 + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.414 = 33.968 {tex}\simeq{/tex} 34
solution_html:
Suppose the second slit is covered with a glass sheet. Then,
I2 = 50% of I1 = {tex}\frac{I_{1}}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}{/tex} = 2
r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{r+1}{r-1}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1}\right)^{2}{/tex}
= (3 + {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex})2 = 17 + {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
= 17 + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.414 = 33.968 {tex}\simeq{/tex} 34

question_html: A narrow slit is illuminated by a parallel beam of monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} equals to 6000 {tex}\overset \circ{A}{/tex} and the angular width of the central maxima in the resulting diffraction pattern is measured. When the slit is next illuminated by the light of wavelength {tex}\lambda^{\prime},{/tex} the angular width decrease by 30%. Calculate the value of wavelength {tex}\lambda^{\prime}{/tex}.
question_text: A narrow slit is illuminated by a parallel beam of monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} equals to 6000 {tex}\overset \circ{A}{/tex} and the angular width of the central maxima in the resulting diffraction pattern is measured. When the slit is next illuminated by the light of wavelength {tex}\lambda^{\prime},{/tex} the angular width decrease by 30%. Calculate the value of wavelength {tex}\lambda^{\prime}{/tex} .
explanation: The angular width of the central maximum in the first case, {tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of central maximum in the second case, {tex}2 \theta^{\prime}=\frac{2 \lambda^{\prime}}{a}{/tex} But {tex}2 \theta^{\prime}{/tex} = 70% of 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} [2 {tex}\theta{/tex} decreases by 30%] {tex}\frac{2 \lambda^{\prime}}{a}=\frac{70}{100} \times \frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{7}{10} \lambda=\frac{7}{10} \times{/tex} 6000 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex} = 4200 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex}
solution: The angular width of the central maximum in the first case, {tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of central maximum in the second case, {tex}2 \theta^{\prime}=\frac{2 \lambda^{\prime}}{a}{/tex} But {tex}2 \theta^{\prime}{/tex} = 70% of 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} [2 {tex}\theta{/tex} decreases by 30%] {tex}\frac{2 \lambda^{\prime}}{a}=\frac{70}{100} \times \frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{7}{10} \lambda=\frac{7}{10} \times{/tex} 6000 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex} = 4200 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex}
solution_html:
The angular width of the central maximum in the first case,
{tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}
The angular width of central maximum in the second case,
{tex}2 \theta^{\prime}=\frac{2 \lambda^{\prime}}{a}{/tex}
But {tex}2 \theta^{\prime}{/tex} = 70% of 2{tex}\theta{/tex} [2{tex}\theta{/tex} decreases by 30%]
{tex}\frac{2 \lambda^{\prime}}{a}=\frac{70}{100} \times \frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{7}{10} \lambda=\frac{7}{10} \times{/tex} 6000 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex} = 4200 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex}

question_text: Write two points to distinguish between interference and diffraction fringes.
explanation: In interference contrast between maxima and minima in interference is very high. In diffraction contrast between maxima and minima in diffraction is relatively low. In interference, the width of the fringes is equal. In diffraction, the width of the fringes is not equal.
solution: In interference contrast between maxima and minima in interference is very high. In diffraction contrast between maxima and minima in diffraction is relatively low. In interference, the width of the fringes is equal. In diffraction, the width of the fringes is not equal.

subject: Physics
question_html: Why are coherent sources necessary to produce a sustained interference pattern?
question_text: Why are coherent sources necessary to produce a sustained interference pattern?
explanation: Coherent sources have a constant phase difference. This ensures that the positions of maxima and minima do not change with time i.e., a sustained interference pattern is obtained.
solution: Coherent sources have a constant phase difference. This ensures that the positions of maxima and minima do not change with time i.e., a sustained interference pattern is obtained.
solution_html: Coherent sources have a constant phase difference. This ensures that the positions of maxima and minima do not change with time i.e., a sustained interference pattern is obtained.

subject: Physics
question_html: When light travels from a rarer to a denser medium, it loses speed. Does this loss imply a reduction in energy carried by the light waves?
question_text: When light travels from a rarer to a denser medium, it loses speed. Does this loss imply a reduction in energy carried by the light waves?
explanation: No, this loss does not imply a reduction in energy carried by the light waves. The energy of any wave depends upon its frequency which remains the same whenever light wave travels from one medium to another medium, thus we may lose the speed, not the energy.
solution: No, this loss does not imply a reduction in energy carried by the light waves. The energy of any wave depends upon its frequency which remains the same whenever light wave travels from one medium to another medium, thus we may lose the speed, not the energy.
solution_html: No, this loss does not imply a reduction in energy carried by the light waves. The energy of any wave depends upon its frequency which remains the same whenever light wave travels from one medium to another medium, thus we may lose the speed, not the energy.

1. The refracted wavefront CE is shown in Fig. (i)

    sin i = {tex}\frac{B C}{A C}=\frac{v_{1} t}{A C}{/tex}; sin r = {tex}\frac{A E}{A C}=\frac{v_{2} t}{A C}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}{/tex} or {tex}{ }^{1} \mu_{2}=\frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}{/tex} = a constant
    This proves Snell's law of refraction.


  2. Explanation. The time taken by any disturbance to travel from incident wavefront to the refracted or reflected wavefront is same.

question_html:
In Young's double-slit experiment, the intensity of light at a point on the screen where the path difference is {tex}\lambda{/tex} is k units. Find the intensity at a point where the path difference is

  1. {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
question_text: In Young's double-slit experiment, the intensity of light at a point on the screen where the path difference is {tex}\lambda{/tex} is k units. Find the intensity at a point where the path difference is {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
explanation: Intensity at any point on the screen, I = I 1 + I 2 + {tex}2 \sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}}{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex} Let I 0 be the intensity of either source. Then I 1 = I 2 = I 0 , and I = 2I 0 (1 + cos {tex}\phi{/tex} ) = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} When p = {tex}\lambda{/tex} , {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 4 I 0 cos 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} = 4I 0 = k When p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{4}, \phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ,= 4 I 0 {tex}\times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 2 I 0 = {tex}\frac{k}{2}{/tex} When p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}, \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} = 4 I 0 {tex}\times \frac{1}{4}{/tex} = I 0 = {tex}\frac{k}{4}{/tex} When p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}, \phi=\pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} = 0
solution: Intensity at any point on the screen, I = I 1 + I 2 + {tex}2 \sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}}{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex} Let I 0 be the intensity of either source. Then I 1 = I 2 = I 0 , and I = 2I 0 (1 + cos {tex}\phi{/tex} ) = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} When p = {tex}\lambda{/tex} , {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 4 I 0 cos 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} = 4I 0 = k When p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{4}, \phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} ,= 4 I 0 {tex}\times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 2 I 0 = {tex}\frac{k}{2}{/tex} When p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}, \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} = 4 I 0 {tex}\times \frac{1}{4}{/tex} = I 0 = {tex}\frac{k}{4}{/tex} When p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}, \phi=\pi{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} = 0
solution_html:
Intensity at any point on the screen,
I = I1 + I2 + {tex}2 \sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}}{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex}
Let I0 be the intensity of either source. Then I1 = I2 = I0, and I = 2I0 (1 + cos {tex}\phi{/tex}) = 4I0 cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
When p = {tex}\lambda{/tex}, {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I0 cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 4 I0 cos2{tex}\pi{/tex} = 4I0 = k

  1. When p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{4}, \phi=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I0 cos2 {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex},= 4 I0 {tex}\times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 2 I0 = {tex}\frac{k}{2}{/tex}
  2. When p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{3}, \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I0 cos2 {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} = 4 I0 {tex}\times \frac{1}{4}{/tex} = I0 = {tex}\frac{k}{4}{/tex}
  3. When p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}, \phi=\pi{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = 4I0 cos2 {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} = 0

question_html:
A parallel beam of monochromatic light falls normally on a narrow slit of width a to produce a diffraction pattern on the screen placed parallel to the plane of the slit. Use Huygens' principle to explain that

  1. the central bright maxima is twice as wide as the other maxima.
  2. the intensity falls as we move to successive maxima away from the centre on either side.
question_text: A parallel beam of monochromatic light falls normally on a narrow slit of width a to produce a diffraction pattern on the screen placed parallel to the plane of the slit. Use Huygens' principle to explain that the central bright maxima is twice as wide as the other maxima. the intensity falls as we move to successive maxima away from the centre on either side.
explanation: At the central point (O) the contribution from each half in SS 1 is in phase with that from the corresponding part in SS 2 . Hence, O is a maxima. At the point M where S 2 M - S 1 M = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} Phase difference between each wavelet from SS 1 and corresponding wavelet from SS 2 = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} Hence, M would be a minima. All such points (path difference = n {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} ) are also minima. Similarly, all points, for which path difference = (2n +1) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} , are maxima but with decreasing intensity. From the figure, Half angular width of central maxima = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The size of central maxima will be reduced to half and intensity of central maxima will be four times if slit is made double the original width.
solution: At the central point (O) the contribution from each half in SS 1 is in phase with that from the corresponding part in SS 2 . Hence, O is a maxima. At the point M where S 2 M - S 1 M = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} Phase difference between each wavelet from SS 1 and corresponding wavelet from SS 2 = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} Hence, M would be a minima. All such points (path difference = n {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} ) are also minima. Similarly, all points, for which path difference = (2n +1) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} , are maxima but with decreasing intensity. From the figure, Half angular width of central maxima = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The size of central maxima will be reduced to half and intensity of central maxima will be four times if slit is made double the original width.
solution_html:
1. At the central point (O) the contribution from each half in SS1 is in phase with that from the corresponding part in SS2. Hence, O is a maxima.

    At the point M where S2M - S1M = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} Phase difference between each wavelet from SS1 and corresponding wavelet from SS2 = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} Hence, M would be a minima.
    All such points (path difference = n{tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}) are also minima. Similarly, all points, for which path difference = (2n +1){tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}, are maxima but with decreasing intensity.

  2. From the figure, Half angular width of central maxima = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The size of central maxima will be reduced to half and intensity of central maxima will be four times if slit is made double the original width.

question_html:
1. A monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident normally on a narrow slit of width 'a' to produce a diffraction pattern on the screen placed at a distance D from the slit. With the help of a relevant diagram, deduce the conditions for obtaining maxima and minima on the screen.
  2. Use these conditions to show that angular width of central maximum is twice the angular width of secondary maximum.
  3. Explain why the maxima at {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} become weaker and weaker with increasing n.
question_text: A monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident normally on a narrow slit of width 'a' to produce a diffraction pattern on the screen placed at a distance D from the slit. With the help of a relevant diagram, deduce the conditions for obtaining maxima and minima on the screen. Use these conditions to show that angular width of central maximum is twice the angular width of secondary maximum. Explain why the maxima at {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} become weaker and weaker with increasing n.
explanation: In a single-slit diffraction experiment, monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} passes through a slit of width {tex}a{/tex} , producing a diffraction pattern on a screen placed at a distance {tex}D{/tex} . Minima condition: The minima occur when the path difference between light from the top and bottom of the slit is an integer multiple of the wavelength, causing destructive interference. This occurs at angles {tex}\theta{/tex} given by: {tex} a \sin \theta=m \lambda \text { for } m= \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3, \ldots{/tex} Maxima condition: Maxima do not occur at regular intervals. However, they can be approximated by: {tex} a \sin \theta=\left(m+\frac{1}{2}\right) \lambda \text { for } m=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots{/tex} A diagram would show a central bright fringe at {tex}\theta=0{/tex} and alternating dark and bright fringes. The condition for first minimum on the screen is a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta{/tex} = sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of the central fringe on the screen = 2 {tex}\theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} Angular width of the first secondary diffraction fringes = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} Hence the angular width of central fringe is twice the angular width of first secondary fringe. Maxima become weaker and weaker with increasing n This is because the effective part of the wavefront, contributing to the maxima, becomes smaller and smaller, with the increasing n.
solution: In a single-slit diffraction experiment, monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} passes through a slit of width {tex}a{/tex} , producing a diffraction pattern on a screen placed at a distance {tex}D{/tex} . Minima condition: The minima occur when the path difference between light from the top and bottom of the slit is an integer multiple of the wavelength, causing destructive interference. This occurs at angles {tex}\theta{/tex} given by: {tex} a \sin \theta=m \lambda \text { for } m= \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3, \ldots{/tex} Maxima condition: Maxima do not occur at regular intervals. However, they can be approximated by: {tex} a \sin \theta=\left(m+\frac{1}{2}\right) \lambda \text { for } m=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots{/tex} A diagram would show a central bright fringe at {tex}\theta=0{/tex} and alternating dark and bright fringes. The condition for first minimum on the screen is a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta{/tex} = sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of the central fringe on the screen = 2 {tex}\theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} Angular width of the first secondary diffraction fringes = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} Hence the angular width of central fringe is twice the angular width of first secondary fringe. Maxima become weaker and weaker with increasing n This is because the effective part of the wavefront, contributing to the maxima, becomes smaller and smaller, with the increasing n.
solution_html:
1. In a single-slit diffraction experiment, monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} passes through a slit of width {tex}a{/tex}, producing a diffraction pattern on a screen placed at a distance {tex}D{/tex}.
    Minima condition:
    The minima occur when the path difference between light from the top and bottom of the slit is an integer multiple of the wavelength, causing destructive interference. This occurs at angles {tex}\theta{/tex} given by:
    {tex} a \sin \theta=m \lambda \text { for } m= \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3, \ldots{/tex}
    Maxima condition:
    Maxima do not occur at regular intervals. However, they can be approximated by:
    {tex} a \sin \theta=\left(m+\frac{1}{2}\right) \lambda \text { for } m=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots{/tex}
    A diagram would show a central bright fringe at {tex}\theta=0{/tex} and alternating dark and bright fringes.
  2. The condition for first minimum on the screen is
    a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta{/tex} = sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    The angular width of the central fringe on the screen = 2 {tex}\theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}
    Angular width of the first secondary diffraction fringes = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    Hence the angular width of central fringe is twice the angular width of first secondary fringe.
  3. Maxima become weaker and weaker with increasing n This is because the effective part of the wavefront, contributing to the maxima, becomes smaller and smaller, with the increasing n.

subject: Physics
question_html: Define the term wavefront. State Huygen's principle. Consider a plane wavefront incident on a thin convex lens. Draw a proper diagram to show how the incident wavefront traverses through the lens and after refraction focusses on the focal point of the lens, giving the shape of the emergent wavefront.
question_text: Define the term wavefront. State Huygen's principle. Consider a plane wavefront incident on a thin convex lens. Draw a proper diagram to show how the incident wavefront traverses through the lens and after refraction focusses on the focal point of the lens, giving the shape of the emergent wavefront.
explanation: When light is emitted from a source, then the particles present around it begins to vibrate. The locus of all such particles which are vibrating in the same phase is termed as wavefront. Huygens' principle: Every point on a wave-front may be considered a source of secondary spherical wavelets which spread out in the forward direction at the speed of light. The new wave-front is the tangential surface to all of these secondary wavelets. ​​​​​ Now when a plane wavefront (parallel rays) is incident on a thin convex lens, the emergent rays are focused on the focal point of the lens. Thus the shape of emerging wavefront is spherical.
solution: When light is emitted from a source, then the particles present around it begins to vibrate. The locus of all such particles which are vibrating in the same phase is termed as wavefront. Huygens' principle: Every point on a wave-front may be considered a source of secondary spherical wavelets which spread out in the forward direction at the speed of light. The new wave-front is the tangential surface to all of these secondary wavelets. ​​​​​ Now when a plane wavefront (parallel rays) is incident on a thin convex lens, the emergent rays are focused on the focal point of the lens. Thus the shape of emerging wavefront is spherical.
solution_html:
When light is emitted from a source, then the particles present around it begins to vibrate. The locus of all such particles which are vibrating in the same phase is termed as wavefront.
Huygens' principle: Every point on a wave-front may be considered a source of secondary spherical wavelets which spread out in the forward direction at the speed of light. The new wave-front is the tangential surface to all of these secondary wavelets.
​​​​​Now when a plane wavefront (parallel rays) is incident on a thin convex lens, the emergent rays are focused on the focal point of the lens. Thus the shape of emerging wavefront is spherical.

solution_html:
Intensity pattern for single slit diffraction is shown below

Intensity pattern for double slit interference pattern is shown.

Difference between diffraction and interference patterns are

  1. In interference pattern, all maxima and all minima are of same width but in the diffraction pattern, width of central maxima is maximum and for successive secondary maxima and it goes on decreasing on both sides of the central maxima.
  2. In interference pattern, each maxima have same intensity or brightness and each minimum are of same darkness while in the diffraction pattern, the intensity of central maxima is largest and it decreases rapidly for successive maxima on both sides of the central maxima.

question_html:
1. Explain two features to distinguish between the interference pattern in Young's double slit experiment with the diffraction pattern obtained due to a single slit.
  2. A monochromatic light of wavelength 500 nm is incident normally on a single slit of width 0.2 mm to produce a diffraction pattern. Find the angular width of the central maximum obtained on the screen. Estimate the number of fringes obtained in Young's double slit experiment with fringe width 0.5 mm, which can be accommodated within the region of total angular spread of the central maximum due to the single slit.
question_text: Explain two features to distinguish between the interference pattern in Young's double slit experiment with the diffraction pattern obtained due to a single slit. A monochromatic light of wavelength 500 nm is incident normally on a single slit of width 0.2 mm to produce a diffraction pattern. Find the angular width of the central maximum obtained on the screen. Estimate the number of fringes obtained in Young's double slit experiment with fringe width 0.5 mm, which can be accommodated within the region of total angular spread of the central maximum due to the single slit.
explanation: The features to distinguish is given as Interference pattern : a) All fringes are of equal width. b) Intensity of all bright bands is equal. Diffraction pattern : a) The width of central maxima is twice the width of higher order band. b) The intensity goes on decreasing for a higher order of diffraction bands. Given, wavelength {tex}( \lambda ) = 500 \mathrm { nm } = 500 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Width of single slit (d) = 0.2 mm {tex}= 0.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Angular width of central fringe {tex}= 2 \times \frac { \lambda } { d }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 500 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 0.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } = \frac { 10 ^ { - 6 } } { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } } = \frac { 1 } { 200 } = 5 \times 10 ^ { - 3 }{/tex} radian Linear width of central fringe of single slit ​​​​​​ {tex}= 5 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { mm } = 5 \mathrm { mm }{/tex} Number of double slit fringe accommodated in central fringe {tex}= \frac { 50 } { 5 } = 10{/tex} fringes.
solution: The features to distinguish is given as Interference pattern : a) All fringes are of equal width. b) Intensity of all bright bands is equal. Diffraction pattern : a) The width of central maxima is twice the width of higher order band. b) The intensity goes on decreasing for a higher order of diffraction bands. Given, wavelength {tex}( \lambda ) = 500 \mathrm { nm } = 500 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Width of single slit (d) = 0.2 mm {tex}= 0.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Angular width of central fringe {tex}= 2 \times \frac { \lambda } { d }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 500 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 0.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } = \frac { 10 ^ { - 6 } } { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } } = \frac { 1 } { 200 } = 5 \times 10 ^ { - 3 }{/tex} radian Linear width of central fringe of single slit ​​​​​​ {tex}= 5 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { mm } = 5 \mathrm { mm }{/tex} Number of double slit fringe accommodated in central fringe {tex}= \frac { 50 } { 5 } = 10{/tex} fringes.
solution_html:
1. The features to distinguish is given as
    Interference pattern :
    a) All fringes are of equal width. 
    b) Intensity of all bright bands is equal.
    Diffraction pattern :
    a) The width of central maxima is twice the width of higher order band.
    b) The intensity goes on decreasing for a higher order of diffraction bands.
  2. Given, wavelength {tex}( \lambda ) = 500 \mathrm { nm } = 500 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
    Width of single slit (d) = 0.2 mm {tex}= 0.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
    Angular width of central fringe {tex}= 2 \times \frac { \lambda } { d }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 2 \times 500 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 0.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } = \frac { 10 ^ { - 6 } } { 2 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } } = \frac { 1 } { 200 } = 5 \times 10 ^ { - 3 }{/tex}radian
    Linear width of central fringe of single slit ​​​​​​
    {tex}= 5 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { mm } = 5 \mathrm { mm }{/tex}
    Number of double slit fringe accommodated in central fringe {tex}= \frac { 50 } { 5 } = 10{/tex} fringes.

question_html:
Find the intensity at a point on a screen in Young's double-slit experiment where the interfering waves of equal intensity have a path difference of

  1. {tex}\frac {\lambda}4{/tex} and
  2. {tex}\frac {\lambda}3{/tex}
question_text: Find the intensity at a point on a screen in Young's double-slit experiment where the interfering waves of equal intensity have a path difference of {tex}\frac {\lambda}4{/tex} and {tex}\frac {\lambda}3{/tex}
explanation: I R = I 1 = I 2 = I 0 path diff. = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{4} \therefore{/tex} phase diff. {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} I R = I 1 + I 2 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} = I 0 + I 0 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{0} I_{0}} \cos \frac \pi 2=2 I_{0}{/tex} , (cos90 0 =0) here, {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{3}=120^{\circ}{/tex} I R = I 1 + I 2 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} = I 0 + I 0 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{0} I_{0}} \cos 120^{\circ}{/tex} = 2I 0 + 2I 0 {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=I_{0}{/tex} , (cos 120 0 =(-1/2))
solution: I R = I 1 = I 2 = I 0 path diff. = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{4} \therefore{/tex} phase diff. {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} I R = I 1 + I 2 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} = I 0 + I 0 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{0} I_{0}} \cos \frac \pi 2=2 I_{0}{/tex} , (cos90 0 =0) here, {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{3}=120^{\circ}{/tex} I R = I 1 + I 2 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos \phi{/tex} = I 0 + I 0 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{0} I_{0}} \cos 120^{\circ}{/tex} = 2I 0 + 2I 0 {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=I_{0}{/tex} , (cos 120 0 =(-1/2))
solution_html:
IR = I1 = I2 = I0

  1. path diff. = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{4} \therefore{/tex} phase diff. {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
    IR = I1 + I2 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos \phi{/tex}
    = I0 + I0 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{0} I_{0}} \cos \frac \pi 2=2 I_{0}{/tex}  , (cos900 =0)
  2. here, {tex}\phi=\frac{2 \pi}{3}=120^{\circ}{/tex}
    IR = I1 + I2 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos \phi{/tex}
    = I0 + I0 + 2 {tex}\sqrt{I_{0} I_{0}} \cos 120^{\circ}{/tex}
    = 2I0 + 2I0{tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=I_{0}{/tex}  , (cos 1200=(-1/2))

question_html: Monochromatic light of wavelength 589 nm is incident from air on a water surface. What are the wavelength, frequency and speed of (a) reflected, and (b) refracted light? Refractive index of water is 1.33.
question_text: Monochromatic light of wavelength 589 nm is incident from air on a water surface. What are the wavelength, frequency and speed of (a) reflected, and (b) refracted light? Refractive index of water is 1.33.
explanation: Given, {tex}\lambda = 589nm{/tex} {tex}c = 3 \times {10^8}m/s,\mu = 1.33{/tex} For reflected light, Wavelength {tex}\lambda = 589nm = 589 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex} {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{589 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 5.09 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex} Hence, the speed, frequency, and wavelength of the reflected light are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz, and 589 nm respectively. Frequency of light does not depend on the property of the medium in which it is travelling. Hence, the frequency of the refracted ray in water will be equal to the frequency of the incident or reflected light in air. {tex}v=\frac{c}{\mu}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{3\times10^8}{1.33}=2.26\times10^8\mathrm m/\mathrm s{/tex} Wavelength of light in water is given by the relation, {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{\nu}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.26 \times 10^{8}}{5.09 \times 10^{14}}{/tex} {tex}=444.007 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=444.01\;nm{/tex} Hence the speed, frequency and wavelength of refracted light are 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz and 444.01 nm respectively.
solution: Given, {tex}\lambda = 589nm{/tex} {tex}c = 3 \times {10^8}m/s,\mu = 1.33{/tex} For reflected light, Wavelength {tex}\lambda = 589nm = 589 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex} {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{589 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 5.09 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex} Hence, the speed, frequency, and wavelength of the reflected light are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz, and 589 nm respectively. Frequency of light does not depend on the property of the medium in which it is travelling. Hence, the frequency of the refracted ray in water will be equal to the frequency of the incident or reflected light in air. {tex}v=\frac{c}{\mu}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{3\times10^8}{1.33}=2.26\times10^8\mathrm m/\mathrm s{/tex} Wavelength of light in water is given by the relation, {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{\nu}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.26 \times 10^{8}}{5.09 \times 10^{14}}{/tex} {tex}=444.007 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=444.01\;nm{/tex} Hence the speed, frequency and wavelength of refracted light are 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz and 444.01 nm respectively.
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\lambda = 589nm{/tex}
{tex}c = 3 \times {10^8}m/s,\mu = 1.33{/tex}

  1. For reflected light,
    Wavelength {tex}\lambda = 589nm = 589 \times {10^{ - 9}}m{/tex}
    {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{589 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 5.09 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex}
    Hence, the speed, frequency, and wavelength of the reflected light are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz, and 589 nm respectively.
  2. Frequency of light does not depend on the property of the medium in which it is travelling. Hence, the frequency of the refracted ray in water will be equal to the frequency of the incident or reflected light in air.
    {tex}v=\frac{c}{\mu}{/tex}
    {tex}v=\frac{3\times10^8}{1.33}=2.26\times10^8\mathrm m/\mathrm s{/tex}
    Wavelength of light in water is given by the relation,
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{c}{\nu}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2.26 \times 10^{8}}{5.09 \times 10^{14}}{/tex}
    {tex}=444.007 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=444.01\;nm{/tex}
    Hence the speed, frequency and wavelength of refracted light are 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s, 5.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz and 444.01 nm respectively.

question_html: Light waves from two coherent sources arrive at two points on a screen with path differences of 0 and {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}. Find the ratio of intensities at the points.
question_text: Light waves from two coherent sources arrive at two points on a screen with path differences of 0 and {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} . Find the ratio of intensities at the points.
explanation: p = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \phi{/tex} p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \phi=\pi{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{P}}{I_{Q}}=\frac{1+\cos 0}{1+\cos \pi}=\frac{1+1}{1-1}{/tex} = 2 : 0
solution: p = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \phi{/tex} p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \phi=\pi{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{P}}{I_{Q}}=\frac{1+\cos 0}{1+\cos \pi}=\frac{1+1}{1-1}{/tex} = 2 : 0
solution_html:
p = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \phi{/tex}
p = {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \phi=\pi{/tex}
{tex}\frac{I_{P}}{I_{Q}}=\frac{1+\cos 0}{1+\cos \pi}=\frac{1+1}{1-1}{/tex} = 2 : 0

question_html: Monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident normally on a narrow slit of width 'a' to produce a diffraction pattern on the screen placed at a distance D from the slit With the help of a relevant diagram, deduce the conditions for maxima and minima on the screen. Use these conditions to show that the angular width of central maxima is twice the angular width of secondary maxima.
question_text: Monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident normally on a narrow slit of width 'a' to produce a diffraction pattern on the screen placed at a distance D from the slit With the help of a relevant diagram, deduce the conditions for maxima and minima on the screen. Use these conditions to show that the angular width of central maxima is twice the angular width of secondary maxima.
explanation: According to the figure, the path difference is given by, NP - LP = NQ = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} a {tex}\theta{/tex} as {tex}\theta{/tex} <<< 1 {tex}\therefore {/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} By dividing the slit into an appropriate number of parts, we find the point P for which {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} are points of minima. {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} are points of maxima The angular width of central maxima, {tex}\theta=\theta_{1}-\theta_{-1} =\frac{\lambda}{a}-\left(-\frac{\lambda}{a}\right) {/tex} {tex}\theta_{2} =\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of the secondary maxima = {tex}\theta_{2}-\theta_{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}-\frac{\lambda}{a}=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} Angular width of central maxima
solution: According to the figure, the path difference is given by, NP - LP = NQ = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cong{/tex} a {tex}\theta{/tex} as {tex}\theta{/tex} <<< 1 {tex}\therefore {/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} By dividing the slit into an appropriate number of parts, we find the point P for which {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} are points of minima. {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} are points of maxima The angular width of central maxima, {tex}\theta=\theta_{1}-\theta_{-1} =\frac{\lambda}{a}-\left(-\frac{\lambda}{a}\right) {/tex} {tex}\theta_{2} =\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of the secondary maxima = {tex}\theta_{2}-\theta_{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}-\frac{\lambda}{a}=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} Angular width of central maxima
solution_html:
According to the figure, the path difference is given by, 
NP - LP = NQ
= a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}  {tex}\cong{/tex} a{tex}\theta{/tex}
as {tex}\theta{/tex} <<< 1
{tex}\therefore {/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex}
By dividing the slit into an appropriate number of parts, we find the point P for which

  1. {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} are points of minima.
  2. {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} are points of maxima

The angular width of central maxima,
{tex}\theta=\theta_{1}-\theta_{-1} =\frac{\lambda}{a}-\left(-\frac{\lambda}{a}\right) {/tex}
{tex}\theta_{2} =\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}
The angular width of the secondary maxima = {tex}\theta_{2}-\theta_{1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}-\frac{\lambda}{a}=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} Angular width of central maxima

question_html: Light waves from two coherent sources have intensities in the ratio of 4 : 9. Find the ratio of intensities of maxima and minima in the interference pattern.
question_text: Light waves from two coherent sources have intensities in the ratio of 4 : 9. Find the ratio of intensities of maxima and minima in the interference pattern.
explanation: r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{9}}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{r+1}{r-1}\right)^{2}=\frac{\left(\frac{2}{3}+1\right)^{2}}{\left(\frac{2}{3}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 25 : 1
solution: r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{9}}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{r+1}{r-1}\right)^{2}=\frac{\left(\frac{2}{3}+1\right)^{2}}{\left(\frac{2}{3}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 25 : 1
solution_html:
r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{9}}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{r+1}{r-1}\right)^{2}=\frac{\left(\frac{2}{3}+1\right)^{2}}{\left(\frac{2}{3}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 25 : 1

question_html: Two wavelengths of sodium light 590 nm, 596 nm are used, in turn, to study the diffraction taking place at a single slit of aperture 4 mm. The distance between the slit and the screen is 2 m. Calculate the separation between the positions of the first maximum of the diffraction pattern obtained in the two cases.
question_text: Two wavelengths of sodium light 590 nm, 596 nm are used, in turn, to study the diffraction taking place at a single slit of aperture 4 mm. The distance between the slit and the screen is 2 m. Calculate the separation between the positions of the first maximum of the diffraction pattern obtained in the two cases.
explanation: Here {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} = 590 nm = 590 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m, {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} = 596 nm = 596 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m, a = 4 mm = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 2m Distance of first secondary maximum from the centre of the screen, x = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{a}{/tex} For the two wavelengths, we have x 1 = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda_{1}}{a}{/tex} and x 2 = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda_{2}}{a}{/tex} Separation between the first two maximum of sodium lines, {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = x 2 - x 1 = {tex}\frac{3 D}{2 a}\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \times 2}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} (596 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 - 590 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 ) {tex}=\frac{3 \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{4}{/tex} = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m
solution: Here {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} = 590 nm = 590 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m, {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} = 596 nm = 596 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m, a = 4 mm = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 2m Distance of first secondary maximum from the centre of the screen, x = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{a}{/tex} For the two wavelengths, we have x 1 = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda_{1}}{a}{/tex} and x 2 = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda_{2}}{a}{/tex} Separation between the first two maximum of sodium lines, {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = x 2 - x 1 = {tex}\frac{3 D}{2 a}\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \times 2}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} (596 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 - 590 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 ) {tex}=\frac{3 \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{4}{/tex} = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m
solution_html:
Here {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} = 590 nm = 590 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9m, {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} = 596 nm = 596 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9m, a = 4 mm = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3m, D = 2m
Distance of first secondary maximum from the centre  of the screen, x = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{a}{/tex}
For the two wavelengths, we have
x1 = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda_{1}}{a}{/tex} and x2 = {tex}\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda_{2}}{a}{/tex}
Separation between the first two maximum of sodium lines,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}x = x2 - x1 = {tex}\frac{3 D}{2 a}\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3 \times 2}{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} (596 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 - 590 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9)
{tex}=\frac{3 \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{4}{/tex} = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6m

question_html:
A monochromatic light of wavelength 500 nm is incident normally on a single slit of width 0.2 mm to produce a diffraction pattern. Find the angular width of the central maximum obtained on the screen.
Estimate the number of fringes obtained in Young's double-slit experiment with fringe width 0.5 mm, which can be accommodated within the region of total angular spread of the central maximum due to single slit.
question_text: A monochromatic light of wavelength 500 nm is incident normally on a single slit of width 0.2 mm to produce a diffraction pattern. Find the angular width of the central maximum obtained on the screen. Estimate the number of fringes obtained in Young's double-slit experiment with fringe width 0.5 mm, which can be accommodated within the region of total angular spread of the central maximum due to single slit.
explanation: Angular width of central maximum, {tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}=\frac{2 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{02 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} rad = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 rad Linear width of central maximum in the diffraction pattern, {tex}\beta_{0}=2 \theta \times D=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} Let n be the number of interference fringes which can be accommodated in the central maximum. Then, n {tex}\times \beta=\beta_{0}{/tex} or n {tex}\times \frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} or n = {tex}\frac{2 d}{a}{/tex}
solution: Angular width of central maximum, {tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}=\frac{2 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{02 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} rad = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 rad Linear width of central maximum in the diffraction pattern, {tex}\beta_{0}=2 \theta \times D=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} Let n be the number of interference fringes which can be accommodated in the central maximum. Then, n {tex}\times \beta=\beta_{0}{/tex} or n {tex}\times \frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} or n = {tex}\frac{2 d}{a}{/tex}
solution_html:
Angular width of central maximum,
{tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}=\frac{2 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{02 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} rad = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 rad
Linear width of central maximum in the diffraction pattern,
{tex}\beta_{0}=2 \theta \times D=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex}
Let n be the number of interference fringes which can be accommodated in the central maximum. Then,
n {tex}\times \beta=\beta_{0}{/tex} or n {tex}\times \frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} or n = {tex}\frac{2 d}{a}{/tex}

question_html: In Young's double slit experiment, deduce the condition for (a) constructive and (b) destructive interference at a point on the screen. Draw a graph showing a variation of intensity in the interference pattern against position x on the screen.
question_text: In Young's double slit experiment, deduce the condition for (a) constructive and (b) destructive interference at a point on the screen. Draw a graph showing a variation of intensity in the interference pattern against position x on the screen.
explanation: Suppose S 1 and S 2 are two fine slits, a small distance d apart. They are illuminated by a strong source S of monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda {/tex} . MN is a screen at a distance D from the slits. Consider a point P at a distance y from 0, the centre of the screen. The path difference between two waves arriving at point Pis equal to S 2 P - S 1 P. Now, (S 2 P) 2 -(S 1 P) 2 {tex}= \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y + \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] - \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y - \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] = 2 y d{/tex} Thus, {tex}S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P = \frac { 2 y d } { S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P }{/tex} But {tex}S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P \approx 2 D{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P \approx \frac { d y } { D }{/tex} For constructive interference (Bright fringes) Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = n \lambda{/tex} , where, n = 0, 1, 2, 3,...... {tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { n D \lambda } { d }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ....] For destructive interference (Dark fringes) Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = ( 2 n - 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex} The distribution of intensity in Young's double slit experiment is as shown below
solution: Suppose S 1 and S 2 are two fine slits, a small distance d apart. They are illuminated by a strong source S of monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda {/tex} . MN is a screen at a distance D from the slits. Consider a point P at a distance y from 0, the centre of the screen. The path difference between two waves arriving at point Pis equal to S 2 P - S 1 P. Now, (S 2 P) 2 -(S 1 P) 2 {tex}= \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y + \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] - \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y - \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] = 2 y d{/tex} Thus, {tex}S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P = \frac { 2 y d } { S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P }{/tex} But {tex}S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P \approx 2 D{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P \approx \frac { d y } { D }{/tex} For constructive interference (Bright fringes) Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = n \lambda{/tex} , where, n = 0, 1, 2, 3,...... {tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { n D \lambda } { d }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ....] For destructive interference (Dark fringes) Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = ( 2 n - 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex} The distribution of intensity in Young's double slit experiment is as shown below
solution_html:
Suppose S1 and S2 are two fine slits, a small distance d apart. They are illuminated by a strong source S of monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda {/tex}. MN is a screen at a distance D from the slits.

Consider a point P at a distance y from 0, the centre of the screen.
The path difference between two waves arriving at point Pis equal to S2P - S1P.
Now, (S2P)2 -(S1P)2
{tex}= \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y + \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] - \left[ D ^ { 2 } + \left( y - \frac { d } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] = 2 y d{/tex}
Thus, {tex}S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P = \frac { 2 y d } { S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P }{/tex}
But {tex}S _ { 2 } P + S _ { 1 } P \approx 2 D{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad S _ { 2 } P - S _ { 1 } P \approx \frac { d y } { D }{/tex}

  1. For constructive interference (Bright fringes)
    Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = n \lambda{/tex} , where,
    n = 0, 1, 2, 3,......
    {tex}\therefore \quad y = \frac { n D \lambda } { d }{/tex} [{tex}\because {/tex} n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ....]
  2. For destructive interference (Dark fringes)
    Path difference {tex}= \frac { d y } { D } = ( 2 n - 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex}

The distribution of intensity in Young's double slit experiment is as shown below

question_html: When monochromatic light is incident on a surface separating two media, the reflected and refracted light both have the same frequency as the incident frequency. Explain, why?
question_text: When monochromatic light is incident on a surface separating two media, the reflected and refracted light both have the same frequency as the incident frequency. Explain, why?
explanation: No, the speed of light is not independent of the colour (wavelength) of the light. The violet colour travels slower than the red light in a glass prism.
solution: No, the speed of light is not independent of the colour (wavelength) of the light. The violet colour travels slower than the red light in a glass prism.
solution_html: No, the speed of light is not independent of the colour (wavelength) of the light. The violet colour travels slower than the red light in a glass prism.

question_html:
1. Use Huygen's geometrical construction to show how a plane wavefront at t = 0 propagates and produces a wavefront at a later time.
  2. Verify, using Huygen's principle, Snell's law of refraction of a plane wave propagating from a denser to a rarer medium.
  3. Illustrate with the help of diagrams the action of
    1. convex lens and
    2. concave mirror, on a plane wavefront incident on it.
question_text: Use Huygen's geometrical construction to show how a plane wavefront at t = 0 propagates and produces a wavefront at a later time. Verify, using Huygen's principle, Snell's law of refraction of a plane wave propagating from a denser to a rarer medium. Illustrate with the help of diagrams the action of convex lens and concave mirror, on a plane wavefront incident on it.
explanation: The surface of constant phase is known as a wavefront. The Geometrical construction of wavefront: To determine the wavefront at t = {tex}\tau{/tex} draw spheres of radius v {tex}\tau{/tex} from each point on F 1 F 2 and draw a common tangent to these spheres to obtain the new position of the wavefront. The ratio of the speed of light in vaccum to the speed of light in the medium is termed as refractive index of medium. Let us consider the medium I, which is optically denser than medium 2. Let the speed of light be v 1 in medium I and v 2 in medium II. Always, note that v 2 > v 1 A plane wave AB propagates and hits the interface at an angle i. and can be the time taken be the wavefront to travel the distance BC. Now, we want to draw the refracted wavefront. We can draw a sphere of radius v 2 with A as centre. Let the surface tangent to the sphere passing through point C, as the refracted wavefront. Now, Let the surface be tangent to the sphere at E. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC sin i = {tex}\frac{v_1 t}{A C}{/tex} and, In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AEC {tex}\iota{/tex} sin r = {tex}\frac{v_2 t}{A C}{/tex} On dividing both the equations, we finally have, {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v_1}{v_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{c}{v_2}}{\frac{c}{v_1}}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} This is the verified Snell's law.
solution: The surface of constant phase is known as a wavefront. The Geometrical construction of wavefront: To determine the wavefront at t = {tex}\tau{/tex} draw spheres of radius v {tex}\tau{/tex} from each point on F 1 F 2 and draw a common tangent to these spheres to obtain the new position of the wavefront. The ratio of the speed of light in vaccum to the speed of light in the medium is termed as refractive index of medium. Let us consider the medium I, which is optically denser than medium 2. Let the speed of light be v 1 in medium I and v 2 in medium II. Always, note that v 2 > v 1 A plane wave AB propagates and hits the interface at an angle i. and can be the time taken be the wavefront to travel the distance BC. Now, we want to draw the refracted wavefront. We can draw a sphere of radius v 2 with A as centre. Let the surface tangent to the sphere passing through point C, as the refracted wavefront. Now, Let the surface be tangent to the sphere at E. In {tex}\triangle{/tex} ABC sin i = {tex}\frac{v_1 t}{A C}{/tex} and, In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AEC {tex}\iota{/tex} sin r = {tex}\frac{v_2 t}{A C}{/tex} On dividing both the equations, we finally have, {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v_1}{v_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{c}{v_2}}{\frac{c}{v_1}}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex} This is the verified Snell's law.
solution_html:
1. The surface of constant phase is known as a wavefront.

    The Geometrical construction of wavefront:
    To determine the wavefront at t = {tex}\tau{/tex} draw spheres of radius v{tex}\tau{/tex} from each point on F1F2 and draw a common tangent to these spheres to obtain the new position of the wavefront.

  2. The ratio of the speed of light in vaccum to the speed of light in the medium is termed as refractive index of medium.
    Let us consider the medium I, which is optically denser than medium 2.
    Let the speed of light be v1 in medium I and v2 in medium II.
    Always, note that v2 > v1
    A plane wave AB propagates and hits the interface at an angle i. and can be the time taken be the wavefront to travel the distance BC.
    Now, we want to draw the refracted wavefront.
    We can draw a sphere of radius v2 with A as centre. Let the surface tangent to the sphere passing through point C, as the refracted wavefront.
    Now,
    Let the surface be tangent to the sphere at E.
    In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC
    sin i = {tex}\frac{v_1 t}{A C}{/tex} and,
    In {tex}\triangle{/tex} AEC {tex}\iota{/tex}
    sin r = {tex}\frac{v_2 t}{A C}{/tex}
    On dividing both the equations, we finally have,
    {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v_1}{v_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{c}{v_2}}{\frac{c}{v_1}}{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{n_2}{n_1}{/tex}
    This is the verified Snell's law.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. When monochromatic light is incident on a surface separating two media, the reflected and refracted light both have the same frequency as the incident frequency. Explain why?
  2. When light travels from a rarer to a denser medium, the speed decreases. Does the reduction in speed imply a reduction in the energy carried by the light wave?
  3. In the wave picture of light, intensity of light is determined by the square of the amplitude of the wave. What determines the intensity of light in the photon picture of light.
question_text: When monochromatic light is incident on a surface separating two media, the reflected and refracted light both have the same frequency as the incident frequency. Explain why? When light travels from a rarer to a denser medium, the speed decreases. Does the reduction in speed imply a reduction in the energy carried by the light wave? In the wave picture of light, intensity of light is determined by the square of the amplitude of the wave. What determines the intensity of light in the photon picture of light.
explanation: Reflection and refraction arise through interaction of incident light with the atomic constituents of matter. Atoms may be viewed as oscillators, which take up the frequency of the external agency (light) causing forced oscillations. The frequency of light emitted by a charged oscillator equals its frequency of oscillation. Thus, the frequency of scattered light equals the frequency of incident light. No, Energy carried by a wave depends on the amplitude of the wave, not on the speed of wave propagation. For a given frequency, intensity of light in the photon picture is determined by the number of photons crossing an unit area per unit time.
solution: Reflection and refraction arise through interaction of incident light with the atomic constituents of matter. Atoms may be viewed as oscillators, which take up the frequency of the external agency (light) causing forced oscillations. The frequency of light emitted by a charged oscillator equals its frequency of oscillation. Thus, the frequency of scattered light equals the frequency of incident light. No, Energy carried by a wave depends on the amplitude of the wave, not on the speed of wave propagation. For a given frequency, intensity of light in the photon picture is determined by the number of photons crossing an unit area per unit time.
solution_html:
1. Reflection and refraction arise through interaction of incident light with the atomic constituents of matter. Atoms may be viewed as oscillators, which take up the frequency of the external agency (light) causing forced oscillations. The frequency of light emitted by a charged oscillator equals its frequency of oscillation. Thus, the frequency of scattered light equals the frequency of incident light.
  2. No, Energy carried by a wave depends on the amplitude of the wave, not on the speed of wave propagation.
  3. For a given frequency, intensity of light in the photon picture is determined by the number of photons crossing an unit area per unit time.

question_html:
When a plane wavefront, of light, of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}, is incident on a narrow slit, an intensity distribution pattern, of the form shown, is observed
on a screen, suitably kept behind the slit. Name the phenomenon observed.

  1. Obtain the conditions for the formation of central maxima and secondary maxima and the minima.
  2. Why is there significant fall in intensity of the secondary maxima compared to the central maxima, whereas in double slit experiment all the bright fringes are of the same intensity?
  3. When the width of the slit is made double the original width, how is the size of the central band affected?
question_text: When a plane wavefront, of light, of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} , is incident on a narrow slit, an intensity distribution pattern, of the form shown, is observed on a screen, suitably kept behind the slit. Name the phenomenon observed. Obtain the conditions for the formation of central maxima and secondary maxima and the minima. Why is there significant fall in intensity of the secondary maxima compared to the central maxima, whereas in double slit experiment all the bright fringes are of the same intensity? When the width of the slit is made double the original width, how is the size of the central band affected?
explanation: The phenomenon observed is diffraction. At the cental maxima: The contributions due to the secondary wavelets, from all parts of the wavefront (at the slit), arrive in phase at the central maxima {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 At the secondary maxima: It is only the contributions from (nearly) {tex}\frac 13{/tex} (or {tex}\frac 15{/tex} , or {tex}\frac 17{/tex} ,...) of the secondary maxima. These occur at points for which {tex}\theta \equiv\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 0, 1, 2, 3,...) At the minima: The contribution, from 'corresponding pairs', of the sub-parts of the incident wavefront, cancel each other and the net contribution, at the location of the minima, is zero. The minima occur at points for which, {tex}\theta=n \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 0, 1, 2, 3,...) There is a significant fall in intensity at the secondary maxima because the intensity there is only due to the contribution of (nearly)( {tex}\frac 13{/tex} or {tex}\frac 15{/tex} or {tex}\frac 17{/tex} , ...,) of the incident wavefronts. The size of the central maxima would get halved when the width of the slit is doubled.
solution: The phenomenon observed is diffraction. At the cental maxima: The contributions due to the secondary wavelets, from all parts of the wavefront (at the slit), arrive in phase at the central maxima {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 At the secondary maxima: It is only the contributions from (nearly) {tex}\frac 13{/tex} (or {tex}\frac 15{/tex} , or {tex}\frac 17{/tex} ,...) of the secondary maxima. These occur at points for which {tex}\theta \equiv\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 0, 1, 2, 3,...) At the minima: The contribution, from 'corresponding pairs', of the sub-parts of the incident wavefront, cancel each other and the net contribution, at the location of the minima, is zero. The minima occur at points for which, {tex}\theta=n \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 0, 1, 2, 3,...) There is a significant fall in intensity at the secondary maxima because the intensity there is only due to the contribution of (nearly)( {tex}\frac 13{/tex} or {tex}\frac 15{/tex} or {tex}\frac 17{/tex} , ...,) of the incident wavefronts. The size of the central maxima would get halved when the width of the slit is doubled.
solution_html:
The phenomenon observed is diffraction.

  1. At the cental maxima: The contributions due to the secondary wavelets, from all parts of the wavefront (at the slit), arrive in phase at the central maxima {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 At the secondary maxima: It is only the contributions from (nearly) {tex}\frac 13{/tex}(or {tex}\frac 15{/tex}, or {tex}\frac 17{/tex},...) of the secondary maxima. These occur at points for which
    {tex}\theta \equiv\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 0, 1, 2, 3,...)
    At the minima:
    The contribution, from 'corresponding pairs', of the sub-parts of the incident wavefront, cancel each other and the net contribution, at the location of the minima, is zero. The minima occur at points for which, {tex}\theta=n \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} (n = 0, 1, 2, 3,...) 
  2. There is a significant fall in intensity at the secondary maxima because the intensity there is only due to the contribution of (nearly)({tex}\frac 13{/tex} or {tex}\frac 15{/tex} or {tex}\frac 17{/tex}, ...,) of the incident wavefronts.
  3. The size of the central maxima would get halved when the width of the slit is doubled.

question_text: In the wave picture of light, the intensity of light is determined the square of the amplitude of the wave. What determines the intensity of light in the photon picture of light?
explanation: In the wave picture of light, the intensity of light at a point is determined by the number of photons incident per unit area around that point.
solution: In the wave picture of light, the intensity of light at a point is determined by the number of photons incident per unit area around that point.
