question_html:
1. State the essential conditions for diffraction of light.
  2. Explain the diffraction of light due to a narrow single slit and the formation of pattern of fringes on the screen.
  3. Find the relation for the width of central maxima in terms of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}, the width of slit a, and separation between slit and screen D.
  4. If the width of the slit is made double the original width, how does it affect the size and intensity of the central band?
question_text: State the essential conditions for diffraction of light. Explain the diffraction of light due to a narrow single slit and the formation of pattern of fringes on the screen. Find the relation for the width of central maxima in terms of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} , the width of slit a, and separation between slit and screen D. If the width of the slit is made double the original width, how does it affect the size and intensity of the central band?
explanation: Condition for diffraction of light is given by: Size of the slit/aperture must be smaller than of the order of wavelength of light. Single slit diffraction is explained by treating different parts of the wave front as the sources of secondary wavelets. Hence, At the central point C on the screen, {tex}\theta{/tex} is zero. Hence, All path differences are zero and all the parts of the slit contribute in phase and give maximum intensity at C. At any other point P, the path difference between two edges of the slit is given by: NP - LP = NQ = a sin {tex}\theta=a \theta\because\sin \theta{/tex} is small Any point P, is direction {tex}\theta{/tex} , is a location of minima if a {tex}\theta{/tex} = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} This can be explained by dividing the slit into an even number of parts. The path difference between waves from successive parts is 180° out of phase and hence cancel each leading to a minimum. Any point P, in direction Q, is a location of maxima if {tex}a \theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} This can be explained by dividing the slit into an odd number of parts. The contributions from successive parts cancel in pairs because of 180° phase difference. The unpaired part produces intensity at P, leading to a maximum. If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the direction of first minima, then {tex}a \theta=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of central maxima is given by = 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{\alpha}{/tex} Linear width of central maxima is given by, {tex}\beta=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}=2 \theta \times D{/tex} If 'a' is doubled, {tex}\beta{/tex} becomes half. Hence, Intensity becomes 4 times.
solution: Condition for diffraction of light is given by: Size of the slit/aperture must be smaller than of the order of wavelength of light. Single slit diffraction is explained by treating different parts of the wave front as the sources of secondary wavelets. Hence, At the central point C on the screen, {tex}\theta{/tex} is zero. Hence, All path differences are zero and all the parts of the slit contribute in phase and give maximum intensity at C. At any other point P, the path difference between two edges of the slit is given by: NP - LP = NQ = a sin {tex}\theta=a \theta\because\sin \theta{/tex} is small Any point P, is direction {tex}\theta{/tex} , is a location of minima if a {tex}\theta{/tex} = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} This can be explained by dividing the slit into an even number of parts. The path difference between waves from successive parts is 180° out of phase and hence cancel each leading to a minimum. Any point P, in direction Q, is a location of maxima if {tex}a \theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} This can be explained by dividing the slit into an odd number of parts. The contributions from successive parts cancel in pairs because of 180° phase difference. The unpaired part produces intensity at P, leading to a maximum. If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the direction of first minima, then {tex}a \theta=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of central maxima is given by = 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{\alpha}{/tex} Linear width of central maxima is given by, {tex}\beta=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}=2 \theta \times D{/tex} If 'a' is doubled, {tex}\beta{/tex} becomes half. Hence, Intensity becomes 4 times.
solution_html:
1. Condition for diffraction of light is given by: Size of the slit/aperture must be smaller than of the order of wavelength of light.


  2. Single slit diffraction is explained by treating different parts of the wave front as the sources of secondary wavelets. Hence,
    At the central point C on the screen, {tex}\theta{/tex} is zero. Hence,  All path differences are zero and all the parts of the slit contribute in phase and give maximum intensity at C.
    At any other point P, the path difference between two edges of the slit is given by:
    NP - LP = NQ
    = a sin {tex}\theta=a \theta\because\sin \theta{/tex} is small
    Any point P, is direction {tex}\theta{/tex}, is a location of minima
    if a{tex}\theta{/tex} = n{tex}\lambda{/tex}
    This can be explained by dividing the slit into an even number of parts. The path difference between waves from successive parts is 180° out of phase and hence cancel each leading to a minimum.
    Any point P, in direction Q, is a location of maxima
    if {tex}a \theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
    This can be explained by dividing the slit into an odd number of parts. The contributions from successive parts cancel in pairs because of 180° phase difference. The unpaired part produces intensity at P, leading to a maximum.
  3. If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the direction of first minima, then
    {tex}a \theta=\lambda{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    The angular width of central maxima is given by
    = 2{tex}\theta{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{\alpha}{/tex}
    Linear width of central maxima is given by,
    {tex}\beta=\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}=2 \theta \times D{/tex}
  4. If 'a' is doubled, {tex}\beta{/tex} becomes half. Hence, Intensity becomes 4 times.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. Figure shows the geometry of path differences for diffraction by a single slit of width a. Give appropriate 'reasoning' to explain why the intensity of light is
    1. maximum at the central point C on the screen.
    2. (nearly) zero for point P on the screen when {tex}\theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
      Hence write an expression for the total linear width of the central maximum on a screen kept at a distance D from the plane of the slit.
  2. Diffraction defines the limit of the ray optics. Give a brief explanation of this statement.
question_text: Figure shows the geometry of path differences for diffraction by a single slit of width a. Give appropriate 'reasoning' to explain why the intensity of light is maximum at the central point C on the screen. (nearly) zero for point P on the screen when {tex}\theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} Hence write an expression for the total linear width of the central maximum on a screen kept at a distance D from the plane of the slit. Diffraction defines the limit of the ray optics. Give a brief explanation of this statement.
explanation: At the central points C( {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o ), the wavelets from the corresponding parts of the two halves LM and MN of the slit have zero path difference i.e., they are in same phase. This gives maximum intensity at C. The path difference between the extreme rays from L and N is p = NP - LP = NQ = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = a {tex}\theta{/tex} [For small {tex}\theta{/tex} , sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} ] Let a {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Then the path difference between any two corresponding points M 1 and M 2 of the two halves of the slit will be p = {tex}\frac{a \theta}{2}=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} This means the contributions from M 1 and M 2 are 180 o out of phase and cancel in the direction {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda} {a}{/tex} . Contributions from the two halves LM and MN cancel each other. Net intensity at P will be zero. {tex}\therefore \quad \theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} is the condition of first minimum. This is the half angular width of the central maximum. Total angular width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} Linear width of the central maximum on the screen at distance D = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} . When size of aperture a >> {tex}\lambda{/tex} , we can ignore diffraction effects. Then the light appears to travel in straight lines and ray optics becomes valid. Since for the validity of ray optics a >> {tex}\lambda{/tex} or {tex}\lambda{/tex} << a or {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0, it implies that ray optics is a limiting case of wave optics.
solution: At the central points C( {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o ), the wavelets from the corresponding parts of the two halves LM and MN of the slit have zero path difference i.e., they are in same phase. This gives maximum intensity at C. The path difference between the extreme rays from L and N is p = NP - LP = NQ = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = a {tex}\theta{/tex} [For small {tex}\theta{/tex} , sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} ] Let a {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Then the path difference between any two corresponding points M 1 and M 2 of the two halves of the slit will be p = {tex}\frac{a \theta}{2}=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} This means the contributions from M 1 and M 2 are 180 o out of phase and cancel in the direction {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda} {a}{/tex} . Contributions from the two halves LM and MN cancel each other. Net intensity at P will be zero. {tex}\therefore \quad \theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} is the condition of first minimum. This is the half angular width of the central maximum. Total angular width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} Linear width of the central maximum on the screen at distance D = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} . When size of aperture a >> {tex}\lambda{/tex} , we can ignore diffraction effects. Then the light appears to travel in straight lines and ray optics becomes valid. Since for the validity of ray optics a >> {tex}\lambda{/tex} or {tex}\lambda{/tex} << a or {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0, it implies that ray optics is a limiting case of wave optics.
solution_html:
1. At the central points C({tex}\theta{/tex} = 0o), the wavelets from the corresponding parts of the two halves LM and MN of the slit have zero path difference i.e., they are in same phase. This gives maximum intensity at C.

    The path difference between the extreme rays from L and N is
    p = NP - LP = NQ = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = a{tex}\theta{/tex} [For small {tex}\theta{/tex}, sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex}]
    Let a{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex}. Then the path difference between any two corresponding points M1 and M2 of the two halves of the slit will be p = {tex}\frac{a \theta}{2}=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}
    This means the contributions from M1 and M2 are 180o out of phase and cancel in the direction {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda} {a}{/tex}. Contributions from the two halves LM and MN cancel each other. Net intensity at P will be zero.
    {tex}\therefore \quad \theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} is the condition of first minimum.
    This is the half angular width of the central maximum.
    Total angular width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}
    Linear width of the central maximum on the screen at distance D = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex}.
  2. When size of aperture a >> {tex}\lambda{/tex}, we can ignore diffraction effects. Then the light appears to travel in straight lines and ray optics becomes valid. Since for the validity of ray optics a >> {tex}\lambda{/tex} or {tex}\lambda{/tex} << a or {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0, it implies that ray optics is a limiting case of wave optics.

question_html:
What will be the effect on the interference fringes in Young's double-slit experiment when,

  1. the width of the source slit is increased?
  2. the monochromatic sources is replaced by another monochromatic source of shorter wavelength?
  3. monochromatic source is replaced by a source of white light?
question_text: What will be the effect on the interference fringes in Young's double-slit experiment when, the width of the source slit is increased? the monochromatic sources is replaced by another monochromatic source of shorter wavelength? monochromatic source is replaced by a source of white light?
explanation: The interference pattern will become less and less sharp. Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} With monochromatic source of shorter wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} , fringe width {tex}\beta{/tex} decreases. The central fringe is white. The closest fringe on either side of white fringe is violet (smallest {tex}\lambda{/tex} ), while the farthest fringe is red (largest {tex}\lambda{/tex} ).
solution: The interference pattern will become less and less sharp. Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} With monochromatic source of shorter wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} , fringe width {tex}\beta{/tex} decreases. The central fringe is white. The closest fringe on either side of white fringe is violet (smallest {tex}\lambda{/tex} ), while the farthest fringe is red (largest {tex}\lambda{/tex} ).
solution_html:
1. The interference pattern will become less and less sharp.
  2. Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
    With monochromatic source of shorter wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}, fringe width {tex}\beta{/tex} decreases.
  3. The central fringe is white. The closest fringe on either side of white fringe is violet (smallest {tex}\lambda{/tex}), while the farthest fringe is red (largest {tex}\lambda{/tex}).

question_html: A parallel beam of light of wavelength 500 nm falls on a narrow slit and the resulting diffraction pattern is observed on a screen 1 m away. It is observed that the first minimum is at a distance of 2.5 mm from the centre of the screen. Find the width of the slit.
question_text: A parallel beam of light of wavelength 500 nm falls on a narrow slit and the resulting diffraction pattern is observed on a screen 1 m away. It is observed that the first minimum is at a distance of 2.5 mm from the centre of the screen. Find the width of the slit.
explanation: Given, D = 1 m, n = 1 x = 2.5 mm = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 500nm = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m Using formula, {tex}x = n\frac{{\lambda D}}{d}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow d = \frac{{n\lambda D}}{x}{/tex} or {tex}d = \frac{{1 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 1}}{{2.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}m{/tex} = 0.2 mm
solution: Given, D = 1 m, n = 1 x = 2.5 mm = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 500nm = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m Using formula, {tex}x = n\frac{{\lambda D}}{d}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow d = \frac{{n\lambda D}}{x}{/tex} or {tex}d = \frac{{1 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 1}}{{2.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}m{/tex} = 0.2 mm
solution_html:
Given, D = 1 m, n = 1
x = 2.5 mm = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 500nm = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9m = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m
Using formula, {tex}x = n\frac{{\lambda D}}{d}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow d = \frac{{n\lambda D}}{x}{/tex}
or {tex}d = \frac{{1 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 1}}{{2.5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}m{/tex} = 0.2 mm

question_html: Two monochromatic waves emanating from two coherent sources have the displacements represented by, y1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t and y2 = a cos {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex}, where {tex}\phi{/tex} is the phase difference between the two displacements. Show that the resultant intensity at a point due to their superposition is given by I = 4I0 cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi} {2}{/tex}, where I0 {tex}\propto{/tex} a2. Hence obtain the conditions for constructive and destructive interference.
question_text: Two monochromatic waves emanating from two coherent sources have the displacements represented by, y 1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t and y 2 = a cos {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex} , where {tex}\phi{/tex} is the phase difference between the two displacements. Show that the resultant intensity at a point due to their superposition is given by I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi} {2}{/tex} , where I 0 {tex}\propto{/tex} a 2 . Hence obtain the conditions for constructive and destructive interference.
explanation: By the principle of superposition, the resultant displacement at the observation point will be y = y 1 + y 2 = a [cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + cos {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex} ] = 2a cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}\cdot{/tex} cos {tex}\left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} Amplitude of the resultant displacement = 2a cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} Intensity {tex}\propto{/tex} (amplitude) 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Intensity, I = 4ka 2 cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} , where k = a proportionality constant. If I 0 is the intensity of each source, then I 0 = ka 2 and I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} For constructive interference: cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex} 1 or {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = n {tex}\pi{/tex} or {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2n {tex}\pi{/tex} For destructive interference : cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = (2n + 1) {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\phi{/tex} = (2n + 1) {tex}\pi{/tex}
solution: By the principle of superposition, the resultant displacement at the observation point will be y = y 1 + y 2 = a [cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + cos {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex} ] = 2a cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}\cdot{/tex} cos {tex}\left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} Amplitude of the resultant displacement = 2a cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} Intensity {tex}\propto{/tex} (amplitude) 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Intensity, I = 4ka 2 cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} , where k = a proportionality constant. If I 0 is the intensity of each source, then I 0 = ka 2 and I = 4I 0 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} For constructive interference: cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex} 1 or {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = n {tex}\pi{/tex} or {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2n {tex}\pi{/tex} For destructive interference : cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = (2n + 1) {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\phi{/tex} = (2n + 1) {tex}\pi{/tex}
solution_html:
By the principle of superposition, the resultant displacement at the observation point will be y = y1 + y2 = a [cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t + cos {tex}(\omega t+\phi){/tex}]
= 2a cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}\cdot{/tex} cos {tex}\left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex}
Amplitude of the resultant displacement = 2a cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Intensity {tex}\propto{/tex} (amplitude)2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Intensity, I = 4ka2 cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}, where k = a proportionality constant.
If I0 is the intensity of each source, then I0 = ka2 and I = 4I0 cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
For constructive interference:
cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\pm{/tex}1 or {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = n{tex}\pi{/tex} or {tex}\phi{/tex} = 2n{tex}\pi{/tex}
For destructive interference :
cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = (2n + 1) {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\phi{/tex} = (2n + 1){tex}\pi{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Two independent monochromatic sources of light cannot produce a sustained interference pattern. Give reason.
question_text: Two independent monochromatic sources of light cannot produce a sustained interference pattern. Give reason.
explanation: For producing sustained interference, the two sources should emit light waves of the same wavelength and continuously. Further, the phase difference between the light waves emitted by the two sources should not change with the passage of time. These conditions can never be fulfilled by two independent sources of light and hence they cannot be used to produce a sustained interference pattern.
solution: For producing sustained interference, the two sources should emit light waves of the same wavelength and continuously. Further, the phase difference between the light waves emitted by the two sources should not change with the passage of time. These conditions can never be fulfilled by two independent sources of light and hence they cannot be used to produce a sustained interference pattern.
solution_html: For producing sustained interference, the two sources should emit light waves of the same wavelength and continuously. Further, the phase difference between the light waves emitted by the two sources should not change with the passage of time. These conditions can never be fulfilled by two independent sources of light and hence they cannot be used to produce a sustained interference pattern.

question_html: A plane wavefront propagating in a medium of refractive index {tex}\mu_{1}{/tex} is incident on a plane surface making an angle of incidence i as shown in fig. It enters into a medium of refractive index {tex}{ }^{\prime} \mu_{2}{ }^{\prime}\left(\mu_{2}>\mu_{1}\right){/tex}. Use Huygens' construction of secondary wavelets to trace the propagation of the refracted wavefront. Hence, verify Snell's law of refraction.
question_text: A plane wavefront propagating in a medium of refractive index {tex}\mu_{1}{/tex} is incident on a plane surface making an angle of incidence i as shown in fig. It enters into a medium of refractive index {tex}{ }^{\prime} \mu_{2}{ }^{\prime}\left(\mu_{2}>\mu_{1}\right){/tex} . Use Huygens' construction of secondary wavelets to trace the propagation of the refracted wavefront. Hence, verify Snell's law of refraction.
explanation: Let PP' represents the surface separating medium 1 and medium 2 as shown in figure. Let v 1 and v 2 represents the speed of light in medium 1 and medium 2 respectively. We assume a plane wavefront AB propagating in the direction A'A incident on the interface at an angle of incidence i. Let t be the time taken by the wavefront to travel the distance BC in medium 1 and AE in medium 2 respectively. {tex}\therefore {/tex} BC = speed of light in medium 1 × time = {tex}v_1{/tex} t [ {tex}\because {/tex} distance = speed {tex} \times {/tex} time] In order to determine the shape of the refracted wavefront, we draw an arc of radius {tex}v_2{/tex} t from the point A in the second medium (the speed of the wave in second medium is {tex}v_2{/tex} and applying the formula, distance = speed × time). Let CE represents a tangent plane drawn from the point C. Then AE = speed of light in medium 2 × time = {tex}v_2{/tex} t {tex}\therefore {/tex} CE would represent the refracted wavefront. In {tex}\Delta A B C{/tex} and {tex}\Delta A E C{/tex} , we have {tex}\frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } t } { A C } \cdot \frac { A C } { v _ { 2 } t } ; \quad \therefore \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } } { v _ { 2 } }{/tex} If c represents the speed of light in vacuum, then {tex}\mu _ { 1 } = \frac { c } { v _ { 1 } } \text { and } \mu _ { 2 } = \frac { c } { v _ { 2 } }{/tex} [since the refractive index of a medium = speed of light in vacuum or air ÷ speed of light at that medium] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v _ { 1 } = \frac { c } { \mu _ { 1 } } \text { and } v _ { 2 } = \frac { c } { \mu _ { 2 } }{/tex} where, µ 1 and µ 2 are the refractive indices of medium 1 and medium 2 respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { c / \mu _ { 1 } } { c / \mu _ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } \Rightarrow \mu _ { 1 } \sin i = \mu _ { 2 } \sin r{/tex} This proves the snell's law of refraction.
solution: Let PP' represents the surface separating medium 1 and medium 2 as shown in figure. Let v 1 and v 2 represents the speed of light in medium 1 and medium 2 respectively. We assume a plane wavefront AB propagating in the direction A'A incident on the interface at an angle of incidence i. Let t be the time taken by the wavefront to travel the distance BC in medium 1 and AE in medium 2 respectively. {tex}\therefore {/tex} BC = speed of light in medium 1 × time = {tex}v_1{/tex} t [ {tex}\because {/tex} distance = speed {tex} \times {/tex} time] In order to determine the shape of the refracted wavefront, we draw an arc of radius {tex}v_2{/tex} t from the point A in the second medium (the speed of the wave in second medium is {tex}v_2{/tex} and applying the formula, distance = speed × time). Let CE represents a tangent plane drawn from the point C. Then AE = speed of light in medium 2 × time = {tex}v_2{/tex} t {tex}\therefore {/tex} CE would represent the refracted wavefront. In {tex}\Delta A B C{/tex} and {tex}\Delta A E C{/tex} , we have {tex}\frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } t } { A C } \cdot \frac { A C } { v _ { 2 } t } ; \quad \therefore \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } } { v _ { 2 } }{/tex} If c represents the speed of light in vacuum, then {tex}\mu _ { 1 } = \frac { c } { v _ { 1 } } \text { and } \mu _ { 2 } = \frac { c } { v _ { 2 } }{/tex} [since the refractive index of a medium = speed of light in vacuum or air ÷ speed of light at that medium] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v _ { 1 } = \frac { c } { \mu _ { 1 } } \text { and } v _ { 2 } = \frac { c } { \mu _ { 2 } }{/tex} where, µ 1 and µ 2 are the refractive indices of medium 1 and medium 2 respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { c / \mu _ { 1 } } { c / \mu _ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } \Rightarrow \mu _ { 1 } \sin i = \mu _ { 2 } \sin r{/tex} This proves the snell's law of refraction.
solution_html:
Let PP' represents the surface separating medium 1 and medium 2 as shown in figure.

Let v1 and v2 represents the speed of light in medium 1 and medium 2 respectively. We assume a plane wavefront AB propagating in the direction A'A incident on the interface at an angle of incidence i. Let t be the time taken by the wavefront to travel the distance BC in medium 1 and AE in medium 2 respectively.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} BC = speed of light in medium 1 × time = {tex}v_1{/tex}t [{tex}\because {/tex} distance = speed {tex} \times {/tex}  time]
In order to determine the shape of the refracted wavefront, we draw an arc of radius {tex}v_2{/tex}t from the point A in the second medium (the speed of the wave in second medium is {tex}v_2{/tex} and applying the formula, distance = speed × time). Let CE represents a tangent plane drawn from the point C.
Then AE = speed of light in medium 2 × time = {tex}v_2{/tex}t
{tex}\therefore {/tex} CE would represent the refracted wavefront.
In {tex}\Delta A B C{/tex} and {tex}\Delta A E C{/tex}, we have 
{tex}\frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } t } { A C } \cdot \frac { A C } { v _ { 2 } t } ; \quad \therefore \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } } { v _ { 2 } }{/tex}
If c represents the speed of light in vacuum, then
{tex}\mu _ { 1 } = \frac { c } { v _ { 1 } } \text { and } \mu _ { 2 } = \frac { c } { v _ { 2 } }{/tex} [since the refractive index of a medium = speed of light in vacuum or air ÷ speed of light at that medium]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad v _ { 1 } = \frac { c } { \mu _ { 1 } } \text { and } v _ { 2 } = \frac { c } { \mu _ { 2 } }{/tex}
where, µ1 and µ2 are the refractive indices of medium 1 and medium 2 respectively.
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { c / \mu _ { 1 } } { c / \mu _ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { \mu _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } \Rightarrow \mu _ { 1 } \sin i = \mu _ { 2 } \sin r{/tex}
This proves the snell's law of refraction.

subject: Physics
question_html: Why cannot two independent monochromatic sources produce sustained interference pattern? Explain.
question_text: Why cannot two independent monochromatic sources produce sustained interference pattern? Explain.
explanation: Two independent monochromatic sources of light cannot produce sustained interference because: If the sources are not coherent, they cannot emit waves continuously. Independent sources, emit the waves, which don't have same phase or a constant phase difference.
solution: Two independent monochromatic sources of light cannot produce sustained interference because: If the sources are not coherent, they cannot emit waves continuously. Independent sources, emit the waves, which don't have same phase or a constant phase difference.
solution_html:
Two independent monochromatic sources of light cannot produce sustained interference because:

  1. If the sources are not coherent, they cannot emit waves continuously.
  2. Independent sources, emit the waves, which don't have same phase or a constant phase difference.

subject: Physics
question_html: Using Huygens' construction, explain how a parallel beam of light on reflection from a concave mirror gets converged.
question_text: Using Huygens' construction, explain how a parallel beam of light on reflection from a concave mirror gets converged.
explanation: Consider that a plane wavefront AB is incident on a concave spherical mirror The lines LA, QO and MB (normals to the incident wavefront AB at the points A, O and B) represent the incident rays. Since the distance AX or BY is smaller than the distance OP, the disturbance will reach the points X and Y on the mirror earlier than it reaches the point P. Therefore, the instant, when the disturbance reaches the point P, the secondary wavelets from the points X and Y will grow into spheres of radii (OP - AX) and (OP - BY) respectively. At this instant, the point P on the mirror has just become the source of secondary wavelet and therefore, the secondary wavelet originating from the point P will be of zero radius at that instant. To find the reflected wavefront (new position of the wavefront after reflection from the concave mirror); with the points X and Y as centres, draw spheres of radii XA' = (OP - AX) and YB' = (OP - BY) respectively. Then, the sphere A'PB', the common envelope of the secondary wavelets issuing out from the points X, P and Y gives the reflected wavefront and the normals A'L', PQ' and B'M' to the reflected wavefront represent the reflected rays. It follows that a plane wavefront incident on a concave spherical mirror is reflected as a converging spherical wavefront. It, thus, explains that why a parallel beam of light on reflection from a concave mirror gets converged.
solution: Consider that a plane wavefront AB is incident on a concave spherical mirror The lines LA, QO and MB (normals to the incident wavefront AB at the points A, O and B) represent the incident rays. Since the distance AX or BY is smaller than the distance OP, the disturbance will reach the points X and Y on the mirror earlier than it reaches the point P. Therefore, the instant, when the disturbance reaches the point P, the secondary wavelets from the points X and Y will grow into spheres of radii (OP - AX) and (OP - BY) respectively. At this instant, the point P on the mirror has just become the source of secondary wavelet and therefore, the secondary wavelet originating from the point P will be of zero radius at that instant. To find the reflected wavefront (new position of the wavefront after reflection from the concave mirror); with the points X and Y as centres, draw spheres of radii XA' = (OP - AX) and YB' = (OP - BY) respectively. Then, the sphere A'PB', the common envelope of the secondary wavelets issuing out from the points X, P and Y gives the reflected wavefront and the normals A'L', PQ' and B'M' to the reflected wavefront represent the reflected rays. It follows that a plane wavefront incident on a concave spherical mirror is reflected as a converging spherical wavefront. It, thus, explains that why a parallel beam of light on reflection from a concave mirror gets converged.
solution_html:
Consider that a plane wavefront AB is incident on a concave spherical mirror The lines LA, QO and MB (normals to the incident wavefront AB at the points A, O and B) represent the incident rays.

Since the distance AX or BY is smaller than the distance OP, the disturbance will reach the points X and Y on the mirror earlier than it reaches the point P. Therefore, the instant, when the disturbance reaches the point P, the secondary wavelets from the points X and Y will grow into spheres of radii (OP - AX) and (OP - BY) respectively. At this instant, the point P on the mirror has just become the source of secondary wavelet and therefore, the secondary wavelet originating from the point P will be of zero radius at that instant.
To find the reflected wavefront (new position of the wavefront after reflection from the concave mirror); with the points X and Y as centres, draw spheres of radii XA' = (OP - AX) and YB' = (OP - BY) respectively. Then, the sphere A'PB', the common envelope of the secondary wavelets issuing out from the points X, P and Y gives the reflected wavefront and the normals A'L', PQ' and B'M' to the reflected wavefront represent the reflected rays. It follows that a plane wavefront incident on a concave spherical mirror is reflected as a converging spherical wavefront.
It, thus, explains that why a parallel beam of light on reflection from a concave mirror gets converged.

question_html:
Explain by drawing a suitable diagram that the interference pattern in a double-slit is actually a superposition of single-slit diffraction from each slit.
Write two basic features that distinguish the interference pattern from those seen in a coherently illuminated single slit.
question_text: Explain by drawing a suitable diagram that the interference pattern in a double-slit is actually a superposition of single-slit diffraction from each slit. Write two basic features that distinguish the interference pattern from those seen in a coherently illuminated single slit.
explanation: The diagram, given here, shows several fringes, due to double-slit interference, 'contained' in a broad diffraction peak. When the separation between the slits is large compared to their width, the diffraction pattern becomes very flat and we observe the two-slit interference pattern. Basic features that distinguish the interference pattern from those seen in a coherently illuminated single slit.: The interference pattern has a number of equally spaced bright and dark bands while the diffraction pattern has a central bright maxima which is twice as wide as the other maxima. Interference pattern is the superposition of two waves originating from two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is a superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit. For a single slit of width 'a' the first null of diffraction pattern occurs at an angle of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} . At the same angle of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} , we get a maximum for two narrow slits separated by a distance a.
solution: The diagram, given here, shows several fringes, due to double-slit interference, 'contained' in a broad diffraction peak. When the separation between the slits is large compared to their width, the diffraction pattern becomes very flat and we observe the two-slit interference pattern. Basic features that distinguish the interference pattern from those seen in a coherently illuminated single slit.: The interference pattern has a number of equally spaced bright and dark bands while the diffraction pattern has a central bright maxima which is twice as wide as the other maxima. Interference pattern is the superposition of two waves originating from two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is a superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit. For a single slit of width 'a' the first null of diffraction pattern occurs at an angle of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} . At the same angle of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} , we get a maximum for two narrow slits separated by a distance a.
solution_html:
The diagram, given here, shows several fringes, due to double-slit interference, 'contained' in a broad diffraction peak. When the separation between the slits is large compared to their width, the diffraction pattern becomes very flat and we observe the two-slit interference pattern.

Basic features that distinguish the interference pattern from those seen in a coherently illuminated single slit.:

  1. The interference pattern has a number of equally spaced bright and dark bands while the diffraction pattern has a central bright maxima which is twice as wide as the other maxima.
  2. Interference pattern is the superposition of two waves originating from two narrow slits. The diffraction pattern is a superposition of a continuous family of waves originating from each point on a single slit.
  3. For a single slit of width 'a' the first null of diffraction pattern occurs at an angle of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}. At the same angle of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}, we get a maximum for two narrow slits separated by a distance a.

question_html:

  2. Show that the angular width of first diffraction fringe is half that of the central fringe.
  3. Explain why the maxima at {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} become weaker and weaker with increasing n.
question_text: Using Huygens's construction of secondary wavelets explains how a diffraction pattern is obtained on a screen due to a narrow slit on which a monochromatic beam of light is incident normally. Show that the angular width of first diffraction fringe is half that of the central fringe. Explain why the maxima at {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} become weaker and weaker with increasing n.
explanation: We can regard the total contributions of the wavefront LN at some point P on the screen, as the resultant effect of the superposition of its wavelets like LM, MM 2 , M 2 N. These have to be superposed taking into account their proper phase differences. We, therefore, get maxima and minima, i.e., a diffraction pattern, on the screen. Maxima and minima are produced when the path difference between waves is a whole number of wavelengths or an odd number of half wavelengths respectively. Conditions for first minima on the screen asin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Angular width of the central fringe on the screen (from the figure) {tex}=2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} Angular width of first diffraction fringe (From fig) {tex}=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} For the first diffraction, the angular width of the fringe is half that of the central fringe. Maxima becomes weaker and weaker with increasing n. This is because the effective part of the wavefront, contributing to the maxima becomes smaller and smaller, with increasing n.

solution_html:
1. We can regard the total contributions of the wavefront LN at some point P on the screen, as the resultant effect of the superposition of its wavelets like LM, MM2, M2N. These have to be superposed taking into account their proper phase differences.

    We, therefore, get maxima and minima, i.e., a diffraction pattern, on the screen. Maxima and minima are produced when the path difference between waves is a whole number of wavelengths or an odd number of half wavelengths respectively.

  2. Conditions for first minima on the screen
    asin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\lambda{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Angular width of the central fringe on the screen (from the figure)
    {tex}=2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}
    Angular width of first diffraction fringe (From fig)
    {tex}=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
    For the first diffraction, the angular width of the fringe is half that of the central fringe.
  3. Maxima becomes weaker and weaker with increasing n. This is because the effective part of the wavefront, contributing to the maxima becomes smaller and smaller, with increasing n.

subject: Physics
question_html: When a parallel beam of a monochromatic source of light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident on a single slit of width a, show how the diffraction pattern is formed at the screen by the interference of the wavelets from the slit. Show that, besides the central maxima at {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0, secondary maxima are observed at {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} and the minima at {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex}. Why do secondary maxima get weaker in intensity with increasing n?
question_text: When a parallel beam of a monochromatic source of light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident on a single slit of width a, show how the diffraction pattern is formed at the screen by the interference of the wavelets from the slit. Show that, besides the central maxima at {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0, secondary maxima are observed at {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} and the minima at {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} . Why do secondary maxima get weaker in intensity with increasing n?
explanation: The diffraction pattern formed can be understood by adding the contributions from the different wavelets of the incident wavefront, with their proper phase differences. For the central point, we imagine the slit to be divided into two equal halves. The contribution of corresponding wavelets, in the two halves, are in phase with each other. Hence we get a maxima at the central point. The entire incident wavefront contributes to this maxima. Maxima and minima are produced when the path difference between waves is a whole number of wavelengths or an odd number of half wavelengths respectively. All other points, for which {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} , get a net non zero contribution from all the wavelets. Hence all such points are at the points of maxima. Points for which {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} , the net contribution, from all the wavelets, is zero. Hence these points are point of minima. We thus get a diffraction pattern on the screen, made up of points of maxima and minima. Secondary maxima keep on getting weaker in intensity, with increasing n. This is because, at the First secondary maxima, the net contribution is only from (effectively) {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} rd of the incident wavefront on the slit. Second secondary maxima, the net contribution is only from (effectively) {tex}\frac 15{/tex} th of the incident wavefront on the slit and so on.
solution: The diffraction pattern formed can be understood by adding the contributions from the different wavelets of the incident wavefront, with their proper phase differences. For the central point, we imagine the slit to be divided into two equal halves. The contribution of corresponding wavelets, in the two halves, are in phase with each other. Hence we get a maxima at the central point. The entire incident wavefront contributes to this maxima. Maxima and minima are produced when the path difference between waves is a whole number of wavelengths or an odd number of half wavelengths respectively. All other points, for which {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} , get a net non zero contribution from all the wavelets. Hence all such points are at the points of maxima. Points for which {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} , the net contribution, from all the wavelets, is zero. Hence these points are point of minima. We thus get a diffraction pattern on the screen, made up of points of maxima and minima. Secondary maxima keep on getting weaker in intensity, with increasing n. This is because, at the First secondary maxima, the net contribution is only from (effectively) {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} rd of the incident wavefront on the slit. Second secondary maxima, the net contribution is only from (effectively) {tex}\frac 15{/tex} th of the incident wavefront on the slit and so on.
solution_html:
1. The diffraction pattern formed can be understood by adding the contributions from the different wavelets of the incident wavefront, with their proper phase differences. For the central point, we imagine the slit to be divided into two equal halves. The contribution of corresponding wavelets, in the two halves, are in phase with each other. Hence we get a maxima at the central point. The entire incident wavefront contributes to this maxima. Maxima and minima are produced when the path difference between waves is a whole number of wavelengths or an odd number of half wavelengths respectively.
    All other points, for which {tex}\theta=\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}, get a net non zero contribution from all the wavelets. Hence all such points are at the points of maxima.
    Points for which {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex}, the net contribution, from all the wavelets, is zero. Hence these points are point of minima.
    We thus get a diffraction pattern on the screen, made up of points of maxima and minima.

    Secondary maxima keep on getting weaker in
    intensity, with increasing n. This is because, at the
  2. First secondary maxima, the net contribution is only from (effectively) {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}rd of the incident wavefront on the slit.
  3. Second secondary maxima, the net contribution is only from (effectively) {tex}\frac 15{/tex}th of the incident wavefront on the slit and so on.

question_html: If the two slits in Young's double-slit experiment have width ratio 4 : 1, deduce the ratio of intensity at maxima and minima in the interference pattern.
question_text: If the two slits in Young's double-slit experiment have width ratio 4 : 1, deduce the ratio of intensity at maxima and minima in the interference pattern.
explanation: Amplitude ratio of the interfering waves, r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{w_{1}}{w_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{1}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\therefore \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{r+1}{r-1}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{2+1}{2-1}\right)^{2}=\frac{9}{1}{/tex} = 9 : 1
solution: Amplitude ratio of the interfering waves, r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{w_{1}}{w_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{1}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\therefore \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{r+1}{r-1}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{2+1}{2-1}\right)^{2}=\frac{9}{1}{/tex} = 9 : 1
solution_html:
Amplitude ratio of the interfering waves,
r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{w_{1}}{w_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{1}}{/tex} = 2
{tex}\therefore \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{r+1}{r-1}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{2+1}{2-1}\right)^{2}=\frac{9}{1}{/tex} = 9 : 1

question_html: Estimate the angular separation between the first-order maximum and a third-order minimum of the diffraction pattern due to a single slit of width 1 mm, when the light of wavelength 600 nm is incident normal on it.
question_text: Estimate the angular separation between the first-order maximum and a third-order minimum of the diffraction pattern due to a single slit of width 1 mm, when the light of wavelength 600 nm is incident normal on it.
explanation: Angular position of nth maximum, {tex}\theta_{n}^{\prime}{/tex} = (2n + 1) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 a}{/tex} Angular position of first order maximum, {tex}\theta_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 a}{/tex} Angular position of nth minimum, {tex}\theta_{n}=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} Angular position of 3rd order minimum, {tex}\theta_{3}=\frac{3 \lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required angular separation {tex}=\theta_{3}-\theta_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 a}=\frac{3 \times 600 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 rad.
solution: Angular position of nth maximum, {tex}\theta_{n}^{\prime}{/tex} = (2n + 1) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 a}{/tex} Angular position of first order maximum, {tex}\theta_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 a}{/tex} Angular position of nth minimum, {tex}\theta_{n}=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex} Angular position of 3rd order minimum, {tex}\theta_{3}=\frac{3 \lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required angular separation {tex}=\theta_{3}-\theta_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 a}=\frac{3 \times 600 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 rad.
solution_html:
Angular position of nth maximum,
{tex}\theta_{n}^{\prime}{/tex} = (2n + 1){tex}\frac{\lambda}{2 a}{/tex}
Angular position of first order maximum, {tex}\theta_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 a}{/tex}
Angular position of nth minimum, {tex}\theta_{n}=\frac{n \lambda}{a}{/tex}
Angular position of 3rd order minimum, {tex}\theta_{3}=\frac{3 \lambda}{a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required angular separation
{tex}=\theta_{3}-\theta_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 a}=\frac{3 \times 600 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 rad.

subject: Physics
question_html: Does the appearance of bright and dark fringes in the interference pattern violate, in any way, the conservation of energy? Explain.
question_text: Does the appearance of bright and dark fringes in the interference pattern violate, in any way, the conservation of energy? Explain.
explanation: We know that the resultant intensity of the interference is I = 4a 2 cos 2 {tex}\frac{8}{2}{/tex} From the above equation it is clear that the intensity at bright points is 4a 2 and at dark points it is zero. According to the law of conservation of energy, the energy cannot be destroyed. Here also the energy is not destroyed but only transferred from the points of minimum intensity to the points of maximum intensity. Intensity varies from 0 to 4a 2 , and the average is still 2a 2 . It is equal to the uniform intensity 2a’ which will be present in the absence of the interference phenomenon due to the two waves. Therefore, the formation of interference fringes is in accordance with the law of conservation of energy.
solution: We know that the resultant intensity of the interference is I = 4a 2 cos 2 {tex}\frac{8}{2}{/tex} From the above equation it is clear that the intensity at bright points is 4a 2 and at dark points it is zero. Here also the energy is not destroyed but only transferred from the points of minimum intensity to the points of maximum intensity. Intensity varies from 0 to 4a 2 , and the average is still 2a 2 . It is equal to the uniform intensity 2a’ which will be present in the absence of the interference phenomenon due to the two waves. Therefore, the formation of interference fringes is in accordance with the law of conservation of energy.

According to the law of conservation of energy, the energy cannot be destroyed. Here also the energy is not destroyed but only transferred from the points of minimum intensity to the points of maximum intensity.
Intensity varies from 0 to 4a2, and the average is still 2a2. It is equal to the uniform intensity 2a’ which will be present in the absence of the interference phenomenon due to the two waves. Therefore, the formation of interference fringes is in accordance with the law of conservation of energy.

question_html: Define a wavefront.
question_text: Define a wavefront.
explanation: When light is emitted from a source, then the particles present around the source in all directions begins to vibrate. The locus of all such particles which are vibrating in the same phase is termed as wavefront.
solution: When light is emitted from a source, then the particles present around the source in all directions begins to vibrate. The locus of all such particles which are vibrating in the same phase is termed as wavefront.
solution_html: When light is emitted from a source, then the particles present around the source in all directions begins to vibrate. The locus of all such particles which are vibrating in the same phase is termed as wavefront.

question_html: Why does the intensity of the secondary maximum become less as compared to the central maximum?
question_text: Why does the intensity of the secondary maximum become less as compared to the central maximum?
explanation: The central maximum is due to the constructive interference of wavelets from all parts of the slit. With the increase in the value of n, the wavelets from lesser and lesser parts of the slit produce constructive interference to form a secondary maximum. Hence the intensity of secondary maximum decreases with the increase in the value of n.
solution: The central maximum is due to the constructive interference of wavelets from all parts of the slit. With the increase in the value of n, the wavelets from lesser and lesser parts of the slit produce constructive interference to form a secondary maximum. Hence the intensity of secondary maximum decreases with the increase in the value of n.
solution_html: The central maximum is due to the constructive interference of wavelets from all parts of the slit. With the increase in the value of n, the wavelets from lesser and lesser parts of the slit produce constructive interference to form a secondary maximum. Hence the intensity of secondary maximum decreases with the increase in the value of n.

question_html: Define the term 'coherent sources' which are required to produce an interference pattern in Young's double slit experiment.
question_text: Define the term 'coherent sources' which are required to produce an interference pattern in Young's double slit experiment.
explanation: Two monochromatic sources, which produce two different light waves, having a constant phase difference or a zero phase difference between each other are known as coherent sources.
solution: Two monochromatic sources, which produce two different light waves, having a constant phase difference or a zero phase difference between each other are known as coherent sources.
solution_html: Two monochromatic sources, which produce two different light waves, having a constant phase difference or a zero phase difference between each other are known as coherent sources.

subject: Physics
question_html: Show that maximum intensity in interference pattern is four times the intensity due to each slit. Hence show that interference involves only redistribution of energy.
question_text: Show that maximum intensity in interference pattern is four times the intensity due to each slit. Hence show that interference involves only redistribution of energy.
explanation: Suppose the amplitude of waves from each slit is a. Therefore, intensity due to each slit = a 2 . When interference is destructive, the resultant amplitude = a - a = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Minimum intensity = 0 When interference is constructive, the resultant amplitude = a + a = 2a {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum intensity = (2 a) 2 = 4 a 2 = 4 times the intensity due to each slit, which was to be proved. Also, average intensity in the interference pattern = {tex}\frac {0\ +\ 4\ a^2}{2}{/tex} = 2 a 2 = sum of intensities due to two slits. Hence we conclude that interference involves only redistribution of energy keeping the total energy fixed.
solution: Suppose the amplitude of waves from each slit is a. Therefore, intensity due to each slit = a 2 . When interference is destructive, the resultant amplitude = a - a = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Minimum intensity = 0 When interference is constructive, the resultant amplitude = a + a = 2a {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum intensity = (2 a) 2 = 4 a 2 = 4 times the intensity due to each slit, which was to be proved. Also, average intensity in the interference pattern = {tex}\frac {0\ +\ 4\ a^2}{2}{/tex} = 2 a 2 = sum of intensities due to two slits. Hence we conclude that interference involves only redistribution of energy keeping the total energy fixed.
solution_html:
Suppose the amplitude of waves from each slit is a.
Therefore, intensity due to each slit = a2.
When interference is destructive, the resultant amplitude = a - a = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Minimum intensity = 0
When interference is constructive, the resultant amplitude = a + a = 2a
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum intensity = (2 a)2 = 4 a2 = 4 times the intensity due to each slit, which was to be proved.
Also, average intensity in the interference pattern = {tex}\frac {0\ +\ 4\ a^2}{2}{/tex} = 2 a2 = sum of intensities due to two slits.
Hence we conclude that interference involves only redistribution of energy keeping the total energy fixed.

question_html: The ratio of the intensities at minima in the interference pattern is 9 : 25. What will be the ratio of the widths of the two slits in the Young's double slit experiment?
question_text: The ratio of the intensities at minima in the interference pattern is 9 : 25. What will be the ratio of the widths of the two slits in the Young's double slit experiment?
explanation: Intensity is proportional to width of slit. So, amplitude a 1 and a 2 is proportional to the square root of the width of the slit. {tex}\therefore \frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{W _1}}}{{{W _2}}}}{/tex} Here W 1 and W 2 represent the widths of the two slits. Now, {tex}\frac{{{I_{\min }}}}{{{I_{\max }}}} = \frac{{{{({a_1} - {a_2})}^2}}}{{{{({a_1} + {a_2})}^2}}} = \frac{{{{\left( {1 - \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {1 + \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9}{{25}} = \frac{{{{\left( {1 - \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {1 + \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{3}{5} = \frac{{1 - \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}}}{{1 + \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}}}{/tex} or {tex}8\frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}} = 2{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = 4{/tex} Thus, {tex}\sqrt {\frac{{{W _1}}}{{{W _2}}}} = \frac{4}{1}{/tex} or , {tex}\frac{{{W _1}}}{{{W _2}}} = \frac{{16}}{1} \Rightarrow {W _1}:{W _2}{/tex} = 16 : 1
solution: Intensity is proportional to width of slit. So, amplitude a 1 and a 2 is proportional to the square root of the width of the slit. {tex}\therefore \frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{W _1}}}{{{W _2}}}}{/tex} Here W 1 and W 2 represent the widths of the two slits. Now, {tex}\frac{{{I_{\min }}}}{{{I_{\max }}}} = \frac{{{{({a_1} - {a_2})}^2}}}{{{{({a_1} + {a_2})}^2}}} = \frac{{{{\left( {1 - \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {1 + \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9}{{25}} = \frac{{{{\left( {1 - \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {1 + \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{3}{5} = \frac{{1 - \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}}}{{1 + \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}}}{/tex} or {tex}8\frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}} = 2{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = 4{/tex} Thus, {tex}\sqrt {\frac{{{W _1}}}{{{W _2}}}} = \frac{4}{1}{/tex} or , {tex}\frac{{{W _1}}}{{{W _2}}} = \frac{{16}}{1} \Rightarrow {W _1}:{W _2}{/tex} = 16 : 1
solution_html:
Intensity is proportional to width of slit. So, amplitude a1 and a2 is proportional to the square root of the width of the slit.
{tex}\therefore \frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{W _1}}}{{{W _2}}}}{/tex}
Here W1 and W2 represent the widths of the two slits.
Now, {tex}\frac{{{I_{\min }}}}{{{I_{\max }}}} = \frac{{{{({a_1} - {a_2})}^2}}}{{{{({a_1} + {a_2})}^2}}} = \frac{{{{\left( {1 - \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {1 + \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9}{{25}} = \frac{{{{\left( {1 - \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {1 + \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}  or {tex}\frac{3}{5} = \frac{{1 - \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}}}{{1 + \frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}}}}{/tex}
or {tex}8\frac{{{a_2}}}{{{a_1}}} = 2{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{{{a_1}}}{{{a_2}}} = 4{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\sqrt {\frac{{{W _1}}}{{{W _2}}}} = \frac{4}{1}{/tex}
or , {tex}\frac{{{W _1}}}{{{W _2}}} = \frac{{16}}{1} \Rightarrow {W _1}:{W _2}{/tex} = 16 : 1

question_html: A parallel beam of monochromatic light falls normally on a narrow slit of width a to produce a diffraction pattern on the screen placed parallel to the plane of the slit. Use Huygens' principle to explain that the central bright maxima are twice as wide as the other maxima.
question_text: A parallel beam of monochromatic light falls normally on a narrow slit of width a to produce a diffraction pattern on the screen placed parallel to the plane of the slit. Use Huygens' principle to explain that the central bright maxima are twice as wide as the other maxima.
explanation: In diffraction patterns, the intensity will be minimum at an angle {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda} {a}{/tex} . So there will be the first minimum at an angle {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda} {a}{/tex} , on either side of the central maximum. Hence, the width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda} {a}{/tex} , whereas the width of secondary {tex}\frac{maxima}{minima}=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
solution: In diffraction patterns, the intensity will be minimum at an angle {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda} {a}{/tex} . So there will be the first minimum at an angle {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda} {a}{/tex} , on either side of the central maximum. Hence, the width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda} {a}{/tex} , whereas the width of secondary {tex}\frac{maxima}{minima}=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}

In diffraction patterns, the intensity will be minimum at an angle {tex}\theta=\frac{n \lambda} {a}{/tex}. So there will be the first minimum at an angle {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda} {a}{/tex}, on either side of the central maximum.
Hence, the width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda} {a}{/tex}, whereas the width of secondary {tex}\frac{maxima}{minima}=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Describe briefly how a diffraction pattern is obtained on a screen due to a single narrow slit illuminated by a monochromatic source of light. Hence, obtain the conditions for the angular width of secondary maxima and secondary minima.
  2. Two wavelengths of sodium light of 590 nm and 596 nm are used in turn to study the diffraction taking place at a single slit of aperture {tex}2 \times {10^{ - 6}}m{/tex}. The distance between the slit and the screen is 1.5m. Calculate the separation between the positions of first maxima of the diffraction pattern obtained in the two cases.
question_text: Describe briefly how a diffraction pattern is obtained on a screen due to a single narrow slit illuminated by a monochromatic source of light. Hence, obtain the conditions for the angular width of secondary maxima and secondary minima. Two wavelengths of sodium light of 590 nm and 596 nm are used in turn to study the diffraction taking place at a single slit of aperture {tex}2 \times {10^{ - 6}}m{/tex} . The distance between the slit and the screen is 1.5m. Calculate the separation between the positions of first maxima of the diffraction pattern obtained in the two cases.
explanation: A single narrow slit is illuminated by a monochromatic source of light. The diffraction pattern is obtained on the screen placed in front of the slits. There is a central bright region called as central maximum. All the waves reaching this region are in phase hence the intensity is maximum. On both side of central maximum, there are alternate dark and bright regions, the intensity becoming weaker away from the center. The intensity at any point P on the screen depends on the path difference between the waves arising from different parts of the wave-front at the slit. Diffraction of light at a single slit A parallel beam of light with a plane wavefront WW' is made to fall on a single slit AB. As width of the slit AB = dis of the order of wavelength of light, therefore, diffraction occurs on passing through the slit. The wavelets from the single wavefront reach the centre O on the screen in same phase and hence, interfere constructively to give central maximum (bright fringe). The diffraction pattern obtained on the screen consists of a central bright band having alternate dark and weak bright band of decreasing intensity on both sides. Consider a point P on the screen at which wavelets travelling in a direction making an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with CO are brought to focus by the lens. The wavelets from points A and B will have a path difference equal to BN. From the right angled {tex}\Delta A N B{/tex} , we have BN = AB sin {tex}\theta {/tex} or BN = d sin {tex}\theta {/tex} . To establish the condition for secondary minima, the slit is divided into 2,4,6... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from parts such that corresponding wavelets from successive regions interfere with path difference {tex}\lambda / 2{/tex} or for nth secondary minlmum.the slit can be divided into 2n equal parts. Hence, for nth secondary minimum, path difference= {tex}= d \sin \theta _ { n } = n \lambda{/tex} . or {tex}\sin \theta _ { n } = \frac { n \lambda } { d } ( n = 1,2,3 , \dots ){/tex} To establish the condition for secondary maxima, the slit is divided into 3,5,7... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from alternate regions interfere with path difference of A./2 or for nth secondary maximum, the slit can be divided into (2n+ 1)equal parts. Hence, for nth secondary maximum {tex}d \sin \theta _ { n } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3,...) For {tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 590 \mathrm { mm }{/tex} Location of 1 maxima {tex}y _ { 1 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex} If {tex}n = 1 \Rightarrow y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex} For {tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 596 \mathrm { nm }{/tex} Location of III maxima {tex}y _ { 2 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex} , if n = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow y _ { 2 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Path difference {tex}= y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D } { 2 d } \left( \lambda _ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 1 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \times 1.5 } { 2 \times 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } ( 596 - 590 ) \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} {tex}= 6.75 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
solution: A single narrow slit is illuminated by a monochromatic source of light. The diffraction pattern is obtained on the screen placed in front of the slits. There is a central bright region called as central maximum. All the waves reaching this region are in phase hence the intensity is maximum. On both side of central maximum, there are alternate dark and bright regions, the intensity becoming weaker away from the center. The intensity at any point P on the screen depends on the path difference between the waves arising from different parts of the wave-front at the slit. Diffraction of light at a single slit A parallel beam of light with a plane wavefront WW' is made to fall on a single slit AB. As width of the slit AB = dis of the order of wavelength of light, therefore, diffraction occurs on passing through the slit. The wavelets from the single wavefront reach the centre O on the screen in same phase and hence, interfere constructively to give central maximum (bright fringe). The diffraction pattern obtained on the screen consists of a central bright band having alternate dark and weak bright band of decreasing intensity on both sides. Consider a point P on the screen at which wavelets travelling in a direction making an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with CO are brought to focus by the lens. The wavelets from points A and B will have a path difference equal to BN. From the right angled {tex}\Delta A N B{/tex} , we have BN = AB sin {tex}\theta {/tex} or BN = d sin {tex}\theta {/tex} . To establish the condition for secondary minima, the slit is divided into 2,4,6... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from parts such that corresponding wavelets from successive regions interfere with path difference {tex}\lambda / 2{/tex} or for nth secondary minlmum.the slit can be divided into 2n equal parts. Hence, for nth secondary minimum, path difference= {tex}= d \sin \theta _ { n } = n \lambda{/tex} . or {tex}\sin \theta _ { n } = \frac { n \lambda } { d } ( n = 1,2,3 , \dots ){/tex} To establish the condition for secondary maxima, the slit is divided into 3,5,7... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from alternate regions interfere with path difference of A./2 or for nth secondary maximum, the slit can be divided into (2n+ 1)equal parts. Hence, for nth secondary maximum {tex}d \sin \theta _ { n } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3,...) For {tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 590 \mathrm { mm }{/tex} Location of 1 maxima {tex}y _ { 1 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex} If {tex}n = 1 \Rightarrow y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex} For {tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 596 \mathrm { nm }{/tex} Location of III maxima {tex}y _ { 2 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex} , if n = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow y _ { 2 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Path difference {tex}= y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D } { 2 d } \left( \lambda _ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 1 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \times 1.5 } { 2 \times 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } ( 596 - 590 ) \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} {tex}= 6.75 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. A single narrow slit is illuminated by a monochromatic source of light. The diffraction pattern is obtained on the screen placed in front of the slits. There is a central bright region called as central maximum. All the waves reaching this region are in phase hence the intensity is maximum. On both side of central maximum, there are alternate dark and bright regions, the intensity becoming weaker away from the center. The intensity at any point P on the screen depends on the path difference between the waves arising from different parts of the wave-front at the slit.
    Diffraction of light at a single slit A parallel beam of light with a plane wavefront WW' is made to fall on a single slit AB. As width of the slit AB = dis of the order of wavelength of light, therefore, diffraction occurs on passing through the slit.

    The wavelets from the single wavefront reach the centre O on the screen in same phase and hence, interfere constructively to give central maximum (bright fringe).
    The diffraction pattern obtained on the screen consists of a central bright band having alternate dark and weak bright band of decreasing intensity on both sides.
    Consider a point P on the screen at which wavelets travelling in a direction making an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with CO are brought to focus by the lens. The wavelets from points A and B will have a path difference equal to BN.
    From the right angled {tex}\Delta A N B{/tex}, we have BN = AB sin{tex}\theta {/tex} or BN = d sin{tex}\theta {/tex}.
    To establish the condition for secondary minima, the slit is divided into 2,4,6... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from parts such that corresponding wavelets from successive regions interfere with path difference {tex}\lambda / 2{/tex} 
    or for nth secondary minlmum.the slit can be divided into 2n equal parts. Hence, for nth secondary minimum, path difference= {tex}= d \sin \theta _ { n } = n \lambda{/tex}.
    or {tex}\sin \theta _ { n } = \frac { n \lambda } { d } ( n = 1,2,3 , \dots ){/tex}
    To establish the condition for secondary maxima, the slit is divided into 3,5,7... equal parts such that corresponding wavelets from alternate regions interfere with path difference of A./2 or for nth secondary maximum, the slit can be divided into (2n+ 1)equal parts.
    Hence, for nth secondary maximum
    {tex}d \sin \theta _ { n } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 }{/tex} (n = 1, 2, 3,...)
  2. For {tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 590 \mathrm { mm }{/tex}
    Location of 1 maxima {tex}y _ { 1 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex}
    If {tex}n = 1 \Rightarrow y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 1 } } { 2 d }{/tex}
    For {tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 596 \mathrm { nm }{/tex}
    Location of III maxima 
    {tex}y _ { 2 } = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex}, if n = 1
    {tex}\Rightarrow y _ { 2 } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 d }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Path difference {tex}= y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } = \frac { 3 D } { 2 d } \left( \lambda _ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 1 } \right){/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 3 \times 1.5 } { 2 \times 2 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } } ( 596 - 590 ) \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex}
    {tex}= 6.75 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}

question_html:
Light waves each of amplitude a and frequency {tex}\omega{/tex}, emanating from two coherent light sources superpose at a point. If the displacements due to these waves is given by
y1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t and y2 = a cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex}),
where {tex}\phi{/tex} is the phase difference between the two. Obtain the expression for the resultant intensity at the point.
question_text: Light waves each of amplitude a and frequency {tex}\omega{/tex} , emanating from two coherent light sources superpose at a point. If the displacements due to these waves is given by y 1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t and y 2 = a cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ), where {tex}\phi{/tex} is the phase difference between the two. Obtain the expression for the resultant intensity at the point.
explanation: The displacements due to the waves are given y 1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t and y 2 = a cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) The resultant displacement is given by y = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + a cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) = a[cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) + cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t] = {tex}a\left[2 \cos \frac{(\omega t+\phi)+\omega t}{2} \cos \frac{(\omega t+\phi)-\omega t}{2}\right]{/tex} = {tex}2a \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right) \cos \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = {tex}A \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} where A = 2a cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} is the amplitude of the resultant wave. The intensity of light is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude of the resultant wave i.e. I = A 2 or I = 4a 2 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
solution: The displacements due to the waves are given y 1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t and y 2 = a cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) The resultant displacement is given by y = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + a cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) = a[cos ( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\phi{/tex} ) + cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t] = {tex}a\left[2 \cos \frac{(\omega t+\phi)+\omega t}{2} \cos \frac{(\omega t+\phi)-\omega t}{2}\right]{/tex} = {tex}2a \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right) \cos \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} = {tex}A \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex} where A = 2a cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} is the amplitude of the resultant wave. The intensity of light is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude of the resultant wave i.e. I = A 2 or I = 4a 2 cos 2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
The displacements due to the waves are given
y1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t and y2 = a cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex})
The resultant displacement is given by
y = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t + a cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex})
= a[cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex})  + cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t]
= {tex}a\left[2 \cos \frac{(\omega t+\phi)+\omega t}{2} \cos \frac{(\omega t+\phi)-\omega t}{2}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}2a \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right) \cos \frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}A \cos \left(\omega t+\frac{\phi}{2}\right){/tex}
where A = 2a cos {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex} is the amplitude of the resultant wave.
The intensity of light is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude of the resultant wave i.e.
I = A2
or I = 4a2 cos2 {tex}\frac{\phi}{2}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Write three characteristic features to distinguish between the interference fringes in Young's double-slit experiment and the diffraction pattern obtained due to a narrow single slit.
  2. A parallel beam of light of wavelength 500 nm falls on a narrow slit and the resulting diffraction pattern is observed on a screen 1 m away. It is observed that the first minima are at a distance of 2.5 mm away from the centre. Find the width of the slit.
question_text: Write three characteristic features to distinguish between the interference fringes in Young's double-slit experiment and the diffraction pattern obtained due to a narrow single slit. A parallel beam of light of wavelength 500 nm falls on a narrow slit and the resulting diffraction pattern is observed on a screen 1 m away. It is observed that the first minima are at a distance of 2.5 mm away from the centre. Find the width of the slit.
explanation: S.No Interference Diffraction 1. The width of central maxima is the same as that of the other fringes. The width of central maxima is more than of the other fringes. 2. All bright fringes are of equal intensity. The intensity of secondary maxima keeps on decreasing. 3. A large number of fringes. Only a small number of fringes. {tex}y_{n} =\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex} , {tex}\lambda=500 \times10^{-9}{/tex} m, D=1m.and y n = 2.5 x 10 -3 m thus the width of the slit is given by:- {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d =\frac{n \lambda D}{y_{n}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore d=\frac{1 \times 500 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} \mathrm{m} {/tex} d = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m = 0.2 mm
solution: S.No Interference Diffraction 1. The width of central maxima is the same as that of the other fringes. The width of central maxima is more than of the other fringes. 2. All bright fringes are of equal intensity. The intensity of secondary maxima keeps on decreasing. 3. A large number of fringes. Only a small number of fringes. {tex}y_{n} =\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex} , {tex}\lambda=500 \times10^{-9}{/tex} m, D=1m.and y n = 2.5 x 10 -3 m thus the width of the slit is given by:- {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d =\frac{n \lambda D}{y_{n}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore d=\frac{1 \times 500 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} \mathrm{m} {/tex} d = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m = 0.2 mm
solution_html:
1. S.No Interference Diffraction
    1. The width of central maxima is the same as that of the other fringes. The width of central maxima is more than of the other fringes.
    2. All bright fringes are of equal intensity. The intensity of secondary maxima keeps on decreasing.
    3. A large number of fringes. Only a small number of fringes.
  2. {tex}y_{n} =\frac{n \lambda D}{d}{/tex}  , {tex}\lambda=500 \times10^{-9}{/tex} m, D=1m.and yn = 2.5 x 10-3 m  thus the width of the slit is given by:-
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d =\frac{n \lambda D}{y_{n}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore d=\frac{1 \times 500 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} \mathrm{m} {/tex}
    d = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m = 0.2 mm

    2. Light waves each of amplitude a and frequency {tex}\omega{/tex}, emanating from two coherent light sources superimpose at a point If the displacements due to these waves are given by y1 = a cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t and y2 = cos ({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\phi{/tex}), where {tex}\phi{/tex} is the phase difference between the two, obtain the expression for the resultant intensity at the point
  2. In Young’s double-slit experiment using monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}, the intensity of light at a point on the screen where path difference is {tex}\lambda{/tex}, is K units.
