question_text: The intensity at the central maxima (O) in a Young's double slit setup in Fig. is I 0 . If the distance OP equals one-third of fringe width of the pattern, then show that the intensity at point P would be {tex}\frac{I_0}{4}{/tex} .
explanation: Given, {tex}OP = y_ n{/tex} The distance OP equals one-third of fringe width of the pattern i.e. y n = {tex} \frac { \beta } { 3 } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( \frac { D \lambda } { d } \right) {/tex} {tex}(\because \beta= \frac { D \lambda } { d } ){/tex} {tex}= \frac { D \lambda } { 3 d } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y _ { n } } { D } = \frac { \lambda } { 3 }{/tex} Path difference, S 2 P - S 1 P {tex} = \frac { d y _ { n } } { D } = \frac { \lambda } { 3 }{/tex} we know that , Phase difference {tex}= \frac { 2 \pi } { \lambda } \times{/tex} Path difference {tex}= \frac { 2 \pi } { \lambda } \times \frac { \lambda } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Phase difference {tex} = \frac{{2\pi }}{3}{/tex} . Intensity at point P is given by I = I 0 cos 2 {tex}\phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = I 0 (cos {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} ) 2 = I 0 (-cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} ) 2 = I 0 {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{I_{0}}{4}{/tex} Hence, the intensity at point P would be {tex}\frac{{{I_0}}}{4}{/tex} .
solution: Given, {tex}OP = y_ n{/tex} The distance OP equals one-third of fringe width of the pattern i.e. y n = {tex} \frac { \beta } { 3 } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( \frac { D \lambda } { d } \right) {/tex} {tex}(\because \beta= \frac { D \lambda } { d } ){/tex} {tex}= \frac { D \lambda } { 3 d } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y _ { n } } { D } = \frac { \lambda } { 3 }{/tex} Path difference, S 2 P - S 1 P {tex} = \frac { d y _ { n } } { D } = \frac { \lambda } { 3 }{/tex} we know that , Phase difference {tex}= \frac { 2 \pi } { \lambda } \times{/tex} Path difference {tex}= \frac { 2 \pi } { \lambda } \times \frac { \lambda } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Phase difference {tex} = \frac{{2\pi }}{3}{/tex} . Intensity at point P is given by I = I 0 cos 2 {tex}\phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = I 0 (cos {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} ) 2 = I 0 (-cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} ) 2 = I 0 {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{I_{0}}{4}{/tex} Hence, the intensity at point P would be {tex}\frac{{{I_0}}}{4}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given, {tex}OP = y_ n{/tex}
The distance OP equals one-third of fringe width of the pattern
i.e. yn = {tex} \frac { \beta } { 3 } {/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 3 } \left( \frac { D \lambda } { d } \right) {/tex}{tex}(\because \beta= \frac { D \lambda } { d } ){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { D \lambda } { 3 d } {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y _ { n } } { D } = \frac { \lambda } { 3 }{/tex}
Path difference, S2P - S1P {tex} = \frac { d y _ { n } } { D } = \frac { \lambda } { 3 }{/tex}
we know that ,
Phase difference {tex}= \frac { 2 \pi } { \lambda } \times{/tex} Path difference
 {tex}= \frac { 2 \pi } { \lambda } \times \frac { \lambda } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Phase difference {tex} = \frac{{2\pi }}{3}{/tex}.
Intensity at point P is given by
I = I0 cos2 {tex}\phi{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = I0 (cos {tex}\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex})2
= I0 (-cos {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex})2 = I0 {tex}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{I_{0}}{4}{/tex}
Hence, the intensity at point P would be {tex}\frac{{{I_0}}}{4}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. A plane wavefront approaches a plane surface separating two media. If medium 1 is optically denser and medium 2 is optically rarer, using Huygens' principle, explain and show how a refracted wavefront is constructed? Verify Snell's law.
  2. When a light wave travels from a rarer to a denser medium, the speed decreases. Does it imply a reduction in its energy? Explain.
question_text: A plane wavefront approaches a plane surface separating two media. If medium 1 is optically denser and medium 2 is optically rarer, using Huygens' principle, explain and show how a refracted wavefront is constructed? Verify Snell's law. When a light wave travels from a rarer to a denser medium, the speed decreases. Does it imply a reduction in its energy? Explain.
explanation: Suppose a plane wavefront AB in first medium is incident obliquely on the boundary surface XY and its end A touches the surface at A' at time t = 0 while the other end B reaches the surface at point B' after time-interval t. As the wavefront AB advances, it strikes the points between A and B' of boundary surface. According to Huygen’s principle, secondary spherical wavelets originate from these points. First of all secondary wavelet starts from A, which traverses a distance {tex}AA' =v_2t{/tex} in second medium in time t. In the same time-interval t, the point of wavefront traverses a distance {tex}BB' =v_1t{/tex} in first medium and reaches B', from, where the secondary wavelet now starts. Assuming A as centre, draw an arc of radius {tex}AA' =v_2t{/tex} and draw tangent B' A' on this arc from B'. As the incident wavefront AB advances, the secondary wavelets start from points between A and B', one after the other and will touch A' B' simultaneously. According to Huygen’s principle A' B' is the new position of wavefront AB in the second medium. As the lines drawn normal to wavefront denote the rays, therefore we may say that the incident ray, refracted ray and the normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane. Let the incident wavefront AB and refracted wavefront A' B' make angles i and r respectively with refracting surface XY. Now, in {tex}\Delta A B B'{/tex} , {tex}\sin i = \frac {B B'} {A B} =\frac { v _ { 1 } t}{ A B'} {/tex} In {tex}\Delta A A'B' ,{/tex} {tex} \sin r = \frac{AA'}{AB'}={\frac { v _ { 2 } t } { A B' } }{/tex} Dividing equation (i) by (ii) , we get : {tex}\frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } t / A B' } { v _ { 2 } t / A B' }{/tex} {tex}\frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } } { v _ { 2 } } = \text { constant } = {^1\mu _ { 2 }}{/tex} where, {tex}^{1\mu _ { 2 }}{/tex} = refractive index of second medium w.r.t. first medium. Hence, Snell's law of refraction is verified. No, the energy carried by the wave does not depend on its speed instead, it depends on the frequency of the wave.
solution: Suppose a plane wavefront AB in first medium is incident obliquely on the boundary surface XY and its end A touches the surface at A' at time t = 0 while the other end B reaches the surface at point B' after time-interval t. As the wavefront AB advances, it strikes the points between A and B' of boundary surface. According to Huygen’s principle, secondary spherical wavelets originate from these points. First of all secondary wavelet starts from A, which traverses a distance {tex}AA' =v_2t{/tex} in second medium in time t. In the same time-interval t, the point of wavefront traverses a distance {tex}BB' =v_1t{/tex} in first medium and reaches B', from, where the secondary wavelet now starts. Assuming A as centre, draw an arc of radius {tex}AA' =v_2t{/tex} and draw tangent B' A' on this arc from B'. As the incident wavefront AB advances, the secondary wavelets start from points between A and B', one after the other and will touch A' B' simultaneously. According to Huygen’s principle A' B' is the new position of wavefront AB in the second medium. As the lines drawn normal to wavefront denote the rays, therefore we may say that the incident ray, refracted ray and the normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane. Let the incident wavefront AB and refracted wavefront A' B' make angles i and r respectively with refracting surface XY. Now, in {tex}\Delta A B B'{/tex} , {tex}\sin i = \frac {B B'} {A B} =\frac { v _ { 1 } t}{ A B'} {/tex} In {tex}\Delta A A'B' ,{/tex} {tex} \sin r = \frac{AA'}{AB'}={\frac { v _ { 2 } t } { A B' } }{/tex} Dividing equation (i) by (ii) , we get : {tex}\frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } t / A B' } { v _ { 2 } t / A B' }{/tex} {tex}\frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } } { v _ { 2 } } = \text { constant } = {^1\mu _ { 2 }}{/tex} where, {tex}^{1\mu _ { 2 }}{/tex} = refractive index of second medium w.r.t. first medium. Hence, Snell's law of refraction is verified. No, the energy carried by the wave does not depend on its speed instead, it depends on the frequency of the wave.
solution_html:
1. Suppose a plane wavefront AB in first medium is incident obliquely on the boundary surface XY and its end A touches the surface at A' at time t = 0 while the other end B reaches the surface at point B' after time-interval t.

    As the wavefront AB advances, it strikes the points between A and B' of boundary surface. According to Huygen’s principle, secondary spherical wavelets originate from these points. First of all secondary wavelet starts from A, which traverses a distance {tex}AA' =v_2t{/tex} in second medium in time t. In the same time-interval t, the point of wavefront traverses a distance {tex}BB' =v_1t{/tex} in first medium and reaches B', from, where the secondary wavelet now starts. Assuming A as centre, draw an arc of radius {tex}AA' =v_2t{/tex} and draw tangent B' A' on this arc from B'. As the incident wavefront AB advances, the secondary wavelets start from points between A and B', one after the other and will touch A' B' simultaneously. According to Huygen’s principle A' B' is the new position of wavefront AB in the second medium.
    As the lines drawn normal to wavefront denote the rays, therefore we may say that the incident ray, refracted ray and the normal at the point of incidence all lie in the same plane.
    Let the incident wavefront AB and refracted wavefront A' B' make angles i and r respectively with refracting surface XY. 
    Now, in {tex}\Delta A B B'{/tex},
    {tex}\sin i = \frac {B B'} {A B} =\frac { v _ { 1 } t}{ A B'} {/tex}
    In {tex}\Delta A A'B' ,{/tex}
    {tex} \sin r = \frac{AA'}{AB'}={\frac { v _ { 2 } t } { A B' } }{/tex}
    Dividing equation (i) by (ii) , we get :
    {tex}\frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } t / A B' } { v _ { 2 } t / A B' }{/tex}
    {tex}\frac { \sin i } { \sin r } = \frac { v _ { 1 } } { v _ { 2 } } = \text { constant } = {^1\mu _ { 2 }}{/tex}
    where, {tex}^{1\mu _ { 2 }}{/tex} = refractive index of second medium w.r.t. first medium. Hence, Snell's law of refraction is verified.
  2. No, the energy carried by the wave does not depend on its speed instead, it depends on the frequency of the wave.

subject: Physics
question_html: How will the intensity of maxima and minima, in the Young's double experiment change, if one of the two slits is covered by a transparent paper which transmits only half of light intensity?
question_text: How will the intensity of maxima and minima, in the Young's double experiment change, if one of the two slits is covered by a transparent paper which transmits only half of light intensity?
explanation: If one of the slit transmits only half of the light intensity, then the intensity in the interference pattern will vary from maximum to minimum but not become zero. Therefore contrast will be reduced & the interference pattern will become less sharp. So we can say that the intensity of maxima decreases and the intensity of minima increases.
solution: If one of the slit transmits only half of the light intensity, then the intensity in the interference pattern will vary from maximum to minimum but not become zero. Therefore contrast will be reduced & the interference pattern will become less sharp. So we can say that the intensity of maxima decreases and the intensity of minima increases.
solution_html: If one of the slit transmits only half of the light intensity, then the intensity in the interference pattern will vary from maximum to minimum but not become zero. Therefore contrast will be reduced & the interference pattern will become less sharp. So we can say that the intensity of maxima decreases and the intensity of minima increases.

question_html: When monochromatic light travels from one medium to another its wavelength changes but the frequency remains the same. Explain.
question_text: When monochromatic light travels from one medium to another its wavelength changes but the frequency remains the same. Explain.
explanation: Frequency is the characteristic of the source while wavelength is the characteristic of the medium. When monochromatic light travels from one medium to another, its speed changes, so its wavelength {tex}(\lambda=\frac{c}{\nu}){/tex} changes but frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} remains unchanged.
solution: Frequency is the characteristic of the source while wavelength is the characteristic of the medium. When monochromatic light travels from one medium to another, its speed changes, so its wavelength {tex}(\lambda=\frac{c}{\nu}){/tex} changes but frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} remains unchanged.
solution_html: Frequency is the characteristic of the source while wavelength is the characteristic of the medium. When monochromatic light travels from one medium to another, its speed changes, so its wavelength {tex}(\lambda=\frac{c}{\nu}){/tex}changes but frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} remains unchanged.

question_text: What is the effect on the interference fringes in Young's double-slit experiment if the screen is moved away from the plane of the slits?
explanation: As fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} , so as the screen is moved away from the slits (D increases), fringe width increases.
solution: As fringe width {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} , so as the screen is moved away from the slits (D increases), fringe width increases.

subject: Physics
question_html: In a single slit diffraction experiment, when a tiny circular obstacle is placed in the path of light from a distant source, a bright spot is seen at the centre of the shadow of the obstacle. Explain, why?
question_text: In a single slit diffraction experiment, when a tiny circular obstacle is placed in the path of light from a distant source, a bright spot is seen at the centre of the shadow of the obstacle. Explain, why?
explanation: The bright spot seen at the centre of the shadow of the obstacle is due to constructive interference of light waves diffracted from the edge of the circular obstacle.
solution: The bright spot seen at the centre of the shadow of the obstacle is due to constructive interference of light waves diffracted from the edge of the circular obstacle.
solution_html: The bright spot seen at the centre of the shadow of the obstacle is due to constructive interference of light waves diffracted from the edge of the circular obstacle.

subject: Physics
question_html: When light travels from a rarer to a denser medium, the speed decreases. Does this decrease in speed imply a decrease in the energy carried by the light wave? Justify your answer.
question_text: When light travels from a rarer to a denser medium, the speed decreases. Does this decrease in speed imply a decrease in the energy carried by the light wave? Justify your answer.
explanation: The energy of a photon of light is given by, E = hν, where ‘h’ is Planck’s constant and ‘ν’ is the frequency. When there is a change in medium the frequency of light remains constant and the wavelength of light changes. Thus, the energy of light remains same when it moves from one medium to another.
solution: The energy of a photon of light is given by, E = hν, where ‘h’ is Planck’s constant and ‘ν’ is the frequency. When there is a change in medium the frequency of light remains constant and the wavelength of light changes. Thus, the energy of light remains same when it moves from one medium to another.
solution_html:
The energy of a photon of light is given by, E = hν, where ‘h’ is Planck’s constant and ‘ν’ is the frequency.
When there is a change in medium the frequency of light remains constant and the wavelength of light changes. Thus, the energy of light remains same when it moves from one medium to another.

subject: Physics
question_html: A beam of light consisting of two wavelengths 560 nm and 420 nm is used to obtain interference fringes in a Young's double slit experiment. Find the least distance from the central maximum, where the bright fringes, due to both the wavelengths coincide. The distance between the two slits is 4.0 mm and the screen is at a distance of 1.0 m from the slits.
question_text: A beam of light consisting of two wavelengths 560 nm and 420 nm is used to obtain interference fringes in a Young's double slit experiment. Find the least distance from the central maximum, where the bright fringes, due to both the wavelengths coincide. The distance between the two slits is 4.0 mm and the screen is at a distance of 1.0 m from the slits.
explanation: Here it is given that, D = distance of screen from the slits = 1 m , d = distance between two slits {tex} = 4 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m } {/tex} {tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 560 \mathrm { nm }{/tex} and {tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 420 \mathrm { nm }{/tex} Let n th order bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _1}{/tex} coincides with {tex} (n + 1)^{th }{/tex} order bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { D n \lambda _ { 1 } } { d } = \frac { D ( n + 1 ) \lambda _ { 2 } } { d }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad n \lambda _ { 1 } = ( n + 1 ) \lambda _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { n + 1 } { n } = \frac { \lambda _ { 1 } } { \lambda _ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}1 + \frac { 1 } { n } = \frac { 560 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 420 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } \Rightarrow 1 + \frac { 1 } { n } = \frac { 4 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { n } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow n = 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} The Least distance from the central fringe where bright fringe of two wavelengths coincides = Distance of 3rd order bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y _ { n } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 1 } } { d } = \frac { 3 \times 1 \times 560 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 4 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } }{/tex} {tex}y _ { n } = 420 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m } = 0.42 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y _ { n } = 0.42 \mathrm { mm }{/tex} Thus, 3 rd bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _1}{/tex} and 4 th bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _2}{/tex} coincide at 0.42 mm from central fringe.
solution: Here it is given that, D = distance of screen from the slits = 1 m , d = distance between two slits {tex} = 4 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m } {/tex} {tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 560 \mathrm { nm }{/tex} and {tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 420 \mathrm { nm }{/tex} Let n th order bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _1}{/tex} coincides with {tex} (n + 1)^{th }{/tex} order bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { D n \lambda _ { 1 } } { d } = \frac { D ( n + 1 ) \lambda _ { 2 } } { d }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad n \lambda _ { 1 } = ( n + 1 ) \lambda _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { n + 1 } { n } = \frac { \lambda _ { 1 } } { \lambda _ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}1 + \frac { 1 } { n } = \frac { 560 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 420 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } \Rightarrow 1 + \frac { 1 } { n } = \frac { 4 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { n } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow n = 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} The Least distance from the central fringe where bright fringe of two wavelengths coincides = Distance of 3rd order bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y _ { n } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 1 } } { d } = \frac { 3 \times 1 \times 560 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 4 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } }{/tex} {tex}y _ { n } = 420 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m } = 0.42 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad y _ { n } = 0.42 \mathrm { mm }{/tex} Thus, 3 rd bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _1}{/tex} and 4 th bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _2}{/tex} coincide at 0.42 mm from central fringe.
solution_html:
Here it is given that,
D = distance of screen from the slits = 1 m ,
d = distance between two slits {tex} = 4 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m } {/tex} 
{tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 560 \mathrm { nm }{/tex} and {tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 420 \mathrm { nm }{/tex}
Let nth order bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _1}{/tex} coincides with {tex} (n + 1)^{th }{/tex} order bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { D n \lambda _ { 1 } } { d } = \frac { D ( n + 1 ) \lambda _ { 2 } } { d }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad n \lambda _ { 1 } = ( n + 1 ) \lambda _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { n + 1 } { n } = \frac { \lambda _ { 1 } } { \lambda _ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}1 + \frac { 1 } { n } = \frac { 560 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 420 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } \Rightarrow 1 + \frac { 1 } { n } = \frac { 4 } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { n } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow n = 3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex}The Least distance from the central fringe where bright fringe of two wavelengths coincides
= Distance of 3rd order bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y _ { n } = \frac { 3 D \lambda _ { 1 } } { d } = \frac { 3 \times 1 \times 560 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 4 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } }{/tex}
{tex}y _ { n } = 420 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m } = 0.42 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y _ { n } = 0.42 \mathrm { mm }{/tex}
Thus, 3rd bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _1}{/tex} and 4th bright fringe of {tex}{\lambda _2}{/tex}  coincide at 0.42 mm from central fringe.

question_html: For a given single slit, the diffraction pattern is obtained on a fixed screen, first by using red light and then with blue light. In which case, will the central maxima, in the observed diffraction pattern, have a larger angular width?
question_text: For a given single slit, the diffraction pattern is obtained on a fixed screen, first by using red light and then with blue light. In which case, will the central maxima, in the observed diffraction pattern, have a larger angular width?
explanation: Angular width of central maxima is given by {tex}2 \theta = \frac { 2 \lambda } { a }{/tex} Since {tex}\lambda _ { r } ^ { \prime } > \lambda _ { b }{/tex} , the wavelength of red colour is greater than that of blue colour. So, the angular width of red colour is large.
solution: Angular width of central maxima is given by {tex}2 \theta = \frac { 2 \lambda } { a }{/tex} Since {tex}\lambda _ { r } ^ { \prime } > \lambda _ { b }{/tex} , the wavelength of red colour is greater than that of blue colour. So, the angular width of red colour is large.
solution_html:
Angular width of central maxima is given by {tex}2 \theta = \frac { 2 \lambda } { a }{/tex} 
Since {tex}\lambda _ { r } ^ { \prime } > \lambda _ { b }{/tex}, the wavelength of red colour is greater than that of blue colour.
So, the angular width of red colour is large.

question_html:
In Young's double-slit experiment, the two slits 0.15 mm apart are illuminated by monochromatic light of wavelength 450 nm. The screen is 1.0 m away from the slits. Find the distance of the second

  1. bright fringe,
  2. dark fringe from the maximum.
question_text: In Young's double-slit experiment, the two slits 0.15 mm apart are illuminated by monochromatic light of wavelength 450 nm. The screen is 1.0 m away from the slits. Find the distance of the second bright fringe, dark fringe from the maximum.
explanation: Distance of the second bright fringe from the central maximum, x 2 = {tex}\frac{2 D \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 \times 1.0 \times 450 \times 10^{-9}}{0.15 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} m = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 6 mm Distance of the second dark fringe from the central maximum, {tex}x_{2}^{\prime}{/tex} = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 1) {tex}\frac{D \lambda}{2 d}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{3}{2} \times{/tex} 3 mm = 4.5 mm
solution: Distance of the second bright fringe from the central maximum, x 2 = {tex}\frac{2 D \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 \times 1.0 \times 450 \times 10^{-9}}{0.15 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} m = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 6 mm Distance of the second dark fringe from the central maximum, {tex}x_{2}^{\prime}{/tex} = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 1) {tex}\frac{D \lambda}{2 d}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{3}{2} \times{/tex} 3 mm = 4.5 mm
solution_html:
1. Distance of the second bright fringe from the central maximum,
    x2 = {tex}\frac{2 D \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 \times 1.0 \times 450 \times 10^{-9}}{0.15 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} m
    = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m = 6 mm
  2. Distance of the second dark fringe from the central maximum,
    {tex}x_{2}^{\prime}{/tex} = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 - 1) {tex}\frac{D \lambda}{2 d}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{3}{2} \times{/tex} 3 mm
    = 4.5 mm

subject: Physics
question_html: Diffraction is common in sound but not common in light waves? Why?
question_text: Diffraction is common in sound but not common in light waves? Why?
explanation: The diffraction effect is more pronounced if the size of the obstacle or aperture is of the order of the wavelength of the waves. As the wavelength of light 10 -6 m is much smaller than the size of the objects around us, so diffraction of light is not easily seen. But sound waves have a large wavelength. They get easily diffracted by the objects around us.
solution: The diffraction effect is more pronounced if the size of the obstacle or aperture is of the order of the wavelength of the waves. As the wavelength of light 10 -6 m is much smaller than the size of the objects around us, so diffraction of light is not easily seen. But sound waves have a large wavelength. They get easily diffracted by the objects around us.
solution_html: The diffraction effect is more pronounced if the size of the obstacle or aperture is of the order of the wavelength of the waves. As the wavelength of light 10-6 m is much smaller than the size of the objects around us, so diffraction of light is not easily seen. But sound waves have a large wavelength. They get easily diffracted by the objects around us.

subject: Physics
question_html: Laser light of wavelength 630 nm incident on a pair of slits produces an interference pattern in which the bright fringes are separated by 8.1 mm. A second light produces an interference pattern in which the fringes are separated by 7.2 mm Calculate the wavelength of the second light.
question_text: Laser light of wavelength 630 nm incident on a pair of slits produces an interference pattern in which the bright fringes are separated by 8.1 mm. A second light produces an interference pattern in which the fringes are separated by 7.2 mm Calculate the wavelength of the second light.
explanation: Here {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} = 630 nm, {tex}\beta_{1}{/tex} = 8.1 nm, {tex}\beta_{2}{/tex} = 7.2 mm, {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} = ? Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} For constant D and d, {tex}\beta \propto \lambda{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda_{2}=\frac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}} \times \lambda_{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{7.2 \mathrm{~mm}}{8.1 \mathrm{~mm}} \times{/tex} 630 nm = 560 nm
solution: Here {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} = 630 nm, {tex}\beta_{1}{/tex} = 8.1 nm, {tex}\beta_{2}{/tex} = 7.2 mm, {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} = ? Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} For constant D and d, {tex}\beta \propto \lambda{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda_{2}=\frac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}} \times \lambda_{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{7.2 \mathrm{~mm}}{8.1 \mathrm{~mm}} \times{/tex} 630 nm = 560 nm
solution_html:
Here {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} = 630 nm, {tex}\beta_{1}{/tex} = 8.1 nm,
{tex}\beta_{2}{/tex} = 7.2 mm, {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} = ?
Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
For constant D and d, {tex}\beta \propto \lambda{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lambda_{2}=\frac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}} \times \lambda_{1}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7.2 \mathrm{~mm}}{8.1 \mathrm{~mm}} \times{/tex} 630 nm = 560 nm

subject: Physics
question_html:
State Huygens' postulates of wave theory. Sketch the wavefront emerging from a

  1. point source of light and
  2. linear source of light like a slit.
question_text: State Huygens' postulates of wave theory. Sketch the wavefront emerging from a point source of light and linear source of light like a slit.
explanation: The postulates of Huygens's wave theory are: Every particle of the medium situated on the wave-front acts as a new wave-source from which fresh waves originates. These waves are called secondary wavelets. The secondary wavelets travel in the medium in all directions with the speed of the original wave in the medium.
solution: The postulates of Huygens's wave theory are: Every particle of the medium situated on the wave-front acts as a new wave-source from which fresh waves originates. These waves are called secondary wavelets. The secondary wavelets travel in the medium in all directions with the speed of the original wave in the medium.
solution_html: The postulates of Huygens's wave theory are: Every particle of the medium situated on the wave-front acts as a new wave-source from which fresh waves originates. These waves are called secondary wavelets. The secondary wavelets travel in the medium in all directions with the speed of the original wave in the medium.

subject: Physics
question_html:
What type of wavefront will emerge from a

  1. point source
  2. distant light source?
question_text: What type of wavefront will emerge from a point source distant light source?
explanation: When source of light is a point source, the wavefront is spherical. Example - Light coming from an electric bulb. At very large distances from the source, a portion of spherical or cylindrical wavefront appears to be plane wavefront. Example - Light coming from the sun.
solution: When source of light is a point source, the wavefront is spherical. Example - Light coming from an electric bulb. At very large distances from the source, a portion of spherical or cylindrical wavefront appears to be plane wavefront. Example - Light coming from the sun.
solution_html:
1. When source of light is a point source, the wavefront is spherical. Example - Light coming from an electric bulb.
  2. At very large distances from the source, a portion of spherical or cylindrical wavefront appears to be plane wavefront. Example - Light coming from the sun.

question_html: Differentiate between a ray and a wavefront.
question_text: Differentiate between a ray and a wavefront.
explanation: A wavefront is a surface through the points, having the same phase of disturbances. While a ray of light is a path along which light travels. It is always perpendicular to the wavefront and in outward direction from the source.
solution: A wavefront is a surface through the points, having the same phase of disturbances. While a ray of light is a path along which light travels. It is always perpendicular to the wavefront and in outward direction from the source.

question_html: In Young's double slit experiment, monochromatic light of wavelength 630 nm illuminates the pair of slits and produces an interference pattern in which two consecutive bright fringes are separated by 8.1 mm. Another source of monochromatic light produces the interference pattern in which the two consecutive bright fringes are separated by 7.2 mm. Find the wavelength of light from the second source. What is the effect on the interference fringes, when the monochromatic source is replaced by a source of white light?
question_text: In Young's double slit experiment, monochromatic light of wavelength 630 nm illuminates the pair of slits and produces an interference pattern in which two consecutive bright fringes are separated by 8.1 mm. Another source of monochromatic light produces the interference pattern in which the two consecutive bright fringes are separated by 7.2 mm. Find the wavelength of light from the second source. What is the effect on the interference fringes, when the monochromatic source is replaced by a source of white light?
explanation: Here, we are given young's double slit experiment. Wavelength of monochromatic light, {tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 630 \mathrm { nm } = 630 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Fringe width, {tex}\beta _ { 1 } = 8.1 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Fringe width, {tex}\beta _ { 2 } = 7.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Let d be the slit width and D the distance between slit and screen, then we have {tex}\beta = D \lambda / d{/tex} For given Young's double slit experiment, D and d are constants {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \beta _ { 1 }}{ \beta _ { 2 }} = \frac {\lambda _ { 1 } }{ \lambda _ { 2 }}{/tex} Wavelength of light from the second source {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda _ { 2 } = \frac { \beta _ { 2 } } { \beta _ { 1 } } \times \lambda _ { 1 } = \frac { 7.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 8.1 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } \times 630 \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 8 } { 9 } \times 630 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } = 560 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 560 \mathrm { nm }{/tex} The coloured fringe pattern would be obtained if monochromatic light is replaced by white light. Because if the monochromatic source is replaced by white light, then we will not be able to see the interference fringes because white light is not a coherent source of light.
solution: Here, we are given young's double slit experiment. Wavelength of monochromatic light, {tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 630 \mathrm { nm } = 630 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Fringe width, {tex}\beta _ { 1 } = 8.1 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Fringe width, {tex}\beta _ { 2 } = 7.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} Let d be the slit width and D the distance between slit and screen, then we have {tex}\beta = D \lambda / d{/tex} For given Young's double slit experiment, D and d are constants {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \beta _ { 1 }}{ \beta _ { 2 }} = \frac {\lambda _ { 1 } }{ \lambda _ { 2 }}{/tex} Wavelength of light from the second source {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda _ { 2 } = \frac { \beta _ { 2 } } { \beta _ { 1 } } \times \lambda _ { 1 } = \frac { 7.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 8.1 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } \times 630 \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 8 } { 9 } \times 630 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } = 560 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 560 \mathrm { nm }{/tex} The coloured fringe pattern would be obtained if monochromatic light is replaced by white light. Because if the monochromatic source is replaced by white light, then we will not be able to see the interference fringes because white light is not a coherent source of light.
solution_html:
Here, we are given young's double slit experiment.
Wavelength of monochromatic light, {tex}\lambda _ { 1 } = 630 \mathrm { nm } = 630 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
Fringe width, {tex}\beta _ { 1 } = 8.1 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
Fringe width, {tex}\beta _ { 2 } = 7.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
Let d be the slit width and D the distance between slit and screen, then we have 
{tex}\beta = D \lambda / d{/tex}
For given Young's double slit experiment, D and d are constants
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { \beta _ { 1 }}{ \beta _ { 2 }} = \frac {\lambda _ { 1 } }{ \lambda _ { 2 }}{/tex}
Wavelength of light from the second source
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda _ { 2 } = \frac { \beta _ { 2 } } { \beta _ { 1 } } \times \lambda _ { 1 } = \frac { 7.2 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } { 8.1 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } } \times 630 \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 8 } { 9 } \times 630 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } = 560 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
{tex}\lambda _ { 2 } = 560 \mathrm { nm }{/tex}
The coloured fringe pattern would be obtained if monochromatic light is replaced by white light. Because if the monochromatic source is replaced by white light, then we will not be able to see the interference fringes because white light is not a coherent source of light.

question_html:
The figure shows a double slit experimental set up for observing interference fringes due to different interference component colours of white light. What would be the predominant colour of the fringes observed at the point

  1. O (the central point)
  2. P, where, S2 P - S1P =  {tex}\frac {\lambda _b}{2}{/tex} (Here, {tex}{\lambda _b}{/tex} is the wavelength of the blue colour).
question_text: The figure shows a double slit experimental set up for observing interference fringes due to different interference component colours of white light. What would be the predominant colour of the fringes observed at the point O (the central point) P, where, S 2 P - S 1 P = {tex}\frac {\lambda _b}{2}{/tex} (Here, {tex}{\lambda _b}{/tex} is the wavelength of the blue colour).
explanation: At point O, white colour fringe is obtained. As all components of colour wavelength undergo constructive interference fringe pattern . In the second case, yellow colour fringe would be obtained at P because the light of blue colour interfere destructively and hence this colour would subtract from white light .
solution: At point O, white colour fringe is obtained. As all components of colour wavelength undergo constructive interference fringe pattern . In the second case, yellow colour fringe would be obtained at P because the light of blue colour interfere destructively and hence this colour would subtract from white light .
solution_html:
1. At point O, white colour fringe is obtained. As all components of colour wavelength undergo constructive interference fringe pattern .
  2. In the second case, yellow colour fringe would be obtained at P because the light of blue colour interfere destructively and hence this colour would subtract from white light .

question_html: In a single slit diffraction experiment, a monochromatic source of light of wavelength {tex} \lambda {/tex} illuminates a narrow slit of width 'a'. Show giving appropriate reasoning, that the half angular width of the central maximum in the observed pattern is (nearly) equal to {tex} \frac{\lambda }{a}{/tex}.
question_text: In a single slit diffraction experiment, a monochromatic source of light of wavelength {tex} \lambda {/tex} illuminates a narrow slit of width 'a'. Show giving appropriate reasoning, that the half angular width of the central maximum in the observed pattern is (nearly) equal to {tex} \frac{\lambda }{a}{/tex} .
explanation: Here, intensity of central maxima will be maximum and that of other will gradually decrease, on this basis graph will be drawn. For central bright fringe, {tex}\theta = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} For first dark fringe, {tex}a \sin \theta = \pm \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta = \pm \frac { \lambda } { a }{/tex} If {tex}\theta {/tex} is small, then {tex}\sin \theta \approx \theta{/tex} So, {tex}\theta = \pm \frac { \lambda } { a }{/tex} Since the diffraction pattern is obtained by splitting plane parallel waves into two equal halves. Central maxima is having secondary maxima and minima on both sides of central maxima i.e. on the positive and negative halves. So, the half angular width of central maximum is {tex}\theta = \sin \theta = \frac { \lambda } { a }{/tex}
solution: Here, intensity of central maxima will be maximum and that of other will gradually decrease, on this basis graph will be drawn. For central bright fringe, {tex}\theta = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} For first dark fringe, {tex}a \sin \theta = \pm \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta = \pm \frac { \lambda } { a }{/tex} If {tex}\theta {/tex} is small, then {tex}\sin \theta \approx \theta{/tex} So, {tex}\theta = \pm \frac { \lambda } { a }{/tex} Since the diffraction pattern is obtained by splitting plane parallel waves into two equal halves. Central maxima is having secondary maxima and minima on both sides of central maxima i.e. on the positive and negative halves. So, the half angular width of central maximum is {tex}\theta = \sin \theta = \frac { \lambda } { a }{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, intensity of central maxima will be maximum and that of other will gradually decrease, on this basis graph will be drawn.
For central bright fringe, {tex}\theta = 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
For first dark fringe, {tex}a \sin \theta = \pm \lambda{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta = \pm \frac { \lambda } { a }{/tex}
If {tex}\theta {/tex} is small, then {tex}\sin \theta \approx \theta{/tex}
So, {tex}\theta = \pm \frac { \lambda } { a }{/tex}

Since the diffraction pattern is obtained by splitting plane parallel waves into two equal halves.
Central maxima is having secondary maxima and minima on both sides of central maxima i.e. on the positive and negative halves. 
So, the half angular width of central maximum is {tex}\theta = \sin \theta = \frac { \lambda } { a }{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: In a single slit diffraction experiment, when a tiny circular obstacle is placed in the path of light from a distant source, a bright spot is seen at the centre of the shadow of the obstacle. Explain why? State two points of difference between the interference pattern obtained in Young's double slit experiment and the diffraction pattern due to a single slit?
question_text: In a single slit diffraction experiment, when a tiny circular obstacle is placed in the path of light from a distant source, a bright spot is seen at the centre of the shadow of the obstacle. Explain why? State two points of difference between the interference pattern obtained in Young's double slit experiment and the diffraction pattern due to a single slit?
explanation: When a tiny circular obstacle is placed in the path of light from a distant source, a bright spot is seen at the center of the shadow of the obstacle. This is because light waves are diffracted from the edge of the circular obstacle, which interferes constructively at the center of the shadow. This constructive interference produces a bright spot. Difference between Interference and Diffraction: Interference occurs due to the superposition of two distinct waves coming from two coherent sources of light. The diffraction occurs as a result of the secondary wavelets coming from different parts of the same wavefront. In the pattern of interference, all the bright fringes have the same intensity. In a diffraction pattern, all the bright fringes are not of the same intensity.
solution: When a tiny circular obstacle is placed in the path of light from a distant source, a bright spot is seen at the center of the shadow of the obstacle. This is because light waves are diffracted from the edge of the circular obstacle, which interferes constructively at the center of the shadow. This constructive interference produces a bright spot. Difference between Interference and Diffraction: Interference occurs due to the superposition of two distinct waves coming from two coherent sources of light. The diffraction occurs as a result of the secondary wavelets coming from different parts of the same wavefront. In the pattern of interference, all the bright fringes have the same intensity. In a diffraction pattern, all the bright fringes are not of the same intensity.
solution_html:
When a tiny circular obstacle is placed in the path of light from a distant source, a bright spot is seen at the center of the shadow of the obstacle. This is because light waves are diffracted from the edge of the circular obstacle, which interferes constructively at the center of the shadow. This constructive interference produces a bright spot.

Difference between Interference and Diffraction: 

  1. Interference occurs due to the superposition of two distinct waves coming from two coherent sources of light. The diffraction occurs as a result of the secondary wavelets coming from different parts of the same wavefront.
  2. In the pattern of interference, all the bright fringes have the same intensity. In a diffraction pattern, all the bright fringes are not of the same intensity.

question_html:
In a single slit diffraction experiment, a slit of width d is illuminated by red light of wavelength 650 nm. For what value of d will

  1. the first minimum fall is at an angle of diffraction of 30°and
  2. the first maximum fall is at an angle of diffraction of 30°?
question_text: In a single slit diffraction experiment, a slit of width d is illuminated by red light of wavelength 650 nm. For what value of d will the first minimum fall is at an angle of diffraction of 30°and the first maximum fall is at an angle of diffraction of 30°?
explanation: In single slit diffraction pattern, first minimum occurs at {tex}d \sin \theta = \lambda{/tex} [ {tex}\theta {/tex} and {tex}\lambda{/tex} are diffraction angle and wavelength of the light used] {tex}\therefore {/tex} Slit width, {tex}d = \frac { \lambda } { \sin \theta }{/tex} ....(a) Given, {tex}\lambda = 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m } ~and~ \theta = 30 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Now from equation (a) we get slit width, {tex}d = \frac { 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { \sin 30 ^ { \circ } } = \frac { 650 } { ( 1 / 2 ) } \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} {tex}= 1300 \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} m {tex}\therefore d = 1.3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m } = 1.3 ~\mu m{/tex} In single slit diffraction pattern, maximum and minima occurs as per the below diagram - Now for first maximum, {tex}d \sin \theta = \frac { 3 \lambda } { 2 } \left[using,~ d \sin \theta = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 } \right]{/tex} where, n = 1(for first maximum) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d = \frac { 3 \lambda } { 2 \sin \theta }{/tex} where, {tex}\theta = 30 ^ { \circ } , \lambda = 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}\therefore d = \frac { 3 \lambda } { 2 \sin \theta }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \times 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 2 \times \sin 30 ^ { \circ } }{/tex} {tex}= 1950 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}\therefore d = 1.95 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m } = 1.95~\mu m{/tex}
solution: In single slit diffraction pattern, first minimum occurs at {tex}d \sin \theta = \lambda{/tex} [ {tex}\theta {/tex} and {tex}\lambda{/tex} are diffraction angle and wavelength of the light used] {tex}\therefore {/tex} Slit width, {tex}d = \frac { \lambda } { \sin \theta }{/tex} ....(a) Given, {tex}\lambda = 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m } ~and~ \theta = 30 ^ { \circ }{/tex} Now from equation (a) we get slit width, {tex}d = \frac { 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { \sin 30 ^ { \circ } } = \frac { 650 } { ( 1 / 2 ) } \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} {tex}= 1300 \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} m {tex}\therefore d = 1.3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m } = 1.3 ~\mu m{/tex} In single slit diffraction pattern, maximum and minima occurs as per the below diagram - Now for first maximum, {tex}d \sin \theta = \frac { 3 \lambda } { 2 } \left[using,~ d \sin \theta = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 } \right]{/tex} where, n = 1(for first maximum) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d = \frac { 3 \lambda } { 2 \sin \theta }{/tex} where, {tex}\theta = 30 ^ { \circ } , \lambda = 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}\therefore d = \frac { 3 \lambda } { 2 \sin \theta }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \times 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 2 \times \sin 30 ^ { \circ } }{/tex} {tex}= 1950 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}\therefore d = 1.95 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m } = 1.95~\mu m{/tex}
solution_html:
1. In single slit diffraction pattern, first minimum occurs at {tex}d \sin \theta = \lambda{/tex} [{tex}\theta {/tex} and {tex}\lambda{/tex} are diffraction angle and wavelength of the light used]
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Slit width, {tex}d = \frac { \lambda } { \sin \theta }{/tex}....(a)
    Given, {tex}\lambda = 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m } ~and~ \theta = 30 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
    Now from equation (a) we get slit width, {tex}d = \frac { 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { \sin 30 ^ { \circ } } = \frac { 650 } { ( 1 / 2 ) } \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex}
    {tex}= 1300 \times 10 ^ { - 9 }{/tex} m
    {tex}\therefore d = 1.3 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m } = 1.3 ~\mu m{/tex}
  2. In single slit diffraction pattern, maximum and minima occurs as per the below diagram -

    Now for first maximum,
    {tex}d \sin \theta = \frac { 3 \lambda } { 2 } \left[using,~ d \sin \theta = ( 2 n + 1 ) \frac { \lambda } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
    where, n = 1(for first maximum)
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d = \frac { 3 \lambda } { 2 \sin \theta }{/tex}
    where, {tex}\theta = 30 ^ { \circ } , \lambda = 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore d = \frac { 3 \lambda } { 2 \sin \theta }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 3 \times 650 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } } { 2 \times \sin 30 ^ { \circ } }{/tex}
    {tex}= 1950 \times 10 ^ { - 9 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore d = 1.95 \times 10 ^ { - 6 } \mathrm { m } = 1.95~\mu m{/tex}

1. Why do we not encounter diffraction effects of light in everyday observations?
  2. In the observed diffraction pattern due to a single slit, how will the width of central maximum be affected, if
    1. the width of the slit is doubled
    2. the wavelength of the light used is increased?
      Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: Why do we not encounter diffraction effects of light in everyday observations? In the observed diffraction pattern due to a single slit, how will the width of central maximum be affected, if the width of the slit is doubled the wavelength of the light used is increased? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: We do not encounter diffraction effects of light in everyday observations. To observe diffraction, size of obstacle/aperture must be comparable with wavelength of light but in daily observations size of obstacle/aperture is much larger than the wavelength of light. The angular width of central maxima is given by, {tex}\theta = 2 \lambda d{/tex} The width of central maxima is reduced to half when slit width, d is doubled Increases with the increase of wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} .
solution: We do not encounter diffraction effects of light in everyday observations. To observe diffraction, size of obstacle/aperture must be comparable with wavelength of light but in daily observations size of obstacle/aperture is much larger than the wavelength of light. The angular width of central maxima is given by, {tex}\theta = 2 \lambda d{/tex} The width of central maxima is reduced to half when slit width, d is doubled Increases with the increase of wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. We do not encounter diffraction effects of light in everyday observations. To observe diffraction, size of obstacle/aperture must be comparable with wavelength of light but in daily observations size of obstacle/aperture is much larger than the wavelength of light. 
  2. The angular width of central maxima is given by, {tex}\theta = 2 \lambda d{/tex}
    The width of central maxima is
    1. reduced to half when slit width, d is doubled
    2. Increases with the increase of wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex}.

question_text: What is meant by a wavefront? What is the shape of the wavefront of a beam of parallel rays?
explanation: The locus of all the particles of the medium, which at any instant are vibrating in the same phase, is called the wavefront. Corresponding to a beam of parallel rays of light, the wavefront is plane in shape.
solution: The locus of all the particles of the medium, which at any instant are vibrating in the same phase, is called the wavefront. Corresponding to a beam of parallel rays of light, the wavefront is plane in shape.

  1. the first minimum fall at an angle of diffraction of 30oand

question_text: A slit of width 'a' is illuminated by red light of wavelength 6500 {tex}\overset \circ{A}{/tex} . For what value of 'a' will the first minimum fall at an angle of diffraction of 30 o and the first maximum fall at an angle of diffraction of 30 o ?
explanation: For first minimum of the diffraction pattern, a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore a=\frac{\lambda}{\sin \theta}=\frac{6,500 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6,500 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}}{0.5}{/tex} = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m For the first secondary maximum of the diffraction pattern, a sin {tex}\theta=\frac{3 \lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore a=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 \sin \theta}=\frac{3 \times 6,500 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}}{2 \times \sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex} = 1.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m
solution: For first minimum of the diffraction pattern, a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore a=\frac{\lambda}{\sin \theta}=\frac{6,500 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6,500 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}}{0.5}{/tex} = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m For the first secondary maximum of the diffraction pattern, a sin {tex}\theta=\frac{3 \lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore a=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 \sin \theta}=\frac{3 \times 6,500 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}}{2 \times \sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex} = 1.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m
solution_html:
1. For first minimum of the diffraction pattern,
    a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore a=\frac{\lambda}{\sin \theta}=\frac{6,500 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6,500 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}}{0.5}{/tex} = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6m
  2. For the first secondary maximum of the diffraction pattern,
    a sin {tex}\theta=\frac{3 \lambda}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore a=\frac{3 \lambda}{2 \sin \theta}=\frac{3 \times 6,500 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}}{2 \times \sin 30^{\circ}}{/tex}
    = 1.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6m

question_html:
Draw the diagram showing the intensity distribution of light on the screen for diffraction of light at a single slit. How is the width of central maxima affected if

  1. the width of the slit is doubled ;
  2. the wavelength of the light used is increased?

What happens to the width of the central maxima if the whole apparatus is immersed in water and why?
question_text: Draw the diagram showing the intensity distribution of light on the screen for diffraction of light at a single slit. How is the width of central maxima affected if the width of the slit is doubled ; the wavelength of the light used is increased? What happens to the width of the central maxima if the whole apparatus is immersed in water and why?
explanation: For intensity distribution of light in diffraction at a signal slit, Width of the central maximum is given by {tex}\beta_{0}=\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex} When wavelength {tex}(\lambda){/tex} of light used is increased, the width of central maximum increases. When width (a) of the slit is increased, the width of central maximum decreases. Wavelength of light in water decreases, so width of central maximum also decreases.
solution: For intensity distribution of light in diffraction at a signal slit, Width of the central maximum is given by {tex}\beta_{0}=\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex} When wavelength {tex}(\lambda){/tex} of light used is increased, the width of central maximum increases. When width (a) of the slit is increased, the width of central maximum decreases. Wavelength of light in water decreases, so width of central maximum also decreases.
solution_html:
For intensity distribution of light in diffraction at a signal slit,

Width of the central maximum is given by {tex}\beta_{0}=\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex}

  1. When wavelength {tex}(\lambda){/tex} of light used is increased, the width of central maximum increases.
  2. When width (a) of the slit is increased, the width of central maximum decreases.
    Wavelength of light in water decreases, so width of central maximum also decreases.

question_html: How does the fringe width of interference fringes change, when whole apparatus of Young's experiment is kept in a liquid of refraction index 1.3?
question_text: How does the fringe width of interference fringes change, when whole apparatus of Young's experiment is kept in a liquid of refraction index 1.3?
explanation: The fringe width in a Young's double slit experiment is given by {tex}\omega = \frac{{\lambda D}}{d}{/tex} When light enters a denser medium, its wavelength decreases by a factor 1.3 and hence the fringe width also decreases by a factor 1.3. Therefore, the fringe width will decrease.
solution: The fringe width in a Young's double slit experiment is given by {tex}\omega = \frac{{\lambda D}}{d}{/tex} When light enters a denser medium, its wavelength decreases by a factor 1.3 and hence the fringe width also decreases by a factor 1.3. Therefore, the fringe width will decrease.
solution_html:
The fringe width in a Young's double slit experiment is given by
{tex}\omega = \frac{{\lambda D}}{d}{/tex}
When light enters a denser medium, its wavelength decreases by a factor 1.3 and hence the fringe width also decreases by a factor 1.3. Therefore, the fringe width will decrease.

question_html: How is a wave front defined? Using Huygen's construction draw a figure showing the propagation of a plane wave reflecting at the interface of the two media. Show that the angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection.
question_text: How is a wave front defined? Using Huygen's construction draw a figure showing the propagation of a plane wave reflecting at the interface of the two media. Show that the angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection.
explanation: When light is emitted from a source, then the particles present around it begins to vibrate. The locus of all such particles which are vibrating in the same phase is termed as wavefront. Let PQ be reflecting surface. Let a plane wavefront AB moving through the medium (air) towards the surface PQ meet at the point B. Let c be the velocity of light and t be the time of A to reach A' then AA' = ct. By the Huygen's principle, secondary wavelets starts from B and cover a distance ct in time t and reaches at B'. To obtain new wavefront, draw circles with point B as centre and ct (AA' = BB') as radius. Draw a tangent A'B' from the point A'. Then A'B' represents the reflected wavelets which travels at right angle. Therefore, incident wavefront AB and reflected wavefront A'B' and normal lies in the same plane. In {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABA' and B'BA' AA' = BB' = ct [ {tex}\because{/tex} AA' = BB' = BD = radii of same circle] BA' = BA' [common] {tex}\angle BAA' = \Delta BB'A'{/tex} [each 90°] {tex}\therefore \Delta ABA' \cong \angle DBA'{/tex} [by R.H.S.] {tex}\angle ABA' = \angle B'A'B{/tex} [C.P.C.T ] {tex}\therefore{/tex} Incident angle i = reflected angle r {tex}\angle i = \angle r{/tex} Hence the proof.
solution: When light is emitted from a source, then the particles present around it begins to vibrate. The locus of all such particles which are vibrating in the same phase is termed as wavefront. Let PQ be reflecting surface. Let a plane wavefront AB moving through the medium (air) towards the surface PQ meet at the point B. Let c be the velocity of light and t be the time of A to reach A' then AA' = ct. By the Huygen's principle, secondary wavelets starts from B and cover a distance ct in time t and reaches at B'. To obtain new wavefront, draw circles with point B as centre and ct (AA' = BB') as radius. Draw a tangent A'B' from the point A'. Then A'B' represents the reflected wavelets which travels at right angle. Therefore, incident wavefront AB and reflected wavefront A'B' and normal lies in the same plane. In {tex}\Delta{/tex} ABA' and B'BA' AA' = BB' = ct [ {tex}\because{/tex} AA' = BB' = BD = radii of same circle] BA' = BA' [common] {tex}\angle BAA' = \Delta BB'A'{/tex} [each 90°] {tex}\therefore \Delta ABA' \cong \angle DBA'{/tex} [by R.H.S.] {tex}\angle ABA' = \angle B'A'B{/tex} [C.P.C.T ] {tex}\therefore{/tex} Incident angle i = reflected angle r {tex}\angle i = \angle r{/tex} Hence the proof.
solution_html:
When light is emitted from a source, then the particles present around it begins to vibrate. The locus of all such particles which are vibrating in the same phase is termed as wavefront. 


Let PQ be reflecting surface. Let a plane wavefront AB moving through the medium (air) towards the surface PQ meet at the point B. Let c be the velocity of light and t be the time of A to reach A' then AA' = ct.
By the Huygen's principle, secondary wavelets starts from B and cover a distance ct in time t and reaches at B'.
To obtain new wavefront, draw circles with point B as centre and ct (AA' = BB') as radius. Draw a tangent A'B' from the point A'.
Then A'B' represents the reflected wavelets which travels at right angle. Therefore, incident wavefront AB and reflected wavefront A'B' and normal lies in the same plane.
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABA' and B'BA'
AA' = BB' = ct [{tex}\because{/tex} AA' = BB' = BD = radii of same circle]
BA' = BA' [common]
{tex}\angle BAA' = \Delta BB'A'{/tex}[each 90°]
{tex}\therefore \Delta ABA' \cong \angle DBA'{/tex} [by R.H.S.]
{tex}\angle ABA' = \angle B'A'B{/tex} [C.P.C.T ]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Incident angle i = reflected angle r
{tex}\angle i = \angle r{/tex}
Hence the proof.

question_html: In a Young's double slit experiment, the two slits are kept 2 mm apart and the screen is positioned 140 cm away from the plane of the slits. The slits are illuminated with light of wavelength 600 nm. Find the distance of the third bright fringe, from the central maximum, in the interference pattern obtained on the screen.If the wavelength of the incident light were changed to 480 nm, find out the shift in the position of third bright fringe from the central maximum.
question_text: In a Young's double slit experiment, the two slits are kept 2 mm apart and the screen is positioned 140 cm away from the plane of the slits. The slits are illuminated with light of wavelength 600 nm. Find the distance of the third bright fringe, from the central maximum, in the interference pattern obtained on the screen.If the wavelength of the incident light were changed to 480 nm, find out the shift in the position of third bright fringe from the central maximum.
explanation: Given, d = 2 mm {tex} = 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} D = 140 cm = 1.40 m {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm {tex}= 600 \times {10^{ - 9}} = 6 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} m Position of bright fringes is given by {tex}{x_n} = n\lambda \frac{D}{d}{/tex} Distance of the third bright fringe is {tex}{x_3} = 3\lambda \frac{D}{d}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x_3} = 3 \times 6 \times {10^{ - 7}} \times \frac{{1.40}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 12.6 \times {10^{ - 4}} = 1.26 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} = 1.26 mm For {tex}\lambda {/tex} = 480 nm {tex} = 480 \times {10^{ - 9}} = 4.8 \times {10^{ - 7}}m{/tex} Distance of the third bright fringe is {tex}{x_3} = 3\lambda \frac{D}{d}{/tex} {tex} = 3 \times 4.8 \times {10^{ - 7}} \times \frac{{1.40}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 10.08 \times {10^{ - 4}} = 1.008 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} {tex} = 1.01 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} = 1.01 mm {tex}\therefore{/tex} Shift in the position of third bright fringe = 1.26 - 1.01 = 0.25 mm.
solution: Given, d = 2 mm {tex} = 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} D = 140 cm = 1.40 m {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm {tex}= 600 \times {10^{ - 9}} = 6 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} m Position of bright fringes is given by {tex}{x_n} = n\lambda \frac{D}{d}{/tex} Distance of the third bright fringe is {tex}{x_3} = 3\lambda \frac{D}{d}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x_3} = 3 \times 6 \times {10^{ - 7}} \times \frac{{1.40}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 12.6 \times {10^{ - 4}} = 1.26 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} = 1.26 mm For {tex}\lambda {/tex} = 480 nm {tex} = 480 \times {10^{ - 9}} = 4.8 \times {10^{ - 7}}m{/tex} Distance of the third bright fringe is {tex}{x_3} = 3\lambda \frac{D}{d}{/tex} {tex} = 3 \times 4.8 \times {10^{ - 7}} \times \frac{{1.40}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 10.08 \times {10^{ - 4}} = 1.008 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} {tex} = 1.01 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} = 1.01 mm {tex}\therefore{/tex} Shift in the position of third bright fringe = 1.26 - 1.01 = 0.25 mm.
solution_html:
Given, d = 2 mm {tex} = 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex}
D = 140 cm = 1.40 m
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm {tex}= 600 \times {10^{ - 9}} = 6 \times {10^{ - 7}}{/tex} m
Position of bright fringes is given by
{tex}{x_n} = n\lambda \frac{D}{d}{/tex}
Distance of the third bright fringe is
{tex}{x_3} = 3\lambda \frac{D}{d}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {x_3} = 3 \times 6 \times {10^{ - 7}} \times \frac{{1.40}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = 12.6 \times {10^{ - 4}} = 1.26 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex}
= 1.26 mm
For {tex}\lambda {/tex} = 480 nm {tex} = 480 \times {10^{ - 9}} = 4.8 \times {10^{ - 7}}m{/tex}
Distance of the third bright fringe is
{tex}{x_3} = 3\lambda \frac{D}{d}{/tex}
{tex} = 3 \times 4.8 \times {10^{ - 7}} \times \frac{{1.40}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = 10.08 \times {10^{ - 4}} = 1.008 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex}
{tex} = 1.01 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} = 1.01 mm
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Shift in the position of third bright fringe
= 1.26 - 1.01 = 0.25 mm.

question_html: Light of wavelength 550 nm is incident as a parallel beam on a slit of width 0.1 mm. Find the angular width and the linear width of the principal maxima in the resulting diffraction pattern on a screen kept at a distance of 1.1 m from the slit. Which of these widths would not change if the screen were moved to a distance of 2.2 m from the slit?
question_text: Light of wavelength 550 nm is incident as a parallel beam on a slit of width 0.1 mm. Find the angular width and the linear width of the principal maxima in the resulting diffraction pattern on a screen kept at a distance of 1.1 m from the slit. Which of these widths would not change if the screen were moved to a distance of 2.2 m from the slit?
explanation: Here {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 550 nm = 550 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m, a = 0.1 mm = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 1.1m Angular width of principal maximum, {tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}=\frac{2 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 rad Linear width of principal maximum, {tex}\beta_{0}=\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.011 = 0.0121 m = 12.1 mm When the screen is moved to a distance of 2.2 m, the angular width would not change because it is independent of this distance D.
solution: Here {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 550 nm = 550 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m, a = 0.1 mm = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 1.1m Angular width of principal maximum, {tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}=\frac{2 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 rad Linear width of principal maximum, {tex}\beta_{0}=\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.011 = 0.0121 m = 12.1 mm When the screen is moved to a distance of 2.2 m, the angular width would not change because it is independent of this distance D.
solution_html:
Here {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 550 nm = 550 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9m, a = 0.1 mm = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3m, D = 1.1m
Angular width of principal maximum,
{tex}2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}=\frac{2 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 rad
Linear width of principal maximum,
{tex}\beta_{0}=\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.011 = 0.0121 m = 12.1 mm
When the screen is moved to a distance of 2.2 m, the angular width would not change because it is independent of this distance D.

question_html:
A parallel beam of monochromatic light falls normally on a narrow slit and the light, coming out of the slit, is obtained on a screen, kept behind, parallel to the slit plane.
What kind of pattern do we observe on the screen and why? How does the

  1. angular width
  2. linear width of the principal maximum, in this pattern change when the screen is moved, parallel to itself, away from the slit plane?

State two points of difference between this pattern and the interference pattern observed in the Young's double slit experiment.
question_text: A parallel beam of monochromatic light falls normally on a narrow slit and the light, coming out of the slit, is obtained on a screen, kept behind, parallel to the slit plane. What kind of pattern do we observe on the screen and why? How does the angular width linear width of the principal maximum, in this pattern change when the screen is moved, parallel to itself, away from the slit plane? State two points of difference between this pattern and the interference pattern observed in the Young's double slit experiment.
explanation: The light on passing through the narrow slit undergoes diffraction. A diffraction pattern consisting of alternate bright and dark bands is obtained on the screen. Angular width of principal maximum, 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} It is not affected when screen is moved away (D increases) from the slit plane. Now linear width x of the central maximum is given by x = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} Thus if the screen is moved away the linear width of the central maximum will increase too. Difference between interference and diffraction In interference all the fringes will be of equal intensity but in diffraction the central maximum will have high intensity and in the rest of the fringes intensity falls rapidly. In interference all the fringes will be of equal width but in diffraction the central maximum will have the highest width and for the other fringes width will diminish fast.
solution: The light on passing through the narrow slit undergoes diffraction. A diffraction pattern consisting of alternate bright and dark bands is obtained on the screen. Angular width of principal maximum, 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} It is not affected when screen is moved away (D increases) from the slit plane. Now linear width x of the central maximum is given by x = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex} Thus if the screen is moved away the linear width of the central maximum will increase too. Difference between interference and diffraction In interference all the fringes will be of equal intensity but in diffraction the central maximum will have high intensity and in the rest of the fringes intensity falls rapidly. In interference all the fringes will be of equal width but in diffraction the central maximum will have the highest width and for the other fringes width will diminish fast.
solution_html:
The light on passing through the narrow slit undergoes diffraction. A diffraction pattern consisting of alternate bright and dark bands is obtained on the screen.

  1. Angular width of principal maximum,
    2{tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}
    It is not affected when screen is moved away (D increases) from the slit plane.
  2. Now linear width x of the central maximum is given by
    x = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda D}{a}{/tex}
    Thus if the screen is moved away the linear width of the central maximum will increase too.

Difference between interference and diffraction

  1. In interference all the fringes will be of equal intensity but in diffraction the central maximum will have high intensity and in the rest of the fringes intensity falls rapidly.
  2. In interference all the fringes will be of equal width but in diffraction the central maximum will have the highest width and for the other fringes width will diminish fast.

question_html: A parallel beam of light of wavelength 600 nm is incident normally on a slit of width a. If the distance between the slits and the screen is 0.8 m and the distance of 2nd order maximum from the centre of the screen is 1.5 mm, calculate the width of the slit.
question_text: A parallel beam of light of wavelength 600 nm is incident normally on a slit of width a . If the distance between the slits and the screen is 0.8 m and the distance of 2nd order maximum from the centre of the screen is 1.5 mm, calculate the width of the slit.
explanation: Given {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm = 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m, D = 0.8 m y 2 = 1.5 mm = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, n = 2, a = ? Position of n th maximum in diffraction of a single slit y n = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda D}{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda D}{y_n}{/tex} Substituting given values a = {tex}\left(2+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.8}{1.5 \times 10^{-8}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2} \times 4.0 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} m = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 0.8 mm
solution: Given {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm = 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m, D = 0.8 m y 2 = 1.5 mm = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, n = 2, a = ? Position of n th maximum in diffraction of a single slit y n = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda D}{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda D}{y_n}{/tex} Substituting given values a = {tex}\left(2+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.8}{1.5 \times 10^{-8}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2} \times 4.0 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} m = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 0.8 mm
solution_html:
Given {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm = 600 {tex}\times{/tex}10-9 m = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m, D = 0.8 m
y2 = 1.5 mm = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex}10-3 m, n = 2, a = ?
Position of nth maximum in diffraction of a single slit
yn = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda D}{a}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{\lambda D}{y_n}{/tex}
Substituting given values a = {tex}\left(2+\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.8}{1.5 \times 10^{-8}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{5}{2} \times 4.0 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-4}{/tex} m = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex}10-3 m = 0.8 mm

question_html: How is Huygen's principle used to obtain the diffraction pattern due to a single slit? Show the plot of the variation of intensity with angle and state the reason for the reduction in the intensity of secondary maxima compared to the central maximum.
question_text: How is Huygen's principle used to obtain the diffraction pattern due to a single slit? Show the plot of the variation of intensity with angle and state the reason for the reduction in the intensity of secondary maxima compared to the central maximum.
explanation: According to Huygens principle The net effect at any point due to a number of wavelets is equal to sum total of contribution of all wavelets with proper phase difference. The point O is maxima because contribution from each half of the slit S 1 S 2 is in phase i.e. the path difference is zero.

question_html:
In a single slit diffraction pattern, how does the angular width of central maximum change, when

  1. the slit width is decreased,
  2. the distance between the slit and the screen is increased and
  3. light of smaller wavelength is used?
    Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: In a single slit diffraction pattern, how does the angular width of central maximum change, when the slit width is decreased, the distance between the slit and the screen is increased and light of smaller wavelength is used? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: Linear width of central maximum, {tex}\beta_{0}=\frac{2 \mathrm{D} \lambda}{a}{/tex} Angular width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{\beta_{0}}{D}=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} When slit width 'a' decreases, angular width increases. When distance D between the slit and screen is increased, angular width does not change. When light of smaller wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is used, angular width decreases.
solution: Linear width of central maximum, {tex}\beta_{0}=\frac{2 \mathrm{D} \lambda}{a}{/tex} Angular width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{\beta_{0}}{D}=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} When slit width 'a' decreases, angular width increases. When distance D between the slit and screen is increased, angular width does not change. When light of smaller wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is used, angular width decreases.
solution_html:
Linear width of central maximum,
{tex}\beta_{0}=\frac{2 \mathrm{D} \lambda}{a}{/tex}
Angular width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{\beta_{0}}{D}=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}

  1. When slit width 'a' decreases, angular width increases.
  2. When distance D between the slit and screen is increased, angular width does not change.
  3. When light of smaller wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is used, angular width decreases.

question_html: Two narrow slits are illuminated by a single monochromatic source. Name the pattern obtained on the screen. One of the slits is now completely covered. What is the name of the pattern now obtained on the screen? Draw intensity pattern obtained in the two cases. Also, write two differences between the patterns obtained in the above two cases.
question_text: Two narrow slits are illuminated by a single monochromatic source. Name the pattern obtained on the screen. One of the slits is now completely covered. What is the name of the pattern now obtained on the screen? Draw intensity pattern obtained in the two cases. Also, write two differences between the patterns obtained in the above two cases.
explanation: With two narrow slits, an interference pattern is obtained. When one slit is completely covered, the diffraction pattern is obtained. For intensity distribution curve for interference, see Fig. For intensity distribution curve for diffraction, see Fig. Interference Diffraction 1. All the bright fringes are of same intensity. Intensity of bright fringes decreases with the increasing order. 2. All the bright fringes are of equal width. Central bright fringe is twice as wide as any secondary bright fringe. 3. Regions of dark fringes are perfectly dark. Regions of dark fringes are not perfectly dark. 4. Maxima occur at {tex}\theta=n \frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} Minima occur at {tex}\theta=n \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
solution: With two narrow slits, an interference pattern is obtained. When one slit is completely covered, the diffraction pattern is obtained. For intensity distribution curve for interference, see Fig. For intensity distribution curve for diffraction, see Fig. Interference Diffraction 1. All the bright fringes are of same intensity. Intensity of bright fringes decreases with the increasing order. 2. All the bright fringes are of equal width. Central bright fringe is twice as wide as any secondary bright fringe. 3. Regions of dark fringes are perfectly dark. Regions of dark fringes are not perfectly dark. 4. Maxima occur at {tex}\theta=n \frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} Minima occur at {tex}\theta=n \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
solution_html:
With two narrow slits, an interference pattern is obtained.
When one slit is completely covered, the diffraction pattern is obtained.
For intensity distribution curve for interference, see Fig.

For intensity distribution curve for diffraction, see Fig.

Interference Diffraction
1. All the bright fringes are of same intensity. Intensity of bright fringes decreases with the increasing order.
2. All the bright fringes are of equal width. Central bright fringe is twice as wide as any secondary bright fringe.
3. Regions of dark fringes are perfectly dark. Regions of dark fringes are not perfectly dark.
4. Maxima occur at {tex}\theta=n \frac{\lambda}{d}{/tex} Minima occur at {tex}\theta=n \frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
