question_html:
How is a wavefront different from a ray? Draw the geometrial shape of the wavefronts, when

  1. light diverges from a point source and
  2. light emerges out of a convex lens when a point source is placed at its focus.
question_text: How is a wavefront different from a ray? Draw the geometrial shape of the wavefronts, when light diverges from a point source and light emerges out of a convex lens when a point source is placed at its focus.
explanation: A wavefront is defined as a surface of constant phase. A wavefront is the locus of all points in the medium that have the same phase. The ray, at each point of a wavefront, is normal to the wavefront at that point. The ray indicates the direction of propagation of wave while the wavefront is the surface of constant phase. The geometrical shape of the wavefronts are Light diverges from a point source Light emerges out of a convex lens when a point source is placed at its focus.
solution: A wavefront is defined as a surface of constant phase. A wavefront is the locus of all points in the medium that have the same phase. The ray, at each point of a wavefront, is normal to the wavefront at that point. The ray indicates the direction of propagation of wave while the wavefront is the surface of constant phase. The geometrical shape of the wavefronts are Light diverges from a point source Light emerges out of a convex lens when a point source is placed at its focus.
solution_html:
A wavefront is defined as a surface of constant phase. A wavefront is the locus of all points in the medium that have the same phase.
The ray, at each point of a wavefront, is normal to the wavefront at that point. The ray indicates the direction of propagation of wave while the wavefront is the surface of constant phase.

The geometrical shape of the wavefronts are

  1. Light diverges from a point source
  2. Light emerges out of a convex lens when a point source is placed at its focus.

question_html: Yellow light of wavelength 6000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} produces fringes of width 0.8 mm in Young's double-slit experiment. What will be the fringe width if the light source is replaced by another monochromatic source of wavelength 7500 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} and the separation between the slits is doubled?
question_text: Yellow light of wavelength 6000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} produces fringes of width 0.8 mm in Young's double-slit experiment. What will be the fringe width if the light source is replaced by another monochromatic source of wavelength 7500 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} and the separation between the slits is doubled?
explanation: Here {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} = 6000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} , {tex}\beta_{1}{/tex} = 0.8 mm, {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} = 7500 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} Fringe width in first case, {tex}\beta_{1}=\frac{D \lambda_{1}}{d}{/tex} Fringe width in second case, {tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{D \lambda_{2}}{2 d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}=\frac{D \lambda_{2} / 2 d}{D \lambda_{1} / d}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} or {tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}} \cdot \beta_{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{7500 \overset \circ A}{6000 \overset \circ A} \times{/tex} 0.8 mm = 0.5 mm
solution: Here {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} = 6000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} , {tex}\beta_{1}{/tex} = 0.8 mm, {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} = 7500 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} Fringe width in first case, {tex}\beta_{1}=\frac{D \lambda_{1}}{d}{/tex} Fringe width in second case, {tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{D \lambda_{2}}{2 d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}=\frac{D \lambda_{2} / 2 d}{D \lambda_{1} / d}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} or {tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}} \cdot \beta_{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{7500 \overset \circ A}{6000 \overset \circ A} \times{/tex} 0.8 mm = 0.5 mm
solution_html:
Here {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} = 6000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}, {tex}\beta_{1}{/tex} = 0.8 mm,
{tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} = 7500 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}
Fringe width in first case,
{tex}\beta_{1}=\frac{D \lambda_{1}}{d}{/tex}
Fringe width in second case,
{tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{D \lambda_{2}}{2 d}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\beta_{2}}{\beta_{1}}=\frac{D \lambda_{2} / 2 d}{D \lambda_{1} / d}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex}
or {tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}} \cdot \beta_{1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{7500 \overset \circ A}{6000 \overset \circ A} \times{/tex} 0.8 mm = 0.5 mm

question_text: Why does a soap bubble show beautiful colours when illuminated by white light?
explanation: Light waves reflected from the upper and lower surfaces of a thin film interfere. Since the conditions for bright and dark fringes are satisfied at different positions for different wavelengths, so colored fringes are observed.
solution: Light waves reflected from the upper and lower surfaces of a thin film interfere. Since the conditions for bright and dark fringes are satisfied at different positions for different wavelengths, so colored fringes are observed.

question_html: Define the term wavefront? What are the assumptions on which Huygens' principle is based? Describe Huygens' geometrical construction for the propagation of wavefronts in a medium.
question_text: Define the term wavefront? What are the assumptions on which Huygens' principle is based? Describe Huygens' geometrical construction for the propagation of wavefronts in a medium.
explanation: A wave front is the locus of all the points in space that reach a particular distance by a propagating wave in same phase at any instant. Huygen's principle is based on two assumptions: Each point of the wavefront behaves like a source of secondary wavelets. These secondary wavelets spread out in all directions with the same speed as that of the original wave. When we draw an envelope in the forward direction of the secondary wavelets at any instant, then this envelope tells the new position of the wavefront at that instant.
solution: A wave front is the locus of all the points in space that reach a particular distance by a propagating wave in same phase at any instant. Huygen's principle is based on two assumptions: Each point of the wavefront behaves like a source of secondary wavelets. These secondary wavelets spread out in all directions with the same speed as that of the original wave. When we draw an envelope in the forward direction of the secondary wavelets at any instant, then this envelope tells the new position of the wavefront at that instant.
solution_html:
A wave front is the locus of all the points in space that reach a particular distance by a propagating wave in same phase at any instant.
Huygen's principle is based on two assumptions:

  1. Each point of the wavefront behaves like a source of secondary wavelets. These secondary wavelets spread out in all directions with the same speed as that of the original wave.
  2. When we draw an envelope in the forward direction of the secondary wavelets at any instant, then this envelope tells the new position of the wavefront at that instant.

question_html: What is the interference of light? Give one example from daily life. State the necessary conditions for sustained interference.
question_text: What is the interference of light? Give one example from daily life. State the necessary conditions for sustained interference.
explanation: When two light waves from different coherent sources meet together, then the distribution of energy due to one wave is disturbed by the other. This modification in the distribution of light energy due to super- position of two light waves is called Interference of light. Examples of the interference of light is demonstrated by the light reflected from a film of oil floating on water. The conditions for the interference of light are as under: Light source should be monochromatic. Both the waves should be of same frequency.
solution: When two light waves from different coherent sources meet together, then the distribution of energy due to one wave is disturbed by the other. This modification in the distribution of light energy due to super- position of two light waves is called Interference of light. Examples of the interference of light is demonstrated by the light reflected from a film of oil floating on water. The conditions for the interference of light are as under: Light source should be monochromatic. Both the waves should be of same frequency.
solution_html:
When two light waves from different coherent sources meet together, then the distribution of energy due to one wave is disturbed by the other. This modification in the distribution of light energy due to super- position of two light waves is called Interference of light.
Examples of the interference of light is demonstrated by the light reflected from a film of oil floating on water. The conditions for the interference of light are as under:

  1. Light source should be monochromatic.
  2. Both the waves should be of same frequency.

question_html: What is a wavefront? What is the geometrical shape of a wave front of light emerging out of a convex lens, when point source is placed at its focus? Using Huygens' principle show that, for a parallel beam incident on a reflecting surface, the angle of reflection is equal to the angle of incidence.
question_text: What is a wavefront? What is the geometrical shape of a wave front of light emerging out of a convex lens, when point source is placed at its focus? Using Huygens' principle show that, for a parallel beam incident on a reflecting surface, the angle of reflection is equal to the angle of incidence.
explanation: A wavefront is an imaginary surface representing corresponding points of a wave that vibrate in unison. A wavefront is the set of all locations in a medium where the wave is at the same phase. The shape of the wavefront in case of a light emerging out of a convex lens, when a point source is placed at its focus is a plane. Let ‘v’ be the speed of the light wave in the medium and ‘t’ be the time taken by the secondary wavelets to move from the point B to C Then the distance. BC = vt In order to construct the reflected wave front, Let us draw a sphere of radium AE (vt), CE represents the tangent drawn from the point e to this sphere, This tangent represents the position of the new reflected wave front. From the figure it is observed that AE = BC = vt. the triangles EAC and BAC are congruent {tex}\therefore{/tex} i = r. This is the Law of reflection.
solution: A wavefront is an imaginary surface representing corresponding points of a wave that vibrate in unison. A wavefront is the set of all locations in a medium where the wave is at the same phase. The shape of the wavefront in case of a light emerging out of a convex lens, when a point source is placed at its focus is a plane. Let ‘v’ be the speed of the light wave in the medium and ‘t’ be the time taken by the secondary wavelets to move from the point B to C Then the distance. BC = vt In order to construct the reflected wave front, Let us draw a sphere of radium AE (vt), CE represents the tangent drawn from the point e to this sphere, This tangent represents the position of the new reflected wave front. From the figure it is observed that AE = BC = vt. the triangles EAC and BAC are congruent {tex}\therefore{/tex} i = r. This is the Law of reflection.
solution_html:
A wavefront is an imaginary surface representing corresponding points of a wave that vibrate in unison. A wavefront is the set of all locations in a medium where the wave is at the same phase.
The shape of the wavefront in case of a light emerging out of a convex lens, when a point source is placed at its focus is a plane.

Let ‘v’ be the speed of the light wave in the medium and ‘t’ be the time taken by the secondary wavelets to move from the point B to C Then the distance. BC = vt

In order to construct the reflected wave front, Let us draw a sphere of radium AE (vt), CE represents the tangent drawn from the point e to this sphere, This tangent represents the position of the new reflected wave front.
From the figure it is observed that AE = BC = vt. the triangles EAC and BAC are congruent
{tex}\therefore{/tex} i = r.
This is the Law of reflection.

explanation: When the distance d between the two coherent sources is large, the fringe width {tex}(\beta \propto \frac{1}{d}){/tex} becomes too small to be detected. The interference pattern cannot be observed.

question_html:
State the postulates of Huygens' wave theory. Sketch the wavefront that corresponds to a beam of light

  1. coming from a very far away source, and
  2. diverging radially from a point source.
question_text: State the postulates of Huygens' wave theory. Sketch the wavefront that corresponds to a beam of light coming from a very far away source, and diverging radially from a point source.
explanation: The postulates of Huygens's wave theory are: Every particle of the medium situated on the wave-front acts as a new wave-source from which fresh waves originates. These waves are called secondary wavelets. The secondary wavelets travel in the medium in all directions with the speed of the original wave in the medium.

These waves are called secondary wavelets. The secondary wavelets travel in the medium in all directions with the speed of the original wave in the medium.

These waves are called secondary wavelets. The secondary wavelets travel in the medium in all directions with the speed of the original wave in the medium.

question_html: Two coherent sources have intensities in the ratio 25 : 16. Find the ratio of the intensities of maxima to minima, after the interference of light occurs.
question_text: Two coherent sources have intensities in the ratio 25 : 16. Find the ratio of the intensities of maxima to minima, after the interference of light occurs.
explanation: Amplitude ratio, r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{25}{16}}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{(r+1)^{2}}{(r-1)^{2}}=\frac{\left(\frac{5}{4}+1\right)^{2}}{\left(\frac{5}{4}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 81 : 1
solution: Amplitude ratio, r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{25}{16}}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{(r+1)^{2}}{(r-1)^{2}}=\frac{\left(\frac{5}{4}+1\right)^{2}}{\left(\frac{5}{4}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 81 : 1
solution_html:
Amplitude ratio,
r = {tex}\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{25}{16}}=\frac{5}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{(r+1)^{2}}{(r-1)^{2}}=\frac{\left(\frac{5}{4}+1\right)^{2}}{\left(\frac{5}{4}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 81 : 1

subject: Physics
question_html: The central fringe obtained with a Lloyd's mirror set up for interference is found to be dark, whereas it is bright with a biprism. Why?
question_text: The central fringe obtained with a Lloyd's mirror set up for interference is found to be dark, whereas it is bright with a biprism. Why?
explanation: The central fringe obtained with a Lloyd's mirror is dark because the reflection of light from the denser mirror surface introduces a path difference of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} . In the case of biprism, the waves from both the coherent sources reach the central point in the same phase and so the central fringe is bright.
solution: The central fringe obtained with a Lloyd's mirror is dark because the reflection of light from the denser mirror surface introduces a path difference of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} . In the case of biprism, the waves from both the coherent sources reach the central point in the same phase and so the central fringe is bright.
solution_html: The central fringe obtained with a Lloyd's mirror is dark because the reflection of light from the denser mirror surface introduces a path difference of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}. In the case of biprism, the waves from both the coherent sources reach the central point in the same phase and so the central fringe is bright.

question_html: Two plane monochromatic waves propagating in the same direction with amplitudes A and 2 A and differing in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} rad superpose. Calculate the amplitude of the resultant wave.
question_text: Two plane monochromatic waves propagating in the same direction with amplitudes A and 2 A and differing in phase by {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} rad superpose. Calculate the amplitude of the resultant wave.
explanation: Here A 1 = A, A 2 = 2 A, {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} A R = {tex}\sqrt{A_{1}^{2}+A_{2}^{2}+2 A_{1} A_{2} \cos \phi}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{A^{2}+(2 A)^{2}+2 A \times 2 A \cos \frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{5 A^{2}+4 A^{2} \times \frac{1}{2}}=\sqrt{7 A^{2}}=\sqrt{7}{/tex} A
solution: Here A 1 = A, A 2 = 2 A, {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} A R = {tex}\sqrt{A_{1}^{2}+A_{2}^{2}+2 A_{1} A_{2} \cos \phi}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{A^{2}+(2 A)^{2}+2 A \times 2 A \cos \frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{5 A^{2}+4 A^{2} \times \frac{1}{2}}=\sqrt{7 A^{2}}=\sqrt{7}{/tex} A
solution_html:
Here A1 = A, A2 = 2 A, {tex}\phi=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
AR = {tex}\sqrt{A_{1}^{2}+A_{2}^{2}+2 A_{1} A_{2} \cos \phi}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{A^{2}+(2 A)^{2}+2 A \times 2 A \cos \frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{5 A^{2}+4 A^{2} \times \frac{1}{2}}=\sqrt{7 A^{2}}=\sqrt{7}{/tex} A

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive the condition 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}t = n{tex}\lambda{/tex} for darkness when monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is normally incident on a thin oil film of thickness t and refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex}.
question_text: Derive the condition 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} t = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} for darkness when monochromatic light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is normally incident on a thin oil film of thickness t and refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} .
explanation: Fig. shows the rays reflected from the upper and lower surfaces of a thin film of thickness t for almost normal incidence. Clearly, the path difference between the reflected rays R 1 and R 2 {tex}\approx{/tex} 2 t in oil = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} t But ray R 1 suffers an extra path difference of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} due to its reflection from the upper denser surface of oil, so the net path difference between R 1 and R 2 is p = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} For a dark fringe, p = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} = (2n + 1) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} or 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} t = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} This is the required condition for a dark fringe.
solution: Fig. shows the rays reflected from the upper and lower surfaces of a thin film of thickness t for almost normal incidence. Clearly, the path difference between the reflected rays R 1 and R 2 {tex}\approx{/tex} 2 t in oil = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} t But ray R 1 suffers an extra path difference of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} due to its reflection from the upper denser surface of oil, so the net path difference between R 1 and R 2 is p = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} For a dark fringe, p = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} t + {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} = (2n + 1) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} or 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} t = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} This is the required condition for a dark fringe.
solution_html:
Fig. shows the rays reflected from the upper and lower surfaces of a thin film of thickness t for almost normal incidence.

Clearly, the path difference between the reflected rays R1 and R2
{tex}\approx{/tex} 2 t in oil = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}t
But ray R1 suffers an extra path difference of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} due to its reflection from the upper denser surface of oil, so the net path difference between R1 and R2 is
p = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}t + {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}
For a dark fringe,
p = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}t + {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} = (2n + 1) {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} or 2 {tex}\mu{/tex}t = n{tex}\lambda{/tex}
This is the required condition for a dark fringe.

question_html: Give relation between path difference and wavelength for constructive interference between two waves.
question_text: Give relation between path difference and wavelength for constructive interference between two waves.
explanation: For constructive interference, the path difference between two waves must be an integral multiple of {tex}\lambda{/tex} i.e., p = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} , n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ...
solution: For constructive interference, the path difference between two waves must be an integral multiple of {tex}\lambda{/tex} i.e., p = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} , n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ...
solution_html: For constructive interference, the path difference between two waves must be an integral multiple of {tex}\lambda{/tex} i.e., p = n{tex}\lambda{/tex}, n = 0, 1, 2, 3, ...

subject: Physics
question_html: State two conditions for sustained interference of light. Draw the variation of intensity with a position, in the interference pattern of Young's double-slit experiment.
question_text: State two conditions for sustained interference of light. Draw the variation of intensity with a position, in the interference pattern of Young's double-slit experiment.
explanation: The conditions for sustained or permanent interference is as follows: The two sources of light must be coherent which means the two light waves emitted by them must have a constant phase difference or in the same phase. The two sources must emit light of the same wavelength but the amplitudes between them should differ as little as possible. The emitted waves should be preferably of the same amplitude to get completely dark fringes.
solution: The conditions for sustained or permanent interference is as follows: The two sources of light must be coherent which means the two light waves emitted by them must have a constant phase difference or in the same phase. The two sources must emit light of the same wavelength but the amplitudes between them should differ as little as possible. The emitted waves should be preferably of the same amplitude to get completely dark fringes.
solution_html:
The conditions for sustained or permanent interference is as follows:

  1. The two sources of light must be coherent which means the two light waves emitted by them must have a constant phase difference or in the same phase.
  2. The two sources must emit light of the same wavelength but the amplitudes between them should differ as little as possible. The emitted waves should be preferably of the same amplitude to get completely dark fringes.

question_html: The fringe width in Young's double-slit interference pattern is 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m, when red light of wavelength 6400 {tex}\overset\circ{A}{/tex} is used. By how much will it change, if blue light of wavelength 4000 {tex}\overset\circ{A}{/tex} is used?
question_text: The fringe width in Young's double-slit interference pattern is 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m, when red light of wavelength 6400 {tex}\overset\circ{A}{/tex} is used. By how much will it change, if blue light of wavelength 4000 {tex}\overset\circ{A}{/tex} is used?
explanation: {tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}} \times \beta_{1}=\frac{4000}{6400} \times{/tex} 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m {tex}\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}{/tex} = (2.4 - 1.5) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m
solution: {tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}} \times \beta_{1}=\frac{4000}{6400} \times{/tex} 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m {tex}\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}{/tex} = (2.4 - 1.5) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 m
solution_html:
{tex}\beta_{2}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}} \times \beta_{1}=\frac{4000}{6400} \times{/tex} 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m
{tex}\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}{/tex} = (2.4 - 1.5) {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 m

subject: Physics
question_html: In Young's double-slit experiment, light of wavelength 6000 {tex}\overset\circ{A}{/tex} is used to get an interference pattern on a screen. The fringe width changes by 1.5 mm, when the screen is brought towards the double slit by 50 cm. Find the distance between the two slits.
question_text: In Young's double-slit experiment, light of wavelength 6000 {tex}\overset\circ{A}{/tex} is used to get an interference pattern on a screen. The fringe width changes by 1.5 mm, when the screen is brought towards the double slit by 50 cm. Find the distance between the two slits.
explanation: Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} As {tex}\lambda{/tex} and d are constant, so the change in fringe width is given by {tex}\Delta \beta=\frac{\lambda \Delta D}{d}{/tex} Distance between the two slits, d = {tex}\frac{\lambda \Delta D}{\Delta \beta}=\frac{6000 \times 10^{-10} \times 50 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 0.2 mm
solution: Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} As {tex}\lambda{/tex} and d are constant, so the change in fringe width is given by {tex}\Delta \beta=\frac{\lambda \Delta D}{d}{/tex} Distance between the two slits, d = {tex}\frac{\lambda \Delta D}{\Delta \beta}=\frac{6000 \times 10^{-10} \times 50 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 0.2 mm
solution_html:
Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
As {tex}\lambda{/tex} and d are constant, so the change in fringe width is given by
{tex}\Delta \beta=\frac{\lambda \Delta D}{d}{/tex}
Distance between the two slits,
d = {tex}\frac{\lambda \Delta D}{\Delta \beta}=\frac{6000 \times 10^{-10} \times 50 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
= 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m = 0.2 mm

subject: Physics
question_html: In a Young's double-slit experiment, red light of wavelength 6000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} is used and the nth bright fringe is obtained at a point P on the screen. Keeping the same setting, the source is replaced by the green light of 5000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} and now (n + 1)th bright fringe is obtained at the point P. Calculate the value of n.
question_text: In a Young's double-slit experiment, red light of wavelength 6000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} is used and the nth bright fringe is obtained at a point P on the screen. Keeping the same setting, the source is replaced by the green light of 5000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} and now (n + 1)th bright fringe is obtained at the point P. Calculate the value of n.
explanation: Let x be the distance of point P from the centre of the screen. When red light ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} ) is used, nth bright fringe is obtained at point P {tex}\therefore \quad x=\frac{n D \lambda}{d}=\frac{n D \times 6000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}{/tex} When green light ( {tex}\lambda^{\prime}{/tex} = 5000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} ) is used, (n + 1)th bright fringe is obtained at the same point P {tex}\therefore \quad x=\frac{(n+1) D \lambda^{\prime}}{d}=\frac{(n+1) D \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}{/tex} Equating the two values of x, we get {tex}\frac{n D \times 6000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}=\frac{(n+1) D \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}{/tex} or 6n = 5(n + 1) or n = 5
solution: Let x be the distance of point P from the centre of the screen. When red light ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} ) is used, nth bright fringe is obtained at point P {tex}\therefore \quad x=\frac{n D \lambda}{d}=\frac{n D \times 6000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}{/tex} When green light ( {tex}\lambda^{\prime}{/tex} = 5000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex} ) is used, (n + 1)th bright fringe is obtained at the same point P {tex}\therefore \quad x=\frac{(n+1) D \lambda^{\prime}}{d}=\frac{(n+1) D \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}{/tex} Equating the two values of x, we get {tex}\frac{n D \times 6000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}=\frac{(n+1) D \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}{/tex} or 6n = 5(n + 1) or n = 5
solution_html:
Let x be the distance of point P from the centre of the screen.
When red light ({tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}) is used, nth bright fringe is obtained at point P
{tex}\therefore \quad x=\frac{n D \lambda}{d}=\frac{n D \times 6000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}{/tex}
When green light ({tex}\lambda^{\prime}{/tex} = 5000 {tex}\overset \circ A{/tex}) is used, (n + 1)th bright fringe is obtained at the same point P
{tex}\therefore \quad x=\frac{(n+1) D \lambda^{\prime}}{d}=\frac{(n+1) D \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}{/tex}
Equating the two values of x, we get
{tex}\frac{n D \times 6000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}=\frac{(n+1) D \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}{d}{/tex}
or 6n = 5(n + 1)
or n = 5

question_html: Light of wavelength 600 nm falls normally on a slit of width 1.2 {tex}\mu{/tex}m producing Fraunhoffer diffraction pattern on a screen. Calculate the angular position of the first minimum and the angular width of the central maximum.
question_text: Light of wavelength 600 nm falls normally on a slit of width 1.2 {tex}\mu{/tex} m producing Fraunhoffer diffraction pattern on a screen. Calculate the angular position of the first minimum and the angular width of the central maximum.
explanation: Here a = 1.2 {tex}\mu{/tex} m = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm = 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}=\frac{600 \times 10^{-9}}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o The central maximum spreads on both sides of the screen {tex}\therefore{/tex} Angular spread of central maximum = 60 o
solution: Here a = 1.2 {tex}\mu{/tex} m = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm = 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}=\frac{600 \times 10^{-9}}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o The central maximum spreads on both sides of the screen {tex}\therefore{/tex} Angular spread of central maximum = 60 o
solution_html:
Here a = 1.2 {tex}\mu{/tex}m = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6m,
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 600 nm = 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m
sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}=\frac{600 \times 10^{-9}}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o
The central maximum spreads on both sides of the screen
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Angular spread of central maximum = 60o

question_html:
What is the effect on the interference fringes in Young's double-slit experiment if

  1. the separation between the slits is halved, and
  2. the source slit is moved closer to the double-slit?
    Justify your answer.
question_text: What is the effect on the interference fringes in Young's double-slit experiment if the separation between the slits is halved, and the source slit is moved closer to the double-slit? Justify your answer.
explanation: Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} . When separation d between the slits is halved, fringe width is doubled. The fringes became indistinct because the interference patterns due to various parts of the source slit overlap.
solution: Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} . When separation d between the slits is halved, fringe width is doubled. The fringes became indistinct because the interference patterns due to various parts of the source slit overlap.
solution_html:
1. Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}. When separation d between the slits is halved, fringe width is doubled.
  2. The fringes became indistinct because the interference patterns due to various parts of the source slit overlap.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two coherent sources, whose intensity ratio is 16 : 1 produce interference fringes. Calculate the ratio of the intensity of maxima and minima in the fringe system.
question_text: Two coherent sources, whose intensity ratio is 16 : 1 produce interference fringes. Calculate the ratio of the intensity of maxima and minima in the fringe system.
explanation: Let the intensities of individual source be 16 I and I Bright fringe (maximum) intensity, I max = {tex}\left(\sqrt{I_{1}}+\sqrt{I_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} Dark fringe (minimum) intensity, I min = {tex}\left(\sqrt{I}_{1}-\sqrt{I}_{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{\left(\sqrt{I}_{1}+\sqrt{I}_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(\sqrt{I}_{1}-\sqrt{I}_{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{(4+1)^{2}}{(4-1)^{2}}{/tex} = 25 : 9
solution: Let the intensities of individual source be 16 I and I Bright fringe (maximum) intensity, I max = {tex}\left(\sqrt{I_{1}}+\sqrt{I_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} Dark fringe (minimum) intensity, I min = {tex}\left(\sqrt{I}_{1}-\sqrt{I}_{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{\left(\sqrt{I}_{1}+\sqrt{I}_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(\sqrt{I}_{1}-\sqrt{I}_{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{(4+1)^{2}}{(4-1)^{2}}{/tex} = 25 : 9
solution_html:
Let the intensities of individual source be 16 I and I
Bright fringe (maximum) intensity,
Imax = {tex}\left(\sqrt{I_{1}}+\sqrt{I_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
Dark fringe (minimum) intensity,
Imin = {tex}\left(\sqrt{I}_{1}-\sqrt{I}_{2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{\left(\sqrt{I}_{1}+\sqrt{I}_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(\sqrt{I}_{1}-\sqrt{I}_{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{(4+1)^{2}}{(4-1)^{2}}{/tex} = 25 : 9

subject: Physics
question_html:
State the condition for diffraction of light to occur. In the diffraction at a single slit experiment, how would the width and the intensity of central maximum change, if

  1. the slit width is halved and
  2. visible light of longer wavelength is used?
question_text: State the condition for diffraction of light to occur. In the diffraction at a single slit experiment, how would the width and the intensity of central maximum change, if the slit width is halved and visible light of longer wavelength is used?
explanation: Diffraction of light is highly pronounced if the size of the obstacle or aperture is comparable to the wavelength of the light used. Width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex} If slit width a is halved, width of central maximum is doubled. Its area becomes 4 times and hence intensity becomes one-fourth of the initial intensity. If visible light of longer wavelength is used, the width of central maximum increases and hence intensity decreases.
solution: Diffraction of light is highly pronounced if the size of the obstacle or aperture is comparable to the wavelength of the light used. Width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex} If slit width a is halved, width of central maximum is doubled. Its area becomes 4 times and hence intensity becomes one-fourth of the initial intensity. If visible light of longer wavelength is used, the width of central maximum increases and hence intensity decreases.
solution_html:
Diffraction of light is highly pronounced if the size of the obstacle or aperture is comparable to the wavelength of the light used.
Width of central maximum = {tex}\frac{2 D \lambda}{a}{/tex}

  1. If slit width a is halved, width of central maximum is doubled. Its area becomes 4 times and hence intensity becomes one-fourth of the initial intensity.
  2. If visible light of longer wavelength is used, the width of central maximum increases and hence intensity decreases.

subject: Physics
question_html: Two sources of intensity I1 and I2 undergo interference in Young's double slit experiment. Show that {tex}\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{a_{1}+a_{2}}{a_{1}-a_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}, where a1 and a2 are the amplitudes of disturbance for two sources S1 and S2.
question_text: Two sources of intensity I 1 and I 2 undergo interference in Young's double slit experiment. Show that {tex}\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\frac{a_{1}+a_{2}}{a_{1}-a_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} , where a 1 and a 2 are the amplitudes of disturbance for two sources S 1 and S 2 .
explanation: When two light waves of amplitudes a 1 and a 2 and having phase difference interfere, the resultant amplitude is a = {tex}\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+2 a_{1} a_{2} \cos \phi}{/tex} As intensity {tex}\propto{/tex} (amplitude) 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = ka 2 = k ( {tex}a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}{/tex} + 2a 1 a 2 cos {tex}\phi{/tex} ) When {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0, cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 1 and the intensity is maximum, I max = k ( {tex}a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}{/tex} + 2a 1 a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) = k (a 1 + a 2 ) 2 When {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} , cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = -1, the intensity is minimum, I min = k ( {tex}a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}{/tex} - 2a 1 a 2 ) = k (a 1 - a 2 ) 2 {tex}\therefore\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{k\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}}{k\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}}=\left(\frac{a_{1}+a_{2}}{a_{1}-a_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
solution: When two light waves of amplitudes a 1 and a 2 and having phase difference interfere, the resultant amplitude is a = {tex}\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+2 a_{1} a_{2} \cos \phi}{/tex} As intensity {tex}\propto{/tex} (amplitude) 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I = ka 2 = k ( {tex}a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}{/tex} + 2a 1 a 2 cos {tex}\phi{/tex} ) When {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0, cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 1 and the intensity is maximum, I max = k ( {tex}a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}{/tex} + 2a 1 a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) = k (a 1 + a 2 ) 2 When {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} , cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = -1, the intensity is minimum, I min = k ( {tex}a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}{/tex} - 2a 1 a 2 ) = k (a 1 - a 2 ) 2 {tex}\therefore\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{k\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}}{k\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}}=\left(\frac{a_{1}+a_{2}}{a_{1}-a_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
When two light waves of amplitudes a1 and a2 and having phase difference interfere, the resultant amplitude is
a = {tex}\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+2 a_{1} a_{2} \cos \phi}{/tex}
As intensity {tex}\propto{/tex} (amplitude)2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = ka2 = k ({tex}a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}{/tex} + 2a1a2 cos {tex}\phi{/tex})
When {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0, cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 1 and the intensity is maximum,
Imax = k ({tex}a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}{/tex} + 2a1a2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) = k (a1 + a2)2
When {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex}, cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = -1, the intensity is minimum,
Imin = k ({tex}a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}{/tex} - 2a1a2) = k (a1 - a2)2
{tex}\therefore\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{k\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}}{k\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}}=\left(\frac{a_{1}+a_{2}}{a_{1}-a_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex}

question_html: In Young's double-slit experiment, two slits are separated by 3 mm distance and illuminated by the light of wavelength 480 nm. The screen is 2 m from the plane of the slits. Calculate the separation between the 8th bright fringe and the 3rd dark fringe observed with respect to the central bright fringe.
question_text: In Young's double-slit experiment, two slits are separated by 3 mm distance and illuminated by the light of wavelength 480 nm. The screen is 2 m from the plane of the slits. Calculate the separation between the 8th bright fringe and the 3rd dark fringe observed with respect to the central bright fringe.
explanation: Required distance = x 8 - {tex}x_{3}^{\prime}=\frac{8 D \lambda}{d}-\frac{5 D \lambda}{2 d}=\frac{11 D \lambda}{2 d}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{11 \times 2 \times 480 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.76 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 1.76 mm.
solution: Required distance = x 8 - {tex}x_{3}^{\prime}=\frac{8 D \lambda}{d}-\frac{5 D \lambda}{2 d}=\frac{11 D \lambda}{2 d}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{11 \times 2 \times 480 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.76 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 1.76 mm.
solution_html:
Required distance
= x8 - {tex}x_{3}^{\prime}=\frac{8 D \lambda}{d}-\frac{5 D \lambda}{2 d}=\frac{11 D \lambda}{2 d}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{11 \times 2 \times 480 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 1.76 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m = 1.76 mm.

subject: Physics
question_html: Obtain the relation, a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} for the first minimum of the diffraction pattern of a single slit of width a using light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
question_text: Obtain the relation, a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} for the first minimum of the diffraction pattern of a single slit of width a using light of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\because{/tex} from the diagram the path difference between the waves from L and N = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Where 'a' is the slit separation The path difference = {tex}\lambda{/tex} when the first minimum is obtained at P (Imagine the slit be divided into two halves, for each wavelet from the first half of the slit has a corresponding wavelet from the second half of slit differing by a path of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} and here cancel each other) Hence, the condition for the first minimum would be: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
solution: {tex}\because{/tex} from the diagram the path difference between the waves from L and N = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Where 'a' is the slit separation The path difference = {tex}\lambda{/tex} when the first minimum is obtained at P (Imagine the slit be divided into two halves, for each wavelet from the first half of the slit has a corresponding wavelet from the second half of slit differing by a path of {tex}\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} and here cancel each other) Hence, the condition for the first minimum would be: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}

{tex}\because{/tex} from the diagram the path difference between the waves from L and N = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
Where 'a' is the slit separation

subject: Physics
question_html:
What changes in the interference pattern in Young's double-slit experiment will be observed when

  1. the light of smaller frequency is used?
  2. the apparatus is immersed in water?
question_text: What changes in the interference pattern in Young's double-slit experiment will be observed when the light of smaller frequency is used? the apparatus is immersed in water?
explanation: In Young's double-slit experiment, the fringe width is given by {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{D c}{d \nu}{/tex} Clearly, When light of smaller frequency ( {tex}\nu{/tex} ) is used, fringe width increases. Wavelength of light in water decreases, so fringe width {tex}(\beta \propto \lambda){/tex} also decreases.
solution: In Young's double-slit experiment, the fringe width is given by {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{D c}{d \nu}{/tex} Clearly, When light of smaller frequency ( {tex}\nu{/tex} ) is used, fringe width increases. Wavelength of light in water decreases, so fringe width {tex}(\beta \propto \lambda){/tex} also decreases.
solution_html:
In Young's double-slit experiment, the fringe width is given by {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}=\frac{D c}{d \nu}{/tex}
Clearly,

  1. When light of smaller frequency ({tex}\nu{/tex}) is used, fringe width increases.
  2. Wavelength of light in water decreases, so fringe width {tex}(\beta \propto \lambda){/tex} also decreases.

question_html: A narrow monochromatic beam of light of intensity I is incident on a glass plate. Another identical glass plate is kept close to the first one and parallel to it. Each plate reflects 25% of the incident light and transmits the remaining. Calculate the ratio of minimum and maximum intensity in the interference pattern formed by two beams obtained after reflection from each plate.
question_text: A narrow monochromatic beam of light of intensity I is incident on a glass plate. Another identical glass plate is kept close to the first one and parallel to it. Each plate reflects 25% of the incident light and transmits the remaining. Calculate the ratio of minimum and maximum intensity in the interference pattern formed by two beams obtained after reflection from each plate.
explanation: Let I be the intensity of beam 1 incident on the first glass plate. Each plate reflects 25% of light incident on it and transmits 75%. Therefore, I 1 = I; I 2 = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{I}{4}{/tex} I 3 = {tex}\frac{75}{100}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} I I 4 = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex} I 3 = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{3}{16}{/tex} I I 5 = {tex}\frac{75}{100}{/tex} I 4 = {tex}\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{3}{16}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{9}{64}{/tex} I {tex}\therefore{/tex} Amplitude ratio of beams 2 and 5 is r = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{I_{2}}{I_{5}}}=\sqrt{\frac{I}{4} \times \frac{64}{9 I}}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{\min }}{I_{\max }}=\left[\frac{r-1}{r+1}\right]^{2}=\left[\frac{\frac{4}{3}-1}{\frac{4}{3}+1}\right]^{2}=\frac{1}{49}{/tex} = 1 : 49
solution: Let I be the intensity of beam 1 incident on the first glass plate. Each plate reflects 25% of light incident on it and transmits 75%. Therefore, I 1 = I; I 2 = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{I}{4}{/tex} I 3 = {tex}\frac{75}{100}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} I I 4 = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex} I 3 = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{3}{16}{/tex} I I 5 = {tex}\frac{75}{100}{/tex} I 4 = {tex}\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{3}{16}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{9}{64}{/tex} I {tex}\therefore{/tex} Amplitude ratio of beams 2 and 5 is r = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{I_{2}}{I_{5}}}=\sqrt{\frac{I}{4} \times \frac{64}{9 I}}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_{\min }}{I_{\max }}=\left[\frac{r-1}{r+1}\right]^{2}=\left[\frac{\frac{4}{3}-1}{\frac{4}{3}+1}\right]^{2}=\frac{1}{49}{/tex} = 1 : 49
solution_html:
Let I be the intensity of beam 1 incident on the first glass plate. Each plate reflects 25% of light incident on it and transmits 75%.
Therefore,
I1 = I;
I2 = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{I}{4}{/tex}
I3 = {tex}\frac{75}{100}{/tex}I = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}I
I4 = {tex}\frac{25}{100}{/tex} I3 = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{3}{16}{/tex} I
I5 = {tex}\frac{75}{100}{/tex} I4 = {tex}\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{3}{16}{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{9}{64}{/tex} I

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Amplitude ratio of beams 2 and 5 is
r = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{I_{2}}{I_{5}}}=\sqrt{\frac{I}{4} \times \frac{64}{9 I}}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{I_{\min }}{I_{\max }}=\left[\frac{r-1}{r+1}\right]^{2}=\left[\frac{\frac{4}{3}-1}{\frac{4}{3}+1}\right]^{2}=\frac{1}{49}{/tex} = 1 : 49

question_html: When a thin transparent film is placed just in front of one of the slits in Young's double-slit experiment using white light, what change results in the fringe system?
question_text: When a thin transparent film is placed just in front of one of the slits in Young's double-slit experiment using white light, what change results in the fringe system?
explanation: If {tex}\mu{/tex} is the refractive index and t the thickness of the thin film, then the entire interference pattern gets displaced by distance, {tex}\Delta x=\frac{D}{d}{/tex} ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1)t As {tex}\mu{/tex} depend on {tex}\lambda{/tex} (increase with decreasing {tex}\lambda{/tex} ), the violet fringe is shifted farther than the red fringe. So there is a kind of dispersion in the central white fringe.
solution: If {tex}\mu{/tex} is the refractive index and t the thickness of the thin film, then the entire interference pattern gets displaced by distance, {tex}\Delta x=\frac{D}{d}{/tex} ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1)t As {tex}\mu{/tex} depend on {tex}\lambda{/tex} (increase with decreasing {tex}\lambda{/tex} ), the violet fringe is shifted farther than the red fringe. So there is a kind of dispersion in the central white fringe.
solution_html:
If {tex}\mu{/tex} is the refractive index and t the thickness of the thin film, then the entire interference pattern gets displaced by distance,
{tex}\Delta x=\frac{D}{d}{/tex} ({tex}\mu{/tex} - 1)t
As {tex}\mu{/tex} depend on {tex}\lambda{/tex} (increase with decreasing {tex}\lambda{/tex}), the violet fringe is shifted farther than the red fringe. So there is a kind of dispersion in the central white fringe.

subject: Physics
question_html: What is meant by the term interference of light? Write any two conditions necessary for obtaining well-defined and sustained interference pattern of light.
question_text: What is meant by the term interference of light? Write any two conditions necessary for obtaining well-defined and sustained interference pattern of light.
explanation: When two light waves from different coherent sources meet together, then the distribution of energy due to one wave is disturbed by the other. This modification in the distribution of light energy due to super- position of two light waves is called Interference of light. Conditions for sustained interference pattern: The two sources of light must be coherent with same frequency or wavelength and with same phase or constant phase difference. The two sources should preferably have the same amplitude.
solution: When two light waves from different coherent sources meet together, then the distribution of energy due to one wave is disturbed by the other. This modification in the distribution of light energy due to super- position of two light waves is called Interference of light. Conditions for sustained interference pattern: The two sources of light must be coherent with same frequency or wavelength and with same phase or constant phase difference. The two sources should preferably have the same amplitude.
solution_html:
When two light waves from different coherent sources meet together, then the distribution of energy due to one wave is disturbed by the other. This modification in the distribution of light energy due to super- position of two light waves is called Interference of light.
Conditions for sustained interference pattern:

  1. The two sources of light must be coherent with same frequency or wavelength and with same phase or constant phase difference.
  2. The two sources should preferably have the same amplitude.

question_text: Consider a monochromatic ray incident on a film of uniform thickness t and refractive index {tex}\mu{/tex} . Derive the condition for a dark fringe, when viewed from the same side of the film.

solution: Correct option is D. {tex}\lambda(\mu-1){/tex} Shift = {tex}\Delta \text X{/tex} Width of one fringe = {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Delta \text X{/tex} = {tex}(\mu-1) t=1 \lambda{/tex} For one maximum shift: t = {tex}\frac {\lambda}{\mu-1}{/tex}

Width of one fringe = {tex}\lambda{/tex}
{tex}\Delta \text X{/tex} = {tex}(\mu-1) t=1 \lambda{/tex}
For one maximum shift:
t = {tex}\frac {\lambda}{\mu-1}{/tex}

question_html: The refractive index of glass is 1.5 and that of water is 1.3, the speed of light in water is 2.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1. What is the speed of light in glass?
question_text: The refractive index of glass is 1.5 and that of water is 1.3, the speed of light in water is 2.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 . What is the speed of light in glass?
explanation: We know that refractive index of medium is n = {tex}\frac{speed\; of \;light\; in \;vaccum}{phase \;velocity\; of\; light\; in\; vacuum}{/tex} n = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{c}}{\mathbf{v}}{/tex} , c is the velocity of light in vaccum and v is the velocity in that medium n = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{c}}{\mathbf{v}}{/tex} 1.3 = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{c}}{2.25 \times 10^{8}}{/tex} c = 2.925 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s For glass: c = 2.925 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s n glass = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{C}}{\mathbf{V}_{\text {glass }}}{/tex} 1.5 = {tex}\frac{2.925 \times 10^{8}}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{glass}}}{/tex} v glass = 1.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s
solution: We know that refractive index of medium is n = {tex}\frac{speed\; of \;light\; in \;vaccum}{phase \;velocity\; of\; light\; in\; vacuum}{/tex} n = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{c}}{\mathbf{v}}{/tex} , c is the velocity of light in vaccum and v is the velocity in that medium n = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{c}}{\mathbf{v}}{/tex} 1.3 = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{c}}{2.25 \times 10^{8}}{/tex} c = 2.925 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s For glass: c = 2.925 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s n glass = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{C}}{\mathbf{V}_{\text {glass }}}{/tex} 1.5 = {tex}\frac{2.925 \times 10^{8}}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{glass}}}{/tex} v glass = 1.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s
solution_html:
We know that refractive index of medium is n = {tex}\frac{speed\; of \;light\; in \;vaccum}{phase \;velocity\; of\; light\; in\; vacuum}{/tex}
n = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{c}}{\mathbf{v}}{/tex},
c  is the velocity of light in vaccum and v is the velocity in that medium
n = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{c}}{\mathbf{v}}{/tex}
1.3 = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{c}}{2.25 \times 10^{8}}{/tex}
c = 2.925 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s
For glass:
c = 2.925 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s
nglass = {tex}\frac{\mathbf{C}}{\mathbf{V}_{\text {glass }}}{/tex}
1.5 = {tex}\frac{2.925 \times 10^{8}}{\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{glass}}}{/tex}
vglass = 1.95 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s

question_html: The angular width of a central maximum in the Fraunhofer diffraction pattern of a slit is measured. The slit is illuminated by the light of wavelength 6000 {tex}\overset \circ{A}{/tex}. When the slit is illuminated by the light of another wavelength, the angular width decreases by 30%. Calculate the wavelength of this light. The same decrease in the angular-width of the central maximum is obtained when the original apparatus is immersed in a liquid. Find the refractive index of the liquid.
question_text: The angular width of a central maximum in the Fraunhofer diffraction pattern of a slit is measured. The slit is illuminated by the light of wavelength 6000 {tex}\overset \circ{A}{/tex} . When the slit is illuminated by the light of another wavelength, the angular width decreases by 30%. Calculate the wavelength of this light. The same decrease in the angular-width of the central maximum is obtained when the original apparatus is immersed in a liquid. Find the refractive index of the liquid.
explanation: In single slit diffraction, first minimum occurs at a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} or sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} As {tex}\lambda{/tex} << a, so {tex}\theta \simeq{/tex} sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of the central maximum is {tex}\phi=2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\phi_{2}}{\phi_{1}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda_{2}=\frac{\phi_{2}}{\phi_{1}} \cdot \lambda_{1}=\frac{70}{100} \times{/tex} 6000 = 4200 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex} [ {tex}\because \phi_{2}{/tex} = 70% of {tex}\phi_{1}{/tex} ] When the apparatus is immersed in the liquid, the decrease in angular width is the same. This indicates that the wavelength of light in the liquid is also 4200 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex} . {tex}\mu=\frac{\lambda}{\lambda_{l}}=\frac{6000}{4200}{/tex} = 1.43
solution: In single slit diffraction, first minimum occurs at a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} or sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} As {tex}\lambda{/tex} << a, so {tex}\theta \simeq{/tex} sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex} The angular width of the central maximum is {tex}\phi=2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\phi_{2}}{\phi_{1}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda_{2}=\frac{\phi_{2}}{\phi_{1}} \cdot \lambda_{1}=\frac{70}{100} \times{/tex} 6000 = 4200 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex} [ {tex}\because \phi_{2}{/tex} = 70% of {tex}\phi_{1}{/tex} ] When the apparatus is immersed in the liquid, the decrease in angular width is the same. This indicates that the wavelength of light in the liquid is also 4200 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex} . {tex}\mu=\frac{\lambda}{\lambda_{l}}=\frac{6000}{4200}{/tex} = 1.43
solution_html:
In single slit diffraction, first minimum occurs at
a sin {tex}\theta=\lambda{/tex} or sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}
As {tex}\lambda{/tex} << a, so {tex}\theta \simeq{/tex} sin {tex}\theta=\frac{\lambda}{a}{/tex}

The angular width of the central maximum is
{tex}\phi=2 \theta=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\phi_{2}}{\phi_{1}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex}
or {tex}\lambda_{2}=\frac{\phi_{2}}{\phi_{1}} \cdot \lambda_{1}=\frac{70}{100} \times{/tex} 6000 = 4200 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex} [{tex}\because \phi_{2}{/tex} = 70% of {tex}\phi_{1}{/tex}]
When the apparatus is immersed in the liquid, the decrease in angular width is the same. This indicates that the wavelength of light in the liquid is also 4200 {tex}\overset \circ {A}{/tex}.
{tex}\mu=\frac{\lambda}{\lambda_{l}}=\frac{6000}{4200}{/tex} = 1.43

subject: Physics
question_html: In a Young's double experiment, the slits are 1.5 mm apart. When the slits are illuminated by a monochromatic light source and the screen is kept 1 m apart from the slits, width of 10 fringes is measured as 3.93 mm. Calculate the wavelength of light used. What will be the width of 10 fringes when the distance between the slits and the screen is increased by 0.5 m. The source of light used remains the same.
question_text: In a Young's double experiment, the slits are 1.5 mm apart. When the slits are illuminated by a monochromatic light source and the screen is kept 1 m apart from the slits, width of 10 fringes is measured as 3.93 mm. Calculate the wavelength of light used. What will be the width of 10 fringes when the distance between the slits and the screen is increased by 0.5 m. The source of light used remains the same.
explanation: In first case : d = 1.5 mm = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 1 m Width of 10 fringes = 3.93 mm {tex}\therefore{/tex} Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{3.93}{10}{/tex} = 0.393 mm = 0.393 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m Wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{\beta d}{D}=\frac{0.393 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1}{/tex} = 5.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m In second case : D' = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5 m, d = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m Width of 10 fringes = 10 {tex}\beta^{\prime}=\frac{10 D^{\prime} \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10 \times 1.5 \times 5.895 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 5.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m
solution: In first case : d = 1.5 mm = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 1 m Width of 10 fringes = 3.93 mm {tex}\therefore{/tex} Fringe width, {tex}\beta=\frac{3.93}{10}{/tex} = 0.393 mm = 0.393 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m Wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{\beta d}{D}=\frac{0.393 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1}{/tex} = 5.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m In second case : D' = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5 m, d = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m Width of 10 fringes = 10 {tex}\beta^{\prime}=\frac{10 D^{\prime} \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10 \times 1.5 \times 5.895 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 5.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m
solution_html:
In first case :
d = 1.5 mm = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m, D = 1 m
Width of 10 fringes = 3.93 mm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Fringe width,
{tex}\beta=\frac{3.93}{10}{/tex} = 0.393 mm = 0.393 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m
Wavelength,
{tex}\lambda=\frac{\beta d}{D}=\frac{0.393 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1}{/tex}
= 5.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m
In second case :
D' = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5 m,
d = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m
Width of 10 fringes = 10 {tex}\beta^{\prime}=\frac{10 D^{\prime} \lambda}{d}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{10 \times 1.5 \times 5.895 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 5.895 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m

question_html:
Red light of wavelength 750 nm enters a glass plate of refractive index 1.5. If the velocity of light in a vacuum is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1, calculate in the glass

  1. frequency
  2. velocity and
  3. wavelength of light.
question_text: Red light of wavelength 750 nm enters a glass plate of refractive index 1.5. If the velocity of light in a vacuum is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 , calculate in the glass frequency velocity and wavelength of light.
explanation: Given, Refractive index of the glass plate, n g = 1.5 Wavelength of light in vacuum, {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{v}}{/tex} = 750 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m Velocity of light in vacuum, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 Frequency of light in vacuum, v = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda_{v}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}}{750 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz For the light refracted in glass, frequency v remains unchanged but, wavelength and speed changes. Frequency of light in glass = Frequency of light in vacuum = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz Velocity of light in glass, v g = {tex}\frac{c}{n_{g}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{1.5}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\frac{c}{n_{g}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{1.5}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 Wavelength of light in glass, {tex}\lambda_{g}=\frac{\lambda_{v}}{n_{g}}=\frac{750 \mathrm{nm}}{1.5}{/tex} = 500 nm
solution: Given, Refractive index of the glass plate, n g = 1.5 Wavelength of light in vacuum, {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{v}}{/tex} = 750 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m Velocity of light in vacuum, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 Frequency of light in vacuum, v = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda_{v}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}}{750 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz For the light refracted in glass, frequency v remains unchanged but, wavelength and speed changes. Frequency of light in glass = Frequency of light in vacuum = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz Velocity of light in glass, v g = {tex}\frac{c}{n_{g}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{1.5}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\frac{c}{n_{g}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{1.5}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 Wavelength of light in glass, {tex}\lambda_{g}=\frac{\lambda_{v}}{n_{g}}=\frac{750 \mathrm{nm}}{1.5}{/tex} = 500 nm
solution_html:
Given,
Refractive index of the glass plate, ng = 1.5
Wavelength  of light in vacuum, {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{v}}{/tex} = 750 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m
Velocity of light in vacuum, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1
Frequency of light in vacuum,
v = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda_{v}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}}{750 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{/tex}
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz
For the light refracted in glass, frequency v remains unchanged but, wavelength and speed changes.

  1. Frequency of  light in glass = Frequency of light in vacuum = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz
  2. Velocity of light in glass, vg = {tex}\frac{c}{n_{g}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{1.5}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\frac{c}{n_{g}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8} m s^{-1}}{1.5}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1
  3. Wavelength  of light in glass, {tex}\lambda_{g}=\frac{\lambda_{v}}{n_{g}}=\frac{750 \mathrm{nm}}{1.5}{/tex} = 500 nm

question_html: In Young's double-slit experiment, the slits are 0.2 mm apart and the screen is 1.5 m away. It is observed that the distance between the central bright fringe and the fourth dark fringe is 1.8 cm. Find the wavelength of light used.
question_text: In Young's double-slit experiment, the slits are 0.2 mm apart and the screen is 1.5 m away. It is observed that the distance between the central bright fringe and the fourth dark fringe is 1.8 cm. Find the wavelength of light used.
explanation: Here d =0.2 mm =0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 1.5 m, {tex}x_{4}^{\prime}{/tex} = 1.8 cm = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m The distance of nth dark fringe from the central bright fringe is given by {tex}x_{n}^{\prime}{/tex} = (2n - 1) {tex}\frac{D \lambda}{2 d}{/tex} , {tex}\therefore x_{4}^{\prime}=\frac{7}{2} \cdot \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 d x_{4}^{\prime}}{7 D}=\frac{2 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.8 \times 10^{-2}}{7 \times 1.5}{/tex} = 6.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m
solution: Here d =0.2 mm =0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 1.5 m, {tex}x_{4}^{\prime}{/tex} = 1.8 cm = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m The distance of nth dark fringe from the central bright fringe is given by {tex}x_{n}^{\prime}{/tex} = (2n - 1) {tex}\frac{D \lambda}{2 d}{/tex} , {tex}\therefore x_{4}^{\prime}=\frac{7}{2} \cdot \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 d x_{4}^{\prime}}{7 D}=\frac{2 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.8 \times 10^{-2}}{7 \times 1.5}{/tex} = 6.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m
solution_html:
Here d =0.2 mm =0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m,
D = 1.5 m, {tex}x_{4}^{\prime}{/tex} = 1.8 cm = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 m
The distance of nth dark fringe from the central bright fringe is given by
{tex}x_{n}^{\prime}{/tex} = (2n - 1) {tex}\frac{D \lambda}{2 d}{/tex},
{tex}\therefore x_{4}^{\prime}=\frac{7}{2} \cdot \frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
or {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 d x_{4}^{\prime}}{7 D}=\frac{2 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.8 \times 10^{-2}}{7 \times 1.5}{/tex}
= 6.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m

question_html: When two narrow slits separated by a small distance are illuminated by a light of wavelength 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m, interference fringes of width 0.5 mm are obtained on a screen. What should be the wavelength of the light source to obtain fringes 0.3 mm wide, if the distance between the screen and the slits is reduced to half of the initial value.
question_text: When two narrow slits separated by a small distance are illuminated by a light of wavelength 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m, interference fringes of width 0.5 mm are obtained on a screen. What should be the wavelength of the light source to obtain fringes 0.3 mm wide, if the distance between the screen and the slits is reduced to half of the initial value.
explanation: Here {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m, {tex}\beta{/tex} = 0.5 mm = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m As {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{D}=\frac{\lambda}{\beta}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 10 -3 On reducing the distance between the screen and the slits to half of its initial value, {tex}\beta^{\prime}{/tex} = 0.3 mm = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D' = {tex}\frac{D}{2}{/tex} and d' = d {tex}\therefore \lambda^{\prime}=\beta^{\prime} \frac{d^{\prime}}{D^{\prime}}{/tex} = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\times \frac{d}{D / 2}{/tex} = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\frac{d}{D}{/tex} = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m
solution: Here {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m, {tex}\beta{/tex} = 0.5 mm = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m As {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{D}=\frac{\lambda}{\beta}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 10 -3 On reducing the distance between the screen and the slits to half of its initial value, {tex}\beta^{\prime}{/tex} = 0.3 mm = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D' = {tex}\frac{D}{2}{/tex} and d' = d {tex}\therefore \lambda^{\prime}=\beta^{\prime} \frac{d^{\prime}}{D^{\prime}}{/tex} = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\times \frac{d}{D / 2}{/tex} = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\frac{d}{D}{/tex} = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m
solution_html:
Here {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m, {tex}\beta{/tex} = 0.5 mm = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m
As {tex}\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d}{D}=\frac{\lambda}{\beta}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 10-3
On reducing the distance between the screen and the slits to half of its initial value,
{tex}\beta^{\prime}{/tex} = 0.3 mm = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m, D' = {tex}\frac{D}{2}{/tex} and d' = d
{tex}\therefore \lambda^{\prime}=\beta^{\prime} \frac{d^{\prime}}{D^{\prime}}{/tex} = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times \frac{d}{D / 2}{/tex} = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\frac{d}{D}{/tex}
= 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m

subject: Physics
question_html: Explain the statement light added to light can produce darkness.
question_text: Explain the statement light added to light can produce darkness .
explanation: When two light waves of equal amplitude meet at a point in opposite phases, the resultant amplitude and hence intensity become zero at that point. That is when light added to light undergoes destructive interference, it produces darkness.
solution: When two light waves of equal amplitude meet at a point in opposite phases, the resultant amplitude and hence intensity become zero at that point. That is when light added to light undergoes destructive interference, it produces darkness.
solution_html: When two light waves of equal amplitude meet at a point in opposite phases, the resultant amplitude and hence intensity become zero at that point. That is when light added to light undergoes destructive interference, it produces darkness.

subject: Physics
question_html:
In Young's double-slit experiment, red light of wavelength 620 nm is used and the two slits are 0.3 mm apart. Interference fringes observed on a screen are found to be 1.3 mm apart. Calculate

  1. the distance of slits from the screen and
  2. the fringe width if this distance is doubled.
question_text: In Young's double-slit experiment, red light of wavelength 620 nm is used and the two slits are 0.3 mm apart. Interference fringes observed on a screen are found to be 1.3 mm apart. Calculate the distance of slits from the screen and the fringe width if this distance is doubled.
explanation: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 620 nm = 620 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m, d = 0.3 mm = 0.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m {tex}\beta{/tex} = 1.3 mm = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m D = {tex}\frac{\beta d}{\lambda}=\frac{1.3 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{620 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 0.629 m {tex}\beta^{\prime}=\frac{D^{\prime} \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 D \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 \times 0.629 \times 620 \times 10^{-9}}{0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 2.6 mm
solution: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 620 nm = 620 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m, d = 0.3 mm = 0.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m {tex}\beta{/tex} = 1.3 mm = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m D = {tex}\frac{\beta d}{\lambda}=\frac{1.3 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{620 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 0.629 m {tex}\beta^{\prime}=\frac{D^{\prime} \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 D \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 \times 0.629 \times 620 \times 10^{-9}}{0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m = 2.6 mm
solution_html:
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 620 nm = 620 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m, d = 0.3 mm = 0.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m
{tex}\beta{/tex} = 1.3 mm = 1.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m

  1. D = {tex}\frac{\beta d}{\lambda}=\frac{1.3 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{620 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 0.629 m
  2. {tex}\beta^{\prime}=\frac{D^{\prime} \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 D \lambda}{d}=\frac{2 \times 0.629 \times 620 \times 10^{-9}}{0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
    = 2.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m = 2.6 mm

subject: Physics
question_html: A two-slit Young’s interference experiment is done with monochromatic light of wavelength 6000 {tex}\overset \circ{A}{/tex}. The slits are 2 mm apart and fringes are observed on a screen placed 10 cm away from the slits and it is found that the interference pattern shifts by 5 mm, when a transparent plate of thickness 0.5 mm is introduced in the path of one of the slits. What is the refractive index of the transparent plate?
question_text: A two-slit Young’s interference experiment is done with monochromatic light of wavelength 6000 {tex}\overset \circ{A}{/tex} . The slits are 2 mm apart and fringes are observed on a screen placed 10 cm away from the slits and it is found that the interference pattern shifts by 5 mm, when a transparent plate of thickness 0.5 mm is introduced in the path of one of the slits. What is the refractive index of the transparent plate?
explanation: Extra path difference introduced due to insertion of glass plate of thickness t, p = ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1)t {tex}\therefore{/tex} By using, ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1)t = {tex}n \lambda=n \cdot \frac{\beta d}{D}{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}\right){/tex} we get the fringe shift, {tex}\Delta x=n \beta=\frac{D}{d}{/tex} ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) t Now d = 2 mm = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 10 cm = 0.1 m, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6000 {tex}\overset \circ{A}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m, {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = 5 mm = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, t = 0.5 mm = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m {tex}\therefore \quad \mu-1=\frac{\Delta x \cdot d}{D \cdot t}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.2 Hence {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.2
solution: Extra path difference introduced due to insertion of glass plate of thickness t, p = ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1)t {tex}\therefore{/tex} By using, ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1)t = {tex}n \lambda=n \cdot \frac{\beta d}{D}{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}\right){/tex} we get the fringe shift, {tex}\Delta x=n \beta=\frac{D}{d}{/tex} ( {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) t Now d = 2 mm = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, D = 10 cm = 0.1 m, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6000 {tex}\overset \circ{A}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m, {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = 5 mm = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, t = 0.5 mm = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m {tex}\therefore \quad \mu-1=\frac{\Delta x \cdot d}{D \cdot t}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.2 Hence {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.2
solution_html:
Extra path difference introduced due to insertion of glass plate of thickness t,
p = ({tex}\mu{/tex} - 1)t
{tex}\therefore{/tex} By using, ({tex}\mu{/tex} - 1)t = {tex}n \lambda=n \cdot \frac{\beta d}{D}{/tex} {tex}\left(\because \beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}\right){/tex}
we get the fringe shift, {tex}\Delta x=n \beta=\frac{D}{d}{/tex} ({tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) t
Now d = 2 mm = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3m, D = 10 cm = 0.1 m, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6000 {tex}\overset \circ{A}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7m, {tex}\Delta{/tex}x = 5 mm = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3m, t = 0.5 mm = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3m
{tex}\therefore \quad \mu-1=\frac{\Delta x \cdot d}{D \cdot t}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 0.2
Hence {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.2
